topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gaussian Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Gaussian Distribution**, also known as the **Normal Distribution**, is a continuous probability distribution that is symmetric about its mean, depicting that data near the mean are more frequent in occurrence than data far from the mean. In the context of **Pattern Recognition**, the Gaussian distribution is the most widely used parametric model for class-conditional probability densities $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ because of its strong mathematical properties and the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** which justifies its use in modeling real-world feature vectors.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Pattern Recognition, the Gaussian distribution is the foundational building block for **parametric classifiers**, **Bayes decision theory**, and **discriminant functions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Gaussian distributions form the basis of parametric pattern classification. The continuous-valued feature vectors are modeled as $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i, \Sigma_i)$, and the classifier is built upon likelihoods derived from this assumption.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom of **1000 KTU B.Tech students** and you measure their CGPA. Most students will score around **7.0 to 8.0 CGPA** (the peak of the distribution), a moderate number will score around 6.0 or 9.0, and only a **rare few** will score below 4.0 or above 9.5. If you plot a histogram of these scores, the bell-shaped curve you obtain is a **Gaussian distribution**.

- The **center** of the bell represents the average CGPA (mean $\mu$).
- The **width** of the bell represents how spread out the scores are (variance $\sigma^2$).
- A **narrow bell** = consistent batch; a **wide bell** = highly diverse batch.

> [!NOTE]
> In Pattern Recognition, when we measure a feature (e.g., petal length in the famous Iris dataset), we assume the values cluster around a mean with a bell-shaped spread. This assumption allows us to compute the **likelihood** of observing a new feature vector and classify it accordingly.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The normalizing constant $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}$ ensures $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(x) \, dx = \mathbf{1}$.
- The variance $\sigma^2$ is always **non-negative**: $\sigma^2 \geq \mathbf{0}$.
- The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ in the multivariate case must be **symmetric positive semi-definite**.
- The Mahalanobis distance $r^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ is dimensionless and obeys $r^2 \geq \mathbf{0}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Univariate Gaussian Distribution
A scalar random variable $X \in \mathbb{R}$ follows a Gaussian distribution if its **Probability Density Function (PDF)** is given by:

$$
p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

where:
- $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **mean** (location parameter).
- $\sigma^2 > 0$ is the **variance** (scale parameter).
- Notation: $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

### 2.1.2 Multivariate (d-Dimensional) Gaussian Distribution
For a feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the PDF generalizes to:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\, \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)
$$

where:
- $\boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the **mean vector**.
- $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ is the **covariance matrix** (must be symmetric positive definite).
- $\vert \Sigma \vert$ is the **determinant** of $\Sigma$.

### 2.1.3 Key Properties
- **Symmetry**: $p(\boldsymbol{\mu} + \delta) = p(\boldsymbol{\mu} - \delta)$ for any $\delta$.
- **Mode = Mean = Median**: All measures of central tendency coincide.
- **Linear Transformation**: If $\mathbf{x} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \Sigma)$ and $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$, then $\mathbf{y} \sim \mathcal{N}(A\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b},\, A\Sigma A^T)$.
- **Marginal & Conditional Distributions** of a multivariate Gaussian are themselves Gaussian.
- **Mahalanobis Distance**: $r^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ measures the distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ in units of standard deviation.

## 2.2 Why & How: The Intuition Behind Each Step

- **Why Normalize?**: The factor $\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}}$ ensures the total probability integrates to 1.
- **Why $\Sigma^{-1}$?**: The inverse covariance acts as a **distance metric**. When $\Sigma = \sigma^2 I$ (isotropic case), Mahalanobis distance reduces to Euclidean distance scaled by $1/\sigma^2$.
- **How Does Classification Use It?**: In a **minimum-error-rate Bayes classifier**, we compute $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \cdot P(\omega_i)$ for each class. A Gaussian assumption gives a **closed-form discriminant function** that is **quadratic** in $\mathbf{x}$ (QDF) or **linear** (LDF) when covariances are shared.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition**: Spectral features modeled as Gaussians in GMM-HMM systems.
- **Biometric Authentication**: Face and fingerprint features assumed Gaussian.
- **Medical Diagnostics**: Patient test results (e.g., cholesterol levels) modeled as Gaussian for anomaly detection.
- **Radar & SONAR**: Signal returns modeled as Gaussian for target detection.
- **Quality Control in Manufacturing**: Defect detection via Mahalanobis distance from the mean.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Univariate PDF | $p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$, $\sigma > 0$ |
| 2 | Multivariate PDF | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| 3 | MLE Mean | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_k$ | Sample mean |
| 4 | MLE Covariance | $\hat{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(\mathbf{x}_k-\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}})(\mathbf{x}_k-\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}})^T$ | $d \times d$ matrix |
| 5 | Mahalanobis Distance | $r^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ | Scalar, $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | Bayes Discriminant | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T\Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) - \frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_i \vert + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | QDF |
| 7 | LDF (equal $\Sigma$) | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T\mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$ | Linear boundary |
| 8 | Linear Transform | $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b} \Rightarrow \mathbf{y}\sim \mathcal{N}(A\boldsymbol{\mu}+\mathbf{b}, A\Sigma A^T)$ | Matrix result |
| 9 | Whitening Transform | $\mathbf{y} = \Lambda^{-1/2}U^T(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ | Yields $\mathbf{y}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I)$ |
| 10 | Bhattacharyya Bound | $P(\text{error}) \leq \sqrt{P(\omega_1)P(\omega_2)}\,\exp(-\kappa)$ | Chernoff bound |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MLE for the Mean of a Univariate Gaussian

**Setup:** Given $N$ i.i.d. samples $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$ drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the joint likelihood is:

$$
L(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} p(x_k \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_k-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

The log-likelihood is more convenient:

$$
\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = \ln L = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi) - N\ln\sigma - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\mu)^2
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k - \mu) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\mu$:**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k - \mu) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} x_k - N\mu = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} x_k
$$

**Intuition:** The MLE estimate of the mean is simply the **arithmetic average** of the samples.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MLE for the Variance of a Univariate Gaussian

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\ell$ w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\mu)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\sigma^2$:**

$$
\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\mu)^2
$$

$$
N\sigma^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\mu)^2
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\hat{\mu})^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction (KTU Favorite Trick Question):** The MLE estimator $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}$ uses $N$ in the denominator and is **biased** (its expected value is $\frac{N-1}{N}\sigma^2$). The **unbiased** estimator uses $N-1$. The bias vanishes as $N \to \infty$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bivariate Gaussian Density (d = 2 Case)

For $d=2$, the covariance matrix is:

$$
\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1^2 & \sigma_{12} \\ \sigma_{12} & \sigma_2^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The determinant is:

$$
\vert \Sigma \vert = \sigma_1^2\sigma_2^2 - \sigma_{12}^2
$$

The inverse is:

$$
\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{\vert \Sigma \vert}\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_2^2 & -\sigma_{12} \\ -\sigma_{12} & \sigma_1^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The bivariate PDF becomes:

$$
p(x_1, x_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{\sigma_1^2\sigma_2^2 - \sigma_{12}^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\sigma_2^2(x_1-\mu_1)^2 - 2\sigma_{12}(x_1-\mu_1)(x_2-\mu_2) + \sigma_1^2(x_2-\mu_2)^2}{2(\sigma_1^2\sigma_2^2 - \sigma_{12}^2)}\right)
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Discriminant Function for Two Gaussian Classes

**Setup:** Two classes $\omega_1, \omega_2$ with Gaussian likelihoods $p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_i) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i, \Sigma_i)$ and priors $P(\omega_i)$.

The Bayes decision rule is to assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_1$ if $P(\omega_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) > P(\omega_2 \mid \mathbf{x})$, i.e.:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_1)P(\omega_1) > p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_2)P(\omega_2)
$$

**Step 1 — Take natural log of both sides (monotonic):**

$$
\ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_1) + \ln P(\omega_1) > \ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_2) + \ln P(\omega_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Gaussian log-density:**

$$
-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_1 \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1)^T\Sigma_1^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1) + \ln P(\omega_1) - \frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi
$$

$$
>
$$

$$
-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_2 \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T\Sigma_2^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2) + \ln P(\omega_2) - \frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel common $-\frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi$ terms and rearrange:**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1)^T\Sigma_1^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1) + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T\Sigma_2^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)
$$

$$
- \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{\vert \Sigma_1 \vert}{\vert \Sigma_2 \vert} + \ln\frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the quadratic terms:**

$$
(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T\Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \mathbf{x}^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i
$$

**Result:** The discriminant function is a **quadratic polynomial in $\mathbf{x}$**, yielding a **Quadratic Discriminant Function (QDF)** with a hyperquadric decision boundary (hyperplane, hyperellipsoid, hyperparaboloid, or hyperhyperboloid).

**Special Case — Linear Discriminant Function (LDF):** If $\Sigma_1 = \Sigma_2 = \Sigma$ (common covariance), then $\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ terms cancel, and we get:

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \ln\frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}
$$

This is **linear in $\mathbf{x}$**, giving a **hyperplane** decision boundary.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Mahalanobis Distance and Contours of Constant Density

The set of points $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying $p(\mathbf{x}) = c$ (constant) is:

$$
(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) = -2\ln\!\left(c\,(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}\right) = r^2
$$

This is the equation of an **ellipsoid** centered at $\boldsymbol{\mu}$, with principal axes along the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$, and axis lengths proportional to $\sqrt{\lambda_i}\,r$, where $\lambda_i$ are eigenvalues of $\Sigma$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The Mahalanobis distance $r^2$ follows a **chi-squared ($\chi^2_d$) distribution** with $d$ degrees of freedom. The probability of $\mathbf{x}$ lying inside the ellipsoid $r^2 \leq c$ is $P(\chi^2_d \leq c)$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Gaussian Density Computation Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the **end-to-end computational flow** for evaluating a multivariate Gaussian density at a test point $\mathbf{x}$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Vector x in R^d] --> B[Compute Mean Vector mu]
    B --> C[Subtract Mean: delta = x - mu]
    C --> D[Compute Inverse Covariance Sigma Inv]
    D --> E[Quadratic Form: delta transpose times Sigma Inv times delta]
    E --> F[Mahalanobis Squared Distance r squared]
    F --> G[Exponentiate Negative Half: exp of negative r squared by 2]
    H[Determinant of Sigma] --> I[Normalization Constant: 1 over 2 pi power d by 2 times sqrt of det Sigma]
    I --> J[Multiply by Exponent]
    G --> J
    J --> K[Final Output: Probability Density p of x]
    
    style A fill:#E1F5FF,stroke:#0277BD
    style K fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style F fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style J fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
```

## 4.2 Architecture Flow: Bayes Classifier with Gaussian Class-Conditional Densities

This diagram maps the **functional pipeline** of a parametric Bayes classifier built upon Gaussian assumptions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Training Samples Class omega 1] --> M1[Estimate mu 1 and Sigma 1]
    P2[Training Samples Class omega 2] --> M2[Estimate mu 2 and Sigma 2]
    
    M1 --> D1[Compute Gaussian Likelihood p of x given omega 1]
    M2 --> D2[Compute Gaussian Likelihood p of x given omega 2]
    
    PR1[Prior P of omega 1] --> C1[Posterior Product: Likelihood times Prior]
    PR2[Prior P of omega 2] --> C2[Posterior Product: Likelihood times Prior]
    
    D1 --> C1
    D2 --> C2
    
    C1 --> COMP[Compare Posterior Values]
    C2 --> COMP
    
    COMP --> DEC{Decision Rule}
    DEC -->|Class 1| OUT1[Assign to omega 1]
    DEC -->|Class 2| OUT2[Assign to omega 2]
    
    style M1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
    style M2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style DEC fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17
    style OUT1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style OUT2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
```
