# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: State the properties of the multivariate normal distribution. 
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
1. It is **completely specified** by its mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and covariance matrix $\Sigma$.
2. The marginal and conditional distributions are **also normal**.
3. The Mahalanobis distance $(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ follows a $\chi^2_d$ distribution.
4. Linear transformation of a Gaussian yields a Gaussian: $\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{b} \sim \mathcal{N}(A\boldsymbol{\mu}+\mathbf{b}, A\Sigma A^T)$.
5. The level sets of constant density are **ellipsoids** defined by the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between univariate and multivariate Gaussian distribution. 
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Univariate | Multivariate |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Dimension | $d=1$ | $d > 1$ |
| Parameters | $\mu$, $\sigma^2$ (2 scalars) | $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ ($d$ scalars), $\Sigma$ ($d(d+1)/2$ unique scalars) |
| Density Form | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2})$ | $\frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}}\exp(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}))$ |
| Geometry | 1-D bell curve | Multivariate ellipsoidal contours |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Derive the Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) of the mean and variance for a univariate Gaussian distribution. State whether the MLE for variance is biased. (7 Marks)** 
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Model Solution:**

Given samples $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the joint likelihood is:

$$
L(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{k=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_k-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Log-likelihood:**

$$
\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi) - N\ln\sigma - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\mu)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\mu) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$
\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} x_k
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\sigma^2$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\mu)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sigma^2$:**

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\hat{\mu})^2
$$

**Step 6 — Bias Analysis:**

$$
E[\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}] = E\!\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\hat{\mu})^2\right] = \frac{N-1}{N}\sigma^2
$$

Since $E[\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}] \neq \sigma^2$, the MLE estimate is **biased**. The unbiased estimator uses $N-1$ in the denominator: $s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_k-\hat{\mu})^2$.

---

**`(b)` For a two-class problem with class-conditional densities $p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_i) = \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i, \Sigma_i)$, derive the Bayes quadratic discriminant function. When does it reduce to a linear discriminant function? (7 Marks)** 
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bayes Decision Rule:**

Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_1$ if $p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_1)P(\omega_1) > p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_2)P(\omega_2)$.

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$
\ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_1) + \ln P(\omega_1) > \ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_2) + \ln P(\omega_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute multivariate Gaussian log-density:**

$$
-\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_1 \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1)^T\Sigma_1^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1) + \ln P(\omega_1)
$$

$$
>
$$

$$
-\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_2 \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T\Sigma_2^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2) + \ln P(\omega_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $-\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi)$ and rearrange:**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1)^T\Sigma_1^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_1) + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T\Sigma_2^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)
$$

$$
- \frac{1}{2}\ln \frac{\vert \Sigma_1 \vert}{\vert \Sigma_2 \vert} + \ln \frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}
$$

**Step 5 — Expand quadratics:**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma_1^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^T\Sigma_1^{-1}\mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^T\Sigma_1^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_1
$$

$$
+ \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma_2^{-1}\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^T\Sigma_2^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^T\Sigma_2^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_2
$$

$$
- \frac{1}{2}\ln \frac{\vert \Sigma_1 \vert}{\vert \Sigma_2 \vert} + \ln \frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}
$$

This is the **Quadratic Discriminant Function (QDF)**.

**Step 6 — Linear Reduction Condition:**

If $\Sigma_1 = \Sigma_2 = \Sigma$, then the $\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ terms **cancel exactly**, leaving:

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_1) + \ln \frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}
$$

This is a **Linear Discriminant Function (LDF)** with a hyperplane decision boundary.

**Additionally, if $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2)$ (equal priors) and $\Sigma_1 = \Sigma_2$, the LDF further reduces to a simple distance-based classifier** assigning $\mathbf{x}$ to the nearest mean in the Mahalanobis sense.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`(a)` Explain the concept of Mahalanobis distance. Derive its relationship with the chi-squared distribution and state its role in anomaly detection. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**

The Mahalanobis distance from a point $\mathbf{x}$ to a distribution $\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \Sigma)$ is:

$$
r^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric Interpretation:**

$r^2$ measures the squared distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to the mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$, **normalized** by the covariance structure. When $\Sigma = I$, it reduces to the Euclidean distance.

**Step 3 — Chi-Squared Relationship:**

Consider the change of variable $\mathbf{y} = \Sigma^{-1/2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$, where $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ is the matrix square root of $\Sigma^{-1}$. Then:

$$
r^2 = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} y_i^2
$$

Since $\mathbf{y} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, I)$, each $y_i$ is an independent standard normal, and $r^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d} y_i^2$ follows a **chi-squared distribution with $d$ degrees of freedom**: $r^2 \sim \chi^2_d$.

**Step 4 — Anomaly Detection Rule:**

A point is declared an **anomaly** if $r^2 > \chi^2_{d,\alpha}$, the upper $(1-\alpha)$ quantile of the chi-squared distribution. Common threshold: $\alpha = 0.01$ gives 99% confidence.

**Application:** In industrial quality control, a manufactured component whose features lie outside the $r^2 = \chi^2_{d, 0.99}$ ellipsoid is flagged as defective.

---

**`(b)` Given two 2-D Gaussian classes with means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1, 2)^T$, $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (4, 6)^T$ and equal isotropic covariance $\Sigma = 2I$, and equal priors $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2) = 0.5$, derive the decision boundary equation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Equal covariance and equal priors imply the LDF applies.

**Step 2 — LDF formula with $\Sigma = 2I$:**

Since $\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{2}I$, the discriminant becomes:

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = (\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2)^T \cdot \frac{1}{2}I \cdot \mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^T \cdot \frac{1}{2}I \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^T \cdot \frac{1}{2}I \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}_1)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1-4 \\ 2-6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}(-3x_1 - 4x_2) + \frac{1}{4}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_1)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute constant term:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = 16 + 36 = 52, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = 1 + 4 = 5
$$

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{3}{2}x_1 - 2x_2 + \frac{1}{4}(52 - 5) = -\frac{3}{2}x_1 - 2x_2 + \frac{47}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision boundary** $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$:

$$
-\frac{3}{2}x_1 - 2x_2 + \frac{47}{4} = 0
$$

Multiplying through by 4:

$$
-6x_1 - 8x_2 + 47 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{6x_1 + 8x_2 = 47}
$$

This is the **hyperplane** (in 2-D, a line) that separates the two Gaussian classes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the determinant term** $\ln \vert \Sigma \vert$ when deriving QDF costs **2-3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Sign errors in discriminant derivation**: when you "bring to one side", the sign of $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$ vs $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2$ flips. Always double-check.
> 3. **Bias question trap**: Students often claim MLE variance is unbiased. The correct answer is **biased** (denominator is $N$, not $N-1$).
> 4. **Cholesky vs Eigen for $\Sigma^{-1}$**: in numerical exams, mentioning computational aspects (e.g., $O(d^3)$ cost) earns extra credit.
> 5. **Isotropic vs Full**: do not confuse $\Sigma = \sigma^2 I$ (isotropic) with diagonal $\Sigma$ (axis-aligned, but different scales per dimension).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition**: Gaussian PDF is fully parameterized by $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\Sigma$. For $d$ dimensions, there are $d + \frac{d(d+1)}{2}$ parameters in total.
- **Normalization**: Always verify the PDF integrates to 1; the constant $(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}$ does this.
- **MLE Estimates**:
  - $\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_k$ (unbiased)
  - $\hat{\Sigma}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}})(\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}})^T$ (biased; use $N-1$ for unbiased).
- **Mahalanobis Distance**: $r^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) \sim \chi^2_d$.
- **QDF vs LDF**: 
  - QDF (different covariances per class) → quadratic boundary.
  - LDF (shared covariance) → linear boundary.
- **Discriminant Function**:
  - $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T\Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$
- **Whitening Transform**: $\mathbf{y} = \Lambda^{-1/2}U^T(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ yields $\mathbf{y} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, I)$.
- **Linear Combination**: $A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b} \sim \mathcal{N}(A\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, A\Sigma A^T)$.
- **Statistical Independence**: Zero off-diagonal entries in $\Sigma$ imply feature independence (for Gaussians only).
- **Application to Bayes Classifier**: Always maximize $p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_i)P(\omega_i)$ over $i$.
- **Decision Rule**: $P(\omega_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) > P(\omega_2 \mid \mathbf{x}) \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} \in \omega_1$.
- **Density Contours**: Level sets of $p(\mathbf{x}) = c$ are **ellipsoids** with axes along eigenvectors of $\Sigma$.
- **Whitening for Preprocessing**: Often a pre-processing step to standardize feature space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** In numerical questions, always **clearly state assumptions** (isotropic/diagonal/full covariance, equal/unequal priors) before deriving. KTU examiners award marks for explicit assumption statements. Also, remember to convert the LDF equation into the standard form $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + w_0$ for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayes Decision Theory and Classifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bayes Decision Theory and Classifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Bayes Decision Theory (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> Bayes Decision Theory is a fundamental statistical approach to pattern classification that provides an optimal framework for making decisions under uncertainty. It uses the joint probability $P(x, \omega_j)$ of observing pattern $x$ belonging to class $\omega_j$, combining **prior probabilities** $P(\omega_j)$ with the **class-conditional density** $p(x \mid \omega_j)$ to compute the **posterior probability** $P(\omega_j \mid x)$. The Bayes decision rule assigns a pattern to the class that minimizes the overall probability of misclassification, thereby achieving the **minimum possible error rate** among all classifiers.

Mathematically, the posterior probability is expressed by **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$$

where the evidence $p(x)$ acts as a normalizing constant ensuring that all posteriors sum to unity:

$$p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)$$

Here, $c$ denotes the total number of pattern classes in the recognition problem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The Medical Diagnosis Problem**
> Imagine you are a doctor deciding whether a patient has Disease A or Disease B based on a single blood test result $x$.
> - **Prior Probability $P(\omega_j)$**: Before seeing the test, you know that 1 in 1000 people in your city has Disease A ($P(\omega_A) = 0.001$) and 1 in 100 has Disease B ($P(\omega_B) = 0.01$). These are your *priors*.
> - **Likelihood $p(x \mid \omega_j)$**: This tells you *how likely this particular test value is* if the patient truly has Disease A or B. For instance, very high $x$ might be very common in Disease A but rare in Disease B.
> - **Posterior $P(\omega_j \mid x)$**: This is the *updated belief* — given the actual test result, what is the probability the patient has the disease?
> - **Bayes' Rule**: Multiplies your prior belief by the likelihood of the evidence, then normalizes. It mathematically represents *learning from data*.
> - **Decision**: You pick the disease with the higher posterior — this is the **Bayes Optimal Decision**.

The brilliance of Bayes Decision Theory is that it explicitly models **uncertainty** and provides a **provably optimal** decision rule. No other classifier using the same information can have a lower error rate on average.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & KTU-Mandated Vocabulary

| Term | Symbol | Physical / Mathematical Meaning |
|------|--------|---------------------------------|
| Pattern | $x$ | A $d$-dimensional feature vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| Class | $\omega_j$ | The $j$-th category; $j = 1, 2, \dots, c$ |
| Prior Probability | $P(\omega_j)$ | Belief about class $j$ before observing $x$ |
| Likelihood | $p(x \mid \omega_j)$ | Probability density of $x$ given class $\omega_j$ |
| Evidence | $p(x)$ | Total density of $x$ across all classes |
| Posterior Probability | $P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Updated belief about class $j$ after observing $x$ |
| Risk / Loss | $R(\alpha_i \mid x)$ | Expected loss for taking action $\alpha_i$ given $x$ |
| Decision Boundary | $P(\omega_i \mid x) = P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Locus of equal posterior probability |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Bayes Error Rate (Irreducible Error)**
> The error rate achieved by the optimal Bayes decision rule is the **theoretical lower bound** on classification error. No classifier, no matter how sophisticated, can beat this bound given the same information. This is the *Bayes Risk* — the holy grail of pattern recognition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Building Blocks of Bayesian Classification

Bayes Decision Theory rests on four foundational pillars. A KTU examiner expects every student to articulate these fluently.

#### Pillar 1 — Prior Probability $P(\omega_j)$
- Represents our **a priori** knowledge about the relative frequency of each class in the population.
- For a $c$-class problem, the priors must satisfy:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j) = 1, \quad P(\omega_j) \geq 0 \ \forall j$$
- In the absence of information, the **principle of indifference** suggests using uniform priors: $P(\omega_j) = \frac{1}{c}$.

#### Pillar 2 — Class-Conditional Density (Likelihood) $p(x \mid \omega_j)$
- Describes the statistical distribution of feature vectors within class $\omega_j$.
- Each class has its own density function — these are typically modeled (e.g., Gaussian, exponential) or estimated (e.g., Parzen windows, $k$-NN).
- The function must satisfy:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(x \mid \omega_j) \, dx = 1, \quad p(x \mid \omega_j) \geq 0$$

#### Pillar 3 — Evidence (Marginal Density) $p(x)$
- The total probability density of observing the feature vector $x$, summed over all classes:
$$p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)$$
- For a two-class problem: $p(x) = p(x \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1) + p(x \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2)$.

#### Pillar 4 — Posterior Probability $P(\omega_j \mid x)$
- The probability that the pattern originated from class $\omega_j$ given the observed feature $x$.
- Computed via **Bayes' Formula**:
$$P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$$
- Posteriors also form a probability distribution: $\sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j \mid x) = 1$.

### 2.2 Bayes Decision Rule — Minimum Error Rate

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Minimum-Error-Rate Decision Rule**
> For a $c$-class problem, decide $\omega_i$ if and only if:
> $$P(\omega_i \mid x) = \max_{j=1,\dots,c} P(\omega_j \mid x)$$
> Equivalently (since $p(x)$ is common to all terms), decide $\omega_i$ if:
> $$p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i) = \max_{j=1,\dots,c} p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$$

**Why does this minimize the error rate?**
The probability of error when deciding $\omega_i$ is $1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$. To minimize this, we must **maximize $P(\omega_i \mid x)$** — choosing the class with the largest posterior probability.

### 2.3 Bayes Risk and the General Decision Rule

In many engineering applications (e.g., medical diagnosis, fraud detection, military target recognition), the consequences of different errors are **unequal**. Bayes' framework elegantly handles this via the **loss function** $\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)$ — the cost incurred when action $\alpha_i$ is taken while the true class is $\omega_j$.

The **conditional risk** (expected loss) for action $\alpha_i$ given $x$:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Generalized Bayes Decision Rule (Minimum Risk)**
> Choose action $\alpha_i$ that minimizes the conditional risk:
> $$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \min_{k=1,\dots,a} R(\alpha_k \mid x)$$
> where $a$ is the total number of available actions.

**Special Case — 0-1 Loss Function:**
If we set $\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i = j \\ 1 & \text{if } i \neq j \end{cases}$, all misclassifications are equally costly. In this case:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j \neq i} P(\omega_j \mid x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

Minimizing the risk again reduces to **maximizing the posterior** — the minimum-error-rate rule is recovered as a special case.

### 2.4 Discriminant Functions

A **discriminant function** $g_i(x)$ is any function whose maximum over all classes indicates the appropriate class:

$$\text{Decide } \omega_i \iff g_i(x) = \max_{j} g_j(x)$$

Equivalent formulations (all yielding the same decision):

| Form | Discriminant Function |
|------|----------------------|
| Posterior | $g_i(x) = P(\omega_i \mid x)$ |
| Bayes Factor | $g_i(x) = p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$ |
| Logarithmic | $g_i(x) = \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ |
| Risk-based | $g_i(x) = -R(\alpha_i \mid x)$ |

The **decision boundary** between classes $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ is the set of points $x$ for which:

$$g_i(x) = g_j(x)$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$ | Foundation of all Bayesian inference |
| 2 | Total Probability | $p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ | Normalizing constant |
| 3 | Minimum-Error Rule | $\omega^* = \arg\max_j P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Optimal classification |
| 4 | Posterior in terms of likelihood | $P(\omega_j \mid x) \propto p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ | Dropping the constant |
| 5 | Log Posterior Decision | $\text{Choose } \omega_i \text{ if } \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) > \ln p(x \mid \omega_j) + \ln P(\omega_j)$ | Numerically stable |
| 6 | Conditional Risk | $R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Asymmetric cost modeling |
| 7 | Bayes Risk (overall) | $R = \int R(\alpha(x) \mid x) \, p(x) \, dx$ | Expected global loss |
| 8 | Minimum Error Probability | $P(\text{error}) = 1 - \int \max_j P(\omega_j \mid x) \, p(x) \, dx$ | Lower bound on error |
| 9 | Two-class Log-Ratio | $\text{Decide } \omega_1 \text{ if } \ln \frac{p(x \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1)}{p(x \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2)} > 0$ | LRT framework |
| 10 | Bivariate Gaussian | $p(x) = \frac{1}{2\pi \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)\right]$ | Most common likelihood model |

> [!NOTE] **Exam Tip:** Always use the **logarithmic form** of discriminant functions in numerical problems — it converts products into sums, prevents underflow, and is what most KTU examiners expect in long derivations.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Medical Diagnosis (Healthcare AI)**: Bayes classifiers detect tumors, diabetes, and cardiac abnormalities with sensitivity/specificity trade-offs encoded via loss functions.
2. **Spam Email Filtering**: Naive Bayes is the classical workhorse — words are features, classes are *spam* / *ham*.
3. **Fault Diagnosis in Mechanical Systems**: Vibration spectra classified using Bayes risk with asymmetric costs (false negatives on a cracked turbine blade are catastrophic).
4. **Speaker / Speech Recognition**: HMMs (Hidden Markov Models) with Gaussian emissions are essentially sequential Bayes classifiers.
5. **Autonomous Vehicles**: Pedestrian detection uses Bayesian fusion of camera and LIDAR data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

We start from the definition of **joint probability** in two ways. For any two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

By the symmetry of the intersection:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \, P(A) = P(A \mid B) \, P(B)$$

Equating these and solving for $P(A \mid B)$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Substituting pattern-recognition variables** ($A \to \omega_j$, $B \to x$, $P(\cdot) \to P(\cdot)$, $P(\cdot \mid \cdot) \to p(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ for continuous features):

$$\boxed{\,P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}\,}$$

For a multi-class problem, expand the denominator using the **Law of Total Probability**:

$$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{c} p(x \mid \omega_k) \, P(\omega_k)$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum-Error-Rate Decision Rule

Consider a $c$-class problem. The probability of correctly classifying $x$ when we decide class $\omega_i$ is:

$$P(\text{correct} \mid x, \text{decide } \omega_i) = P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

Therefore, the probability of error is:

$$P(\text{error} \mid x, \text{decide } \omega_i) = 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

To minimize error, we maximize $P(\omega_i \mid x)$ over all $i$:

$$\omega^* = \arg\max_{i=1,\dots,c} P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

The overall error rate is obtained by integrating over the input space:

$$P(\text{error}) = \int P(\text{error} \mid x) \, p(x) \, dx = \int \left[1 - \max_i P(\omega_i \mid x)\right] p(x) \, dx$$

Since $p(x) \geq 0$ and the integral is linear, minimizing the integrand pointwise minimizes the integral. Hence the **pointwise-max-posterior rule is globally optimal**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayes Risk Minimization

Suppose actions $\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_a$ are possible, and $\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)$ is the loss when action $\alpha_i$ is taken under true class $\omega_j$. Given $x$, the expected loss (conditional risk) of action $\alpha_i$ is:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

**Derivation of this expression:**

- Step 1: The probability of the true class being $\omega_j$ given $x$ is $P(\omega_j \mid x)$.
- Step 2: If we take action $\alpha_i$ and the true class is $\omega_j$, the loss is $\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)$.
- Step 3: The expected loss is the sum of (loss $\times$ probability) over all possible true classes — this is the definition of expectation.

The overall (unconditional) risk is:

$$R = \int R(\alpha(x) \mid x) \, p(x) \, dx$$

Since $p(x) \geq 0$, minimizing the integral requires minimizing $R(\alpha(x) \mid x)$ at every $x$. This yields the **generalized Bayes decision rule**:

$$\text{Choose } \alpha_i \text{ that minimizes } R(\alpha_i \mid x)$$

**Special Case — Symmetric 0-1 Loss:**

$$\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) = \begin{cases} 0 & i = j \\ 1 & i \neq j \end{cases}$$

Substituting:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j \neq i} 1 \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

Minimizing $1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$ is equivalent to maximizing $P(\omega_i \mid x)$, confirming the minimum-error-rate rule.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bayes Classifier for Gaussian Likelihoods

Assume each class $\omega_j$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu_j$ and covariance $\Sigma_j$:

$$p(x \mid \omega_j) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma_j \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_j)^T \Sigma_j^{-1} (x - \mu_j)\right]$$

The discriminant function (log-posterior, dropping $\ln p(x)$) is:

$$g_j(x) = \ln p(x \mid \omega_j) + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

Expanding:

$$g_j(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_j)^T \Sigma_j^{-1} (x - \mu_j) - \frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_j \vert + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

Dropping the constant $-\frac{d}{2}\ln 2\pi$:

$$g_j(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_j)^T \Sigma_j^{-1} (x - \mu_j) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_j \vert + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

**Case 4a — Identical Covariances ($\Sigma_j = \Sigma$ for all $j$):**

The quadratic term $-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma \vert$ and $-\frac{1}{2}x^T \Sigma^{-1} x$ are common to all classes and cancel. The decision boundary becomes **linear**:

$$g_j(x) = \mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_j + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

This is the formula for the **Bayes Linear Classifier** in canonical form:

$$g_j(x) = W_j^T x + w_{j0}$$

where $W_j = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_j$ and $w_{j0} = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_j + \ln P(\omega_j)$.

**Case 4b — Diagonal but Distinct Covariances:**

$$g_j(x) = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{d} \frac{(x_i - \mu_{ji})^2}{\sigma_{ji}^2} - \sum_{i=1}^{d} \ln \sigma_{ji} + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

This yields a **piecewise-linear decision boundary** (linear in each dimension).

**Case 4c — General Distinct Covariances:**

$$g_j(x) = x^T A_j x + B_j^T x + C_j$$

where $A_j = -\frac{1}{2}\Sigma_j^{-1}$, $B_j = \Sigma_j^{-1} \mu_j$, $C_j = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma_j^{-1} \mu_j - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_j \vert + \ln P(\omega_j)$.

This is the **generalized quadratic Bayes classifier**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Boundary Shapes

| Covariance Assumption | Decision Boundary | Discriminant Function Form |
|------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$ (all classes) | Linear (hyperplane) | $g_j(x) = W_j^T x + w_{j0}$ |
| $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ (shared) | Linear (hyperplane) | $g_j(x) = W_j^T x + w_{j0}$ |
| $\Sigma_i$ diagonal, distinct | Piecewise-modulated hyperplane | Quadratic in $x$ (decoupled axes) |
| $\Sigma_i$ general, distinct | General quadric (ellipsoid/hyperbola) | $x^T A_j x + B_j^T x + C_j$ |

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight:** When all classes share the same covariance, the quadratic term cancels and the decision boundary is *always* a hyperplane. This is why **LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis)** emerges naturally from Bayes theory under homoscedastic Gaussianity.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Bayes Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Vector x] --> B[Compute Likelihoods p of x given omega j]
    C[Prior Probabilities P of omega j] --> D[Multiply with Likelihoods]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Compute Joint Densities p of x given omega j times P of omega j]
    E --> F[Sum All Joint Densities = Evidence p of x]
    F --> G[Compute Posterior P of omega j given x]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Apply Decision Rule]
    I[Loss Function lambda] --> H
    H --> J[Select Class with Maximum Posterior or Minimum Risk]
    J --> K[Output Class Label omega star]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style I fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Two-Class Bayes Decision Boundary Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Region R1: P of omega 1 given x greater than P of omega 2 given x] -->|Decision: omega 1| B[Classify as omega 1]
    C[Decision Boundary: P of omega 1 given x equals P of omega 2 given x] -->|Boundary points| D[Equal Posterior Region]
    E[Region R2: P of omega 2 given x greater than P of omega 1 given x] -->|Decision: omega 2| F[Classify as omega 2]
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style C fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Concept Map of Bayes Decision Theory

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Bayes Decision Theory))
    Foundations
      Prior Probability
      Likelihood Function
      Evidence Marginal
      Posterior Probability
      Bayes Theorem
    Decision Rules
      Minimum Error Rate
      Minimum Risk
      0-1 Loss Function
      Asymmetric Loss
    Discriminant Functions
      Posterior Form
      Log Posterior Form
      Decision Boundaries
    Gaussian Classifiers
      Linear Classifier
      Quadratic Classifier
      LDA
      QDA
    Applications
      Medical Diagnosis
      Spam Filtering
      Speech Recognition
      Fault Detection
```

### 4.4 Derivation Flow of the Bayes Optimal Classifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with prior P of omega j and likelihood p of x given omega j] --> S2[Apply Bayes Theorem]
    S2 --> S3[Obtain posterior P of omega j given x]
    S3 --> S4{Derive Decision Criterion}
    S4 -->|Minimize Error| S5[Choose class with max posterior]
    S4 -->|Minimize Risk| S6[Incorporate loss function lambda]
    S5 --> S7[Form Discriminant Function g of j of x]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Specialize to Gaussian likelihoods]
    S8 --> S9{Compare Covariances}
    S9 -->|Identical| S10[Linear Boundary]
    S9 -->|Diagonal Distinct| S11[Modulated Linear Boundary]
    S9 -->|General Distinct| S12[Quadratic Boundary]
    style S1 fill:#17becf,color:#fff
    style S12 fill:#bcbd22,color:#fff
```

### 4.5 Probability Wheel — Relationship Between Priors, Likelihood, and Posteriors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Total Probability Space: p of x] --> B[Class omega 1 Region: P of omega 1]
    A --> C[Class omega 2 Region: P of omega 2]
    A --> D[Class omega c Region: P of omega c]
    B --> B1[Likelihood p of x given omega 1]
    C --> C1[Likelihood p of x given omega 2]
    D --> D1[Likelihood p of x given omega c]
    B1 --> E1[Posterior P of omega 1 given x]
    C1 --> E2[Posterior P of omega 2 given x]
    D1 --> E3[Posterior P of omega c given x]
    E1 --> F[Normalization: Sum equals 1]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    style A fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style F fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Q1a]**

**Q1. State and prove Bayes' theorem in the context of pattern recognition. Why is it called the "fundamental theorem of pattern recognition"?**

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' theorem relates the posterior probability of a class given a feature vector to the prior probability and class-conditional density:

$$P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(x)} \quad \text{where} \quad p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{c} p(x \mid \omega_k) P(\omega_k)$$

**Proof:** By the definition of conditional probability, $P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{P(\omega_j \cap x)}{p(x)}$. Also, $P(\omega_j \cap x) = p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$. Substituting yields Bayes' formula.

It is called the "fundamental theorem of pattern recognition" because it allows us to **invert** the conditional probability — i.e., compute $P(\omega_j \mid x)$ from $p(x \mid \omega_j)$ — enabling optimal classification by selecting the maximum posterior.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Q1b]**

**Q2. Differentiate between prior probability, likelihood, evidence, and posterior probability. How does Bayes' theorem establish the link between them?**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Notation |
|------|------------|----------|
| Prior | Probability of class before observing data | $P(\omega_j)$ |
| Likelihood | Density of feature given class | $p(x \mid \omega_j)$ |
| Evidence | Total density of $x$ over all classes | $p(x) = \sum_j p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ |
| Posterior | Updated class probability given $x$ | $P(\omega_j \mid x)$ |

Bayes' theorem **links** them: $P(\omega_j \mid x) = \dfrac{p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$. The prior is updated by the likelihood (data evidence) to give the posterior. The evidence serves as a normalizing constant ensuring the posteriors sum to one. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question Paper, Q8]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Consider a two-class problem with equal priors $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2) = 0.5$ and univariate Gaussian likelihoods:
$$p(x \mid \omega_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-0)^2}{2}\right], \quad p(x \mid \omega_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-3)^2}{2}\right]$$
Find the Bayes decision boundary and compute $P(\text{error})$.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Derive the discriminant function for a multivariate Gaussian classifier with $c$ classes and **shared** covariance matrix $\Sigma$. Show that the decision boundary is a hyperplane.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Given equal priors, the Bayes rule reduces to comparing likelihoods: $p(x \mid \omega_1) \overset{\omega_1}{\underset{\omega_2}{\gtrless}} p(x \mid \omega_2)$.

Taking logarithms:
$$-\frac{x^2}{2} \overset{\omega_1}{\underset{\omega_2}{\gtrless}} -\frac{(x-3)^2}{2}$$

Multiply by $-2$ and flip the inequality:
$$x^2 \overset{\omega_2}{\underset{\omega_1}{\gtrless}} (x-3)^2$$
$$x^2 \overset{\omega_2}{\underset{\omega_1}{\gtrless}} x^2 - 6x + 9$$
$$0 \overset{\omega_2}{\underset{\omega_1}{\gtrless}} -6x + 9$$
$$x \overset{\omega_1}{\underset{\omega_2}{\gtrless}} 1.5$$

**Decision Boundary:** $x^* = 1.5$

**Error Probability:** By symmetry, $P(\text{error}) = P(\omega_2) \cdot P(x \leq 1.5 \mid \omega_2) = 0.5 \cdot \Phi\!\left(\frac{1.5 - 3}{1}\right) = 0.5 \cdot \Phi(-1.5) = 0.5 \times 0.0668 = 0.0334$

So $P(\text{error}) \approx 3.34\%$.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

For class $\omega_j$ with mean $\mu_j$ and shared covariance $\Sigma$:

$$p(x \mid \omega_j) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_j)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu_j)\right]$$

The discriminant function:
$$g_j(x) = \ln p(x \mid \omega_j) + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

Expanding the quadratic form:
$$(x-\mu_j)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu_j) = x^T \Sigma^{-1} x - 2\mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} x + \mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_j$$

Substituting:
$$g_j(x) = -\frac{1}{2}x^T \Sigma^{-1} x + \mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_j - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma \vert + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

Since $-\frac{1}{2}x^T \Sigma^{-1} x$ and $-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma \vert$ are **common to all classes**, they cancel when comparing $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$. The simplified discriminant:

$$g_j(x) = \mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_j + \ln P(\omega_j) = W_j^T x + w_{j0}$$

This is **linear in $x$**, so the decision boundary $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$ is a **hyperplane**. $\blacksquare$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Q9]**

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* A pattern recognition system for tumor classification uses the following loss matrix with priors $P(\omega_1) = 0.95$ (benign) and $P(\omega_2) = 0.05$ (malignant):

$$\lambda(\alpha \mid \omega) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 100 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

where rows are actions ($\alpha_1$ = predict benign, $\alpha_2$ = predict malignant) and columns are true classes. Given $P(\omega_2 \mid x) = 0.1$, determine the optimal action using the Bayes minimum-risk rule.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For a three-class problem with multivariate Gaussian likelihoods $\Sigma_1 \neq \Sigma_2 \neq \Sigma_3$, derive the explicit form of the **quadratic discriminant function** $g_j(x)$ and state the nature of the decision boundary between any two classes.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

For action $\alpha_1$ (predict benign):
$$R(\alpha_1 \mid x) = \lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1 \mid x) + \lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2 \mid x) = 0 \cdot 0.9 + 100 \cdot 0.1 = 10$$

For action $\alpha_2$ (predict malignant):
$$R(\alpha_2 \mid x) = \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1 \mid x) + \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2 \mid x) = 1 \cdot 0.9 + 0 \cdot 0.1 = 0.9$$

Since $R(\alpha_2 \mid x) = 0.9 < R(\alpha_1 \mid x) = 10$, the optimal action is **$\alpha_2$: predict malignant**.

**Engineering insight:** Even with low posterior $P(\omega_2 \mid x) = 0.1$, the high cost of missing a tumor (100) makes the minimum-risk decision favor the malignant prediction. This demonstrates the **asymmetric cost** benefit of Bayes risk minimization over the simple max-posterior rule.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

For multivariate Gaussian with class-specific mean $\mu_j$ and covariance $\Sigma_j$:

$$p(x \mid \omega_j) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma_j \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_j)^T \Sigma_j^{-1} (x-\mu_j)\right]$$

Discriminant function:
$$g_j(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_j)^T \Sigma_j^{-1} (x-\mu_j) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_j \vert + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

Expanding:
$$g_j(x) = -\frac{1}{2}x^T \Sigma_j^{-1} x + \mu_j^T \Sigma_j^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma_j^{-1} \mu_j - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_j \vert + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

In compact form:
$$\boxed{\,g_j(x) = x^T A_j x + B_j^T x + C_j\,}$$

where:
- $A_j = -\frac{1}{2}\Sigma_j^{-1}$ (a $d \times d$ symmetric matrix)
- $B_j = \Sigma_j^{-1} \mu_j$ (a $d \times 1$ vector)
- $C_j = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma_j^{-1} \mu_j - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_j \vert + \ln P(\omega_j)$ (a scalar)

**Decision boundary between $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$:** Setting $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$ yields:
$$x^T (A_i - A_j) x + (B_i - B_j)^T x + (C_i - C_j) = 0$$

This is a **generalized quadric surface** in $\mathbb{R}^d$ — it can be a hyperplane, hyper-ellipsoid, hyperboloid, or paraboloid depending on the matrix $A_i - A_j$. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Normalization Trap (4-mark penalty):** Students often write Bayes' formula without the denominator $p(x)$, or forget that posteriors must sum to 1. Always state $p(x) = \sum_j p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ explicitly.
> 2. **Log Trick Oversight:** When comparing two likelihoods, NEVER multiply the densities directly — take logarithms first to avoid numerical underflow. Examiners deduct 2 marks for raw product comparisons.
> 3. **0-1 Loss vs. General Loss:** The minimum-error-rate rule is ONLY valid for the symmetric 0-1 loss. For asymmetric costs, you MUST use the minimum-risk rule with the full loss matrix. Confusing these costs ~3 marks.
> 4. **Covariance Cancellation:** When all $\Sigma_j = \Sigma$, the quadratic term cancels. Students often retain it, producing incorrect *quadratic* decision boundaries when the answer is *linear*. This is the #1 reason for losing 4–5 marks on 14-mark problems.
> 5. **Discriminant Function Sign:** Some students write $g_j(x) = -R(\alpha_j \mid x)$ but forget the negative sign. Re-read your formula before submitting.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ **Bayes' Theorem** $P(\omega_j \mid x) = \dfrac{p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$ — must know by heart.
- ⭐ **Minimum-Error Decision Rule:** choose the class with the **maximum posterior probability**.
- ⭐ **Minimum-Risk Rule:** choose the action that **minimizes conditional risk** $R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_j \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$.
- ⭐ **0-1 Loss** $\Rightarrow$ minimum-risk reduces to minimum-error (max posterior).
- ⭐ **Discriminant functions** are equivalent formulations: posterior, joint, log-posterior, or negative-risk.
- ⭐ **Decision boundary** is the locus $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$.
- ⭐ For **shared covariance** Gaussians → **linear boundary** (LDA).
- ⭐ For **distinct covariances** Gaussians → **quadratic boundary** (QDA).
- ⭐ The Bayes classifier is **optimal**: no other classifier can achieve a lower error rate given the same information.
- ⭐ The **Bayes risk** is the *theoretical lower bound* on classification error — the "irreducible" error.
- ⭐ Always use the **logarithmic form** of discriminant functions for numerical stability.
- ⭐ Know the **Gaussian PDF** formula in both univariate and multivariate forms.
- ⭐ Be fluent in deriving $g_j(x)$ for both shared and distinct covariance cases — these are recurring 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimal Solutions for Minimum Error and Minimum Risk Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Optimal Solutions for Minimum Error and Minimum Risk Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Optimal Solutions for Minimum Error and Minimum Risk Criteria

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Decision Theory (Foundational Definition)**
> Bayesian Decision Theory is a fundamental statistical approach in Pattern Recognition that provides a probabilistic framework for making optimal classification decisions under uncertainty. It quantifies the consequences of incorrect decisions through a **loss function (λ)** and uses prior probabilities **P(ωⱼ)** and class-conditional probability density functions **p(x | ωⱼ)** to derive decision boundaries that minimize either the **probability of error (Minimum Error Criterion)** or the **expected loss (Minimum Risk Criterion)**.

The optimal Bayes decision rule is the gold standard against which all other classifiers are benchmarked because it yields the **lowest achievable error rate** (Bayes Error) for a given feature distribution.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Doctor's Diagnosis**
> Imagine you visit a doctor with a set of symptoms (your **feature vector x**: fever, cough, X-ray report). The doctor must decide between two possible diseases: Flu (ω₁) or COVID-19 (ω₂).
>
> - **Prior Knowledge**: The doctor already knows that, in your city, Flu is more common than COVID-19. This is the **prior probability P(ωⱼ)**.
> - **Likelihood**: Given your symptoms, the doctor estimates the probability of observing them under each disease — this is **p(x | ωⱼ)**.
> - **Posterior Belief**: Using **Bayes' Theorem**, the doctor updates the prior to get the posterior probability **P(ωⱼ | x)**, i.e., "Given these symptoms, how likely is the patient to have each disease?"
> - **Minimum Error Rule**: A casual doctor picks the disease with the highest posterior probability (lowest chance of being wrong).
> - **Minimum Risk Rule**: An experienced doctor weighs the **cost of misdiagnosis**. Missing COVID-19 (treating it as Flu) could be fatal, while misdiagnosing Flu as COVID-19 only causes inconvenience. So the doctor uses a **loss function** to balance these consequences and may decide differently than the simple "most likely" rule.

### 1.1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Probabilistic Notations (KTU Standard Symbols)**
>
> | Symbol | Meaning |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | **ωⱼ** | The j-th class (pattern category) |
> | **x** | Feature vector of dimension **d** (i.e., x ∈ ℝᵈ) |
> | **P(ωⱼ)** | Prior probability of class ωⱼ (a priori belief) |
> | **p(x \| ωⱼ)** | Class-conditional PDF (likelihood of x given class ωⱼ) |
> | **p(x)** | Mixture (evidence) density: p(x) = Σⱼ P(ωⱼ)·p(x \| ωⱼ) |
> | **P(ωⱼ \| x)** | Posterior probability (a posteriori belief) |
> | **λ(αᵢ \| ωⱼ)** | Loss incurred for taking action αᵢ when true class is ωⱼ |
> | **R(αᵢ \| x)** | Conditional risk (expected loss) for action αᵢ given x |
> | **ε*** | Bayes Error Rate (theoretical minimum achievable error) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bayesian Decision Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The Bayesian framework transforms pattern classification into a systematic decision-making process. Below is the operational logic that KTU examiners expect you to reproduce.

### 2.1.1 Step 1: Establishing the Probabilistic Foundation

We begin with a set of **c mutually exclusive and exhaustive classes** {ω₁, ω₂, …, ωc} and a feature vector **x ∈ ℝᵈ**. The classification system requires three fundamental quantities:

- **Prior Probability P(ωⱼ)**: Captures the baseline likelihood of class ωⱼ before observing x. By definition:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j) = 1$$

- **Class-Conditional PDF p(x | ωⱼ)**: Describes the statistical distribution of features within each class.
- **Joint Probability**: Obtained via the product rule of probability.

$$P(\omega_j, x) = P(\omega_j) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_j)$$

### 2.1.2 Step 2: Bayes' Theorem — The "Why" Behind Posterior Computation

The product rule also allows us to write the joint probability by conditioning on x first:

$$P(\omega_j, x) = p(x) \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

Equating both expressions and solving for the posterior gives **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{P(\omega_j) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_j)}{p(x)}$$

where the **evidence** (denominator) is computed as:

$$p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_j)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why"**: The denominator p(x) is merely a normalization constant that ensures all posteriors sum to 1. For **decision-making**, we can often ignore p(x) because it scales all posteriors equally. This observation is critical for KTU derivations.

### 2.1.3 Step 3: Defining the Loss Function and Conditional Risk

A decision rule **α(x)** maps each feature vector x to one of the c classes (or, more generally, to one of **a actions** {α₁, …, αₐ}). The **loss function**:

$$\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)$$

quantifies the penalty for choosing action αᵢ when the true state of nature is ωⱼ. The **conditional risk** (a.k.a. **expected loss** or **posterior expected loss**) is:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

### 2.1.4 Step 4: The Bayes Decision Rule (Minimum Risk)

To minimize the **overall risk**, we select the action that minimizes the conditional risk:

$$\alpha^* = \arg\min_{i} \; R(\alpha_i \mid x)$$

The resulting **Bayes risk** is the minimum achievable expected loss across all possible decision rules.

## 2.2 Minimum Error Rate Classification (Maximum A Posteriori — MAP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-One Loss Function (Symmetric Loss)**
> When misclassification costs are equal for all error types, the loss function is:
>
> $$\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i = j \quad (\text{correct classification}) \\ 1 & \text{if } i \neq j \quad (\text{misclassification}) \end{cases}$$
>
> Under this loss, minimizing risk is **mathematically equivalent to minimizing the probability of error**.

Under the 0-1 loss, the conditional risk for deciding ωᵢ becomes:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j \neq i} 1 \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

Minimizing this risk is equivalent to **maximizing the posterior probability**:

$$\text{Decide } \omega^* \quad \text{if} \quad P(\omega^* \mid x) > P(\omega_j \mid x) \quad \forall j \neq *$$

This is the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) rule**, also called the **minimum error Bayes classifier**.

## 2.3 Two-Category Special Case (Binary Classification)

For **c = 2** classes, we use a **likelihood ratio test (LRT)** by comparing the two posteriors:

$$P(\omega_1 \mid x) \gtrless P(\omega_2 \mid x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad P(\omega_1 \mid x) + P(\omega_2 \mid x) = 1$$

Substituting Bayes' theorem and using the fact that p(x) is a common factor:

$$\frac{P(\omega_1) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x)} \gtrless \frac{P(\omega_2) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_2)}{p(x)}$$

The decision rule simplifies to comparing the **likelihood ratio** against a threshold:

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x \mid \omega_2)} \gtrless \frac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)} = \Lambda_0$$

- If **Λ(x) > Λ₀** → Decide **ω₁**
- If **Λ(x) < Λ₀** → Decide **ω₂**
- The decision boundary occurs where **Λ(x) = Λ₀**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimax Criterion (Why It Matters)**
> When the prior probabilities are unknown or variable, the **minimax** strategy minimizes the *worst-case* (maximum) risk over all possible priors. It is invoked when the cost of an error depends critically on the prior — for example, in rare-disease diagnosis where the prior might be over- or underestimated.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the following table — these are the equations that appear in 80% of KTU derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(\omega_j \mid x) = \dfrac{P(\omega_j) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_j)}{p(x)}$ | $0 \leq P(\omega_j \mid x) \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Mixture Density (Evidence) | $p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_j)$ | Non-negative, integrates to 1 |
| 3 | Prior Sum Constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j) = 1$ | Probability axiom |
| 4 | Posterior Sum Constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j \mid x) = 1$ | Normalized over classes |
| 5 | Conditional Risk | $R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Expected loss per action |
| 6 | Overall Bayes Risk | $R = \int R(\alpha(x) \mid x) \cdot p(x) \, dx$ | Integral over feature space |
| 7 | 0-1 Loss MAP Rule | Decide $\omega^*$ s.t. $P(\omega^* \mid x) = \max_j P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Minimizes P(error) |
| 8 | Likelihood Ratio Test | $\Lambda(x) = \dfrac{p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x \mid \omega_2)} \gtrless \dfrac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$ | Two-class decision |
| 9 | Log-Likelihood Form | $\ln p(x \mid \omega_1) - \ln p(x \mid \omega_2) \gtrless \ln \dfrac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$ | Numerical stability |
| 10 | Bayes Error | $\varepsilon^* = \int \min\{P(\omega_1 \mid x), P(\omega_2 \mid x)\} \cdot p(x) \, dx$ | Theoretical minimum |
| 11 | Generalised LRT (c-class) | $\text{Decide } \omega_i \text{ if } P(\omega_i \mid x) = \max_j P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Equivalent form |
| 12 | Discriminant Function | $g_i(x) = P(\omega_i \mid x) \; \text{or} \; g_i(x) = \ln P(\omega_i) + \ln p(x \mid \omega_i)$ | Equivalent forms |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is Bayesian Classification Used in Industry?**
>
> - **Medical Diagnosis (Healthcare)**: Bayesian networks power clinical decision support systems (e.g., IBM Watson Health).
> - **Spam Filtering (NLP)**: Naive Bayes classifiers — a simplified Bayes classifier assuming feature independence — remain the industry baseline for email spam detection.
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Robotics)**: Object detection (pedestrian vs. vehicle vs. sign) uses Bayesian fusion of LiDAR, camera, and radar likelihoods.
> - **Credit Card Fraud Detection (FinTech)**: Minimum-risk classifiers allow banks to weight the cost of false negatives (missed fraud) much higher than false positives (legitimate transaction declined).
> - **Speech Recognition (Signal Processing)**: Hidden Markov Models trained with Bayesian parameter estimation underlie systems like Google Speech-to-Text.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Minimum Risk and Minimum Error under 0-1 Loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Hot Topic**: KTU examiners frequently ask students to "show that the minimum error rate classifier is a special case of the minimum risk classifier." This derivation must be reproduced flawlessly.

### 3.1.1 Setup

We have **c classes** and a 0-1 loss function:

$$\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{if } i = j \\ 1, & \text{if } i \neq j \end{cases}$$

The decision rule selects an action αᵢ from {α₁, …, αc}, where action αᵢ is interpreted as "decide the pattern belongs to class ωᵢ."

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Write the general conditional risk for action αᵢ:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the 0-1 loss function. Notice that **only two cases** exist for a fixed i:

- When **j = i**: the term is 0 · P(ωᵢ | x) = 0 (correct classification contributes no loss).
- When **j ≠ i**: the term is 1 · P(ωⱼ | x) = P(ωⱼ | x) (every misclassification contributes the posterior of the true class).

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{c} 1 \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x) + 0 \cdot P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

**Step 3**: Factor out the case j = i from the total sum to simplify. By the axiom of total probability, Σⱼ P(ωⱼ | x) = 1. Hence:

$$\sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{c} P(\omega_j \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j \mid x) - P(\omega_i \mid x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

**Step 4**: Substitute back into the risk expression:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

**Step 5**: To minimize risk, we must find the action αᵢ that minimizes [1 − P(ωᵢ | x)]. Since 1 is a constant, this is equivalent to:

$$\min_i \left[ 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x) \right] \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \max_i \, P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

**Step 6**: The optimal Bayes decision rule becomes:

$$\text{Choose } \alpha^* \text{ such that } P(\omega^* \mid x) = \max_{j=1,\dots,c} P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

**Step 7**: The probability of error given x is exactly the minimum conditional risk, because for the optimal action α*:

$$P(\text{error} \mid x) = 1 - P(\omega^* \mid x) = \min_i R(\alpha_i \mid x)$$

**Step 8**: To get the overall error rate, integrate over the feature space:

$$P(\text{error}) = \int P(\text{error} \mid x) \cdot p(x) \, dx = \int \left[ 1 - P(\omega^* \mid x) \right] \cdot p(x) \, dx$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: Under the 0-1 symmetric loss, the Bayes decision rule that minimizes expected loss is **identical** to the rule that minimizes the probability of error. Therefore, the minimum error classifier is a special case of the minimum risk classifier.

## 3.2 Derivation: Two-Category Bayes Decision Rule (Likelihood Ratio Test)

### 3.2.1 Setup

Two classes ω₁ and ω₂ with priors P(ω₁) and P(ω₂) and class-conditional PDFs p(x | ω₁) and p(x | ω₂).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: The MAP rule states: decide ω₁ if P(ω₁ | x) > P(ω₂ | x), else decide ω₂.

$$P(\omega_1 \mid x) > P(\omega_2 \mid x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Decide } \omega_1$$

**Step 2**: Apply Bayes' theorem to both posteriors:

$$\frac{P(\omega_1) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x)} > \frac{P(\omega_2) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_2)}{p(x)}$$

**Step 3**: Since p(x) > 0, multiply both sides by p(x) to cancel it:

$$P(\omega_1) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_1) > P(\omega_2) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_2)$$

**Step 4**: Divide both sides by P(ω₂) · p(x | ω₂) (assumed non-zero) to obtain the likelihood ratio test:

$$\frac{p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x \mid \omega_2)} > \frac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$$

**Step 5**: Define the **likelihood ratio** Λ(x) and the **threshold** Λ₀:

$$\Lambda(x) \equiv \frac{p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x \mid \omega_2)}, \qquad \Lambda_0 \equiv \frac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$$

**Step 6**: The decision rule becomes:

$$\Lambda(x) \gtrless \Lambda_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{cases} \Lambda(x) > \Lambda_0 : \text{Decide } \omega_1 \\ \Lambda(x) < \Lambda_0 : \text{Decide } \omega_2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 7 (Log Form for Numerical Stability)**: Taking the natural logarithm of both sides (monotonic, so inequality direction is preserved):

$$\ln p(x \mid \omega_1) - \ln p(x \mid \omega_2) \gtrless \ln P(\omega_2) - \ln P(\omega_1)$$

This form is computationally preferred in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Loss Function Variations and Their Effect on Decision

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: Different loss functions lead to different optimal decisions. The matrix below summarizes the canonical cases.

| Loss Function Type | Mathematical Form | Resulting Decision Rule | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **0-1 Loss (Symmetric)** | $\lambda = 0$ if correct, $\lambda = 1$ if wrong | Maximize $P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | All errors equally costly |
| **Asymmetric 2-class** | $\lambda_{11} = \lambda_{22} = 0$, $\lambda_{12} = a$, $\lambda_{21} = b$ | $\Lambda(x) \gtrless \dfrac{a \cdot P(\omega_2)}{b \cdot P(\omega_1)}$ | Some errors costlier than others |
| **General c-class** | $\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j)$ arbitrary non-negative | Minimize $R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_j \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Custom cost structures |
| **Minimax (no prior info)** | Worst-case optimal | Choose prior that maximizes min risk | Unknown / variable priors |
| **Reject Option** | $\lambda(\text{reject}) < \lambda(\text{error})$ | Reject if $\max P(\omega_j \mid x) < t$ | Ambiguous patterns |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Numerical Decision with Asymmetric Loss

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model KTU-Style Problem (Solved)**
>
> **Problem**: A two-class problem with classes ω₁ and ω₂ has prior probabilities P(ω₁) = 0.3 and P(ω₂) = 0.7. For a given x, the class-conditional densities are p(x | ω₁) = 0.4 and p(x | ω₂) = 0.2. The loss function is:
> $$\lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_1) = 0, \quad \lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_2) = 2, \quad \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_1) = 5, \quad \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_2) = 0$$
> Find the optimal decision using (a) the MAP rule, and (b) the minimum risk rule.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — MAP (Minimum Error) Rule**:

**Step 1**: Compute posteriors using Bayes' theorem.

$$P(\omega_1 \mid x) = \frac{0.3 \times 0.4}{p(x)}, \quad P(\omega_2 \mid x) = \frac{0.7 \times 0.2}{p(x)}$$

$$P(\omega_1 \mid x) = \frac{0.12}{p(x)}, \quad P(\omega_2 \mid x) = \frac{0.14}{p(x)}$$

**Step 2**: Since P(ω₂ | x) = 0.14/p(x) > P(ω₁ | x) = 0.12/p(x), the MAP rule decides **ω₂**.

**Part (b) — Minimum Risk Rule**:

**Step 1**: Compute the conditional risk for each action.

$$R(\alpha_1 \mid x) = \lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_1) \cdot P(\omega_1 \mid x) + \lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_2) \cdot P(\omega_2 \mid x)$$

$$R(\alpha_1 \mid x) = 0 \cdot \frac{0.12}{p(x)} + 2 \cdot \frac{0.14}{p(x)} = \frac{0.28}{p(x)}$$

$$R(\alpha_2 \mid x) = \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_1) \cdot P(\omega_1 \mid x) + \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_2) \cdot P(\omega_2 \mid x)$$

$$R(\alpha_2 \mid x) = 5 \cdot \frac{0.12}{p(x)} + 0 \cdot \frac{0.14}{p(x)} = \frac{0.60}{p(x)}$$

**Step 2**: Compare risks. Since R(α₁ | x) = 0.28/p(x) < R(α₂ | x) = 0.60/p(x), the minimum risk rule decides **α₁ → ω₁**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight**: The MAP rule decided ω₂, but the minimum risk rule decided ω₁. This happens because the cost of misclassifying a true ω₁ as ω₂ (loss = 5) is much higher than the reverse error (loss = 2). When the loss is asymmetric, the two rules **diverge** — a common KTU conceptual trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bayesian Decision Pipeline (Functional Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the full information flow in a Bayesian classifier — from raw feature input to optimal class decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEATURE EXTRACTION MODULE] --> B[CLASS CONDITIONAL PDF ESTIMATION]
    A --> C[PRIOR PROBABILITY MODULE]
    B --> D[BAYES THEOREM ENGINE]
    C --> D
    D --> E[POSTERIOR P COMPUTATION]
    E --> F{OPTIMALITY CRITERION}
    F -->|Minimum Error| G[MAP RULE MAX POSTERIOR]
    F -->|Minimum Risk| H[CONDITIONAL RISK MINIMIZATION]
    G --> I[FINAL CLASS DECISION]
    H --> I
    H --> J[LOSS FUNCTION INPUT]
    J --> H
```

## 4.2 Two-Category Decision Boundary Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R1[DECIDE OMEGA-1 REGION]
        X1[Pattern x falls in R1]
    end
    subgraph DB[DECISION BOUNDARY]
        Y[Likelihood ratio equals threshold]
    end
    subgraph R2[DECIDE OMEGA-2 REGION]
        X2[Pattern x falls in R2]
    end
    X1 -->|Lambda x greater than Lambda-0| Y
    Y -->|Lambda x less than Lambda-0| X2
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Minimum Error vs Minimum Risk

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[INPUT FEATURE VECTOR X] --> P1[COMPUTE POSTERIOR P OMEGA-J GIVEN X]
    P1 --> Q1{WHICH CRITERION}
    Q1 -->|ZERO-ONE LOSS| M1[MAP RULE ARGMAX P OMEGA-J GIVEN X]
    Q1 -->|ARBITRARY LOSS| M2[COMPUTE CONDITIONAL RISK R]
    M2 --> M3[SELECT ACTION WITH MIN R ALPHA-I GIVEN X]
    M1 --> OUT1[DECIDE OMEGA-STAR]
    M3 --> OUT1
```

## 4.4 Discriminant Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT X] --> B[COMPUTE G1 X]
    A --> C[COMPUTE G2 X]
    A --> D[COMPUTE G-C X]
    B --> E[COMPARATOR MODULE]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[SELECT MAX G-I X]
    F --> G[OUTPUT CLASS OMEGA-STAR]
```

## 4.5 Posterior Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PRIOR P OMEGA-J] --> M[MULTIPLIER]
    L[LIKELIHOOD P X GIVEN OMEGA-J] --> M
    M --> N[NUMERATOR]
    E[EVIDENCE P X] --> D[DIVIDER]
    N --> D
    D --> POST[POSTERIOR P OMEGA-J GIVEN X]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: When asked to "explain the Bayesian classification pipeline," reproduce this kind of block diagram with clear labels. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for neat, labeled diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q1.** Define the term *Posterior Probability* in the context of Bayesian pattern classification. State Bayes' theorem and identify each term in it.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Posterior Probability**: The posterior probability P(ωⱼ | x) is the probability that a pattern belongs to class ωⱼ **after observing** the feature vector x. It represents the updated belief about class membership once evidence is available.

**Bayes' Theorem**:

$$P(\omega_j \mid x) = \frac{P(\omega_j) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_j)}{p(x)}$$

where:
- **P(ωⱼ | x)** = Posterior probability (a posteriori probability)
- **P(ωⱼ)** = Prior probability (a priori probability)
- **p(x | ωⱼ)** = Class-conditional probability density (likelihood)
- **p(x)** = Evidence (mixture density), $p(x) = \sum_j P(\omega_j) p(x \mid \omega_j)$

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q2.** What is the 0-1 loss function? Show that under this loss, the minimum risk classifier reduces to the minimum error classifier.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **0-1 loss function** assigns zero loss for correct classification and unit loss for every misclassification:

$$\lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i = j \\ 1 & \text{if } i \neq j \end{cases}$$

Under this loss, the conditional risk becomes:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j \neq i} 1 \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \mid x)$$

Minimizing R is equivalent to maximizing the posterior P(ωᵢ | x), which is exactly the **MAP (minimum error) rule**. Hence, the minimum error classifier is a special case of the minimum risk classifier under 0-1 loss.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — (14 Marks)

> **Q3(a).** State and explain Bayes' decision theory for pattern classification. Derive the Bayes decision rule for a two-class problem in terms of the likelihood ratio. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

Bayes' decision theory is a probabilistic framework for classification that minimizes the expected misclassification cost (risk) by combining prior knowledge, observed evidence, and a loss function.

For a two-class problem with classes ω₁ and ω₂, the decision rule compares posterior probabilities:

$$P(\omega_1 \mid x) \gtrless P(\omega_2 \mid x)$$

Applying Bayes' theorem to both sides:

$$\frac{P(\omega_1) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x)} \gtrless \frac{P(\omega_2) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_2)}{p(x)}$$

Since p(x) > 0 is common, we cancel it:

$$P(\omega_1) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_1) \gtrless P(\omega_2) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_2)$$

Rearranging gives the **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)**:

$$\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_1)}{p(x \mid \omega_2)} \gtrless \frac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)} = \Lambda_0$$

- If Λ(x) > Λ₀ → decide ω₁
- If Λ(x) < Λ₀ → decide ω₂
- The decision boundary is the locus where Λ(x) = Λ₀.

> **Q3(b).** Define the loss function and conditional risk. For a c-class problem with an arbitrary loss function λ(αᵢ | ωⱼ), derive the optimal Bayes decision rule. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Loss function λ(αᵢ | ωⱼ)**: Quantifies the penalty incurred when action αᵢ is taken and the true class is ωⱼ. It is a non-negative scalar (or matrix) encoding the cost of each decision.

**Conditional Risk R(αᵢ | x)**: The expected loss of taking action αᵢ when x is observed, computed as the loss-weighted average of posteriors:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) \cdot P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

**Derivation of Optimal Rule**:

To minimize the overall risk:

$$R = \int R(\alpha(x) \mid x) \cdot p(x) \, dx$$

Since p(x) ≥ 0, the overall risk is minimized by minimizing R(αᵢ | x) **pointwise** for every x. Thus the Bayes optimal decision is:

$$\alpha^*(x) = \arg\min_{i = 1, \dots, a} \; R(\alpha_i \mid x)$$

If the number of actions equals the number of classes (a = c), then αᵢ corresponds to "decide ωᵢ" and the rule becomes: *decide the class ω* such that the sum of losses against all other classes, weighted by their posteriors, is minimal*.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — (14 Marks)

> **Q4(a).** With a neat diagram, explain the Bayesian classification pipeline. Discuss the role of prior, likelihood, and posterior probabilities. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Bayesian Classification Pipeline** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Feature Extraction**: The raw input is transformed into a feature vector x ∈ ℝᵈ.
2. **Prior Estimation**: P(ωⱼ) is estimated from class frequencies in training data.
3. **Likelihood Estimation**: p(x | ωⱼ) is modelled (e.g., Gaussian) and parameters are estimated.
4. **Posterior Computation via Bayes' Theorem**: Combines prior and likelihood to compute P(ωⱼ | x).
5. **Decision**: Apply MAP (minimum error) or conditional risk (minimum risk) rule to assign a class label.

**Role of Each Probability**:
- **Prior P(ωⱼ)**: Encodes domain knowledge or class prevalence. It is independent of the observed features.
- **Likelihood p(x | ωⱼ)**: Models how features are distributed within each class. It is the "evidence-generating" distribution.
- **Posterior P(ωⱼ | x)**: The final decision variable. It fuses prior belief with observed evidence and is used for the classification step.

> **Q4(b).** Two classes ω₁ and ω₂ have prior probabilities P(ω₁) = 0.4 and P(ω₂) = 0.6. For a particular pattern x, p(x | ω₁) = 0.3 and p(x | ω₂) = 0.5. Compute the posterior probabilities and classify the pattern using (i) the minimum error rule, and (ii) the minimum risk rule with loss matrix:
> $$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
> where Λᵢⱼ = λ(αᵢ | ωⱼ). (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Evidence Computation**:

$$p(x) = P(\omega_1) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_1) + P(\omega_2) \cdot p(x \mid \omega_2)$$

$$p(x) = (0.4)(0.3) + (0.6)(0.5) = 0.12 + 0.30 = 0.42$$

**Step 2 — Posterior Computation**:

$$P(\omega_1 \mid x) = \frac{(0.4)(0.3)}{0.42} = \frac{0.12}{0.42} = 0.2857$$

$$P(\omega_2 \mid x) = \frac{(0.6)(0.5)}{0.42} = \frac{0.30}{0.42} = 0.7143$$

**Step 3(i) — Minimum Error (MAP) Rule**:

Since P(ω₂ | x) = 0.7143 > P(ω₁ | x) = 0.2857, the MAP rule classifies the pattern as **ω₂**.

**Step 3(ii) — Minimum Risk Rule**:

$$R(\alpha_1 \mid x) = \lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1 \mid x) + \lambda(\alpha_1 \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2 \mid x)$$

$$R(\alpha_1 \mid x) = (0)(0.2857) + (4)(0.7143) = 2.8572$$

$$R(\alpha_2 \mid x) = \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1 \mid x) + \lambda(\alpha_2 \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2 \mid x)$$

$$R(\alpha_2 \mid x) = (2)(0.2857) + (0)(0.7143) = 0.5714$$

Since R(α₂ | x) = 0.5714 < R(α₁ | x) = 2.8572, the minimum risk rule classifies the pattern as **ω₂**.

**Conclusion**: In this particular case, both rules agree on **ω₂**, because the likelihood strongly favors ω₂ and the loss asymmetry is not extreme enough to flip the decision.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to Normalize p(x)**: Many students write Bayes' theorem but fail to compute the evidence denominator. Always show the explicit computation of p(x) in numerical problems. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing λ(αᵢ | ωⱼ) Indexing**: The loss matrix convention is that the **column is the true class** and the **row is the action**. Reversing this gives inverted risks. **(Lose up to 2 marks)**
> 3. **Not Cancelling p(x) in LRT**: The denominator p(x) cancels in the likelihood ratio test, but students often carry it through algebraic manipulations. **(Lose 0.5–1 mark)**
> 4. **Ignoring the 0-1 Loss Equivalence**: When asked to show equivalence, students often skip the step Σⱼ P(ωⱼ | x) = 1. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Misinterpreting "Risk" as "Error Rate"**: Risk is *expected loss*, not probability of error. They coincide only under 0-1 loss. **(Lose 1–2 marks on definition questions)**
> 6. **No Diagram in Long Answers**: KTU awards 1–2 marks for neat, labeled block diagrams. Always include one. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Optimal Solutions for Minimum Error and Minimum Risk**
>
> - [x] **Bayes' Theorem** is the cornerstone: $P(\omega_j \mid x) = \dfrac{P(\omega_j) p(x \mid \omega_j)}{p(x)}$
> - [x] **Evidence (denominator)** is the mixture density: $p(x) = \sum_j P(\omega_j) p(x \mid \omega_j)$
> - [x] **Prior** = belief before observation; **Posterior** = belief after observation
> - [x] **MAP (Minimum Error) Rule**: pick the class with the **maximum posterior probability**
> - [x] **0-1 Loss Function**: assigns 0 to correct, 1 to wrong — gives uniform penalty
> - [x] **Minimum Risk Rule**: pick the action that **minimizes the conditional risk** $R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_j \lambda(\alpha_i \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j \mid x)$
> - [x] **Likelihood Ratio Test** for two classes: $\Lambda(x) = p(x \mid \omega_1)/p(x \mid \omega_2) \gtrless P(\omega_2)/P(\omega_1)$
> - [x] **Log-Likelihood Form** preferred numerically: $\ln p(x \mid \omega_1) - \ln p(x \mid \omega_2) \gtrless \ln[P(\omega_2)/P(\omega_1)]$
> - [x] **Bayes Error ε*** is the theoretical minimum — no classifier can beat it
> - [x] **Asymmetric Loss → MAP ≠ Min Risk**: This divergence is the central reason for using minimum risk
> - [x] **Minimax Criterion**: Used when priors are uncertain — minimizes worst-case (maximum) risk
> - [x] **Discriminant Functions** $g_i(x)$: equivalent representations of the same decision; pick class with max $g_i(x)$
> - [x] **Key Constants/Quantities**: ε*, p(x), P(ωⱼ), λ(αᵢ | ωⱼ), R(αᵢ | x), Λ(x), Λ₀ — all must be memorized
> - [x] **Real-World Applications**: medical diagnosis, spam filtering, fraud detection, autonomous vehicles, speech recognition

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Optimal Solutions for Minimum Error and Minimum Risk Criteria**
