topics.title: Normal Density and Discriminant Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Normal Density and Discriminant Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Normal (Gaussian) Density

### Formal Definition
The **Normal Density** (or **Gaussian Distribution**) is a continuous probability distribution characterized by a symmetric, bell-shaped curve. In the context of Pattern Recognition, we predominantly deal with the **multivariate normal density**, which describes the distribution of a *d*-dimensional random feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ belonging to a particular class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *d*-dimensional random vector $\mathbf{x}$ is said to have a **multivariate normal density** if its joint probability density function (PDF) is of the form:
> 
> $$p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$$
> 
> where $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the *d*-dimensional **mean vector** and $\Sigma$ is the *d × d* **covariance matrix** (positive semi-definite).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a crowded classroom where students cluster around their favorite teacher. Most students sit very close to the teacher (high probability), while a few stragglers sit at the back (low probability). If you plot the number of students versus their distance from the teacher, you get a **bell-shaped curve** — that is the Normal Density. 

In pattern recognition, every class (e.g., "spam emails", "handwritten digit 7", "malignant tumor") has a "center of gravity" (the mean) and a "spread" (the covariance). The normal density mathematically captures *how likely* a new pattern is to belong to that class based on its proximity and orientation relative to the cluster.

---

## 1.2 The Discriminant Function

### Formal Definition
A **Discriminant Function** $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ is a scalar function (or set of functions) defined for each class $\omega_i$ such that the classification rule becomes:
> Assign pattern $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.

The decision boundary between class $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ is the set of points $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** Discriminant functions are essentially *mathematical score-sheets*. Every pattern receives a score from each class's score-sheet, and the highest score wins. The boundary where two score-sheets tie is the decision boundary.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a recruitment agency that must decide which job category a candidate fits. The recruiter maintains a list of traits for each job (engineer, manager, designer). A discriminant function is like a **weighted scoring rubric** — for each candidate, it computes a "fit score" for each job. The candidate is assigned to the job where their score is highest. 

The *decision boundary* is the trait profile where the candidate is equally suited for two jobs — a tie.

---

## 1.3 The Link Between Normal Density and Discriminant Functions

The Bayes optimal classifier says: pick the class with maximum **posterior probability** $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$. Using Bayes' theorem:

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $p(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $i$, we can use the equivalent discriminant:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$$

When we assume $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ is **normal**, the discriminant functions $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ take closed-form algebraic expressions, and the resulting decision boundaries become **hyperplanes** (linear) or **hyperquadratics** (quadratic), depending on the covariance structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important for KTU exams?** The entire Module 1 builds toward deriving the discriminant function under three canonical cases of covariance. These three cases are the most frequently tested derivations in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Multivariate Normal Density

The function $p(\mathbf{x})$ is fully determined by two parameters:

1. **The Mean Vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu} = E[\mathbf{x}] \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — the center of the distribution.
2. **The Covariance Matrix** $\Sigma = E[(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T] \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ — a symmetric, positive semi-definite matrix encoding both the spread of each feature (diagonal entries) and the linear correlation between features (off-diagonal entries).

The term $(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ is called the **Mahalanobis Distance** $r^2$ from $\mathbf{x}$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}$. Unlike Euclidean distance, Mahalanobis distance accounts for the shape and orientation of the data cloud.

### The "Why" and "How" Behind the Formula
- **Why the $(2\pi)^{d/2}$?** It is the normalization constant that ensures the integral of $p(\mathbf{x})$ over $\mathbb{R}^d$ equals **1** (a fundamental axiom of probability density).
- **Why $\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}$ in the denominator?** It scales the height of the bell so that wider distributions (larger $\vert\Sigma\vert$) are flatter, preserving unit area.
- **Why the exponential?** It ensures $p(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ and produces a smooth, infinitely differentiable bell shape.

### Key Geometric Properties (KTU High-Yield)
- The density is constant on **ellipsoids** defined by $(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) = c^2$ (constant).
- The **principal axes** of these ellipsoids are the **eigenvectors** of $\Sigma$.
- The **axis lengths** are proportional to $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$ where $\lambda_i$ are the eigenvalues of $\Sigma$.

---

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Discriminant Cases

The form of $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ depends on assumptions about the class-conditional covariance matrices $\Sigma_i$. KTU 2024 syllabus mandates these three cases:

### **Case 1: $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$** (Isotropic, Identical Variance)
- All classes have **spherical**, equally-sized distributions.
- Boundary: **Linear** (a hyperplane).
- Equivalent to the **minimum distance classifier** (uses Euclidean distance to class mean).

### **Case 2: $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$** (Common Covariance, Arbitrary Shape)
- All classes share the *same* covariance but not necessarily spherical.
- Boundary: **Linear** (a hyperplane).
- The decision rule reduces to the **generalized linear classifier** with $\Sigma$ shared.

### **Case 3: $\Sigma_i$ Arbitrary** (No Sharing)
- Each class has its own full covariance.
- Boundary: **Quadratic** (a hyperquadric — hyperellipse, hyperparabola, hyperbola).
- Gives the most general **quadratic classifier**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Univariate Normal PDF | $p(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Special case with $d=1$ |
| 2 | Multivariate Normal PDF | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}}\exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$ | Foundational KTU equation |
| 3 | Mahalanobis Distance | $r^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ | Distance metric normalized by covariance |
| 4 | Bayes Discriminant (general) | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | Monotonic log-transform of posterior |
| 5 | Case 1 Discriminant | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{\Vert\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\Vert^2}{2\sigma^2} + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$ |
| 6 | Case 1 Simplified (equal priors) | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\Vert\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\Vert^2$ | Minimum Euclidean distance |
| 7 | Case 2 Discriminant | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ |
| 8 | Case 3 Discriminant | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_i\vert + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | $\Sigma_i$ arbitrary |
| 9 | Decision Boundary | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) - g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Defines class separation surface |
| 10 | Hyperplane (Case 2) | $\mathbf{w}^T(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_0) = 0$ | Linear boundary in feature space |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Discriminant functions built on multivariate normal densities separate benign vs. malignant tumors using blood marker features.
- **Speech Recognition:** Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) — built from normal densities — classify phonemes.
- **Face Recognition:** Eigenface methods rely on multivariate normal assumptions on face-image features.
- **Quality Control:** Statistical Process Control charts assume measurement noise is normally distributed, and discriminant functions automate defect detection.
- **Radar / Sonar Target Classification:** Targets separated by their acoustic or electromagnetic signatures are classified using normal-density-based discriminant boundaries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discriminant Function Under Case 1 ($\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$)

### **Step 1: Start from the Bayes discriminant (log-posterior form)**

The optimal discriminant for class $\omega_i$ is proportional to the log of the posterior probability:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

The constant $-(d/2)\ln(2\pi)$ is class-independent and can be dropped without affecting the decision.

### **Step 2: Substitute the multivariate normal PDF**

Plug in $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ assuming $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$:

$$\ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\sigma^2 I\vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T (\sigma^2 I)^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)$$

### **Step 3: Evaluate the determinant and inverse**

For a scalar multiple of the identity, the determinant and inverse are simple:

$$\ln\vert\sigma^2 I\vert = \ln(\sigma^{2d}) = 2d \ln \sigma$$

$$(\sigma^2 I)^{-1} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} I$$

### **Step 4: Substitute and simplify**

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - d\ln\sigma - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

The first two terms do not depend on $i$ (they cancel in comparisons), so we drop them:

$$\boxed{g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\Vert\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\Vert^2 + \ln P(\omega_i)}$$

### **Step 5: Decision Boundary**

Setting $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$ and using the identity $\Vert\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}\Vert^2 = \mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}^T\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}^T\boldsymbol{\mu}$:

$$-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[-2\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\right] + \ln P(\omega_i) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[-2\boldsymbol{\mu}_j^T\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_j\right] + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

Collecting terms yields a **linear equation in $\mathbf{x}$** — i.e., a hyperplane. The **weight vector** and **bias** are:

$$\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j, \qquad w_0 = \frac{1}{2}\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^T\boldsymbol{\mu}_j\right) + \sigma^2 \ln\frac{P(\omega_j)}{P(\omega_i)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discriminant Function Under Case 3 ($\Sigma_i$ Arbitrary)

### **Step 1: Full expansion of $\ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$**

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_i\vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

### **Step 2: Expand the quadratic form**

$$(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \mathbf{x}^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$$

### **Step 3: Substitute back into $g_i(\mathbf{x})$**

$$\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) = {} & -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i \\
& - \frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_i\vert + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 4: Decision Boundary**

Setting $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$:

$$-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T(\Sigma_i^{-1} - \Sigma_j^{-1})\mathbf{x} + (\Sigma_i^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \Sigma_j^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^T\mathbf{x} + K_{ij} = 0$$

where $K_{ij}$ is a constant absorbing the remaining scalar terms. This is a **quadratic equation in $\mathbf{x}$**, yielding hyperquadric decision surfaces (ellipses, parabolas, hyperbolae, or pairs of lines).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** If $\Sigma_i = \Sigma_j$ in Case 3, the quadratic term vanishes and we recover the **linear boundary of Case 2**. This unification is a favorite exam question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mahalanobis Distance from First Principles

The Mahalanobis distance of $\mathbf{x}$ from $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is defined as:

$$r = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

### Geometric Justification
If we **whiten** the data by applying the linear transform $\mathbf{y} = \Lambda^{-1/2} \Phi^T(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ where $\Sigma = \Phi\Lambda\Phi^T$ is the eigendecomposition, then in the new space $\mathbf{y}$, the covariance is the identity. The Euclidean distance in $\mathbf{y}$-space corresponds to the Mahalanobis distance in $\mathbf{x}$-space:

$$r^2 = \Vert\mathbf{y}\Vert^2 = \mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Phi \Lambda^{-1} \Phi^T (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mahalanobis over Euclidean?** Euclidean distance treats all directions equally. Mahalanobis distance *stretches* the space along the eigenvectors so that features with larger variance contribute proportionally less. This is the natural metric for normal distributions.

---

## 3.4 Summary Table: Decision Boundary Forms

| Case | Covariance Assumption | Decision Boundary Shape | Discriminant Complexity |
|------|----------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| Case 1 | $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$ | Hyperplane (linear) | Quadratic in $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$, linear in $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Case 2 | $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ | Hyperplane (linear) | Quadratic in $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ via $\Sigma^{-1}$, linear in $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Case 3 | $\Sigma_i$ arbitrary | Hyperquadric (quadratic in $\mathbf{x}$) | Full quadratic in $\mathbf{x}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Bayes Classifier with Normal Densities

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow from raw feature input to final class label, anchored on the normal density and discriminant function pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Pattern x] --> B[Feature Extraction Stage]
    B --> C[Feature Vector x in R^d]
    C --> D{Class Conditional PDFs}
    
    D --> E1["p(x | w1) = Normal mu1, Sigma1"]
    D --> E2["p(x | w2) = Normal mu2, Sigma2"]
    D --> E3["p(x | wc) = Normal muc, Sigmac"]
    
    E1 --> F1[Multiply by Prior P w1]
    E2 --> F2[Multiply by Prior P w2]
    E3 --> F3[Multiply by Prior P wc]
    
    F1 --> G1[Log Transform g1 x]
    F2 --> G2[Log Transform g2 x]
    F3 --> G3[Log Transform gc x]
    
    G1 --> H[Argmax Comparator]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    
    H --> I[Final Class Label omega star]
    
    style A fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Boundary Geometry for the Three Cases

The diagram below maps each covariance case to its corresponding geometric decision surface in a 2D feature space.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CASE1[Case 1: Sigma equals sigma squared I]
        A1["Class 1 mean: mu1"] --- B1["Class 2 mean: mu2"]
        B1 --- C1["Hyperplane Boundary: Linear Midpoint"]
    end
    
    subgraph CASE2[Case 2: Sigma common but tilted]
        A2["Class 1 Cluster: Tilted Ellipse"] --- B2["Class 2 Cluster: Tilted Ellipse"]
        B2 --- C2["Hyperplane Boundary: Tilted Linear Cut"]
    end
    
    subgraph CASE3[Case 3: Sigma i arbitrary]
        A3["Class 1: One ellipse shape"] --- B3["Class 2: Different ellipse shape"]
        B3 --- C3["Quadratic Boundary: Hyperellipse or Parabola"]
    end
    
    style CASE1 fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#333
    style CASE2 fill:#f8f4e8,stroke:#333
    style CASE3 fill:#f8e8f4,stroke:#333
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Pipeline: From Mahalanobis Distance to Class Score

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Input Vector x] --> Sub[Compute x minus mu_i]
    Sub --> Whiten[Apply Sigma_i inverse whitening]
    Whiten --> Norm[Compute Mahalanobis squared r squared]
    Norm --> Prior[Add log Prior P w_i]
    Prior --> LogDet[Subtract 0.5 log det Sigma_i]
    LogDet --> Score[Discriminant Score g_i x]
    Score --> Compare[Compare all g_i x values]
    Compare --> Output[Choose class with max g_i]
    
    style Start fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style Score fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#333
    style Output fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Note:** The "whitening" step in the pipeline is what *transforms* the elliptical contours of a normal density into spherical ones. After whitening, the classifier reduces to a simple Euclidean nearest-centroid rule — this is a powerful conceptual bridge between Case 1 and Case 3.

---

## 4.4 Algebraic Workflow for the Decision Boundary in Case 2

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with g_i x = g_j x] --> B[Substitute Case 2 discriminant]
    B --> C[Cancel class independent constant]
    C --> D[Expand quadratic form x minus mu transposed]
    D --> E[Cancel x transposed Sigma inverse x terms]
    E --> F[Collect coefficients of x]
    F --> G[Isolate constant bias term w0]
    G --> H[Final: w transpose x plus w0 equals 0]
    H --> I[Hyperplane in d dimensions]
    
    style A fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#333
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the multivariate normal density function. State the role of the mean vector and the covariance matrix in defining the distribution.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
The multivariate normal density of a *d*-dimensional random vector $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$$

- **Mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ (1 mark): Specifies the **center** of the distribution in $\mathbb{R}^d$.
- **Covariance matrix** $\Sigma$ (1 mark): A *d × d* symmetric positive-definite matrix that specifies the **spread and orientation** (shape of the ellipsoidal contours).
- The exponential term ensures $p(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ and the normalization constant ensures $\int p(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} = 1$ (1 mark).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is a discriminant function? How is it used in pattern classification?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Definition** (1 mark): A discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ is a scalar function assigned to each class $\omega_i$ such that the classifier assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with the largest discriminant value.
- **Decision Rule** (1 mark): Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.
- **Decision Boundary** (1 mark): The set of points where $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$ forms the boundary between classes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfall):** Students frequently lose **2 to 3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting to drop the class-independent constant $-\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi)$ in Case 1 derivations.
> 2. Confusing $\Sigma$ (the covariance of the *data*) with $\Sigma_i$ (covariance of *class i*). Subscripts matter.
> 3. Stating "linear" vs "quadratic" boundary without justifying it from the covariance assumption.
> 4. Skipping the step where they expand $(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ — full marks require showing this expansion.

---

### **Question B1(A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**Consider a 2-class problem with class-conditional densities being multivariate normal with common covariance matrix $\Sigma$ (Case 2).**
**(a)** Derive the discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ for each class. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Show that the resulting decision boundary is a hyperplane and derive the equation of that hyperplane. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The posterior for class $\omega_i$ is:

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$$

Take the natural log (monotonic, preserves argmax):

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Substitute the multivariate normal density with covariance $\Sigma$ (common to all classes):

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma\vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Since the first two terms are independent of $i$, drop them:

$$\boxed{g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)}$$

(Each step carries 1–2 marks; full expansion earns 7 marks.)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The decision boundary is $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$:

$$-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_j) + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

Expand the quadratic forms:

$$-\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i\right] + \ln P(\omega_i) = -\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} - 2\boldsymbol{\mu}_j^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j\right] + \ln P(\omega_j)$$

The $\mathbf{x}^T\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ terms **cancel** (since $\Sigma$ is common), leaving:

$$(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^T\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j) + \ln\frac{P(\omega_i)}{P(\omega_j)}$$

Define $\mathbf{w} = \Sigma^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)$ and the constant $w_0$ as the right-hand side. The decision boundary is:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0}$$

This is a **linear** equation in $\mathbf{x}$, i.e., a **hyperplane** in $\mathbb{R}^d$.

---

### **Question B1(B)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)** — *ALTERNATIVE*

**For a 2-class classification problem with class-conditional densities $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i, \Sigma_i)$ where $\Sigma_i$ are arbitrary (Case 3).**
**(a)** Derive the quadratic discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ and state all terms. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Under what condition does the quadratic discriminant reduce to a linear hyperplane? Justify mathematically. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Starting from the Bayes discriminant:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Substitute the general multivariate normal:

$$\boxed{g_i(\mathbf{x}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_i\vert + \ln P(\omega_i)}$$

- The first term is the **Mahalanobis quadratic** term.
- The second term is the **log-determinant** correction for varying class volumes.
- The third term is the **prior log-probability**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Setting $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$:

$$-\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T(\Sigma_i^{-1} - \Sigma_j^{-1})\mathbf{x} + (\Sigma_i^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \Sigma_j^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^T\mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i^T\Sigma_i^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i + \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j^T\Sigma_j^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j - \frac{1}{2}\ln\frac{\vert\Sigma_i\vert}{\vert\Sigma_j\vert} + \ln\frac{P(\omega_i)}{P(\omega_j)} = 0$$

The **quadratic term** $\mathbf{x}^T(\Sigma_i^{-1} - \Sigma_j^{-1})\mathbf{x}$ vanishes **if and only if**:

$$\Sigma_i^{-1} - \Sigma_j^{-1} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Sigma_i = \Sigma_j = \Sigma$$

Under this condition, the equation reduces to:

$$(\Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \Sigma^{-1}\boldsymbol{\mu}_j)^T\mathbf{x} + \text{constant} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{w} = \Sigma^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)}$$

This is a **hyperplane**, recovering **Case 2** as a special case of Case 3.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Multivariate Normal PDF** has TWO parameters: $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ (mean vector) and $\Sigma$ (covariance matrix). Both are needed; one alone is insufficient.
- **Mahalanobis distance** $r^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$ is the *natural* distance metric for normal distributions. When $\Sigma = I$, it reduces to Euclidean distance.
- **Three Cases** are exam-favorites:
  - **Case 1** ($\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$): **Linear** boundary using Euclidean distance to class means.
  - **Case 2** ($\Sigma_i = \Sigma$): **Linear** boundary using Mahalanobis distance (with shared $\Sigma$).
  - **Case 3** ($\Sigma_i$ arbitrary): **Quadratic** boundary.
- **Discriminant Function Rule:** Drop class-independent constants freely — only terms that **vary with $i$** matter.
- **Logarithm trick:** The $\ln$ of a normal density is taken because the $\exp$ becomes a linear term, and products of probabilities become sums — drastically simplifying algebra.
- **The weight vector** $\mathbf{w} = \Sigma^{-1}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j)$ points from class $j$ mean toward class $i$ mean, but is *rotated* by $\Sigma^{-1}$ to account for feature correlation.
- **Decision boundary** is the locus of points where $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$ — i.e., where the classifier is "indifferent" between two classes.
- **Most common KTU mistake:** Confusing Case 1 with Case 2. Case 1 assumes $\sigma^2 I$ (spherical), while Case 2 assumes a general common $\Sigma$ (tilted ellipsoids). The latter requires $\Sigma^{-1}$ in the discriminant.
- **Bayes optimality:** Under the normal density assumption, the discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x}\mid\omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ is the **minimum-error-rate** classifier.
- **Geometric intuition:** A multivariate normal density is shaped like a *d-dimensional bell* with ellipsoidal cross-sections. The eigenvectors of $\Sigma$ give the principal axes, and the eigenvalues give their squared lengths.
- **Practical limit:** When $d$ is large and the training set is small, $\Sigma_i$ cannot be reliably estimated, and **diagonal covariance** (Naive Bayes) or **isotropic covariance** (Case 1) are preferred.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decision Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Pattern Recognition**, a **Decision Surface** (also termed a **Decision Boundary** or **Decision Region Boundary**) is a hypersurface embedded in the $d$-dimensional feature space that partitions the space into distinct regions, each corresponding to a particular pattern class $\omega_i$. Formally, for a $c$-class classification problem, a decision surface is the locus of points $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ where two or more discriminant functions $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ are equal in value. For a two-class problem, the decision surface separating class $\omega_1$ from class $\omega_2$ is defined by:

$$\mathbf{x} : g_1(\mathbf{x}) - g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A decision surface is **not** the same as a discriminant function. The discriminant function assigns a *scalar score* to every point; the decision surface is the **equi-score contour** between competing classes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large country divided into multiple states by rivers. The river itself is a *physical boundary* — you cannot see it as a classifier, but you know that crossing it places you in a different state. In pattern recognition:

- The **feature space** $\mathbb{R}^d$ is the country.
- The **pattern classes** $\{\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_c\}$ are the states.
- The **decision surface** is the river — an invisible (mathematical) boundary.
- The **classifier** is the "border patrol" that decides which side of the river a new observation falls on.

When the river is **straight** (a line in 2D, a plane in 3D, a hyperplane in higher dimensions), the classes are called **linearly separable**. When the river **bends and twists** (circles, parabolas, complex curves), the classes are **non-linearly separable** and require more sophisticated boundary functions.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU 2024 Module 1

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions for Board Examination:**

- **Discriminant Function** $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ — A scalar-valued function that quantifies the evidence that a feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ belongs to class $\omega_i$.
- **Decision Rule** — The classification policy: assign $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.
- **Decision Region** $\mathcal{R}_i$ — The set of all points in feature space assigned to class $\omega_i$, bounded by one or more decision surfaces.
- **Linear Decision Surface** — A surface defined by a **first-degree** polynomial in the components of $\mathbf{x}$.
- **Generalized Linear Decision Surface** — A surface that is linear in some *transformed* feature space $\phi(\mathbf{x})$, though non-linear in the original space.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Decision Surfaces

Decision surfaces in pattern recognition are broadly classified by the **mathematical form** of the underlying discriminant functions:

### (a) Linear Decision Surfaces (Two-Class)
Defined by a single hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^d$:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the **weight vector** (normal to the surface).
- $w_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **bias** (or threshold).
- The decision rule is: classify as $\omega_1$ if $g(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, else $\omega_2$.

### (b) Piecewise Linear Decision Surfaces
Constructed by combining multiple linear segments — useful when the global data distribution is not linearly separable but can be partitioned into locally linear regions.

### (c) Quadratic Decision Surfaces
A complete second-degree polynomial yields a general quadratic form:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c = 0$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is a symmetric $d \times d$ matrix. Quadratic surfaces include **ellipsoids, hyperboloids, and paraboloids** in higher dimensions.

### (d) Non-Linear / Generalized Linear Surfaces
The surface is non-linear in $\mathbf{x}$ but linear in a transformed feature space $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ — the basis of the **Kernel Trick** in SVM theory.

## 2.2 Linear Separability — The Critical Concept

A set of pattern classes is **linearly separable** if there exists at least one hyperplane such that every sample of $\omega_1$ lies strictly on one side and every sample of $\omega_2$ lies strictly on the other side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why separability matters in KTU examinations:** A 14-mark question often asks the student to *test* whether a given 2D dataset is linearly separable by solving the inequality system $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + w_0 > 0 \;\; \forall i \in \omega_1$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + w_0 < 0 \;\; \forall i \in \omega_2$.

## 2.3 Distance From a Point to a Hyperplane (The "Geometric Margin")

Given the hyperplane $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$, the **perpendicular distance** from any point $\mathbf{x}_p$ to this surface is:

$$r = \frac{g(\mathbf{x}_p)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

This signed distance is the geometric foundation for the **margin-based classifiers** (Perceptron, SVM).

## 2.4 Multicategory Decision Surfaces

For $c$ classes, the decision surface between $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ is given by:

$$g_{ij}(\mathbf{x}) = g_i(\mathbf{x}) - g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

The total number of such pairwise decision surfaces — assuming every pair has a unique boundary — is $\binom{c}{2} = \dfrac{c(c-1)}{2}$. In many implementations (e.g., linear machines), only $c$ decision surfaces are constructed by treating one class as the reference.

## 2.5 Normalization of Decision Functions

To eliminate scale ambiguity in the weight vector, the decision function is often **normalized** so that:

$$g(\mathbf{x}_k) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k + w_0 = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } \mathbf{x}_k \in \omega_1 \\ -1 & \text{if } \mathbf{x}_k \in \omega_2 \end{cases}$$

This is the **canonical hyperplane** normalization used in SVM theory, where the margin becomes $\dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table as the rapid-revision anchor before any board examination attempt.**

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Geometric Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|:--------|:-----------------------|:------------------|:--------------|
| Hyperplane Definition | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ | Linear surface in $\mathbb{R}^d$ | $g : \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$ |
| Signed Distance to Hyperplane | $r = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Perpendicular distance, sign indicates side | Real number, scalar |
| Margin Between Classes | $\rho = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Width of separating strip (SVM) | Real, positive |
| Pairwise Multiclass Boundary | $g_{ij}(\mathbf{x}) = g_i(\mathbf{x}) - g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Boundary between $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ | $i \neq j$ |
| Pairwise Surface Count | $N_{\text{pairs}} = \dfrac{c(c-1)}{2}$ | Number of boundaries among $c$ classes | Integer, $\geq c$ |
| Quadratic Decision Surface | $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c = 0$ | Ellipsoid, hyperboloid, paraboloid, etc. | $\mathbf{A}$ symmetric |
| Linearly Separable Test | $\exists \mathbf{w}, w_0 : \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + w_0 > 0 \;\forall i \in \omega_1$ | Existence of separating hyperplane | Boolean |
| Augmented Feature Vector | $\mathbf{y} = [1, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d]^T, \quad \mathbf{a} = [w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_d]^T$ | Bias absorbed as extra coordinate | $\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$ |
| Augmented Hyperplane | $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} = 0$ | Hyperplane through origin in $(d+1)$-space | Passes through origin |
| Normalized Canonical Form | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \pm 1$ | Eliminates scale of $\mathbf{w}$ | $g \in \{-1, +1\}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Decision surfaces separate "benign" vs "malignant" tissue in feature space (e.g., based on cell radius, texture).
- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR):** Surfaces distinguish between handwritten digits 0–9 in 784-dimensional pixel space.
- **Spam Filtering:** The hyperplane separates "spam" from "ham" email based on word-frequency features.
- **Biometric Authentication:** Surfaces in iris or fingerprint feature space separate "genuine" from "impostor" attempts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Perpendicular Distance From a Point to a Hyperplane

### Statement
For a hyperplane defined by $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, derive the signed perpendicular distance from an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_p$ to the hyperplane.

### Step 1 — Choose an Arbitrary Point on the Hyperplane
Let $\mathbf{x}_s$ be *any* point satisfying the hyperplane equation. By definition:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s + w_0 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad w_0 = -\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s$$

This relation is the **anchor identity** for the entire derivation; the bias $w_0$ can always be replaced by $-\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s$ when we have a specific surface point.

### Step 2 — Construct the Vector From $\mathbf{x}_s$ to $\mathbf{x}_p$
The displacement vector from the surface point $\mathbf{x}_s$ to the query point $\mathbf{x}_p$ is:

$$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s$$

This vector $\mathbf{r}$ generally has two components: one *perpendicular* to the hyperplane (the distance we want) and one *parallel* to the hyperplane (which is irrelevant for the perpendicular distance).

### Step 3 — Project $\mathbf{r}$ Onto the Normal Direction $\mathbf{w}$
The unit normal to the hyperplane is $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. The signed perpendicular distance is the scalar projection of $\mathbf{r}$ onto $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$:

$$r = \hat{\mathbf{n}}^T \mathbf{r} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T (\mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

The numerator captures the **dot product with the normal**; the denominator **normalizes** the result into a true Euclidean distance.

### Step 4 — Expand the Numerator

$$r = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $w_0 = -\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s$ (the anchor identity)
Replacing $-\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s$ with $w_0$ from Step 1, we get:

$$r = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 6 — Recognize the Discriminant Function
The numerator is exactly $g(\mathbf{x}_p)$. Hence the final, elegantly compact result:

$$\boxed{\,r = \frac{g(\mathbf{x}_p)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Interpretation for Board Exam:** If $r > 0$, the point lies on the **positive side** of the hyperplane (the side toward which $\mathbf{w}$ points). If $r < 0$, it lies on the **negative side**. If $r = 0$, the point is *on* the hyperplane.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Augmented Vector Form (Bias Absorption Trick)

### Purpose
The bias term $w_0$ creates an asymmetry — the hyperplane does **not** pass through the origin. To restore origin-symmetry and simplify subsequent mathematics, we use the **augmentation trick**.

### Step 1 — Define the Augmented Vectors
Introduce a fictitious coordinate $x_0 = 1$ for every pattern and a fictitious weight $w_0$ (the original bias):

$$\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_d \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}, \quad \mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \\ w_2 \\ \vdots \\ w_d \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$$

### Step 2 — Rewrite the Discriminant Function
Expanding $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$:

$$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} = w_0 \cdot 1 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_d x_d = w_0 + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$$

This is **exactly** the original $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$.

### Step 3 — Write the Hyperplane in Augmented Form

$$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$$

The hyperplane now **passes through the origin** of the $(d+1)$-dimensional augmented space, eliminating the constant term entirely. This is essential for the perceptron convergence proof and for SVM duality.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Margin of a Canonical Hyperplane (SVM Foundation)

### Setup
After normalization, every $\mathbf{x}_k \in \omega_1$ satisfies $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k + w_0 = +1$ and every $\mathbf{x}_k \in \omega_2$ satisfies $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k + w_0 = -1$.

### Step 1 — Distance From the Origin to the $+1$ Hyperplane
Using the distance formula derived in Section 3.1 with $g(\mathbf{x}) = +1$:

$$r_{+1} = \frac{+1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 2 — Distance From the Origin to the $-1$ Hyperplane
With $g(\mathbf{x}) = -1$:

$$r_{-1} = \frac{-1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = -\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

### Step 3 — Margin is the Distance Between the Two Parallel Hyperplanes
The width of the separating strip is the difference of the absolute distances:

$$\rho = \left\vert r_{+1} - r_{-1} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} - \left(-\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}\right) \right\vert = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

$$\boxed{\,\rho = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Optimization Implication
Maximizing the margin $\rho$ is equivalent to **minimizing** $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ — the foundational SVM optimization objective:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, w_0} \;\; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_k (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k + w_0) \geq 1 \;\;\forall k$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Class Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Mini-Example for KTU Board Pattern:**

**Problem:** Three classes in 2D have discriminant functions:
$g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 4$, $g_2(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 + x_2 - 2$, $g_3(\mathbf{x}) = -x_2 + 1$.

**Solution (Construction of Decision Surfaces):**

The decision surface between $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ is the locus $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$:
- $g_1 = g_2$: $x_1 + x_2 - 4 = -x_1 + x_2 - 2 \Rightarrow 2x_1 = 2 \Rightarrow x_1 = 1$ (vertical line).
- $g_1 = g_3$: $x_1 + x_2 - 4 = -x_2 + 1 \Rightarrow x_1 + 2x_2 = 5$ (oblique line).
- $g_2 = g_3$: $-x_1 + x_2 - 2 = -x_2 + 1 \Rightarrow -x_1 + 2x_2 = 3$ (oblique line).

These three linear boundaries partition the plane into three decision regions, one per class. This is the **linear machine** approach to multiclass classification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of Decision Surface Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Feature Space in R-d] --> B{Is the class structure linear in x?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Linear Discriminant: w-transpose x + w0 = 0]
    B -- No --> D{Is it linear in some transformed phi of x?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Generalized Linear / Kernel Method]
    D -- No --> F[Use Polynomial or Quadratic Surface]
    C --> G[Compute Weight Vector w and Bias w0 from training data]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Form Decision Rule: assign x to argmax g-i of x]
    H --> I[Output: c Decision Regions separated by c-choose-2 Surfaces]
    I --> J[End: Classify any new pattern x]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Margin-Based Decision Surface Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Raw Input Vector x] --> F1[Feature Extraction Module]
    F1 --> F2[Normalization and Augmentation with x0=1]
    F2 --> F3[Linear Combiner: a-transpose y]
    F3 --> F4{Sign Test}
    F4 -- Positive --> R1[Region R1: Class omega-1]
    F4 -- Negative --> R2[Region R2: Class omega-2]
    F4 -- Zero --> R3[Region on Decision Surface: Reject or Tie-Break]
    F2 -.Distance Computation.-> D[Geometric Margin r = g of x divided by norm of w]
    D --> M[Margin Maximization: minimize norm of w]
```

## 4.3 Multiclass Linear Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input x in R-d] --> G1[Discriminant g1 of x]
    IN --> G2[Discriminant g2 of x]
    IN --> G3[Discriminant g3 of x]
    IN --> G4[Discriminant g-c of x]
    G1 --> A[Argmax Comparator]
    G2 --> A
    G3 --> A
    G4 --> A
    A --> OUT[Output: Class omega-i with maximum g-i of x]
    G1 -.Boundary g1 equals g2.-> S12[Surface S12]
    G1 -.Boundary g1 equals g3.-> S13[Surface S13]
    G2 -.Boundary g2 equals g3.-> S23[Surface S23]
    G1 -.Boundary g1 equals g-c.-> S1c[Surface S1c]
```

## 4.4 Decision Surface Geometry Visualizer (2D Intuition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point of Class omega-1] -. Distance r1 .-> H[Hyperplane: w-transpose x + w0 = 0]
    P2[Point of Class omega-2] -. Distance r2 .-> H
    H --> N[Normal Vector w pointing toward omega-1]
    H --> M[Margin rho = 2 divided by norm of w]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Decision Surface. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, 80–100 words):**

A **decision surface** is a hypersurface in the $d$-dimensional feature space that separates regions corresponding to different pattern classes. Mathematically, it is the locus of points $\mathbf{x}$ for which two or more discriminant functions are equal in value. For a two-class problem with discriminant functions $g_1(\mathbf{x})$ and $g_2(\mathbf{x})$, the decision surface is given by $g_1(\mathbf{x}) - g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 0$. The classification rule assigns any new pattern to the class whose discriminant function yields the largest value. Decision surfaces may be **linear** (hyperplanes), **quadratic**, or **non-linear** depending on the underlying classifier.

---

### Question 2: What is a Canonical Hyperplane? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

A **canonical hyperplane** is a linear decision surface in which the discriminant function $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$ has been **scaled** so that $g(\mathbf{x}) = +1$ for all samples closest to the surface in class $\omega_1$, and $g(\mathbf{x}) = -1$ for all samples closest to the surface in class $\omega_2$. This normalization eliminates the **scale ambiguity** of $(\mathbf{w}, w_0)$ and ensures that the geometric margin between the two classes is exactly $\rho = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. The canonical form is the foundation of the **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** formulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!**
> Common marks lost by students on "Decision Surfaces" questions include:
> 1. **Forgetting the bias $w_0$** when computing distance — always include it.
> 2. **Mixing up the discriminant function and the decision rule** — they are related but distinct.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ in the denominator** of the distance formula — writing only $g(\mathbf{x}_p)$ loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 4. **Not specifying the normal vector's direction** in diagrams — the sign of $g(\mathbf{x})$ determines which side is which class.
> 5. In the canonical form, students often write $g(\mathbf{x}) \geq 1$ but forget the **label $y_k \in \{-1, +1\}$** multiplier needed to combine both classes.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): KTU Module 1 — Internal Choice Option A

**(a)** Define a **linear decision surface** for a two-class problem. Derive the formula for the **signed perpendicular distance** from an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_p$ to the hyperplane $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the **canonical hyperplane**. Show that for a canonical hyperplane, the **margin** between the two classes is $\rho = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. Hence explain how this leads to the SVM optimization problem $\min \dfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ subject to $y_k(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k + w_0) \geq 1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Linear Decision Surface and Distance Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A linear decision surface in $d$-dimensional feature space is a $(d-1)$-dimensional hyperplane defined by:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$$

where $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_d]^T$ is the weight (normal) vector and $w_0$ is the bias. The classification rule is: $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_1$ if $g(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, and $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_2$ otherwise.

**Step 2 — Select a Point on the Hyperplane (1 mark):**
Let $\mathbf{x}_s$ satisfy $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s + w_0 = 0$, so $w_0 = -\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s$.

**Step 3 — Displacement Vector (1 mark):**
$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s$.

**Step 4 — Project Onto the Normal (2 marks):**

$$r = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T (\mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{x}_s)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_s}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result (1 mark):**

$$r = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{g(\mathbf{x}_p)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Part (b) — Canonical Hyperplane and SVM Margin (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Canonical Form Definition (2 marks):**
By rescaling $(\mathbf{w}, w_0) \to (\lambda \mathbf{w}, \lambda w_0)$ for any $\lambda > 0$, we may impose:

$$g(\mathbf{x}_k) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k + w_0 = \begin{cases} +1 & \mathbf{x}_k \in \omega_1 \text{ (closest points)} \\ -1 & \mathbf{x}_k \in \omega_2 \text{ (closest points)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Distance to the +1 Plane (2 marks):**

$$r_{+1} = \frac{g(\mathbf{x}_k)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{+1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 3 — Distance to the -1 Plane (1 mark):**

$$r_{-1} = \frac{-1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 4 — Margin (1 mark):**

$$\rho = \left\vert r_{+1} - r_{-1} \right\vert = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 5 — SVM Optimization (1 mark):** Maximizing $\rho = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$, leading to the standard SVM primal:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, w_0} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_k (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_k + w_0) \geq 1, \;\; \forall k$$

where $y_k = +1$ for $\omega_1$ and $y_k = -1$ for $\omega_2$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): KTU Module 1 — Internal Choice Option B

**(a)** Explain the **augmented vector formulation** of the decision surface. Why is the bias $w_0$ absorbed into the weight vector? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-class problem in 2D, the discriminant functions are:
$g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 4$, $g_2(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 + x_2 - 2$, $g_3(\mathbf{x}) = -x_2 + 1$.

Compute the **pairwise decision surfaces** and find the coordinates of the **point where all three decision boundaries meet** (the Voronoi vertex). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Augmented Vector Formulation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Motivation (2 marks):**
The original decision function $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$ has a constant offset $w_0$ that prevents the hyperplane from passing through the origin. Many proofs (e.g., perceptron convergence, SVM duality) become simpler when the hyperplane is origin-symmetric.

**Step 2 — Definition of Augmented Vectors (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ x_1 \\ \vdots \\ x_d \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \\ \vdots \\ w_d \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the Hyperplane (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The hyperplane now passes through the **origin** of the $(d+1)$-dimensional augmented space. The bias $w_0$ is no longer special — it is just the first component of the augmented weight vector. This unification simplifies gradient computations, perceptron updates, and SVM dual formulations.

**Part (b) — Pairwise Decision Surfaces and Voronoi Vertex (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Boundary $g_1 = g_2$ (2 marks):**
$$x_1 + x_2 - 4 = -x_1 + x_2 - 2 \;\Rightarrow\; 2x_1 = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 1$$
This is a **vertical line**.

**Step 2 — Boundary $g_1 = g_3$ (2 marks):**
$$x_1 + x_2 - 4 = -x_2 + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 + 2x_2 = 5$$
This is an **oblique line**.

**Step 3 — Boundary $g_2 = g_3$ (1 mark):**
$$-x_1 + x_2 - 2 = -x_2 + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; -x_1 + 2x_2 = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 2x_2 - 3$$
This is another **oblique line**.

**Step 4 — Find the Common Intersection (2 marks):**
Substitute $x_1 = 1$ from Step 1 into the equation of Step 2:
$$1 + 2x_2 = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 2$$
Check in Step 3: $x_1 = 2(2) - 3 = 1$ ✓.

**The Voronoi vertex is $(1, 2)$**. Verify: $g_1(1,2) = 1 + 2 - 4 = -1$, $g_2(1,2) = -1 + 2 - 2 = -1$, $g_3(1,2) = -2 + 1 = -1$. All three discriminant functions equal $-1$ at this point, confirming the triple intersection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read the night before the KTU exam):**

- **Decision Surface vs. Decision Region:** A *surface* is the **boundary** (a hypersurface of measure zero in $\mathbb{R}^d$); a *region* is the **volume** it encloses.
- **Hyperplane Equation:** $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ — always include the bias $w_0$ unless explicitly told otherwise.
- **Distance Formula (memorize verbatim):** $r = \dfrac{g(\mathbf{x}_p)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
- **Sign of $r$:** Positive $\Rightarrow$ same side as $\mathbf{w}$; negative $\Rightarrow$ opposite side; zero $\Rightarrow$ on the surface.
- **Linear Separability Test:** Find $\mathbf{w}, w_0$ satisfying $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + w_0 > 0$ for all $i \in \omega_1$ and $< 0$ for all $i \in \omega_2$. If solvable, the classes are linearly separable.
- **Canonical Hyperplane:** Forces $g(\mathbf{x}) = \pm 1$ on the closest points; eliminates the scale ambiguity in $(\mathbf{w}, w_0)$.
- **Margin Formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ — only valid for the canonical form.
- **Multiclass Boundaries:** For $c$ classes, there are $\dfrac{c(c-1)}{2}$ pairwise decision surfaces.
- **Augmentation Trick:** Set $x_0 = 1$ and absorb $w_0$ into the weight vector so that the hyperplane passes through the origin of the $(d+1)$-space.
- **Quadratic Surfaces:** $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c = 0$ — needed when the classes are *not* linearly separable and no simple kernel is appropriate.
- **Perceptron / SVM Connection:** Both rely fundamentally on the linear decision surface; the difference is the **objective** used to find $\mathbf{w}$ — perceptron uses misclassification count, SVM uses margin maximization.
- **Examiner's Hot-Button Phrases:** "Derive the distance" → always include the norm in the denominator. "Show the margin" → always start from the canonical form. "Find the decision surface" → set the two discriminant functions equal to each other.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameter Estimation Methods - Maximum-Likelihood Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parameter Estimation Methods - Maximum-Likelihood Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maximum-Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** is a fundamental parametric statistical inference method used in pattern recognition to estimate the unknown parameters of an assumed probability distribution by maximizing a *likelihood function*. The objective is to find the parameter vector $\theta$ that makes the observed sample data $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ most probable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Maximum-Likelihood Estimation is a statistical procedure that determines the parameter values $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ of a statistical model by maximizing the likelihood function $L(\theta \vert X)$, which measures the probability of observing the given data as a function of the parameters. It produces estimates that are asymptotically unbiased, consistent, and efficient under standard regularity conditions.

Mathematically, the MLE is defined as:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\, L(\theta \mid X) = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\, p(X \mid \theta)$$

The likelihood function $L(\theta \mid X)$ represents the joint probability (or density) of the observed data, treated as a function of the unknown parameter $\theta$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Detective's Best Guess"

Imagine you are a detective investigating a crime scene and you have **50 different suspects** (each representing a different value of $\theta$). Each suspect has a different probability of leaving behind the specific set of evidence found at the scene. Rather than blindly picking one suspect, MLE acts like the detective's logical mind:

- The detective computes the **probability of the evidence given each suspect** ($p(X \mid \theta)$).
- The suspect that makes the observed evidence **most probable** is declared the most likely culprit.
- This suspect is the **Maximum-Likelihood Estimate** $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - **Likelihood** $L(\theta \mid X)$ = Probability of data *given* parameters.
> - **Posterior** $p(\theta \mid X)$ = Probability of parameters *given* data (Bayesian view).
> - **Prior** $p(\theta)$ = Belief about parameters *before* observing data.

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\theta$ | True but unknown parameter vector |
| $\hat{\theta}$ | Estimated parameter value |
| $X = \{x_1,\ldots,x_N\}$ | Independent identically distributed (i.i.d.) sample |
| $p(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Probability density of one sample |
| $L(\theta \mid X)$ | Likelihood function of $\theta$ given data |
| $\ell(\theta)$ | Log-likelihood function |
| $N$ | Sample size (assumed **large** in asymptotic theory) |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU board exam, always state the **three big MLE properties** when asked: (1) **Asymptotically unbiased**, (2) **Consistent**, and (3) **Asymptotically efficient** (achieves the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound). This is a guaranteed 3-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure

The MLE methodology is built upon a structured pipeline that students must internalize:

- **Step 1 — Assume a Probability Model:** Begin by assuming the data follows a known probability distribution family (Gaussian, Bernoulli, Poisson, etc.) parameterized by $\theta$.

- **Step 2 — Write the Joint Probability:** Since samples are i.i.d., the joint probability of observing the full sample is the product of individual probabilities:
$$p(X \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)$$

- **Step 3 — Construct the Likelihood Function:** The likelihood is defined as this joint probability, now considered as a function of $\theta$:
$$L(\theta \mid X) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)$$

- **Step 4 — Apply the Logarithm (Log-Likelihood):** Because products are difficult to differentiate and cause numerical underflow, the natural logarithm is taken. This converts products to sums, which is the **log-likelihood function**:
$$\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta \mid X) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln p(x_i \mid \theta)$$

- **Step 5 — Differentiate and Equate to Zero:** Find the critical point by setting the partial derivative (or gradient) to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

- **Step 6 — Solve for the Estimator:** Solve the resulting equation(s) for $\hat{\theta}$ to obtain the MLE.

- **Step 7 — Verify the Maximum (Second-Order Test):** Confirm that the critical point is a maximum by checking the second derivative (Hessian for multivariate) is negative definite:
$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^2} < 0 \quad \text{at } \theta = \hat{\theta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **log-transform trick** is a KTU-favourite question. Always explain *why* we take the log: (a) it converts multiplication into addition, (b) it prevents numerical underflow with large $N$, (c) it preserves the location of the maximum since $\ln(\cdot)$ is a monotonic function.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — MLE for Standard Distributions

> [!CAUTION]
> The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ used in conditional probabilities is rendered as `\mid` or `\vert` below to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do NOT write `$P(X \mid \theta)$` with raw pipes inside table cells.

| # | Distribution | PDF / PMF $p(x \mid \theta)$ | Log-Likelihood $\ell(\theta)$ | MLE $\hat{\theta}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gaussian (mean $\mu$, known $\sigma^2$) | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $-\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$ | $\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ |
| 2 | Gaussian (mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$ both unknown) | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $-\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$ | $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
| 3 | Bernoulli (probability $p$) | $p^x (1-p)^{1-x}$ | $\sum x_i \ln p + (N - \sum x_i)\ln(1-p)$ | $\hat{p} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ |
| 4 | Binomial ($n$ trials, success prob $p$, $k$ successes) | $\binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ | $\ln\binom{n}{k} + k\ln p + (n-k)\ln(1-p)$ | $\hat{p} = \frac{k}{n}$ |
| 5 | Poisson (rate $\lambda$) | $\frac{\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}}{x!}$ | $\left(\sum x_i\right)\ln\lambda - N\lambda - \sum \ln(x_i!)$ | $\hat{\lambda} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}$ |
| 6 | Exponential (rate $\lambda$) | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $N\ln\lambda - \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | $\hat{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\bar{x}}$ |
| 7 | Uniform $\mathcal{U}(0,\theta)$ | $\frac{1}{\theta} \mathbf{1}_{[0,\theta]}(x)$ | $-N\ln\theta$ subject to $\theta \geq \max(x_i)$ | $\hat{\theta} = \max(x_i)$ |

## 2.3 Properties of the Maximum-Likelihood Estimator

The following properties are heavily tested in KTU University Exams and should be memorized:

- **Consistency:** As the sample size $N \to \infty$, the MLE converges in probability to the true parameter:
$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \xrightarrow{P} \theta$$

- **Asymptotic Unbiasedness:** For large $N$, the bias tends to zero:
$$\lim_{N \to \infty} E[\hat{\theta}_{MLE}] = \theta$$

- **Asymptotic Efficiency:** The MLE achieves the **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** as $N \to \infty$:
$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_{MLE}) \xrightarrow{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N I(\theta)}$$
where $I(\theta)$ is the Fisher Information.

- **Invariance (Functional Equivariance):** If $\hat{\theta}$ is the MLE of $\theta$, then for any function $g(\cdot)$, the MLE of $g(\theta)$ is $g(\hat{\theta})$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** MLE is the workhorse of modern pattern recognition. It underpins:
> - **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)** for speaker recognition,
> - **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** parameter training in speech recognition,
> - **Logistic regression** weight estimation in machine learning,
> - **Naive Bayes classifiers** in text classification,
> - **Bayesian Belief Networks** for medical diagnosis systems.

## 2.4 Fisher Information — A Brief Note

The **Fisher Information Matrix** measures the amount of information the data carries about parameter $\theta$:

$$I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right] = E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right]$$

For $N$ i.i.d. samples, the total Fisher information scales linearly:

$$I_N(\theta) = N \cdot I(\theta)$$

The **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound** states that the variance of any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is bounded below by the inverse of Fisher information:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{I_N(\theta)} = \frac{1}{N \cdot I(\theta)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The MLE is said to be **asymptotically efficient** because it attains this CRLB as $N \to \infty$. This is a standard KTU Part B sub-question.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step derivations** of MLE for the most important distributions, all of which are KTU board favourites. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MLE of a Univariate Gaussian Mean $\mu$ (Variance $\sigma^2$ known)

**Setup:** Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ be i.i.d. samples drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with known variance $\sigma^2$. We seek the MLE of $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Write the joint density.** Because the samples are i.i.d., the joint pdf is the product:

$$L(\mu \mid X) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify by extracting the constant prefactor.** Write the product explicitly:

$$L(\mu \mid X) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\right)^N \prod_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Combine exponentials using the identity $\prod e^{a_i} = e^{\sum a_i}$:**

$$L(\mu \mid X) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\right)^N \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood:**

$$\ell(\mu) = \ln L(\mu \mid X) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $\mu$.** Note that the first term is a constant (does not depend on $\mu$):

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2\right]$$

Apply the chain rule $\frac{d}{d\mu}(x_i - \mu)^2 = -2(x_i - \mu)$:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N} 2(x_i - \mu)(-1) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)$$

**Step 6 — Set the derivative to zero and solve:**

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu) = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N\mu = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Second derivative verification:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial \mu^2} = -\frac{N}{\sigma^2} < 0 \quad \text{(always negative, so maximum is confirmed)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The MLE of the Gaussian mean is the **sample mean** $\bar{x}$, regardless of the value of $\sigma^2$. This is intuitively satisfying — the best guess of the true mean is the empirical average.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MLE of Gaussian Variance $\sigma^2$ (Mean $\mu$ known)

**Setup:** Now estimate $\sigma^2$ assuming $\mu$ is known.

**Step 1 — Log-likelihood from Derivation 1, with $\mu$ treated as constant:**

$$\ell(\sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\sigma^2$ (treat as a single variable):**

Let $S = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$ for brevity:

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma^2} + \frac{S}{2(\sigma^2)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Set equal to zero and solve:**

$$-\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{S}{2(\sigma^2)^2} = 0$$

Multiply both sides by $2(\sigma^2)^2$:

$$-N\sigma^2 + S = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\;\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2\;}$$

**Step 4 — Second derivative test (verify maximum):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \ell}{\partial (\sigma^2)^2} = \frac{N}{2(\sigma^2)^2} - \frac{S}{(\sigma^2)^3} \quad \text{evaluated at } \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{S}{N}$$

$$= \frac{N}{2(S/N)^2} - \frac{S}{(S/N)^3} = \frac{N^3}{2S^2} - \frac{N^3}{S^2} = -\frac{N^3}{2S^2} < 0 \;\checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MLE of Bernoulli Parameter $p$

**Setup:** $N$ i.i.d. samples $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ from $\text{Bernoulli}(p)$. Estimate $p$.

**Step 1 — Likelihood function:**

$$L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}$$

**Step 2 — Log-likelihood:**

$$\ell(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[x_i \ln p + (1-x_i)\ln(1-p)\right] = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\right)\ln p + \left(N - \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i\right)\ln(1-p)$$

Let $k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ (number of successes):

$$\ell(p) = k\ln p + (N-k)\ln(1-p)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero:**

$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{N-k}{1-p} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$\frac{k}{p} = \frac{N-k}{1-p} \;\Longrightarrow\; k(1-p) = (N-k)p$$

$$k - kp = Np - kp \;\Longrightarrow\; k = Np \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\;\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{k}{N} = \frac{\sum x_i}{N}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Second derivative test:**

$$\frac{d^2\ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{k}{p^2} - \frac{N-k}{(1-p)^2} < 0 \quad \text{(always negative, maximum confirmed)} \;\checkmark$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MLE of Poisson Rate Parameter $\lambda$

**Setup:** $N$ i.i.d. samples from $\text{Poisson}(\lambda)$ with PMF $p(x) = \frac{\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}}{x!}$.

**Step 1 — Log-likelihood:**

$$\ell(\lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[x_i \ln\lambda - \lambda - \ln(x_i!)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate:**

$$\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N$$

**Step 3 — Set to zero and solve:**

$$\frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda} - N = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\;\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** $\frac{d^2\ell}{d\lambda^2} = -\frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda^2} < 0 \;\checkmark$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — MLE of Exponential Rate $\lambda$

**Setup:** Samples $x_i > 0$ from $p(x \mid \lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$.

**Step 1 — Log-likelihood:**

$$\ell(\lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[\ln\lambda - \lambda x_i\right] = N\ln\lambda - \lambda\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and set to zero:**

$$\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = \frac{N}{\lambda} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{N}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i} = \frac{1}{\bar{x}}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** $\frac{d^2\ell}{d\lambda^2} = -\frac{N}{\lambda^2} < 0 \;\checkmark$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
> Given 5 independent samples $\{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$ from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, 4)$, estimate $\mu$ using MLE.

**Solution:**

Apply the formula derived in 3.1:

$$\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{5}(2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10) = \frac{30}{5} = 6$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\sigma^2 = 4$ is known, we do not need to estimate the variance. If $\sigma^2$ were also unknown, we would also compute $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{5}\sum (x_i - 6)^2 = \frac{16+4+0+4+16}{5} = 8$.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — MLE Computational Workflow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete **operational pipeline** of Maximum-Likelihood Estimation, from data acquisition to estimator verification. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per the engine's safety rules.

    stepJ -- Yes --> stepL[Output theta hat MLE as Final Estimate]
    stepL --> stepM[Compute Fisher Information and CRLB]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Maximum-Likelihood Estimation (MLE) method. Why is the log-likelihood function preferred over the likelihood function in practical computations?**

**Model Answer:**

Maximum-Likelihood Estimation is a statistical parameter estimation technique that finds the parameter value $\hat{\theta}$ which maximizes the probability (or probability density) of the observed data $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ under an assumed probability model $p(x \mid \theta)$. Formally, $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_\theta L(\theta \mid X)$ where $L(\theta \mid X) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)$.

The log-likelihood $\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta \mid X) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln p(x_i \mid \theta)$ is preferred over $L(\theta \mid X)$ for three reasons: (1) it transforms the product into a sum, which is computationally simpler; (2) it prevents numerical underflow for large $N$ since products of small probabilities approach zero; (3) the natural logarithm is a monotonically increasing function, so the location of the maximum is preserved. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three asymptotic properties of the Maximum-Likelihood Estimator and state the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) inequality.**

**Model Answer:**

The three asymptotic properties of the MLE are:
1. **Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $N \to \infty$.
2. **Asymptotic Unbiasedness:** $\lim_{N \to \infty} E[\hat{\theta}_{MLE}] = \theta$.
3. **Asymptotic Efficiency:** The MLE attains the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound.

The CRLB inequality states that for any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$ and $N$ i.i.d. samples:
$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{N I(\theta)}$$
where $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$ is the Fisher Information. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the Maximum-Likelihood Estimate of the parameter $p$ for a random sample $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ drawn from a Bernoulli distribution with PMF $P(X=1) = p$ and $P(X=0) = 1-p$. Verify that your estimate is indeed a maximum using the second derivative test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the MLE of the rate parameter $\lambda$ for $N$ i.i.d. samples from a Poisson distribution with PMF $P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$. Show that the MLE equals the sample mean. Also compute the Fisher Information for one observation and state the asymptotic variance of the MLE. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) Solution — Bernoulli MLE

**Step 1:** Write the likelihood function. For $N$ i.i.d. Bernoulli samples:

$$L(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood:

$$\ell(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}[x_i \ln p + (1-x_i)\ln(1-p)] = k\ln p + (N-k)\ln(1-p)$$
where $k = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$.

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $p$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{N-k}{1-p} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $p$:

$$k(1-p) = (N-k)p \;\Longrightarrow\; k - kp = Np - kp \;\Longrightarrow\; k = Np$$

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{k}{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}$$

**Step 5:** Second derivative test:

$$\frac{d^2\ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{k}{p^2} - \frac{N-k}{(1-p)^2}$$

At $\hat{p} = k/N$, both terms are negative (assuming $0 < k < N$), so $\frac{d^2\ell}{dp^2} < 0$, confirming a maximum. **(7 marks)**

### Part (b) Solution — Poisson MLE

**Step 1:** Likelihood function for $N$ i.i.d. Poisson samples:

$$L(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\lambda^{x_i} e^{-\lambda}}{x_i!}$$

**Step 2:** Log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[x_i \ln\lambda - \lambda - \ln(x_i!)\right] = \left(\sum x_i\right)\ln\lambda - N\lambda - \sum\ln(x_i!)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\ell}{d\lambda} = \frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda} - N = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\hat{\lambda}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}}$$

**Step 4:** Verification: $\frac{d^2\ell}{d\lambda^2} = -\frac{\sum x_i}{\lambda^2} < 0$ confirms a maximum.

**Step 5:** Fisher Information for one Poisson observation:

$$I(\lambda) = -E\!\left[\frac{d^2\ell_{\text{single}}}{d\lambda^2}\right] = -E\!\left[-\frac{x}{\lambda^2}\right] = \frac{E[X]}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6:** For $N$ i.i.d. observations, $I_N(\lambda) = N/\lambda$. The CRLB is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\lambda}_{MLE}) \geq \frac{1}{I_N(\lambda)} = \frac{\lambda}{N}$$

The MLE attains this bound asymptotically, so $\text{Var}(\hat{\lambda}_{MLE}) \to \lambda/N$ as $N \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the MLE of the mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ of a univariate Gaussian distribution from a sample $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$. Assume both parameters are unknown. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the invariance property of the MLE with a suitable example. Also discuss briefly the relationship between the MLE and the Cramér-Rao Lower Bound. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) Solution — Gaussian MLE (Both Parameters Unknown)

**Step 1:** The joint likelihood is:

$$L(\mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{N}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{N}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3:** Partial derivative with respect to $\mu$ (set to zero):

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i}$$

**Step 4:** Partial derivative with respect to $\sigma^2$ (set to zero):

$$\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{N}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2 = 0$$

Multiply by $2(\sigma^2)^2$ and substitute $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$:

$$-N\sigma^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

**Step 5:** The Hessian matrix is negative definite at $(\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma}^2)$, confirming a maximum. **(7 marks)**

### Part (b) Solution — Invariance Property and CRLB Relationship

**Invariance Property:** If $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ is the MLE of $\theta$, then for any function $g(\theta)$, the MLE of $g(\theta)$ is simply $g(\hat{\theta}_{MLE})$. This is known as the **functional equivariance** of the MLE.

**Example:** From Part (a), $\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{x}$. If we want the MLE of the standard deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$, the invariance property directly gives:

$$\hat{\sigma}_{MLE} = \sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

Note that the MLE of $\sigma$ is **not** the square root of the MLE of $\sigma^2$ in a trivial way — it is direct by invariance. This saves us from re-deriving the entire likelihood maximization for $\sigma$.

**Relationship with CRLB:** The Cramér-Rao Lower Bound establishes the minimum achievable variance for any unbiased estimator:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{N I(\theta)} = \frac{1}{I_N(\theta)}$$

The MLE has a remarkable property: as $N \to \infty$, the MLE becomes **asymptotically efficient**, meaning its variance attains the CRLB. Equivalently:

$$\sqrt{N}(\hat{\theta}_{MLE} - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{1}{I(\theta)}\right)$$
