This is the asymptotic normality of the MLE. For the Gaussian mean, the MLE *exactly* achieves the CRLB for *all* $N$, not just asymptotically. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing likelihood $L(\theta \mid X)$ with probability $P(X \mid \theta)$:** Strictly speaking, likelihood is *not* a probability density in $\theta$ — it does not integrate to one in $\theta$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this distinction is not stated.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the log-likelihood:** Direct differentiation of the product form $L(\theta \mid X) = \prod p(x_i \mid \theta)$ is incorrect; always take $\ln(\cdot)$ first.
> 3. **Skipping the second-derivative test:** For full marks (especially in 7-mark sub-parts), always verify the critical point is a maximum, not a minimum or saddle.
> 4. **Mixing MLE of $\sigma$ and $\sigma^2$:** Remember $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ is the MLE of the variance. The MLE of $\sigma$ is its square root, **not** $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ (the latter is the *unbiased* estimator). Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Boundary cases for Uniform MLE:** For $\mathcal{U}(0,\theta)$, the MLE is $\hat{\theta} = \max(x_i)$, which lies on the boundary. Mentioning this boundary condition is essential for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** MLE finds $\hat{\theta} = \arg\max_\theta L(\theta \mid X) = \arg\max_\theta \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i \mid \theta)$.
- **Log-Likelihood is key:** $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ln p(x_i \mid \theta)$ — used because it converts products to sums and prevents underflow.
- **Procedure:** (1) Assume model → (2) Write joint likelihood → (3) Take log → (4) Differentiate → (5) Set to zero → (6) Solve → (7) Verify with second derivative.
- **Standard MLEs to memorize:** Gaussian $\hat{\mu} = \bar{x}$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$; Bernoulli/Binomial $\hat{p} = k/N$; Poisson $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{x}$; Exponential $\hat{\lambda} = 1/\bar{x}$.
- **Asymptotic Properties:** (1) Consistent, (2) Asymptotically unbiased, (3) Asymptotically efficient (attains CRLB).
- **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{N I(\theta)}$ where $I(\theta) = -E[\partial^2 \ell/\partial \theta^2]$.
- **Invariance Property:** MLE of $g(\theta)$ is $g(\hat{\theta}_{MLE})$ — extremely useful for derived parameters.
- **Asymptotic Normality:** $\sqrt{N}(\hat{\theta}_{MLE} - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1/I(\theta))$ — used to construct confidence intervals.
- **MLE vs. MAP:** MLE uses only likelihood; MAP incorporates the prior $p(\theta)$.
- **MLE vs. Method of Moments:** MLE maximizes likelihood; MoM equates sample and theoretical moments. MLE is generally more efficient.
- **Limitation:** MLE can be biased for small $N$ (e.g., $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}$ underestimates the true variance).
- **Real-world applications:** GMMs, HMMs, logistic regression, Naive Bayes, Bayesian networks — all use MLE in their training pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expectation-Maximization Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Expectation-Maximization Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Expectation-Maximization (EM) Method** is an iterative numerical optimization procedure used in pattern recognition and statistical learning to find **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** or **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimates of parameters in probabilistic models that involve **latent (hidden) variables** or **incomplete data**.

Formally, given a dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ generated from a probabilistic model with parameters $\theta$, where the observed likelihood $\ln p(X \mid \theta)$ is intractable due to unobserved latent variables $Z = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_N\}$, the EM algorithm alternates between two steps:

- **E-Step (Expectation):** Compute the expected value of the complete-data log-likelihood with respect to the posterior distribution of the latent variables given the current parameter estimate.
- **M-Step (Maximization):** Maximize this expected log-likelihood to obtain a new parameter estimate.

The process is repeated until convergence, defined by a threshold $\epsilon$ on the change in log-likelihood or parameter values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The EM algorithm is studied as a foundational tool for parameter estimation in models with incomplete data and is a direct prerequisite for understanding **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)** and **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)** in subsequent modules.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Two-Bias Coin" Problem**
>
> Imagine you have **two biased coins** (Coin A and Coin B) with unknown bias $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$. You randomly pick one coin (with equal probability), toss it 10 times, and record the outcomes (Heads/Tails). However, **you are not told which coin was chosen for each trial** — this is your *hidden variable*.
>
> - **E-Step (Guess Phase):** Based on current estimates of $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$, compute the *probability* that each past toss came from Coin A vs. Coin B.
> - **M-Step (Update Phase):** Using these soft assignments, re-estimate the biases $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$ by taking weighted counts of heads/tails.
>
> Repeat until your estimates stop changing. You converge to the true biases even though you never observed which coin was used! This is the magic of EM.

## 1.3 Why EM is Essential in Pattern Recognition

| Scenario | Role of EM |
|----------|------------|
| **Missing Data** | Recovers parameters when features are partially observed |
| **Latent Class Models** | Discovers hidden clusters (e.g., GMM) |
| **Noisy Observations** | Robustly estimates parameters under measurement uncertainty |
| **Semi-Supervised Learning** | Leverages unlabeled data via posterior inference on labels |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constants / Symbols (KTU Standard Notation)**
> - $X$ — Observed data (known)
> - $Z$ — Latent (hidden) variables (unknown)
> - $\theta$ — Model parameters to be estimated
> - $p(X \mid \theta)$ — Incomplete-data likelihood
> - $p(X, Z \mid \theta)$ — Complete-data likelihood
> - $q(Z)$ — Auxiliary distribution over latent variables
> - $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$ — Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Problem: Intractable Likelihood

When latent variables $Z$ exist, the observed-data likelihood requires marginalization:

$$p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} p(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

This summation (or integration, in continuous cases) is often **computationally infeasible**, especially for high-dimensional $Z$. Direct maximization of $\ln p(X \mid \theta)$ is therefore impossible.

## 2.2 Two-Step Operational Logic

### E-Step (Expectation)
- **Goal:** Form the expectation of the complete-data log-likelihood, conditional on the observed data and the current parameter estimate $\theta^{\text{old}}$.
- **Action:** Compute the posterior $p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}})$ over latent variables and use it to evaluate:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}}} \left[ \ln p(X, Z \mid \theta) \right] = \sum_{Z} p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}}) \, \ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

### M-Step (Maximization)
- **Goal:** Maximize $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}})$ with respect to $\theta$ to obtain the updated estimate.
- **Action:** Solve:

$$\theta^{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}})$$

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind EM — Guarantees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monotonic Convergence Theorem (Dempster et al., 1977):** The EM algorithm is guaranteed to **never decrease** the observed-data log-likelihood at each iteration. That is:
> $$\ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{new}}) \geq \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}})$$
> This guarantees convergence to at least a **local maximum** (or saddle point) of the likelihood surface.

## 2.4 Convergence Criteria

| Criterion | Formula | Practical Use |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| Likelihood change | $\vert \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{new}}) - \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}}) \vert < \epsilon$ | Most common in KTU problems |
| Parameter change | $\vert\vert \theta^{\text{new}} - \theta^{\text{old}} \vert\vert < \epsilon$ | Used when likelihood is hard to compute |
| Max iterations | $t \geq T_{\max}$ | Safety fallback |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol / Equation** | **Meaning** | **KTU Application** |
|------------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| $\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \mathcal{L}(q, \theta) + \text{KL}(q \Vert p)$ | Decomposition identity | Foundation of EM derivation |
| $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)}$ | ELBO (Evidence Lower Bound) | Maximized in E/M steps |
| $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{Z} p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}}) \ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)$ | Expected complete-data log-likelihood | Central object of EM |
| $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | Responsibility (GMM) | E-step for Gaussian mixtures |
| $\mu_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, x_n$ | Updated mean (GMM) | M-step update rule |
| $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}$ | Updated mixing coefficient | M-step update rule |
| $N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$ | Effective number of points in cluster $k$ | M-step update rule |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Speech Recognition:** Training acoustic models in HMMs using the Baum-Welch algorithm (a special case of EM).
- **Computer Vision:** Image segmentation via Gaussian Mixture Models on pixel intensities.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression clustering, haplotype inference.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Density estimation with mixture models for identifying outliers.
- **Recommender Systems:** Matrix factorization with missing entries (collaborative filtering).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the EM Algorithm (Dempster-Laird-Rubin Framework)

### Step 1: Start with Observed Likelihood

We wish to maximize $\ln p(X \mid \theta)$. Introduce any arbitrary distribution $q(Z)$ over the latent variables:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln p(X \mid \theta)$$

**Reasoning:** Multiplying by $q(Z)$ and summing (since $\sum_Z q(Z) = 1$) is a no-op.

### Step 2: Apply Product Rule of Probability

Using the product rule $p(X, Z \mid \theta) = p(Z \mid X, \theta) \, p(X \mid \theta)$:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}$$

**Reasoning:** Substitute $p(X \mid \theta) = \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}$ inside the logarithm.

### Step 3: Multiply and Divide by $q(Z)$

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \left( \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)} \cdot \frac{q(Z)}{p(Z \mid X, \theta)} \right)$$

$$= \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)} + \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{q(Z)}{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}$$

**Reasoning:** Use the logarithm property $\ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b$.

### Step 4: Identify the Two Terms

Define:

- **ELBO:** $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)}$
- **KL Divergence:** $\text{KL}(q \Vert p) = -\sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}{q(Z)}$

The identity becomes:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \mathcal{L}(q, \theta) + \text{KL}(q \Vert p)$$

**Reasoning:** Standard decomposition; $\text{KL} \geq 0$ always.

### Step 5: Apply Jensen's Inequality

Since $\text{KL}(q \Vert p) \geq 0$ for all $q(Z)$ and $\theta$:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) \geq \mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$$

**Reasoning:** This is also directly derivable via Jensen's inequality applied to the concave $\ln$ function. The ELBO is therefore a **lower bound** on the log-likelihood.

### Step 6: E-Step — Tighten the Bound

To make the bound as tight as possible, we minimize $\text{KL}(q \Vert p)$. The KL divergence is zero **if and only if** $q(Z) = p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}})$.

Therefore, the **E-step** sets:

$$q(Z) = p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}})$$

**Reasoning:** At this choice, $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}})$ (the bound is exact).

### Step 7: M-Step — Maximize the ELBO

With $q(Z)$ fixed from the E-step, the ELBO becomes a function of $\theta$ alone:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{Z} p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}}) \ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

The **M-step** computes:

$$\theta^{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}})$$

**Reasoning:** Maximizing $Q$ increases the ELBO, which in turn increases the log-likelihood (or keeps it the same), guaranteeing monotonic improvement.

## 3.2 Worked Example: EM for a 1D Two-Component Gaussian Mixture

**Setup:** Observed data $X = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$ from a mixture of two Gaussians with means $\mu_1, \mu_2$, common variance $\sigma^2$, and mixing coefficients $\pi, 1-\pi$.

### E-Step: Compute Responsibilities

For each data point $x_n$:

$$\gamma(z_{n1}) = \frac{\pi \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_1, \sigma^2)}{\pi \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_1, \sigma^2) + (1-\pi) \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_2, \sigma^2)}$$

$$\gamma(z_{n2}) = 1 - \gamma(z_{n1})$$

### M-Step: Update Parameters

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{n1}) \, x_n}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{n1})}$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{n2}) \, x_n}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{n2})}$$

$$\pi^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{n1})$$

### Convergence Check

$$\vert \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{new}}) - \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}}) \vert < \epsilon$$

If not satisfied, set $\theta^{\text{old}} = \theta^{\text{new}}$ and return to the E-step.

## 3.3 Variants of EM (KTU Reference Table)

| **Variant** | **Description** | **Use Case** |
|-------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **EM (Standard)** | Alternates E and M steps | General-purpose |
| **GEM** (Generalized EM) | M-step only requires $\theta$ that increases $Q$ | When M-step is intractable |
| **CEM** (Classification EM) | Uses hard assignments of $Z$ | Faster, less accurate |
| **ECM** (Expectation-Conditional-Max) | Replaces M-step with multiple CM-steps | Constrained parameter spaces |
| **SEM** (Stochastic EM) | Adds noise to parameters | Avoids local maxima |
| **Baum-Welch** | EM for HMMs | Speech, sequence modeling |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: EM Algorithm Iteration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Observed Data X, Initial Parameters theta0]) --> init[Initialize theta_old with theta0]
    init --> estep{E-Step: Compute Posterior}
    estep --> qdist[Set qZ = pZ given X, theta_old]
    qdist --> qcalc[Compute ELBO Lq_theta]
    qcalc --> mstep{M-Step: Maximize Q}
    mstep --> mupdate[theta_new = argmax Qtheta_theta_old]
    mupdate --> conv{Convergence Test}
    conv -->|Not Converged| update[Set theta_old = theta_new]
    update --> estep
    conv -->|Converged| output[Output theta_new as Final Estimate]
    output --> finish([End])
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of EM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Observed Data X]
        A2[Initial Parameters theta0]
    end

    subgraph ESTEP[E-Step Module]
        B1[Posterior Computation pZ given X, theta_old]
        B2[ELBO Evaluation Lq_theta]
    end

    subgraph MSTEP[M-Step Module]
        C1[Q-Function Computation]
        C2[Parameter Update theta_new]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Convergence Control]
        D1{Likelihood Change less than epsilon}
        D2[Stop and Output theta_new]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    D1 -->|No| B1
    D1 -->|Yes| D2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: EM in the Pattern Recognition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Pattern Recognition System] --> P1[Data Acquisition]
    P1 --> P2[Preprocessing]
    P2 --> P3{Data Complete?}
    P3 -->|Yes| MLE[Direct MLE / MAP Estimation]
    P3 -->|No or Latent Variables Present| EM[EM Algorithm]
    EM --> MLE
    MLE --> P4[Classifier / Model]
    P4 --> P5[Decision / Prediction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The EM algorithm acts as a bridge between incomplete/latent-variable datasets and standard maximum likelihood estimation, enabling parameter recovery in scenarios where direct MLE is mathematically intractable.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]**

### Question 1: Define the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. Mention the role of latent variables in it. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The EM algorithm is an iterative method to find maximum likelihood estimates of parameters $\theta$ in probabilistic models that involve latent (hidden) variables $Z$ alongside observed data $X$. It alternates between:

- **E-Step:** Computing the expected value of the complete-data log-likelihood under the posterior $p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}})$.
- **M-Step:** Maximizing this expectation to obtain updated parameters $\theta^{\text{new}}$.

Latent variables represent unobserved factors (e.g., cluster membership, missing features, hidden states). The EM algorithm's role is to "fill in" the unknown $Z$ probabilistically at each iteration, enabling tractable optimization of an otherwise intractable likelihood. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: State the monotonic convergence property of the EM algorithm. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The EM algorithm guarantees that the observed-data log-likelihood does not decrease at any iteration:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{new}}) \geq \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}})$$

This is because the E-step makes the ELBO equal to the log-likelihood, and the M-step increases the ELBO, which in turn increases the log-likelihood. The algorithm therefore converges to a **local maximum** (or saddle point) of the likelihood function. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, ESE Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm from first principles, starting from the observed-data log-likelihood. Clearly state the role of the ELBO and KL divergence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a two-component 1D Gaussian Mixture Model with means $\mu_1, \mu_2$, common variance $\sigma^2$, and mixing coefficient $\pi$, write down the E-step and M-step update equations. Show the responsibility formula explicitly. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a): Derivation of EM (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Start with the observed-data log-likelihood and introduce an arbitrary distribution $q(Z)$:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln p(X \mid \theta)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $p(X \mid \theta) = \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}$:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply and divide by $q(Z)$, then split the log:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)} + \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{q(Z)}{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the two terms:

- **ELBO:** $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = \sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(X, Z \mid \theta)}{q(Z)}$
- **KL Divergence:** $\text{KL}(q \Vert p) = -\sum_{Z} q(Z) \ln \frac{p(Z \mid X, \theta)}{q(Z)}$

**Step 5:** Since $\text{KL} \geq 0$:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) \geq \mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$$

**Step 6 (E-Step):** To tighten the bound, set $q(Z) = p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}})$, which makes $\text{KL} = 0$.

**Step 7 (M-Step):** Maximize $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{Z} p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}}) \ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)$ to obtain $\theta^{\text{new}}$.

This completes the derivation. **(7 marks — 1 mark per key step)**

---

##### Part (b): EM for Two-Component 1D GMM (7 Marks)

**E-Step:** Compute the responsibility that data point $x_n$ belongs to component 1:

$$\gamma(z_{n1}) = \frac{\pi \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_1, \sigma^2)}{\pi \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_1, \sigma^2) + (1 - \pi) \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_2, \sigma^2)}$$

where $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ and $\gamma(z_{n2}) = 1 - \gamma(z_{n1})$.

**M-Step:** Update parameters using weighted sufficient statistics. Define $N_1 = \sum_n \gamma(z_{n1})$ and $N_2 = \sum_n \gamma(z_{n2})$:

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_1} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{n1}) \, x_n$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_2} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{n2}) \, x_n$$

$$(\sigma^2)^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \left[ \gamma(z_{n1}) (x_n - \mu_1)^2 + \gamma(z_{n2}) (x_n - \mu_2)^2 \right]$$

$$\pi^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_1}{N}$$

Iterate until $\vert \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{new}}) - \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}}) \vert < \epsilon$. **(7 marks — 2 marks E-step, 4 marks M-step, 1 mark iteration logic)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain why direct maximization of the observed-data log-likelihood $\ln p(X \mid \theta)$ is often intractable in the presence of latent variables. How does the EM algorithm overcome this? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the standard EM algorithm with the Generalized EM (GEM) and Classification EM (CEM) variants. In what situations would you prefer each? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a): Intractability of Direct Maximization (7 Marks)

**The Problem:** When latent variables $Z$ exist, the observed-data likelihood requires marginalization:

$$p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{Z} p(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

For discrete $Z$, this sum may have exponentially many terms; for continuous $Z$, it becomes a high-dimensional integral. In both cases, the computation is **intractable**.

Additionally, the log of a sum creates coupling between terms:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \ln \sum_{Z} p(X, Z \mid \theta)$$

This prevents the use of standard MLE techniques (e.g., setting derivatives to zero in closed form), since the derivative does not decouple across data points.

**How EM Overcomes This:** EM works with the **complete-data log-likelihood** $\ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)$, which factorizes nicely (e.g., for i.i.d. data, it becomes a sum over $n$). The latent variables are integrated out probabilistically via the E-step using the current posterior, and the M-step performs tractable optimization on the expected complete-data log-likelihood. The ELBO framework guarantees that each EM iteration improves (or maintains) the true log-likelihood. **(7 marks — 3 marks for intractability explanation, 4 marks for EM's solution)**

---

##### Part (b): Comparison of EM Variants (7 Marks)

| **Criterion** | **EM (Standard)** | **GEM (Generalized EM)** | **CEM (Classification EM)** |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| M-step requirement | Exact maximization of $Q$ | Any $\theta$ that increases $Q$ | Hard assignment of $Z$ |
| Convergence speed | Moderate | Slower per iteration | Faster per iteration |
| Convergence quality | Local maximum of likelihood | Local maximum of likelihood | Mode of conditional likelihood |
| Use case | Default choice | When exact M-step is intractable | Clustering with known labels |
| Stochastic noise | No | No | No |

**When to prefer each:**

- **EM:** Default choice when the M-step has a closed-form solution (e.g., GMM with known covariance structure).
- **GEM:** Preferred when the exact M-step is computationally prohibitive (e.g., neural network parameter estimation). Any improvement in $Q$ suffices.
- **CEM:** Used in hard clustering scenarios (similar to K-means but with probabilistic foundations). Faster but only converges to a local mode of the conditional likelihood, not the marginal likelihood.

**(7 marks — 3 marks for table, 4 marks for preference discussion)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing ELBO and Likelihood:** Students often write $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) = \ln p(X \mid \theta)$ instead of the inequality $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta) \leq \ln p(X \mid \theta)$. Remember: ELBO is a **lower bound**, exact only at the E-step choice.
> 2. **Mixing E and M step roles:** The E-step computes the *expectation* of the log-likelihood (does not maximize $\theta$). The M-step performs the *maximization* (does not compute expectations). Confusing these costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the convergence criterion:** Always state an explicit stopping rule (e.g., $\vert \Delta \ln p \vert < \epsilon$ or max iterations). Omitting this loses a mark.
> 4. **Sign errors in KL divergence:** The KL term must be non-negative, so $\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \mathcal{L} + \text{KL}$ is correct. Students occasionally flip signs and lose derivation marks.
> 5. **Initialization sensitivity:** EM converges to a **local** maximum, not global. Examiners may award bonus marks for noting this in long-answer questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ EM is for **incomplete data** or **latent variable** problems where direct MLE is intractable.
- ✅ Two steps: **E-Step** (compute expected complete-data log-likelihood $Q$) and **M-Step** (maximize $Q$ w.r.t. $\theta$).
- ✅ The **ELBO** $\mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$ is a lower bound on $\ln p(X \mid \theta)$, tight when $q(Z) = p(Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}})$.
- ✅ Identity: $\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \mathcal{L}(q, \theta) + \text{KL}(q \Vert p)$, with $\text{KL} \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Monotonic convergence** is guaranteed: $\ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{new}}) \geq \ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{old}})$.
- ✅ EM converges to a **local maximum** (sensitive to initialization).
- ✅ For GMMs, E-step computes **responsibilities** $\gamma(z_{nk})$; M-step updates means, covariances, and mixing coefficients using weighted averages.
- ✅ Stopping criteria: change in log-likelihood $<\epsilon$, change in parameters $<\epsilon$, or maximum iterations.
- ✅ Variants: GEM (relaxed M-step), CEM (hard assignments), ECM (constrained), SEM (stochastic), Baum-Welch (HMM).
- ✅ EM is a **general recipe**, not tied to a specific model — applicable to any latent-variable probabilistic model.
- ✅ Real-world uses: speech recognition (HMMs), image segmentation (GMMs), recommender systems (matrix factorization), bioinformatics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian Parameter Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bayesian Parameter Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Bayesian Parameter Estimation** is a statistical inference framework where the unknown parameters $\theta$ of a probability distribution governing a pattern class are treated as **random variables** with a known prior distribution $P(\theta)$. Given an observed data set $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ drawn i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) from the underlying distribution, Bayesian estimation combines the prior knowledge with the evidence provided by the samples using **Bayes' Rule** to compute the **posterior density** $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$. Any subsequent inference — point estimation, hypothesis testing, or prediction — is then made by integrating over this posterior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Bayesian Parameter Estimation forms the probabilistic backbone of pattern classification. The examiner frequently tests three estimators derived from this framework:
> 1. **Maximum Likelihood (ML) Estimator** — uniform/flat prior assumption.
> 2. **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Estimator** — mode of the posterior.
> 3. **Bayes (Posterior Mean) Estimator** — expected value of the posterior.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather Reporter's Belief"

Imagine a weather reporter who has a *prior belief* that there is a **60% chance of rain today** (this is the **prior** $P(\theta)$). She then observes new evidence: dark clouds are forming, the barometric pressure is falling, and humidity is rising (this is the **likelihood** $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$). Using **Bayes' Rule**, she updates her belief to obtain a *new* probability of rain — say **85%** (this is the **posterior** $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$).

| Step | Weather Analogy | Bayesian Mathematics |
|---|---|---|
| Initial belief | 60% chance of rain | Prior $P(\theta)$ |
| New observations | Clouds, pressure, humidity | Likelihood $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ |
| Updated belief | 85% chance of rain | Posterior $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ |
| Final decision | Carry umbrella | Estimator $\hat{\theta}$ |

The key insight: **Bayesian estimation never throws away information** — the prior encodes previous knowledge (perhaps from a long meteorological history), and the likelihood encodes the current data. The posterior merges both into a single, updated belief about the parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Frequentist (Classical) Estimation**, $\theta$ is treated as a *fixed but unknown constant*, and one finds a single "best guess" $\hat{\theta}$. In **Bayesian Estimation**, $\theta$ is treated as a *random variable* with a distribution, and inference is performed on the entire posterior distribution $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$.

### 1.3 Why Bayesian Estimation Matters in Pattern Recognition

In pattern recognition problems, we rarely have absolute certainty about class-conditional densities $P(x \mid \omega_j)$. Bayesian parameter estimation provides a **principled, mathematically rigorous mechanism** to:

- **Quantify uncertainty** about parameter values after observing data.
- **Incorporate domain expertise** through the choice of prior.
- **Make optimal decisions** under squared-error loss (posterior mean) or 0–1 loss (MAP).
- **Handle small-sample regimes** where ML estimates are unstable, by "regularizing" with the prior.

### 1.4 Key Symbols and Notation

> [!NOTE]
> Standard notation used throughout KTU board papers:
> - $\theta$ — the unknown parameter (scalar or vector)
> - $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ — the observed data set
> - $P(\theta)$ — **prior** distribution
> - $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ — **likelihood** of data given parameter
> - $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ — **posterior** distribution
> - $P(\mathcal{D})$ — **evidence** (normalizing constant)
> - $\hat{\theta}_{ML}, \hat{\theta}_{MAP}, \hat{\theta}_{Bayes}$ — the three classical estimators
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bayesian Inference Pipeline

The Bayesian estimation procedure follows a strict five-step logical pipeline. Understanding *why* each step exists is essential for board marks.

**Step 1 — Choose the parametric form of the class-conditional density.**
The pattern recognizer hypothesizes that the data is generated from a known family, e.g., a univariate Gaussian $P(x \mid \theta) = P(x \mid \mu, \sigma^2)$. This is a *modelling assumption* justified by physical reasoning or exploratory data analysis.

**Step 2 — Specify the prior distribution $P(\theta)$.**
The prior encodes beliefs about $\theta$ *before* seeing any data. A *conjugate prior* is often chosen so that the posterior belongs to the same family as the prior, leading to closed-form updates.

**Step 3 — Collect the data set $\mathcal{D}$.**
The $n$ samples are assumed i.i.d. — this assumption is critical because it allows the joint likelihood to factorize as a product of marginal likelihoods.

**Step 4 — Compute the posterior using Bayes' Rule.**
This is the mathematical heart of Bayesian estimation. The posterior is proportional to the prior times the likelihood.

**Step 5 — Compute the desired estimator from the posterior.**
Depending on the loss function:
- Squared-error loss $\Rightarrow$ posterior **mean** (Bayes estimator).
- 0–1 loss $\Rightarrow$ posterior **mode** (MAP estimator).
- Uniform prior $\Rightarrow$ reduces to **ML** estimator.

### 2.2 Detailed Formulation

For an i.i.d. data set $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ drawn from $P(x \mid \theta)$, the **joint likelihood** of observing the entire data set factorizes as:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid \theta)
$$

Applying Bayes' Rule to obtain the posterior:

$$
P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}
$$

where the **evidence** $P(\mathcal{D})$ acts purely as a normalization constant:

$$
P(\mathcal{D}) = \int P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta) \, d\theta
$$

For point estimation, we apply a **decision-theoretic criterion**. Under squared-error loss, the optimal Bayesian point estimate is the **posterior mean**:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} = E[\theta \mid \mathcal{D}] = \int \theta \, P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \, d\theta
$$

Under 0–1 loss, the optimal estimate is the **posterior mode** (MAP):

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)
$$

When the prior is uniform (non-informative), the MAP estimate reduces to the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate**:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{ML} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid \theta)
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas verbatim** — they account for roughly **60–70% of Module 2 marks** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Rule (Posterior) | $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \dfrac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$ | Foundation of all Bayesian estimation |
| 2 | Evidence (Normalizer) | $P(\mathcal{D}) = \int P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta) \, d\theta$ | Integral over parameter space |
| 3 | Joint Likelihood (i.i.d.) | $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} P(x_k \mid \theta)$ | Assumes samples are independent |
| 4 | Log-Likelihood | $\ln P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \ln P(x_k \mid \theta)$ | Monotonic transform — easier to maximize |
| 5 | ML Estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{ML} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ | Uniform prior special case |
| 6 | MAP Estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$ | Mode of posterior |
| 7 | Bayes Estimator | $\hat{\theta}_{Bayes} = \int \theta \, P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \, d\theta$ | Mean of posterior (squared loss) |
| 8 | Posterior Variance | $\sigma_{N}^{2} = \left( \dfrac{n}{\sigma^{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} \right)^{-1}$ | For Gaussian mean, known variance |
| 9 | Posterior Mean (Gaussian) | $\mu_{N} = \sigma_{N}^{2} \left( \dfrac{n \hat{\mu}_{ML}}{\sigma^{2}} + \dfrac{\mu_{0}}{\sigma_{0}^{2}} \right)$ | Conjugate Gaussian update |
| 10 | Sufficient Statistic | $T(\mathcal{D}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k$ | All info about $\mu$ for Gaussian is in the sum |
| 11 | ML Mean (Gaussian) | $\hat{\mu}_{ML} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k$ | Sample mean |
| 12 | ML Variance (Gaussian) | $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{ML} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \hat{\mu})^{2}$ | Sample variance (biased) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation is avoided in cells to preserve markdown integrity. Absolute values, when needed, should be written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX renderers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bayesian parameter estimation is the workhorse behind:

- **Spam filtering** — estimating word-distribution parameters for the Naive Bayes classifier.
- **Medical diagnosis** — fusing prior disease prevalence with patient test results.
- **Autonomous vehicle perception** — Kalman filters are sequential Bayesian estimators.
- **Speech recognition** — Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) use Bayesian parameter updates during training.
- **Adaptive control systems** — Bayesian updating of plant model parameters online.

### 2.5 Sufficient Statistics — A Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A statistic $T(\mathcal{D})$ is **sufficient** for $\theta$ if the conditional distribution of the data given the statistic does not depend on $\theta$:
>
> $$P(\mathcal{D} \mid T(\mathcal{D}), \theta) = P(\mathcal{D} \mid T(\mathcal{D}))$$
>
> For the Gaussian distribution with unknown mean $\mu$ and known variance, $T(\mathcal{D}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k$ is sufficient. This is the **Factorization Theorem** (Neyman–Fisher).

Sufficient statistics allow the Bayesian estimator to be expressed purely in terms of $T(\mathcal{D})$ rather than the entire data set — a massive computational saving for large $n$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mandate:** Derivations carry **7 to 10 marks** in any 14-mark Part-B question. Skipping steps or omitting the "because" behind each transition results in **strict 2-mark penalties per logical gap**. The following derivations are presented in full algebraic detail.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Bayes' Rule to the Posterior Density

**Goal:** Starting from the definition of conditional probability, derive the posterior formula.

**Step 1.** By the definition of conditional probability:

$$
P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\theta, \mathcal{D})}{P(\mathcal{D})}
$$

This is the symmetric form of conditional probability: the joint probability of $\theta$ and $\mathcal{D}$ divided by the marginal probability of $\mathcal{D}$.

**Step 2.** Apply the product rule of probability to expand the joint:

$$
P(\theta, \mathcal{D}) = P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)
$$

This substitution is valid because the joint probability of two events can be written as the conditional probability of one given the other, multiplied by the marginal probability of the conditioning event.

**Step 3.** Substitute back into the conditional probability expression:

$$
P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}
$$

This is **Bayes' Rule** in its posterior form. The denominator is the normalizing constant that ensures the posterior integrates to 1.

**Step 4.** For inference purposes, since $P(\mathcal{D})$ does not depend on $\theta$, the proportionality statement is often used:

$$
P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)
$$

This proportionality is the workhorse of Bayesian computation: we maximize the right-hand side over $\theta$ without needing to evaluate the integral in the denominator.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Likelihood Estimator for a Univariate Gaussian

**Setup:** Let $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ be i.i.d. samples from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The likelihood is:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_k - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)
$$

**Step 1.** Combine the product using exponent properties:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \left( \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \right)^{n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 \right)
$$

The exponential of a sum equals the product of exponentials, so we factor the sum of squared errors out of the product.

**Step 2.** Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood (monotonic transformation):

$$
\ln P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2} \ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2} \ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2
$$

The logarithm is a monotonically increasing function, so maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to maximizing the likelihood itself.

**Step 3.** Differentiate with respect to $\mu$ and set to zero for the optimum:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu} \ln P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu) = 0
$$

The derivative of $-(x_k - \mu)^2$ with respect to $\mu$ is $2(x_k - \mu)$, and combined with the $-1/(2\sigma^2)$ prefactor gives the result.

**Step 4.** Solve the resulting equation for $\mu$:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k - n\mu = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\mu}_{ML} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k
$$

This is the familiar **sample mean** — the ML estimate of the Gaussian mean.

**Step 5.** Differentiate with respect to $\sigma^2$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma^2} \ln P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2\sigma^4} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 = 0
$$

The first term comes from differentiating $-(n/2)\ln(\sigma^2)$. The second term comes from differentiating the squared-error term.

**Step 6.** Solve for $\sigma^2$:

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{ML} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \hat{\mu}_{ML})^2
$$

This is the **biased** ML estimate of the variance. The unbiased correction uses $n-1$ in the denominator.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Posterior Density for Gaussian Mean (Known Variance)

**Setup:** Assume $x_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with known $\sigma^2$. Place a Gaussian prior on $\mu$:

$$
P(\mu) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)
$$

**Step 1.** Write the likelihood in the Gaussian form:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 \right)
$$

We drop the leading $\sigma$-dependent constants because they are independent of $\mu$ and do not affect the location of the posterior.

**Step 2.** Write the prior in Gaussian form:

$$
P(\mu) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right)
$$

The constant prefactor $1/\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_0^2}$ is dropped for the same reason.

**Step 3.** Multiply prior and likelihood, and expand the squared terms:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) P(\mu) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right)
$$

Bayes' Rule tells us the posterior is proportional to the product of prior and likelihood.

**Step 4.** Expand the squared-error sum and complete the square in $\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 &= \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k^2 - 2\mu x_k + \mu^2) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k^2 - 2\mu \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k + n\mu^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The expansion uses the standard identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$, applied to each sample and then summed.

**Step 5.** Collect all $\mu^2$ and $\mu$ terms in the exponent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exponent} &= -\frac{1}{2}\left[ \mu^2 \left( \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \right) - 2\mu \left( \frac{\sum x_k}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} \right) + \text{constants} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The coefficient of $\mu^2$ is the inverse of the posterior variance; the coefficient of $-2\mu$ is twice the posterior-mean scaled by the inverse variance.

**Step 6.** Identify the posterior parameters by matching the standard Gaussian form $a\mu^2 - 2b\mu$:

$$
\frac{1}{\sigma_N^2} = \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\mu_N}{\sigma_N^2} = \frac{n \bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2}
$$

The matching follows from the fact that a Gaussian density is uniquely determined by its first two moments.

**Step 7.** Final posterior:

$$
P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = \mathcal{N}\left( \mu_N, \sigma_N^2 \right)
$$

where

$$
\mu_N = \frac{\sigma_N^2}{\sigma^2} n \bar{x} + \frac{\sigma_N^2}{\sigma_0^2} \mu_0
$$

This is a **weighted average of the prior mean and the sample mean**, where the weights are the precisions (inverse variances). It is the canonical "prior + data" Bayesian update.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: The MAP Estimator as a Regularized ML Estimator

**Step 1.** Start with the MAP objective:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)
$$

**Step 2.** Take the log (monotonic):

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} \, \ln P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) + \ln P(\theta)
$$

**Step 3.** Compare to the ML objective $\hat{\theta}_{ML} = \arg\max_{\theta} \ln P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \hat{\theta}_{ML} + \text{prior contribution}
$$

The MAP estimate *regularizes* the ML estimate by penalizing parameter values that have low prior probability.

**Step 4.** For a Gaussian prior $\mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$, the log-prior is:

$$
\ln P(\mu) = -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} + \text{constant}
$$

**Step 5.** The MAP objective becomes:

$$
\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\mu} \, \left[ -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right]
$$

**Step 6.** Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \hat{\mu}_{MAP}) - \frac{\hat{\mu}_{MAP} - \mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} = 0
$$

**Step 7.** Solve for $\hat{\mu}_{MAP}$:

$$
\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \frac{n \sigma_0^2}{\sigma^2 + n\sigma_0^2} \bar{x} + \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2 + n\sigma_0^2} \mu_0
$$

This is a *convex combination* of the sample mean and the prior mean. As $n \to \infty$, $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x}$ (data dominates). As $n \to 0$, $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \mu_0$ (prior dominates). This is the celebrated **regularization effect** of the Bayesian prior.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Estimator | Objective | Loss Function | Prior Required? | Asymptotic Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ML** | $\arg\max P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ | Implicit (no prior) | No | $\hat{\theta}_{ML} \to \theta^*$ as $n \to \infty$ |
| **MAP** | $\arg\max P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$ | 0–1 | Yes | Dominated by data for large $n$ |
| **Bayes** | $\int \theta P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) d\theta$ | Squared error | Yes | Variance $\to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayesian Parameter Estimation Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the full inference pipeline. Node identifiers are alphanumeric to comply with rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Problem] --> B0[Choose Parametric Model]
    B0 --> C0[Specify Prior P of theta]
    C0 --> D0[Collect i.i.d. Data D]
    D0 --> E0[Compute Joint Likelihood]
    E0 --> F0[Apply Bayes Rule]
    F0 --> G0[Posterior P of theta given D]
    G0 --> H0{Estimator Type}
    H0 -->|Mode| I0[MAP Estimator]
    H0 -->|Mean| J0[Bayes Estimator]
    H0 -->|Uniform Prior| K0[ML Estimator]
    I0 --> L0[Final Estimate]
    J0 --> L0
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0[Use in Classifier or Predictor]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Gaussian Mean Estimation

The following diagram maps the functional blocks involved in estimating a Gaussian mean using conjugate Bayesian analysis. This is a frequent 7-mark KTU diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Prior Mean mu0] --> M0[Multiply by 1 divided by sigma0 squared]
    D0[Data Sum of xk] --> M1[Multiply by n divided by sigma squared]
    M0 --> A0[Adder Combines Prior and Data]
    M1 --> A0
    A0 --> C0[Multiply by Posterior Variance sigma N squared]
    C0 --> O0[Posterior Mean muN]
    V0[Compute sigma N squared = 1 divided by n by sigma squared plus 1 by sigma0 squared] --> C0
    V0 --> R0[Posterior Variance sigma N squared]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block — ML, MAP, and Bayes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Unknown Parameter theta] --> P0[Prior P of theta]
    P0 --> T0[Bayes Posterior P of theta given D]
    D0[Data D] --> L0[Likelihood P of D given theta]
    L0 --> T0
    T0 --> E0{Select Estimator}
    E0 -->|Mode of Posterior| R0[MAP Estimate]
    E0 -->|Mean of Posterior| R1[Bayes Estimate]
    E0 -->|Ignore Prior| R2[ML Estimate]
    R0 --> O0[Decision]
    R1 --> O0
    R2 --> O0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each rectangular node represents a computation step. Directed edges show the data flow. The diamond-shaped decision node in the first diagram represents the choice of estimator (Mode, Mean, or Uniform-Prior ML) based on the loss function and prior availability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Bayesian parameter estimation framework. What is the role of the prior distribution in this framework?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bayesian parameter estimation is a statistical inference method where unknown parameters $\theta$ of a probability distribution are treated as random variables with a known **prior distribution** $P(\theta)$. Given the observed data set $\mathcal{D}$, the prior is combined with the likelihood $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ using **Bayes' Rule** to compute the **posterior distribution** $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$:

$$
P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}
$$

The **role of the prior** is to encode existing knowledge or belief about the parameter *before* observing the data. It acts as a regularizer, prevents overfitting in small-sample regimes, and ensures the posterior is well-defined even when the likelihood alone is ambiguous. In the limit of infinite data, the influence of the prior diminishes and the posterior concentrates around the true parameter.

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Maximum Likelihood (ML) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ML Estimator | MAP Estimator |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ | $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$ |
| Prior | Not required (assumed uniform) | Required |
| Computational form | Maximize log-likelihood | Maximize log-likelihood + log-prior |
| Behaviour with small $n$ | High variance, unstable | More stable (regularized) |
| Limiting case | Special case of MAP with flat prior | Reduces to ML when prior is uniform |

The MAP estimator incorporates prior information and therefore gives more reliable estimates when training data is scarce. The ML estimator is preferred when no reliable prior is available.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Bayesian Estimation Questions:**
> - **Losing 2 marks:** Writing Bayes' Rule without defining the terms.
> - **Losing 3 marks:** Confusing MAP (mode) with Bayes estimator (mean). Board papers explicitly test the distinction.
> - **Losing 2 marks:** Forgetting to mention the *conjugate prior* property in Gaussian derivations.
> - **Losing 1 mark each:** Skipping intermediate algebraic steps in the variance update derivation.
> - **Losing 1 mark:** Not stating the limiting behaviour as $n \to \infty$.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** *Derive the posterior distribution of the mean $\mu$ of a univariate Gaussian distribution, assuming known variance $\sigma^2$, with a Gaussian prior $\mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$. Show that the posterior mean is a weighted combination of the prior mean and the sample mean.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Given the prior $\mu_0 = 5$, prior variance $\sigma_0^2 = 4$, sample size $n = 10$, sample mean $\bar{x} = 7$, and known data variance $\sigma^2 = 9$, compute (i) the posterior variance $\sigma_N^2$, (ii) the posterior mean $\mu_N$, and (iii) the MAP estimate of $\mu$.*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1.** Write the joint likelihood assuming i.i.d. samples:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) = \prod_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_k - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)
$$

**Step 2.** Write the prior:

$$
P(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_0^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right)
$$

**Step 3.** Combine via Bayes' Rule, dropping constants not depending on $\mu$:

$$
P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} \right)
$$

**Step 4.** Expand the squared-error sum:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k^2 - 2\mu n\bar{x} + n\mu^2
$$

where $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k$.

**Step 5.** Collect terms in $\mu^2$ and $\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exponent} &= -\frac{1}{2}\left[ \mu^2 \left( \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \right) - 2\mu \left( \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} \right) + \text{const} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6.** Match with the standard Gaussian form. The coefficient of $\mu^2$ is $\frac{1}{\sigma_N^2}$ and twice the coefficient of $\mu$ is $\frac{2\mu_N}{\sigma_N^2}$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{\sigma_N^2} = \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\mu_N}{\sigma_N^2} = \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2}
$$

**Step 7.** Solve for the posterior mean:

$$
\mu_N = \sigma_N^2 \left( \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} \right) = \frac{n\sigma_0^2 \bar{x} + \sigma^2 \mu_0}{n\sigma_0^2 + \sigma^2}
$$

This is the **weighted average** of the prior mean $\mu_0$ and the sample mean $\bar{x}$, with weights proportional to the precisions $1/\sigma_0^2$ and $n/\sigma^2$ respectively.

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: $\mu_0 = 5$, $\sigma_0^2 = 4$, $n = 10$, $\bar{x} = 7$, $\sigma^2 = 9$.

**(i) Posterior variance:**

$$
\sigma_N^2 = \left( \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \right)^{-1} = \left( \frac{10}{9} + \frac{1}{4} \right)^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{10}{9} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{40 + 9}{36} = \frac{49}{36}
$$

$$
\sigma_N^2 = \frac{36}{49} \approx 0.7347
$$

**(ii) Posterior mean:**

$$
\mu_N = \sigma_N^2 \left( \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} \right) = \frac{36}{49} \left( \frac{10 \cdot 7}{9} + \frac{5}{4} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{70}{9} + \frac{5}{4} = \frac{280 + 45}{36} = \frac{325}{36}
$$

$$
\mu_N = \frac{36}{49} \cdot \frac{325}{36} = \frac{325}{49} \approx 6.633
$$

**(iii) MAP estimate:**

For a symmetric Gaussian posterior, the mode equals the mean, so:

$$
\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \mu_N \approx 6.633
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the concept of a sufficient statistic. Prove that for a univariate Gaussian with unknown mean and known variance, $T(\mathcal{D}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k$ is a sufficient statistic for $\mu$.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *For the data set $\mathcal{D} = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$ assumed to be drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, 4)$ with a Gaussian prior $\mathcal{N}(3, 1)$ on $\mu$, compute (i) the sample mean, (ii) the posterior variance, (iii) the posterior mean, and (iv) the Bayes estimate of $\mu$.*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

A statistic $T(\mathcal{D})$ is **sufficient** for $\theta$ if, given the value of $T$, the conditional distribution of the data no longer depends on $\theta$. Formally:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid T(\mathcal{D}), \theta) = P(\mathcal{D} \mid T(\mathcal{D}))
$$

**Proof that $T(\mathcal{D}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k$ is sufficient for $\mu$:**

**Step 1.** Write the joint likelihood:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) = \left( \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \right)^{n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 \right)
$$

**Step 2.** Expand the squared-error sum:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - \mu)^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k^2 - 2\mu \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k + n\mu^2
$$

**Step 3.** Factor the likelihood using the Neyman–Fisher Factorization Theorem:

$$
P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) = \underbrace{\left( \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} \right)^{n/2} \exp\left( -\frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)}_{g(\mathcal{D}, \sigma^2)} \cdot \underbrace{\exp\left( \frac{\mu \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k - n\mu^2/2}{\sigma^2} \right)}_{h(T(\mathcal{D}), \mu)}
$$

The first factor $g$ does not depend on $\mu$; the second factor $h$ depends on the data only through $T(\mathcal{D}) = \sum x_k$. By the Factorization Theorem, $T(\mathcal{D})$ is sufficient for $\mu$. $\blacksquare$

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: $\mathcal{D} = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$, $\sigma^2 = 4$, prior $\mathcal{N}(\mu_0 = 3, \sigma_0^2 = 1)$, $n = 5$.

**(i) Sample mean:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10}{5} = \frac{30}{5} = 6
$$

**(ii) Posterior variance:**

$$
\sigma_N^2 = \left( \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \right)^{-1} = \left( \frac{5}{4} + 1 \right)^{-1} = \left( \frac{9}{4} \right)^{-1} = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444
$$

**(iii) Posterior mean:**

$$
\mu_N = \sigma_N^2 \left( \frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\sigma_0^2} \right) = \frac{4}{9} \left( \frac{5 \cdot 6}{4} + \frac{3}{1} \right) = \frac{4}{9} \left( \frac{30}{4} + 3 \right)
$$

$$
\frac{30}{4} + 3 = \frac{30 + 12}{4} = \frac{42}{4} = 10.5
$$

$$
\mu_N = \frac{4}{9} \cdot 10.5 = \frac{42}{9} = \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.667
$$

**(iv) Bayes estimate:**

For a Gaussian posterior, the posterior mean equals the Bayes (squared-error) estimate:

$$
\hat{\mu}_{Bayes} = \mu_N = \frac{14}{3} \approx 4.667
$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

- **Bayes' Rule** is the central equation: $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$.
- **i.i.d. assumption** is essential for the joint likelihood to factor as a product.
- **ML estimator** is the mode of the likelihood — equivalent to MAP with a uniform prior.
- **MAP estimator** is the mode of the posterior — incorporates prior knowledge.
- **Bayes estimator** (under squared-error loss) is the **mean** of the posterior.
- **Conjugate prior** for a Gaussian likelihood with known variance is a **Gaussian prior** — the posterior remains Gaussian.
- **Posterior variance** $\sigma_N^2 = \left( \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_0^2} \right)^{-1}$ *decreases* as $n$ increases (more data → less uncertainty).
- **Posterior mean** is a **weighted average** of the prior mean and the sample mean; weights are the precisions.
- **Sufficient statistic** for the Gaussian mean is $T(\mathcal{D}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k$ (or equivalently, $\bar{x}$).
- **Limiting behaviour:** as $n \to \infty$, posterior $\to$ delta function at $\hat{\theta}_{ML}$; as $n \to 0$, posterior $\to$ prior.
- **Bias of ML variance:** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{ML}$ is biased; the unbiased estimator divides by $n-1$ instead of $n$.
- **Examiner's favourite derivation:** the Gaussian conjugate update — memorize the formula for $\sigma_N^2$ and $\mu_N$.
- **Common confusion:** MAP and Bayes estimator are *different* (mode vs. mean); they coincide only for symmetric posteriors like the Gaussian.
- **Practical tip:** if the problem says "find the Bayesian estimate" without specifying loss, assume **squared-error loss** (posterior mean) for Gaussian problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Feature Extraction and Dimensionality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Feature Extraction and Dimensionality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Feature Extraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Extraction** is the process of transforming the original, high-dimensional measurement (or pattern) space into a significantly lower-dimensional **feature space**, in such a way that the new representation retains the most essential, discriminative, and task-relevant information from the raw data.

In the formal pattern recognition pipeline, every physical observation (an image, a speech segment, a biomedical signal, a sensor reading) is first measured as a vector in some raw **measurement space** $\mathbb{R}^n$. This raw vector often contains hundreds or thousands of components (pixels, time samples, frequency bins). Directly classifying such high-dimensional raw data is computationally expensive, statistically unreliable, and prone to overfitting. Feature extraction solves this by computing a compact, informative representation.

Mathematically, a feature extractor is a mapping:

$$\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the original measurement vector and $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the resulting feature vector, with $p \ll n$ in most practical systems.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Dimensionality

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensionality** in pattern recognition refers to the number of independent coordinates (features) required to uniquely position a pattern within its feature space. The **dimensionality of a pattern** is therefore the length $p$ of its feature vector $\mathbf{y}$.

Dimensionality is not merely a counting quantity — it fundamentally controls the geometric, statistical, and computational properties of a pattern recognition system. As dimensionality grows, the volume of the feature space grows exponentially, data becomes sparse, and distance measures lose their discriminative power (this phenomenon is known as the **Curse of Dimensionality**).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Wine Sommelier

Imagine you walk into a vineyard with 10,000 chemical analyses of 500 different wines. As a human, you cannot taste a wine by mentally processing all 10,000 chemical numbers. So instead, a sommelier (an expert) describes each wine using a small, meaningful vocabulary: **body (light/full), tannin (low/medium/high), acidity (low/medium/high), aroma notes (fruity/earthy/spicy)**. That is exactly **6 to 8 features** instead of 10,000 numbers.

In this analogy:
- The **10,000 chemical readings** = raw measurement space (high-dimensional).
- The **6 to 8 sommelier descriptors** = extracted feature space (low-dimensional).
- The **sommelier's trained transformation** = the feature extraction function $f(\cdot)$.
- The **6 to 8 descriptors** = the dimensionality $p$ of the pattern.

Just as two wines with similar chemical profiles will receive similar sommelier descriptors, two patterns that belong to the same class will cluster closely in the feature space — that is the entire purpose of feature extraction.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently award 1 mark in Part A just for stating that feature extraction reduces dimensionality while preserving class-discriminative information. Memorize this exact phrasing.

### 1.4 Why Feature Extraction and Dimensionality Reduction Are Needed

1. **Computational tractability:** Classifiers scale with $O(p^2)$ or worse; reducing $p$ from 1000 to 20 gives a 2500× speedup.
2. **Statistical reliability (Hughes phenomenon):** With finite training samples, accuracy first rises and then **falls** as the number of features increases, because the volume of feature space grows exponentially.
3. **Noise and redundancy suppression:** Raw measurements often contain correlated, noisy, or irrelevant components.
4. **Visualization:** Human interpretation is limited to 2D/3D plots.
5. **Storage and transmission efficiency:** Lower-dimensional representations cost less to store and transmit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pattern Recognition Pipeline (Where Feature Extraction Fits)

Feature extraction occupies the central stage of a pattern recognition system. The complete operational chain is:

1. **Sensing / Data Acquisition:** Raw physical signals are captured by sensors (camera, microphone, electrodes).
2. **Preprocessing:** Noise removal, normalization, segmentation, and standardization of the raw signal.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Compute a compact, discriminative feature vector from the preprocessed signal.
4. **Classification / Clustering:** Apply a decision rule (Bayes, k-NN, SVM, neural network) in the feature space.
5. **Post-processing / Interpretation:** Translate decisions into actionable outputs.

The transformation at Step 3 is irreversible in a lossy sense — once raw pixels are mapped to a few features, the original raw data can no longer be exactly recovered. Therefore, the *choice* of features determines the **upper bound** of any classifier's performance. A bad feature extractor cannot be rescued by even the most sophisticated classifier — this is sometimes called the *"no free lunch at the input stage"* principle.

### 2.2 Measurement Space vs Feature Space

| Aspect | Measurement Space $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Feature Space $\mathbb{R}^p$ |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Raw sensor data | Transformed / derived attributes |
| Dimensionality | Very high ($n$ large) | Low ($p \ll n$) |
| Physical meaning | Direct (pixels, voltages) | Indirect, abstract |
| Noise sensitivity | High | Reduced |
| Computation cost | Expensive | Cheap |
| Class separability | Often poor | Designed to be good |

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase "feature space" is a **synonym for pattern space** in many KTU textbooks. Both terms refer to the space in which patterns are represented as points/vectors after feature extraction.

### 2.3 Categories of Features

Features can be broadly classified into:

- **Physical features:** Length, area, perimeter, mass, intensity. Interpretable by humans.
- **Statistical features:** Mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis of pixel/signal intensities.
- **Structural features:** Edges, corners, textures, geometric moments.
- **Spectral features:** Coefficients from Fourier, DCT, wavelet, or PCA transforms.
- **Transform-domain features:** HOG, SIFT, LBP, MFCC — all derived from linear/nonlinear transforms.

### 2.4 Feature Extraction vs Feature Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Selection** chooses a subset of the *original* measurements and discards the rest. **Feature Extraction** *transforms* the original measurements into a new, smaller set, typically using a linear or nonlinear mapping.

| Method | Operation | Output | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feature Selection | Subset selection | Subset of $\mathbf{x}$ | Pick 10 of 100 genes |
| Feature Extraction | Linear/nonlinear mapping | New vector $\mathbf{y} = W\mathbf{x}$ | PCA, LDA, autoencoders |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield formula examiners typically test in Module 2 of PEAET755. Memorize the LaTeX form so you can write it cleanly on the answer script.

| Concept | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Feature vector | $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ | $n$-dimensional column vector |
| Mean vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_k$ | Centroid of $N$ samples |
| Covariance matrix | $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$ | $n \times n$ symmetric, PSD |
| Euclidean distance | $d_E(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Geometric distance |
| Manhattan distance | $d_M(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | $L_1$ norm distance |
| Minkowski distance | $d_p(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert x_i - y_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized $L_p$ |
| Mahalanobis distance | $d_M(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}$ | Scale-invariant |
| Cosine similarity | $\cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{y}}{\Vert\mathbf{x}\Vert \Vert\mathbf{y}\Vert}$ | Direction-based |
| Scatter (within-class) | $S_W = \sum_{i=1}^{c}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in C_i}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T$ | $c$ = no. of classes |
| Scatter (between-class) | $S_B = \sum_{i=1}^{c}N_i(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\boldsymbol{\mu}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$ | $N_i$ = samples in $C_i$ |
| Total scatter | $S_T = S_W + S_B$ | Decomposition identity |
| Volume of unit hypercube | $V_n = 1$ | Constant in $n$ dims |
| Volume of unit hypersphere | $V_n = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)}$ | Decays rapidly as $n\to\infty$ |
| Ratio of hypersphere to hypercube volume | $\frac{V_n^{\text{sphere}}}{V_n^{\text{cube}}} \to 0$ | Tends to **zero** as $n$ grows |
| Hughes phenomenon | $P(e) \approx f(n, N, p)$ | Error $\uparrow$ when $p \gg N$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Important Typographical Rule:** Whenever absolute value, determinant, or norm appears **inside** a markdown table cell, render it as $\vert \cdot \vert$, $\det(\cdot)$, or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ — never use a bare `|` symbol, which breaks markdown table parsing. This is a critical typesetting habit for your answer scripts when you use grid paper + handwritten notation.

### 2.6 The Curse of Dimensionality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Curse of Dimensionality** (term coined by Richard Bellman, 1961) describes the exponential growth of the volume of feature space as dimensionality increases, which in turn makes data sparse, distances meaningless, and classifier generalization poor.

**Geometric Intuition:**
Consider covering a unit hypercube $[0,1]^n$ with a small hypercube of side $\epsilon$. The number of such small cubes needed to fill the unit hypercube is:

$$N(\epsilon, n) = \left(\frac{1}{\epsilon}\right)^n$$

For $\epsilon = 0.1$:
- $n = 1$: $N = 10$
- $n = 10$: $N = 10^{10}$
- $n = 100$: $N = 10^{100}$

This exponential explosion means that to maintain the same *sampling density*, the number of training samples must grow exponentially with dimensionality. Since real datasets are finite, **high-dimensional spaces are mostly empty** — this is the curse.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Feature Extraction Application |
|---|---|
| Medical imaging | Tumor shape, texture, margin sharpness from MRI/CT |
| Speech recognition | MFCC features from audio frames |
| Face recognition | Eigenfaces (PCA), Fisherfaces (LDA) |
| Remote sensing | Spectral indices (NDVI) from multispectral images |
| Bioinformatics | Gene expression signatures from microarrays |
| Industrial fault diagnosis | Vibration spectral features from bearings |
| OCR | HOG features of character images |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mean Vector

**Problem:** Given $N$ pattern vectors $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N \in \mathbb{R}^n$, derive the expression for the sample mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$.

**Step 1 — Definition of the mean of the $i$-th coordinate:**
The $i$-th component of the mean vector is the arithmetic average of the $i$-th components across all $N$ samples.

$$\mu_i = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} x_{k,i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Step 2 — Vectorize the expression:**
Stacking all $n$ such scalar equations into a single vector form gives the result.

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_k \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^n$$

**Step 3 — Verification of dimensions:**
Each $\mathbf{x}_k$ is $n \times 1$, so the sum is $n \times 1$, and dividing by the scalar $N$ retains the same dimension. Hence $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is an $n \times 1$ column vector — consistent with a centroid in $n$-dimensional feature space.

> **Conclusion:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the unique point that minimizes the sum of squared Euclidean distances to all $N$ samples, i.e., it is the geometric centroid of the dataset.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sample Covariance Matrix

**Problem:** Derive the $n \times n$ sample covariance matrix $\Sigma$ for $N$ pattern vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

**Step 1 — Definition of covariance between two scalar random variables $X_i$ and $X_j$:**

$$\text{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = \sigma_{ij} = E[(X_i - \mu_i)(X_j - \mu_j)]$$

**Step 2 — Replace expectations with sample averages:**

$$\sigma_{ij} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(x_{k,i} - \mu_i)(x_{k,j} - \mu_j)$$

**Step 3 — Vector-matrix form using the centered vector $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_k = \mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu}$:**

$$\sigma_{ij} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\tilde{x}_{k,i}\,\tilde{x}_{k,j}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the full $n \times n$ matrix by collecting all $\sigma_{ij}$:**

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_k\,\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_k^T$$

**Step 5 — Compact matrix form using a data matrix $X$ of size $n \times N$:**

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{N}\,\tilde{X}\,\tilde{X}^T, \quad \text{where } \tilde{X} = X - \boldsymbol{\mu}\mathbf{1}^T$$

**Step 6 — Properties of $\Sigma$:**
- Symmetric: $\Sigma = \Sigma^T$
- Positive semi-definite: $\mathbf{v}^T\Sigma\mathbf{v} \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$
- Diagonal entries are variances, off-diagonal are covariances

> **Conclusion:** The covariance matrix completely characterizes the second-order statistical structure (spread, orientation, and correlation) of a multivariate dataset and is the cornerstone of PCA, LDA, and Mahalanobis distance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Mahalanobis Distance

**Problem:** Show that Euclidean distance fails when features have different scales, and derive the scale-invariant Mahalanobis distance.

**Step 1 — Failure of Euclidean distance under scaling:**
Suppose we have two features: *height in meters* and *weight in kilograms*. If we convert height to *millimeters* (a 1000× scaling), Euclidean distance becomes dominated by the height term. The classifier's decisions would change purely because of a unit change — physically meaningless.

**Step 2 — Whitening transformation:**
We seek a linear transformation $W$ such that the transformed data has identity covariance. That is, $W\Sigma W^T = I$, which is satisfied by:

$$W = \Sigma^{-1/2}$$

(using the symmetric square root of $\Sigma$).

**Step 3 — Apply whitening to both vectors:**

$$\mathbf{x}' = W\mathbf{x}, \quad \mathbf{y}' = W\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Euclidean distance in the whitened space:**

$$d^2 = (\mathbf{x}' - \mathbf{y}')^T(\mathbf{x}' - \mathbf{y}') = (W\mathbf{x} - W\mathbf{y})^T(W\mathbf{x} - W\mathbf{y})$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $W = \Sigma^{-1/2}$ and simplify:**

$$d^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T W^T W (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})$$

**Step 6 — Final Mahalanobis distance formula:**

$$d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}$$

> **Conclusion:** Mahalanobis distance is **unitless, scale-invariant, and accounts for feature correlation**. When $\Sigma = I$, it reduces exactly to Euclidean distance.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Volume of Unit Hypersphere (Curse of Dimensionality)

**Problem:** Show that as $n \to \infty$, almost all the volume of a unit hypercube concentrates in its corners (i.e., the inscribed hypersphere's volume becomes negligible).

**Step 1 — Volume of the $n$-dimensional unit hypercube:**

$$V_{\text{cube}} = 1^n = 1$$

**Step 2 — Volume of the $n$-dimensional unit hypersphere:**

$$V_{\text{sphere}}(n) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{n}{2} + 1\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio $r(n) = V_{\text{sphere}} / V_{\text{cube}}$:**

$$r(n) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2 + 1)}$$

**Step 4 — Use Stirling's approximation $\Gamma(n/2+1) \approx \sqrt{2\pi}\,(n/2)^{n/2}e^{-n/2}$:**

$$\frac{V_{\text{sphere}}(n)}{V_{\text{cube}}(n)} \approx \left(\frac{2\pi e}{n}\right)^{n/2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi n}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the limit as $n \to \infty$:**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}(n)}{V_{\text{cube}}(n)} = 0$$

> **Conclusion:** In high dimensions, the **inscribed hypersphere occupies negligible volume** inside the hypercube. The corners — which Euclidean-type distances are bad at reaching — dominate. This geometric fact is the root cause of the curse of dimensionality and motivates non-Euclidean feature design.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Distance Metric

| Distance Metric | Best Used When | Avoid When |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean ($L_2$) | Features have same scale, no correlation | Mixed scales, correlated features |
| Manhattan ($L_1$) | High-dimensional sparse data (e.g., text) | Need to penalize large errors heavily |
| Mahalanobis | Features correlated, different scales | $\Sigma$ is singular (use pseudo-inverse) |
| Cosine | Direction matters more than magnitude (text/documents) | Absolute magnitudes carry meaning |
| Chebyshev ($L_\infty$) | Only the largest single deviation matters | All features equally important |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Pattern Recognition Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data Acquisition] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction]
    C --> D[Feature Space Representation]
    D --> E[Classifier Decision Rule]
    E --> F[Class Label Output]
    G[Domain Knowledge] -.-> C
    H[Training Samples] -.-> C
    H -.-> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show the operational forward flow of a single test pattern. The dotted arrows indicate the *learning* influence — domain knowledge and labeled training samples shape the choice of features and the parameters of the classifier.

---

### 4.2 Feature Extraction as a Mapping Between Spaces

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MSpace["MEASUREMENT SPACE dim n high"]
        X1[Pattern X1 pixel vector]
        X2[Pattern X2 pixel vector]
        X3[Pattern X3 pixel vector]
        XN[Pattern XN pixel vector]
    end
    subgraph FSpace["FEATURE SPACE dim p low"]
        Y1[Feature Y1 compact vector]
        Y2[Feature Y2 compact vector]
        Y3[Feature Y3 compact vector]
        YN[Feature YN compact vector]
    end
    subgraph Ext["FEATURE EXTRACTOR f of x"]
        F1[Linear transform PCA]
        F2[Nonlinear kernel]
        F3[Domain specific operator]
    end
    X1 --> F1 --> Y1
    X2 --> F2 --> Y2
    X3 --> F3 --> Y3
    XN --> F1 --> YN
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each high-dimensional measurement vector (left block) is mapped through the feature extractor (middle block) to a low-dimensional feature vector (right block). The extractor may use linear, nonlinear, or domain-specific operators.

---

### 4.3 Curse of Dimensionality Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph D1["DIMENSIONALITY n 1"]
        P1[1 unit interval contains 10 samples]
    end
    subgraph D10["DIMENSIONALITY n 10"]
        P10[1 unit cube needs 10 power 10 samples for same density]
    end
    subgraph D100["DIMENSIONALITY n 100"]
        P100[1 unit hypercube needs 10 power 100 samples for same density]
    end
    D1 --> D10 --> D100
```

**Reading the diagram:** The required number of training samples to maintain a constant sampling density grows *exponentially* with dimensionality. This is the geometric essence of the curse of dimensionality.

---

### 4.4 Feature Selection vs Feature Extraction Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Need to reduce dimensionality] --> B{Is original feature set interpretable and uncorrelated?}
    B -- YES --> C[FEATURE SELECTION]
    B -- NO --> D[FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    C --> E[Subset search]
    D --> F[Linear PCA LDA]
    D --> G[Nonlinear Autoencoder Kernel PCA]
    E --> H[Reduced feature set subset of original]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```
