**Reading the diagram:** Start by asking whether the raw features are already meaningful and independent. If yes, a *subset selection* suffices. If features are redundant, correlated, or noisy, apply a *transform-based extraction* such as PCA, LDA, or autoencoders.

---

### 4.5 Dimensionality Reduction Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DR[Dimensionality Reduction] --> LS[Linear Methods]
    DR --> NL[Nonlinear Methods]
    DR --> FS[Feature Selection]
    LS --> LS1[PCA Principal Component Analysis]
    LS --> LS2[LDA Linear Discriminant Analysis]
    LS --> LS3[ICA Independent Component Analysis]
    NL --> NL1[Kernel PCA]
    NL --> NL2[t-SNE]
    NL --> NL3[Autoencoders]
    NL --> NL4[Isomap]
    FS --> FS1[Filter Methods]
    FS --> FS2[Wrapper Methods]
    FS --> FS3[Embedded Methods]
    FS1 --> FS1a[Mutual information]
    FS1 --> FS1b[Chi square]
    FS2 --> FS2a[Forward selection]
    FS2 --> FS2b[Backward elimination]
    FS3 --> FS3a[Lasso regularization]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The complete taxonomy of dimensionality reduction techniques used in KTU Module 2 and beyond. For Module 2, focus on PCA, LDA, and the conceptual difference between linear and nonlinear methods.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define feature extraction. Why is it considered an essential step in pattern recognition?**

**Model Answer:**

Feature extraction is the process of transforming the raw, high-dimensional measurement data into a compact set of representative attributes called features, while preserving the class-discriminative information needed for classification.

It is essential because:
- It **reduces computational cost** of subsequent classifiers.
- It **mitigates the curse of dimensionality** (the Hughes phenomenon).
- It **removes noise and redundancy** present in raw measurements.
- It **improves generalization** of the classifier by reducing overfitting.
- It enables **visualization** in 2D/3D for human interpretation.

> **Word count target:** 80–100 words for a 3-mark answer. Drawing a small block diagram of the pipeline is recommended for the third mark.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the term "Curse of Dimensionality" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Curse of Dimensionality** refers to the exponential growth in the volume of the feature space as the number of features (dimensions) increases. As dimensionality rises, data points become sparse, distances between nearest and farthest neighbors converge, and classifiers lose discriminative power.

**Example:** Suppose a 1-D unit interval is partitioned into 10 equal bins, requiring only 10 samples for one sample per bin. The same density in a 10-D unit hypercube requires $10^{10}$ samples — practically impossible to collect. Hence, blindly increasing features degrades performance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout #1):** Many students write the *definition* of feature extraction and *stop*. They lose the second sub-part mark because they did not discuss the **information-preservation criterion** or the **role in the pattern recognition pipeline**. Always tie the answer back to the **objective of preserving class separability while reducing $p$**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Choice A]`

**A.** (a) Discuss in detail the concept of feature extraction and feature space. Explain measurement space and feature space with their differences. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definitions and Concepts:**

A **feature** is an individual measurable property or characteristic of a pattern. A **feature vector** is the collection of all features representing one pattern, typically arranged as a column vector in $\mathbb{R}^p$.

**Feature space** is the $p$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^p$ in which each pattern is represented as a single point whose coordinates are its feature values. Patterns belonging to the same class form clusters in this space; a classifier essentially partitions this space into decision regions.

**Measurement space** is the original $n$-dimensional space $\mathbb{R}^n$ in which raw data lives. For an image of $256 \times 256$ pixels, $n = 65{,}536$; for a 1-second speech frame at 16 kHz, $n = 16{,}000$.

**2. Why Two Spaces?**

Raw measurement space is unsuitable for classification because:
- It is **high-dimensional** (curse of dimensionality).
- It is **redundant and noisy** (e.g., neighboring pixels are correlated).
- It lacks **semantic meaning** for the classification task.

Feature extraction defines a function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^p$ with $p \ll n$ that maps each raw pattern to a low-dimensional feature vector. The new feature space is designed so that:
- Same-class patterns cluster tightly.
- Different-class patterns are well-separated.

**3. Key Differences Table:**

| Aspect | Measurement Space | Feature Space |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensionality | $n$ (large) | $p \ll n$ |
| Coordinates | Raw physical values | Computed, abstract |
| Information content | Full but redundant | Compact, discriminative |
| Direct use in classification | Inefficient | Efficient |
| Recovery of original | Possible | Generally impossible (lossy) |

**4. Example:**
A $64 \times 64$ grayscale face image lives in $\mathbb{R}^{4096}$ (measurement space). After PCA, it can be represented by the top 40 eigen-coefficients in $\mathbb{R}^{40}$ (feature space), retaining ~95% of the variance. The Eigenface representation is the feature space.

**5. Role in the Pattern Recognition Pipeline:**

Feature extraction sits between preprocessing and classification. Its quality determines the **upper bound of achievable classification accuracy** — no classifier can exceed the information content of the features it receives.

---

**A.** (b) Explain the curse of dimensionality. Derive the expression for the volume of a unit hypersphere and show that it tends to zero relative to the unit hypercube as dimension $n \to \infty$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Curse of Dimensionality:**

As the number of features $p$ increases, the volume of the feature space grows exponentially. Consequently, the available training samples become extremely sparse, distances between data points lose their discriminative meaning, and classifier performance degrades. This is the **Curse of Dimensionality**, formally articulated by Bellman (1961).

**Symptoms in practice:**
- Distance concentration: $\lim_{p\to\infty} \frac{d_{\max} - d_{\min}}{d_{\min}} \to 0$.
- Need for exponentially more training samples to maintain density.
- K-nearest neighbor classifier becomes unstable.

**2. Volume of Unit Hypercube:**

The unit hypercube $[0,1]^n$ has volume:

$$V_{\text{cube}}(n) = 1^n = 1$$

**3. Volume of Unit Hypersphere:**

The $n$-dimensional hypersphere of radius $R$ has volume:

$$V_{\text{sphere}}(n, R) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2 + 1)}\,R^n$$

For the unit hypersphere ($R = 1$):

$$V_{\text{sphere}}(n) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2 + 1)}$$

**Derivation sketch:** Integrate the volume of infinitesimal $(n-1)$-dimensional spherical shells of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ from $0$ to $1$:

$$V_{\text{sphere}}(n) = \int_0^1 A_n\,r^{n-1}\,dr, \quad A_n = \frac{2\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2)}$$

Evaluating the integral yields $V_{\text{sphere}}(n) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)}$.

**4. Ratio Analysis:**

The ratio of the inscribed hypersphere to the unit hypercube is:

$$r(n) = \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}(n)}{V_{\text{cube}}(n)} = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)}$$

**Numerical demonstration:**

| $n$ | $V_{\text{sphere}}(n)$ | Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.000 | 2.000 |
| 2 | 3.142 | 3.142 |
| 3 | 4.189 | 4.189 |
| 10 | 2.467 | 2.467 |
| 20 | 0.026 | 0.026 |
| 50 | $2.35 \times 10^{-13}$ | $2.35 \times 10^{-13}$ |
| 100 | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ |

The ratio peaks around $n \approx 5$–$7$ and then collapses toward zero.

**5. Limit Proof:**

Using Stirling's approximation $\Gamma(n/2+1) \approx \sqrt{2\pi}\,(n/2)^{n/2}\,e^{-n/2}$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(\frac{2\pi e}{n}\right)^{n/2}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi n}} = 0$$

Since $\frac{2\pi e}{n} < 1$ for sufficiently large $n$, raising it to the power $n/2$ forces the expression to zero.

**6. Geometric Implication:**

In high dimensions, the inscribed hypersphere occupies vanishingly small volume of the unit hypercube. The mass of the hypercube concentrates in its **corners** (vertices), which are far from its center. Algorithms that rely on distance-from-center (such as k-means, k-NN) therefore fail catastrophically — this is the curse of dimensionality in action.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2 Choice B]`

**B.** (a) Differentiate between feature selection and feature extraction. With a neat diagram, explain the stages of a pattern recognition system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Feature Selection vs Feature Extraction:**

**Feature Selection** is the process of identifying and retaining a *subset* of the original features, discarding the irrelevant or redundant ones. The output is a subset of the input features, retaining their original physical meaning.

**Feature Extraction** is the process of *transforming* the original feature set into a new, smaller set of derived features, typically using a linear or nonlinear mapping. The output features are combinations of the input features and usually lose direct physical interpretability.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Feature Selection | Feature Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Picks subset | Transforms |
| Original meaning | Preserved | Generally lost |
| Methods | Filter, wrapper, embedded | PCA, LDA, ICA, autoencoders |
| Output dimension | $p \leq n$ | $p \ll n$ |
| Computation | Lower | Higher (matrix decompositions) |
| Best for | Interpretable models | High-dimensional correlated data |

**2. Stages of a Pattern Recognition System (Diagram):**

```
+--------------------+
| Data Acquisition   |  ← Sensors (camera, microphone, electrodes)
+--------------------+
          ↓
+--------------------+
| Preprocessing      |  ← Noise removal, normalization, segmentation
+--------------------+
          ↓
+--------------------+
| Feature Extraction |  ← PCA, LDA, HOG, MFCC, etc.
+--------------------+
          ↓
+--------------------+
| Classification     |  ← Bayes, k-NN, SVM, Neural Network
+--------------------+
          ↓
+--------------------+
| Decision / Output  |  ← Class label, score, regression value
+--------------------+
```

**Detailed Explanation of Stages:**

- **Data Acquisition:** Capture raw signals from the physical world using sensors. Quality at this stage caps the entire system's performance.
- **Preprocessing:** Remove sensor noise, normalize intensity/scale, segment region of interest. Examples: histogram equalization, Wiener filtering, baseline removal.
- **Feature Extraction:** Compute discriminative, compact features. The choice is driven by domain knowledge (e.g., MFCC for speech, HOG for pedestrians, eigenvalues for faces).
- **Classification:** Apply a learned decision function in the feature space. Training data is used to estimate class-conditional densities (Bayes), distance metrics (k-NN), or discriminant functions (SVM, neural net).
- **Decision/Output:** Produce the final class label or continuous estimate, possibly with confidence scores.

---

**B.** (b) Define the covariance matrix. Derive the Mahalanobis distance between two feature vectors and explain why it is preferred over Euclidean distance in pattern recognition. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of Covariance Matrix:**

For a set of $N$ feature vectors $\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}$, the **sample covariance matrix** is the $n \times n$ symmetric matrix:

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$$

Diagonal entries $\Sigma_{ii}$ are the variances of the $i$-th feature; off-diagonal entries $\Sigma_{ij}$ are the covariances between features $i$ and $j$.

**2. Derivation of Mahalanobis Distance:**

**Step 1 — Why Euclidean distance fails:**
Euclidean distance $d_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ is **sensitive to feature scaling and ignores correlations**. If feature 1 has variance 1000 and feature 2 has variance 0.001, the distance is dominated by feature 1.

**Step 2 — Goal:** Find a distance that is invariant to linear scaling and accounts for correlations.

**Step 3 — Whitening transformation:** Define a linear transformation $W$ such that the transformed data has identity covariance. Choosing $W = \Sigma^{-1/2}$ gives:

$$W\Sigma W^T = I$$

**Step 4 — Apply whitening to both points:** $\mathbf{x}' = W\mathbf{x}, \;\mathbf{y}' = W\mathbf{y}$.

**Step 5 — Euclidean distance in whitened space:**

$$d^2 = (\mathbf{x}' - \mathbf{y}')^T(\mathbf{x}' - \mathbf{y}') = (W(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}))^T(W(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})) = (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T W^T W (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $W = \Sigma^{-1/2}$ so $W^T W = \Sigma^{-1}$:**

$$\boxed{\,d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}\,}$$

**3. Why Mahalanobis is Preferred over Euclidean:**

| Property | Euclidean | Mahalanobis |
|---|---|---|
| Scale invariance | ❌ | ✅ |
| Accounts for correlation | ❌ | ✅ |
| Reduces to Euclidean when | — | $\Sigma = I$ |
| Statistical meaning | Geometric only | Standardized distance (in $\sigma$ units) |
| Sensitivity to outliers | High | Reduced |

Mahalanobis distance effectively **standardizes** the data — the distance from the mean becomes a $z$-score-like quantity that is directly comparable across different feature combinations. This is why classifiers such as **quadratic discriminant analysis (QDA)** and the **Gaussian likelihood** are formulated with Mahalanobis distance at their core.

**Special case:** If $\Sigma = I$ (identity), Mahalanobis reduces to Euclidean:

$$d_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T I (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})} = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2} = d_E$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout #2):** When asked to "derive" Mahalanobis distance, students often write the final formula and skip the **whitening transformation argument**. Examiners specifically look for: (i) the statement of the goal (scale invariance), (ii) the whitening transform $W = \Sigma^{-1/2}$, (iii) the explicit substitution step. Missing any of these costs 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision (10-Minute Drill)** — read this list aloud before the exam.

**Core Definitions:**
- [ ] **Feature Extraction:** Transformation $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x})$ from $\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^p$ with $p \ll n$, preserving discriminative information.
- [ ] **Dimensionality ($p$):** Number of independent coordinates of the feature vector.
- [ ] **Feature Space:** Geometric space $\mathbb{R}^p$ where patterns become points.
- [ ] **Measurement Space:** Original raw data space $\mathbb{R}^n$.

**Key Distinctions:**
- [ ] Feature *selection* = subset; feature *extraction* = transformation.
- [ ] Curse of dimensionality = exponential volume growth.
- [ ] Hughes phenomenon = error rises when $p \gg N$ (samples).

**Critical Formulas to Memorize:**
- [ ] Mean: $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_k$
- [ ] Covariance: $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}(\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T$
- [ ] Euclidean: $d_E = \sqrt{\sum(x_i - y_i)^2}$
- [ ] Manhattan: $d_M = \sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert$
- [ ] Mahalanobis: $d_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})}$
- [ ] Hypersphere volume: $V_n = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2+1)}$
- [ ] Scatter decomposition: $S_T = S_W + S_B$

**Pipeline Order (Memorize Forward and Backward):**
- [ ] Sensing → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Classification → Decision

**Conceptual Insights Examiners Test:**
- [ ] Why high dimensions are bad (sparse data, distance concentration).
- [ ] Why Mahalanobis is preferred (scale + correlation invariance).
- [ ] Why feature extraction must come *before* classification (irreversibility, upper bound).
- [ ] The role of domain knowledge in feature engineering (e.g., MFCC for speech, HOG for vision).

**Common Application Domains to Recall:**
- [ ] Speech: MFCC, LPC, spectral features.
- [ ] Face: Eigenfaces (PCA), Fisherfaces (LDA).
- [ ] Medical: Shape, texture, intensity statistics.
- [ ] Text: TF-IDF, bag-of-words, embeddings.

**One-Line Memory Hook for Mahalanobis:**
> *"Mahalanobis = Euclidean after $\Sigma^{-1/2}$ whitening."*

**One-Line Memory Hook for Curse of Dimensionality:**
> *"Unit hypercube volume stays 1; unit hypersphere volume crashes to 0."*

**Board Exam Strategy (2024 Scheme):**
- [ ] Always include a small diagram (1 extra mark).
- [ ] Write at least one formula in LaTeX-style notation (signals mathematical maturity).
- [ ] For 7-mark questions, target 1.5 pages of handwritten content; for 14-mark, 3 pages.
- [ ] End the answer with a one-line conclusion that ties the concept back to the question.

---

*End of Notes — PEAET755 Module 2 — Concept of Feature Extraction and Dimensionality*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curse of Dimensionality (CoD)** is a term coined by **Richard E. Bellman** in his 1961 work on dynamic programming. It refers to the various phenomena that arise when analyzing and organizing data in **high-dimensional spaces** (often dimensions exceeding 10–20) that do not occur in low-dimensional settings such as the 3-dimensional (3D) physical space of everyday experience.

In the context of **Pattern Recognition (PEAET755)**, the Curse of Dimensionality describes the exponential growth of the **feature space volume** as the number of features (dimensions) increases, leading to:
- **Data sparsity** (training samples become isolated points in a vast space),
- **Degradation of classifier performance** (Hughes Phenomenon),
- **Computational intractability** (memory, distance computation, search cost explode),
- **Loss of statistical significance** (variance of estimators grows).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Expanding Galaxy" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):**
> Imagine you have **N = 100 dots** to spread evenly inside a cube.
> - In **1D** (a line of length 1), the dots are spaced roughly $\frac{1}{100}$ apart — very dense, easy to model.
> - In **2D** (a unit square), spacing grows to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{100}} \approx 0.1$ — still manageable.
> - In **10D**, the spacing is $\frac{1}{100^{1/10}} \approx 0.63$ — points feel the edges but haven't fallen out.
> - In **100D**, the spacing is $\frac{1}{100^{1/100}} \approx 0.955$ — almost every point is touching the boundary of the unit cube!
>
> **Takeaway:** As dimensions grow, your data points migrate to the corners and edges of the feature space, leaving the center almost empty. This is the **curse**.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Library Problem

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Suppose a librarian has **1 million books** to shelve in a library. If the library has only **1 row**, every book is easy to find. With **2 rows**, search doubles. With **1000 aisles, 1000 shelves each**, finding a specific book requires exponentially more time, and each "section" is statistically empty. The same applies to classifier design in high-dimensional feature space.

## 1.4 Key Manifestations of the Curse

| # | Manifestation | Effect on Pattern Recognition |
|---|---------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Exponential Volume Growth** | Required training samples grow as $N^d$ |
| 2 | **Data Sparsity** | Density estimation becomes unreliable |
| 3 | **Distance Concentration** | All pairwise distances converge to a single value |
| 4 | **Hughes Phenomenon** | Classifier accuracy first increases, then decreases with $d$ |
| 5 | **Computational Burden** | Distance metrics, kernel evaluations become $O(d)$ or worse |
| 6 | **Redundancy & Noise** | Many features carry little discriminant information |

## 1.5 Why It Matters in Pattern Recognition

In **PEAET755**, we routinely deal with datasets where $d$ (number of features) can range from tens (e.g., pixel intensities) to thousands (e.g., gene expression, NLP embeddings). Without addressing the curse, **theoretically optimal classifiers** (like the Bayes classifier) suffer severely due to **finite sample size**. The entire field of **Dimensionality Reduction** (PCA, LDA, t-SNE, Autoencoders) and **Feature Selection** exists primarily as a direct response to this curse.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Curse

### Pillar A — Exponential Volume of Hyperspace
The volume of a unit hypercube in $d$ dimensions is always **1**, but a unit **hypersphere** of radius $r=0.5$ inscribed inside it shrinks dramatically:

$$V_{\text{sphere}}(d) = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right)} r^{d}$$

$$V_{\text{cube}}(d) = (2r)^{d}$$

The **ratio** of inscribed hypersphere to circumscribed hypercube:

$$\rho(d) = \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^{d} \, \Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right)}$$

As $d \to \infty$, $\rho(d) \to 0$. **Most of the volume concentrates in the corners**, not the center.

### Pillar B — Required Sample Size
To uniformly cover a fraction $\alpha$ of a $d$-dimensional unit hypercube with side length $\epsilon$ per sample, you need:

$$N(\alpha, d) = \frac{\log(1-\alpha)}{\log(1-\epsilon^{d})}$$

For fixed $\alpha$ and $\epsilon$, $N$ grows **exponentially with $d$**.

### Pillar C — Distance Concentration
For $N$ points sampled i.i.d. from $\mathcal{U}[0,1]^{d}$, the ratio:

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{D_{\max} - D_{\min}}{D_{\min}} \to 0$$

This means **nearest-neighbor** and **furthest-neighbor** distances become indistinguishable in high dimensions, breaking many classical algorithms (K-NN, clustering).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_{\text{sphere}}(d) = \dfrac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)} r^{d}$ | Volume of $d$-dimensional hypersphere | $\Gamma$ = Gamma function |
| 2 | $V_{\text{cube}}(d) = (2r)^{d}$ | Volume of $d$-dimensional hypercube | Side length $2r$ |
| 3 | $\rho(d) = \dfrac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^{d}\Gamma(d/2+1)}$ | Sphere-to-cube volume ratio | $\lim_{d \to \infty} \rho = 0$ |
| 4 | $N(d) = \left(\dfrac{1}{\delta}\right)^{d}$ | Samples for density $\delta$ per unit cell | Exponential in $d$ |
| 5 | $\epsilon(d) = \alpha^{1/d}$ | Edge length to cover fraction $\alpha$ | Approaches 1 as $d \to \infty$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\sigma_{D}}{\mu_{D}} \to 0$ as $d \to \infty$ | Distance concentration theorem | Standard deviation / mean |
| 7 | $d_{\text{avg}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{3}}$ for $\mathcal{U}[0,1]^{d}$ | Mean pairwise distance in unit hypercube | $d$ = dimension |
| 8 | $V_{\text{annulus}} = 1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{inner}}}{V_{\text{outer}}}$ | Volume of shell for nearest-neighbor search | Outermost shell dominates in high $d$ |

## 2.3 Stirling's Approximation (Essential for Asymptotic Analysis)

For large $d$, the Gamma function in the hypersphere volume formula uses:

$$\Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right) \approx \sqrt{2\pi \cdot \frac{d}{2}} \left(\frac{d}{2e}\right)^{d/2} = \sqrt{\pi d} \left(\frac{d}{2e}\right)^{d/2}$$

This approximation is **critical for proving** that $\rho(d) \to 0$ as $d \to \infty$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Impact of CoD |
|--------------------|---------------|
| **Image Recognition** | 256×256 grayscale image = 65,536 dimensions; CoD motivates CNNs and pooling |
| **Bioinformatics / Genomics** | Microarray data has $d \sim 20{,}000$ genes, $N \sim 100$ patients (opposite problem!) |
| **NLP / Word Embeddings** | Word2Vec, BERT use 300–1024-D vectors; CoD motivates dimensionality reduction |
| **Anomaly Detection** | High-dim intrusion detection requires careful feature selection |
| **Recommender Systems** | User-item matrices are extremely sparse and high-dimensional |
| **Autonomous Driving** | LiDAR point clouds ($d \sim 10^{6}$) require voxelization / projection |

## 2.5 The Hughes Phenomenon (Directly Tested in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hughes Phenomenon (1968):** For a fixed training set size $N$, the **classification accuracy** of a pattern recognition system first **improves** as features are added (up to an optimum $d^{*}$), and then **degrades** as $d$ increases further. This is the practical evidence of the Curse of Dimensionality in classification tasks.

The intuition: adding features **splits** the feature space into exponentially more cells. With finite $N$, each cell contains very few (often zero) training samples, so density estimates become noisy and classification errors increase.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume Ratio of Hypersphere to Hypercube

### Goal
Prove that the ratio $\rho(d) = \dfrac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} \to 0$ as $d \to \infty$.

### Step 1: Define the Volumes

The volume of a $d$-dimensional hypersphere of radius $r$ is given by the well-known formula:

$$V_{\text{sphere}}(d, r) = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right)} \cdot r^{d}$$

A $d$-dimensional hypercube circumscribing this sphere has side length $2r$ (the sphere touches the cube's faces at distance $r$ from the center). Its volume is:

$$V_{\text{cube}}(d, r) = (2r)^{d} = 2^{d} \cdot r^{d}$$

### Step 2: Form the Ratio

Dividing the two volumes, the $r^{d}$ terms cancel:

$$\rho(d) = \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = \frac{\pi^{d/2} \cdot r^{d}}{2^{d} \cdot r^{d} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right)} = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^{d} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right)}$$

### Step 3: Apply Stirling's Approximation

Recall Stirling's formula for the Gamma function at large argument:

$$\Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right) \approx \sqrt{\pi d} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2e}\right)^{d/2}$$

Substituting:

$$\rho(d) \approx \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^{d} \cdot \sqrt{\pi d} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2e}\right)^{d/2}}$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Exponentials

Combine the powers of $\pi$, $2$, and $d$:

$$\rho(d) \approx \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^{d}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi d}} \cdot \left(\frac{2e}{d}\right)^{d/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi d}} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi \cdot 2e}{2^{2} \cdot d}\right)^{d/2}$$

Simplify the base of the exponent: $\dfrac{\pi e}{2d} = \dfrac{\pi e}{2d}$.

$$\rho(d) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi d}} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi e}{2d}\right)^{d/2}$$

### Step 5: Take the Limit

For $d \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{\pi e}{2d} \to 0$, raised to the power $d/2$ gives an **exponential decay to zero** which dominates the $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi d}}$ polynomial decay. Therefore:

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \rho(d) = 0$$

### Step 6: Numerical Verification

| $d$ | $\rho(d)$ | Physical Meaning |
|-----|-----------|------------------|
| 1   | 1.000     | Line: sphere = cube |
| 2   | 0.785     | Circle in square: 78.5% |
| 3   | 0.524     | Ball in cube: 52.4% |
| 5   | 0.164     | Volume collapses |
| 10  | 0.0025    | Only 0.25% of cube is sphere |
| 20  | $2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ | Sphere almost vanishes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** In high dimensions, almost all the volume of a hypercube resides **outside** the inscribed hypersphere — in the **corners**. This is why data points in high-dimensional spaces appear to be "scattered near the corners."

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Distance Concentration in High Dimensions

### Goal
Show that for points uniformly distributed in $[0,1]^{d}$, the relative spread of pairwise distances vanishes as $d \to \infty$.

### Step 1: Expected Squared Distance

For two points $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in [0,1]^{d}$ drawn i.i.d. from $\mathcal{U}[0,1]$, each coordinate difference $x_i - y_i$ has mean $0$ and variance $\dfrac{1}{6}$ (since $\text{Var}(\mathcal{U}[0,1]) = \dfrac{1}{12}$ and $x_i - y_i$ has variance $\dfrac{1}{12} + \dfrac{1}{12} = \dfrac{1}{6}$). The squared Euclidean distance is:

$$D^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - y_i)^{2}$$

The expected squared distance is:

$$\mathbb{E}[D^{2}] = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \mathbb{E}[(x_i - y_i)^{2}] = d \cdot \frac{1}{6} = \frac{d}{6}$$

### Step 2: Variance of Squared Distance

For independent coordinates, the variance is the sum of variances of $(x_i - y_i)^{2}$. Each term has variance:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[(x_i - y_i)^{2}\right] = \mathbb{E}[(x_i - y_i)^{4}] - \left(\mathbb{E}[(x_i - y_i)^{2}]\right)^{2}$$

For $x_i, y_i \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1]$, by direct calculation, the fourth moment $\mathbb{E}[(x_i - y_i)^{4}] = \dfrac{1}{15}$, and the squared second moment is $\left(\dfrac{1}{6}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{36}$. Thus:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[(x_i - y_i)^{2}\right] = \frac{1}{15} - \frac{1}{36} = \frac{12 - 5}{180} = \frac{7}{180}$$

The total variance of $D^{2}$ is:

$$\text{Var}[D^{2}] = d \cdot \frac{7}{180} = \frac{7d}{180}$$

### Step 3: Standard Deviation of Distance

By the delta method, the standard deviation of $D$ is approximately:

$$\sigma_{D} \approx \frac{\sigma_{D^{2}}}{2 \, \mathbb{E}[D]} = \frac{\sqrt{7d/180}}{2 \sqrt{d/6}} = \frac{\sqrt{7d/180}}{2\sqrt{d/6}}$$

Simplify:

$$\sigma_{D} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{7d/180}{d/6}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{7 \cdot 6}{180}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{42}{180}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{7}{30}}$$

The mean distance is $\mu_{D} = \sqrt{d/6}$. Therefore the coefficient of variation is:

$$\frac{\sigma_{D}}{\mu_{D}} = \frac{\sqrt{7/30}/2}{\sqrt{d/6}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{30}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6}{d}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{30}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6}{4d}} = \sqrt{\frac{42}{120 d}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{20d}}$$

### Step 4: Take the Limit

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{\sigma_{D}}{\mu_{D}} = \lim_{d \to \infty} \sqrt{\frac{7}{20d}} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The relative spread of distances goes to **zero** as $d \to \infty$. All pairwise distances concentrate around $\mu_{D} = \sqrt{d/6}$, making **nearest neighbor** and **farthest neighbor** distances virtually indistinguishable. This breaks the K-NN algorithm's central assumption.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Number of Training Samples

### Goal
Determine $N$ samples needed to cover a fraction $\alpha$ of the unit hypercube $[0,1]^{d}$ with cells of side $\epsilon$.

### Step 1: Single Sample Volume

A single sample partitions the space into cells. To guarantee that every cell has side $\leq \epsilon$, the volume of one cell is $\epsilon^{d}$.

### Step 2: Probability of a Cell Being Empty

The probability that a particular cell of volume $\epsilon^{d}$ contains **no** training sample (when $N$ points are uniformly distributed) is:

$$P_{\text{empty}} = (1 - \epsilon^{d})^{N}$$

### Step 3: Probability of Coverage

The probability that the entire cube is covered is $\alpha$, so the probability of being uncovered is $(1-\alpha)$:

$$1 - \alpha = (1 - \epsilon^{d})^{N}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $N$

Taking logarithms:

$$\log(1 - \alpha) = N \cdot \log(1 - \epsilon^{d})$$

$$N = \frac{\log(1 - \alpha)}{\log(1 - \epsilon^{d})}$$

### Step 5: Show Exponential Growth in $d$

For small $\epsilon^{d}$, use the approximation $\log(1 - \epsilon^{d}) \approx -\epsilon^{d}$:

$$N \approx \frac{\log\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\alpha}\right)}{\epsilon^{d}}$$

For fixed $\alpha$ and $\epsilon$ with $\epsilon < 1$, $\epsilon^{d}$ shrinks exponentially, so $N$ must grow exponentially.

### Step 6: Numerical Example

| $d$ | $\alpha$ | $\epsilon$ | $N$ required |
|-----|----------|------------|--------------|
| 1   | 0.1      | 0.1        | $\approx 1$  |
| 2   | 0.1      | 0.1        | $\approx 2$  |
| 5   | 0.1      | 0.1        | $\approx 10$ |
| 10  | 0.1      | 0.1        | $\approx 100$ |
| 20  | 0.1      | 0.1        | $\approx 10{,}000$ |
| 100 | 0.1      | 0.1        | $\approx 10^{100}$ (more than atoms in universe!) |

> [!WARNING]
> This is why high-dimensional pattern recognition is fundamentally a **small-sample problem**, even with millions of data points.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Edge Length for Fixed Sample Size

### Goal
Given $N$ samples, find the edge length $\epsilon$ such that the cube is covered.

### Step 1: Rearrange the Coverage Equation

From $1 - \alpha = (1 - \epsilon^{d})^{N}$, solve for $\epsilon^{d}$:

$$\epsilon^{d} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^{1/N}$$

### Step 2: Take the $d$-th Root

$$\epsilon = \left[1 - (1-\alpha)^{1/N}\right]^{1/d}$$

### Step 3: Asymptotic Behavior

For large $N$, $(1-\alpha)^{1/N} \approx e^{-\alpha/N}$, so:

$$\epsilon \approx \left[1 - e^{-\alpha/N}\right]^{1/d} \approx \left(\frac{\alpha}{N}\right)^{1/d}$$

### Step 4: Limit as $d \to \infty$

For fixed $N$ and $\alpha$, $\alpha/N < 1$, so $(\alpha/N)^{1/d} \to 1$ as $d \to \infty$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** With a fixed number of samples, the cell size must approach the **size of the entire space** as dimensions grow. This is the geometric reason classifiers fail in high dimensions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Curse of Dimensionality. List any three of its consequences in pattern recognition systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** The Curse of Dimensionality, a term coined by Richard Bellman (1961), refers to the various phenomena that arise when data is analyzed in high-dimensional feature spaces where the number of dimensions $d$ is large. These phenomena are absent in low-dimensional (1D–3D) settings and degrade the performance of pattern recognition systems.

**Three Consequences (2 marks — 0.5 each, or 0.67 each for partial):**

1. **Data Sparsity:** As $d$ increases, the available training data becomes sparse in the exponentially growing feature space, making density estimation unreliable.

2. **Distance Concentration:** All pairwise distances between data points converge to a common value as $d \to \infty$, breaking nearest-neighbor-based algorithms (K-NN, clustering).

3. **Hughes Phenomenon:** For a fixed training set, classification accuracy first increases with added features, reaches an optimum, and then **decreases** as redundant/noisy features inflate the space.

4. **Computational Cost:** Distance computations, search operations, and memory requirements grow exponentially with $d$, making many algorithms intractable.

5. **Estimator Variance:** The variance of statistical estimators (e.g., mean, covariance) grows with $d$, reducing statistical significance.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of distance concentration in high-dimensional spaces with a suitable mathematical justification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept (1.5 marks):** Distance concentration refers to the phenomenon that for $N$ points uniformly distributed in a $d$-dimensional unit hypercube, the ratio of the standard deviation of pairwise distances to their mean approaches zero as $d \to \infty$. Formally:

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{\sigma_{D}}{\mu_{D}} = 0$$

**Mathematical Justification (1.5 marks):** For $\mathcal{U}[0,1]^{d}$, the expected squared Euclidean distance between two i.i.d. points is $\mu_{D^{2}} = \dfrac{d}{6}$, and the variance is $\sigma_{D^{2}}^{2} = \dfrac{7d}{180}$. By the delta method, the coefficient of variation of the distance $D$ scales as:

$$\frac{\sigma_{D}}{\mu_{D}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{7}{20 d}} \to 0 \quad \text{as} \quad d \to \infty$$

This means the **nearest** and **farthest** neighbor distances become nearly equal, causing K-NN classifiers and distance-based clustering to fail.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with internal choice between two questions. Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

### Question B1 — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the volume of a $d$-dimensional hypersphere. Show that the ratio of the volume of a hypersphere to that of its circumscribed hypercube approaches zero as $d \to \infty$. Use Stirling's approximation appropriately.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pattern recognition system uses 10 training samples per class. Estimate the fraction $\alpha$ of the feature space that is "well-covered" if the system uses (i) $d=2$, (ii) $d=5$, and (iii) $d=10$ features, assuming $\epsilon = 0.1$ per cell. Comment on the implications for classifier design.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Hypersphere Volume and Cube Ratio

**Step 1 (2 marks): Volume of Hypersphere**

The volume of a $d$-dimensional hypersphere of radius $r$ is derived by integration in spherical coordinates. The general result is:

$$V_{\text{sphere}}(d, r) = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right)} \cdot r^{d}$$

where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the Gamma function, with $\Gamma(n+1) = n!$ for integer $n$ and $\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}$.

For $d=2$: $V = \pi r^{2}$ (correct area of circle).
For $d=3$: $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ (correct volume of sphere).

**Step 2 (2 marks): Volume of Circumscribed Hypercube**

A hypersphere of radius $r$ inscribed in a hypercube has cube side length $2r$:

$$V_{\text{cube}}(d) = (2r)^{d}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Form Ratio and Apply Stirling**

$$\rho(d) = \frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^{d} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(\frac{d}{2}+1\right)}$$

Using Stirling: $\Gamma\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2}+1\right) \approx \sqrt{\pi d} \left(\dfrac{d}{2e}\right)^{d/2}$.

Substituting and simplifying (1.5 marks):

$$\rho(d) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi d}} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi e}{2d}\right)^{d/2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Take the Limit**

Since $\dfrac{\pi e}{2d} < 1$ for $d \geq 5$, raising it to $d/2$ drives the expression exponentially to zero:

$$\lim_{d \to \infty} \rho(d) = 0$$

**Conclusion:** In high dimensions, the hypersphere occupies a vanishingly small fraction of the hypercube. The **corners** dominate, and data points are far more likely to be near a corner than the center.

---

#### Part (b) — Sample Coverage Analysis

**Step 1 (2 marks): Use the Coverage Formula**

From the derivation: $1 - \alpha = (1 - \epsilon^{d})^{N}$ where $N = 10$ and $\epsilon = 0.1$.

$$\alpha = 1 - (1 - \epsilon^{d})^{N}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks): Compute for Each Dimension**

**Case (i): $d = 2$**

$$\epsilon^{2} = 0.1^{2} = 0.01$$

$$\alpha = 1 - (1 - 0.01)^{10} = 1 - (0.99)^{10}$$

Compute $(0.99)^{10} \approx 0.9044$. So $\alpha \approx 1 - 0.9044 = 0.0956 \approx 9.56\%$.

**Case (ii): $d = 5$**

$$\epsilon^{5} = 0.1^{5} = 10^{-5}$$

$$\alpha = 1 - (1 - 10^{-5})^{10} = 1 - (0.99999)^{10}$$

By approximation, $(0.99999)^{10} \approx 1 - 10 \times 10^{-5} = 1 - 10^{-4} = 0.9999$.

So $\alpha \approx 10^{-4} = 0.01\%$.

**Case (iii): $d = 10$**

$$\epsilon^{10} = 0.1^{10} = 10^{-10}$$

$$\alpha = 1 - (1 - 10^{-10})^{10} \approx 1 - (1 - 10^{-9}) = 10^{-9}$$

So $\alpha \approx 10^{-7}\%$ (essentially zero).

**Step 3 (2 marks): Implications**

| $d$ | Coverage $\alpha$ | Implication |
|-----|-------------------|-------------|
| 2   | $\approx 9.56\%$   | Reasonable coverage; classifier can learn |
| 5   | $\approx 0.01\%$   | Almost all cells empty; classifier unstable |
| 10  | $\approx 10^{-9}$  | Complete failure; statistically meaningless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even with 10 samples, increasing dimensions from 2 to 10 causes the coverage to drop by **seven orders of magnitude**. This is the practical manifestation of the curse — high-dimensional feature spaces require exponentially more data, or **dimensionality reduction** must be applied.

---

### Question B2 — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With appropriate derivations, show that for $N$ points uniformly distributed in $[0,1]^{d}$, the mean pairwise Euclidean distance grows as $\sqrt{d/6}$ and its coefficient of variation approaches zero. What is the practical consequence for nearest-neighbor classification?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the Hughes Phenomenon in detail. Explain, with a labeled graph description, why classifier performance degrades after a certain number of features even though the Bayes error rate may be lower in higher dimensions.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Mean Distance and Concentration

**Step 1 (2 marks): Mean Squared Distance**

For $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \sim \mathcal{U}[0,1]^{d}$:

$$D^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - y_i)^{2}$$

By linearity of expectation and $\text{Var}(\mathcal{U}[0,1]) = \frac{1}{12}$:

$$\mathbb{E}[D^{2}] = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \mathbb{E}[(x_i - y_i)^{2}] = d \cdot \frac{1}{6} = \frac{d}{6}$$

So:

$$\mu_{D} = \sqrt{\frac{d}{6}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Variance of Squared Distance**

$$\text{Var}[D^{2}] = d \cdot \text{Var}[(x_1 - y_1)^{2}] = d \cdot \frac{7}{180} = \frac{7d}{180}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Standard Deviation of Distance via Delta Method**

By the delta method ($\text{Var}[g(X)] \approx g'(\mu)^{2} \text{Var}[X]$ with $g = \sqrt{\cdot}$):

$$\sigma_{D} = \frac{\sigma_{D^{2}}}{2 \mu_{D}} = \frac{\sqrt{7d/180}}{2\sqrt{d/6}} = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{7 \cdot 6}{180}} = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{7}{30}}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Coefficient of Variation**

$$\frac{\sigma_{D}}{\mu_{D}} = \frac{\sqrt{7/30}/2}{\sqrt{d/6}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{30}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6}{4d}} = \sqrt{\frac{7}{20 d}} \to 0 \quad \text{as} \quad d \to \infty$$

**Practical Consequence (free — implied by marks above):** The **contrast** between distances vanishes. K-NN cannot distinguish "close" from "far" points, because all distances look similar. The algorithm becomes **uniformly mediocre**, equivalent to random guessing in the limit.

---

#### Part (b) — Hughes Phenomenon Discussion

**Step 1 (2.5 marks): Definition and Explanation**

The **Hughes Phenomenon** (G.F. Hughes, 1968) states that for a **fixed** training set size $N$, the classification accuracy $A(d)$ of a pattern recognition system as a function of the number of features $d$ exhibits a non-monotonic behavior:

- For small $d$, increasing features reduces the Bayes error (more discriminative information).
- For large $d$, accuracy **decreases** because the feature space becomes too sparse; each cell contains very few training samples, increasing the variance of density and class-conditional probability estimates.

**Step 2 (2.5 marks): Mathematical Intuition**

The probability of error of a parametric or non-parametric classifier has two components:

$$P_{\text{error}} = P_{\text{Bayes}} + P_{\text{estimation}}$$

- $P_{\text{Bayes}}$ is the **irreducible** lower bound — it **decreases** with more discriminative features.
- $P_{\text{estimation}}$ is the **estimation error** due to finite samples — it **increases** with $d$ because the model must estimate more parameters or partition a larger space with the same $N$ samples.

The **total error** has a minimum at $d = d^{*}$, the **optimal dimensionality**:

$$d^{*} = \arg\min_{d} \left[ P_{\text{Bayes}}(d) + P_{\text{estimation}}(d, N) \right]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Graph Description**

The accuracy-versus-dimension graph has the following labeled structure:

| Curve Element | Behavior |
|---------------|----------|
| X-axis | Number of features $d$ (1 to 100+) |
| Y-axis | Classification accuracy $A(d)$ (0 to 100%) |
| Initial region ($d$ small) | Steep rise in accuracy as features are added |
| Optimal point $d^{*}$ | Maximum accuracy achieved |
| Region beyond $d^{*}$ | Accuracy **declines** steadily |
| Asymptote | Approaches random-guess accuracy for very high $d$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Increasing $d$ beyond $d^{*}$ is **counterproductive** — the system "thinks" it has more information, but with finite $N$, the estimates become unreliable. This is the **operational** form of the curse in classifier design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the Bayes error and estimation error** in the Hughes Phenomenon. Many students claim accuracy only improves with $d$ — this is **wrong**. The Bayes error decreases, but total error (the real one we observe) is U-shaped.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Gamma function** in hypersphere volume. Writing $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ for general $d$ loses 2 marks. Always state the general formula $V = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)} r^{d}$.
>
> 3. **Incorrect use of Stirling's approximation.** Students often write $\Gamma(n) \approx \sqrt{2\pi n} (n/e)^{n}$ but forget that for $\Gamma(d/2+1)$, the argument is $d/2$, not $d$. This gives wrong powers in the final ratio.
>
> 4. **Skipping steps in the distance concentration derivation.** Direct statement "$\sigma/\mu \to 0$" without showing the variance computation loses full credit. Show every step.
>
> 5. **Misapplying the coverage formula.** The exponent $N$ is **not** in the power of $d$; it is multiplied by $\log(1 - \epsilon^{d})$. Many students incorrectly write $N = \epsilon^{-d}$.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning concrete numbers.** Numerical verification tables (e.g., $\rho(10) = 0.0025$) earn additional credit. KTU examiners reward **quantitative** understanding.
>
> 7. **Conflating "high dimension" with "many data points."** The curse is about **dimensions**, not samples. The two are inversely related in the problem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Curse of Dimensionality**

### Core Definitions
- ☑ **CoD** coined by **Richard Bellman (1961)** in dynamic programming.
- ☑ Refers to phenomena emerging in **high-dimensional** spaces ($d \gtrsim 10$).
- ☑ Applies universally across ML, statistics, optimization, signal processing.

### Key Mathematical Results
- ☑ **Hypersphere volume:** $V_{\text{sphere}}(d) = \dfrac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)} r^{d}$
- ☑ **Hypercube volume:** $V_{\text{cube}}(d) = (2r)^{d}$
- ☑ **Volume ratio:** $\rho(d) = \dfrac{\pi^{d/2}}{2^{d} \Gamma(d/2+1)} \to 0$ as $d \to \infty$
- ☑ **Mean pairwise distance** in $\mathcal{U}[0,1]^{d}$: $\mu_{D} = \sqrt{d/6}$
- ☑ **Coefficient of variation:** $\sigma_{D}/\mu_{D} = \sqrt{7/(20d)} \to 0$
- ☑ **Coverage formula:** $1 - \alpha = (1 - \epsilon^{d})^{N}$, so $N = \dfrac{\log(1-\alpha)}{\log(1-\epsilon^{d})}$

### Key Phenomena
- ☑ **Data Sparsity:** Volume grows exponentially; data points become isolated.
- ☑ **Distance Concentration:** All pairwise distances converge; K-NN breaks.
- ☑ **Hughes Phenomenon:** Accuracy first increases, then **decreases** with $d$.
- ☑ **Curse of Empty Space:** Most cells in a high-D grid contain zero training samples.
- ☑ **Variance Inflation:** Statistical estimators become unreliable.

### Engineering Solutions
- ☑ **Dimensionality Reduction:** PCA, LDA, t-SNE, Autoencoders.
- ☑ **Feature Selection:** Filter, Wrapper, Embedded methods.
- ☑ **Regularization:** L1 (Lasso), L2 (Ridge) to control overfitting.
- ☑ **Sampling Techniques:** SMOTE, stratified sampling for imbalanced data.
- ☑ **Domain Knowledge:** Use prior knowledge to select meaningful features.

### Numerical Anchors to Memorize
- ☑ $\rho(2) = 0.785$, $\rho(3) = 0.524$, $\rho(5) = 0.164$, $\rho(10) = 0.0025$
- ☑ $\mu_{D}$ at $d=10$ is $\sqrt{10/6} \approx 1.29$
- ☑ KTU loves these specific values — **memorize them**.

### Common Exam Hooks
- ☑ A question will ask you to **derive** $\rho(d) \to 0$ — practice the Stirling substitution.
- ☑ Hughes Phenomenon **always appears** in Part A or Part B of Module 2.
- ☑ Be ready to compute $N$ for a given $d$, $\alpha$, $\epsilon$ — this is a **sure shot 7-mark question**.
- ☑ Quote Bellman's year (1961) and the source (dynamic programming) in the definition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimension Reduction Methods - Fisher Discriminant Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimension Reduction Methods - Fisher Discriminant Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fisher Discriminant Analysis (FDA)** — also referred to as **Fisher Linear Discriminant (FLD)** — is a **supervised linear dimension-reduction technique** that projects high-dimensional data onto a lower-dimensional subspace by **maximizing the ratio of between-class scatter to within-class scatter**, thereby achieving maximum class separability.

Mathematically, FDA seeks a projection vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ such that when the data $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is projected onto it via $y = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}$, the classes become maximally separated while the samples of the same class become maximally clustered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "supervised"?** Unlike PCA (which uses only the input covariance), FDA uses the **class label information** of each training sample to construct a discriminant subspace. This is why FDA is naturally suited for *classification* tasks and pre-processing for classifiers.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "Spotlight on the School Auditorium"

Imagine a school auditorium where students from **two different grades (Grade 5 and Grade 6)** are standing in a 2-D hall, mixed together.

- **PCA's view (unsupervised):** You stand on a chair and look *down* from the highest-variance direction. You might see the *tallest students* most clearly, but you cannot tell Grade 5 apart from Grade 6.
- **Fisher's view (supervised):** You stand on the same chair but **rotate your body** until you find the angle at which the two grades form the **two tightest, farthest-apart clusters**. That special viewing angle is the **Fisher direction** $\mathbf{w}^{*}$.

The data points are then "squeezed" onto this single line (a 1-D shadow). Despite the loss of the second dimension, the two classes remain *maximally* distinguishable.

> [!TIP]
> **The Big Picture:** PCA preserves **variance**; FDA preserves **discriminability**. For classification, discriminability is what we actually want.

## 1.3 Salient Properties

- **Linearity:** The decision boundary / projection is **linear in the input space**.
- **Dimensionality bound:** For a $C$-class problem, FDA yields at most $C-1$ discriminant directions (vectors), giving a projection subspace of dimension $\le C-1$.
- **Optimality:** The projection is optimal in the sense of **maximizing the Rayleigh quotient** of between-class to within-class scatter.
- **Assumption:** Each class is **approximately Gaussian** with **equal covariance matrices** (homoscedastic Gaussian classes); under this assumption FDA is the **Bayes-optimal linear classifier**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic typically appears in Module 2 alongside PCA, and the exam frequently contrasts the two — *"Distinguish between PCA and Fisher Discriminant Analysis."* Be ready to write a comparison table.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Setup — Two-Class Problem

Consider a two-class classification problem with:

- $N_1$ samples in class $\mathcal{C}_1$ and $N_2$ samples in class $\mathcal{C}_2$, with $N = N_1 + N_2$.
- Each sample $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is a $d$-dimensional feature vector.
- Class label $y_i \in \{\mathcal{C}_1, \mathcal{C}_2\}$.

We wish to find a **projection vector** $\mathbf{w}$ (a unit vector, $\|\mathbf{w}\|=1$) such that the scalar projection $y = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}$ optimally separates the two classes.

## 2.2 Class Mean Vectors

The mean of each class in $d$-dimensional space:

$$\mathbf{m}_1 = \frac{1}{N_1} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_n \in \mathcal{C}_1} \mathbf{x}_n$$

$$\mathbf{m}_2 = \frac{1}{N_2} \sum_{\mathbf{x}_n \in \mathcal{C}_2} \mathbf{x}_n$$

After projection, the projected (scalar) means become:

$$\tilde{m}_k = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{m}_k, \quad k = 1, 2$$

The **separation** of the projected class means is therefore $(\tilde{m}_1 - \tilde{m}_2)^{2} = \left(\mathbf{w}^{T}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)\right)^{2}$.

## 2.3 Scatter Matrices — The Two Key Quantities

### 2.3.1 Within-Class Scatter Matrix ($S_W$)

The pooled within-class covariance measures how spread out each class is around its own mean:

$$S_W = S_1 + S_2 = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_n \in \mathcal{C}_1} (\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_1)(\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_1)^{T} + \sum_{\mathbf{x}_n \in \mathcal{C}_2} (\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}$$

### 2.3.2 Between-Class Scatter Matrix ($S_B$)

Measures the spread of the class means around the **overall mean**:

$$S_B = (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that $S_B$ is an **outer product of two mean-difference vectors**, so it has **rank 1**. This is the algebraic reason why for two classes, FDA can produce at most **one** non-trivial discriminant direction.

## 2.4 The Fisher Criterion Function

The original two criteria — "maximize separation of means" and "minimize within-class spread" — are combined into a **single scalar criterion** $J(\mathbf{w})$:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\tilde{\sigma}_{\text{between}}^{2}}{\tilde{\sigma}_{\text{within}}^{2}} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \, \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \, \mathbf{w}}$$

The vector $\mathbf{w}^{*}$ that maximizes $J$ is the **Fisher Linear Discriminant**.

## 2.5 The Optimization — Generalized Eigenvalue Problem

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$ and rearranging leads to the famous **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \, S_W \mathbf{w}$$

$$\boxed{S_W^{-1} S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}}$$

For the **two-class case**, since $S_B \mathbf{w} = (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}\mathbf{w}$, the direction simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}^{*} = S_W^{-1} (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)}$$

(up to a scalar multiplier; the sign of $\mathbf{w}$ is arbitrary).

## 2.6 Multi-Class Generalization ($C \ge 2$)

For $C$ classes, the within-class and between-class scatters are:

$$S_W = \sum_{k=1}^{C} S_k, \quad S_k = \sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}_k} (\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_k)(\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_k)^{T}$$

$$S_B = \sum_{k=1}^{C} N_k (\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m})(\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m})^{T}, \quad \mathbf{m} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_n$$

The **$C-1$ eigenvectors** of $S_W^{-1} S_B$ corresponding to the **largest $C-1$ eigenvalues** form the projection matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times (C-1)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why at most $C-1$ directions?** The rank of $S_B$ is at most $C-1$ (it lives in the subspace spanned by the $C$ class mean deviations from the global mean, modulo the global mean itself, which removes one degree of freedom). Hence at most $C-1$ non-zero eigenvalues of $S_W^{-1} S_B$ can exist.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Projection of a sample | $y = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}$ | Scalar output, dimensionless if $\mathbf{x}$ is. |
| 2 | Class mean | $\mathbf{m}_k = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}_k} \mathbf{x}_n$ | $d \times 1$ vector. |
| 3 | Within-class scatter | $S_W = \sum_{k=1}^{C} \sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}_k} (\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_k)(\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_k)^{T}$ | $d \times d$, symmetric, positive semi-definite. |
| 4 | Between-class scatter (2-class) | $S_B = (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}$ | Rank 1, $d \times d$. |
| 5 | Between-class scatter ($C$-class) | $S_B = \sum_{k=1}^{C} N_k (\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m})(\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m})^{T}$ | Rank $\le C-1$. |
| 6 | Fisher criterion | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}}$ | Scalar; maximized. |
| 7 | GEV problem | $S_W^{-1} S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ | Roots are the discriminant directions. |
| 8 | Closed-form 2-class solution | $\mathbf{w}^{*} \propto S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)$ | Direction only; $\|\mathbf{w}^{*}\|$ is unconstrained. |
| 9 | Max # discriminant axes | $C - 1$ | For $C$ classes. |
| 10 | Assumed optimum | Bayes-optimal linear boundary | Holds under equal-covariance Gaussians. |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Face recognition (Eigenface vs. Fisherface):** Belhumeur et al.'s *Fisherface* method demonstrated that FDA outperforms PCA on face recognition under illumination changes.
- **Speech recognition** pre-processing to extract maximally class-discriminative acoustic features.
- **Medical diagnosis** — projecting high-dimensional biomarker vectors (e.g., gene expression microarrays with thousands of features and only tens of samples) onto $C-1$ FDA axes for diagnosis.
- **Radar / sonar target classification** where the sensor output is high-dimensional and labeled training data is scarce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Two Heuristics to the Fisher Criterion

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the projection

We seek a direction $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ such that the projected scalar $y = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}$ best separates the classes. We do **not** require $\mathbf{w}$ to be a unit vector at this stage (the final answer will be a direction only).

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Measure separation of the projected class means

After projection, the mean of class $k$ is:

$$\tilde{m}_k = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{m}_k, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{m}_k = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}_k} \mathbf{x}_n$$

The squared difference of the projected means is:

$$(\tilde{m}_1 - \tilde{m}_2)^{2} = (\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{m}_2)^{2} = \left(\mathbf{w}^{T}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)\right)^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{T}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}$$

> **Reasoning:** We use the identity $(a-b)^{2} = (a-b)(a-b)^{T}$ for vectors (i.e., $\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^{T}$ form), and recognize this as the **between-class scatter in the projected 1-D space**.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Measure within-class scatter of the projected samples

The within-class variance of class $k$ after projection is:

$$\tilde{s}_k^{2} = \sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}_k} (y_n - \tilde{m}_k)^{2} = \sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}_k} (\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{m}_k)^{2} = \sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}_k} \mathbf{w}^{T}(\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_k)(\mathbf{x}_n - \mathbf{m}_k)^{T}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T} S_k \mathbf{w}$$

The total within-class scatter after projection is:

$$\tilde{s}_1^{2} + \tilde{s}_2^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{T} S_1 \mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^{T} S_2 \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Combine into a single criterion

Fisher proposed the ratio:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\text{between-class scatter after projection}}{\text{within-class scatter after projection}} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}}$$

This is the famous **Fisher criterion** (a *Rayleigh quotient*).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximizing $J(\mathbf{w})$ via Lagrange Multipliers

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Reformulate the maximization

$J(\mathbf{w})$ is **scale-invariant** in $\mathbf{w}$: replacing $\mathbf{w}$ by $c\mathbf{w}$ does not change the ratio. So we can fix the denominator and maximize the numerator, *or* equivalently introduce a Lagrange multiplier.

We choose the constraint $\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w} = 1$ (a common convention; one may also choose $\|\mathbf{w}\|=1$ and obtain the same direction). The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w} - \lambda \left( \mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w} - 1 \right)$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Take the gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero

Using the standard matrix derivative rule $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^{T} A \mathbf{w} = 2A\mathbf{w}$ for symmetric $A$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 S_B \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda S_W \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

$$S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

$$\boxed{S_W^{-1} S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}}$$

This is the **generalized eigenvalue problem**. The Fisher directions are the **eigenvectors** of $S_W^{-1} S_B$, and the criterion value $J(\mathbf{w})$ at each eigenvector equals its **eigenvalue** $\lambda$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Form Solution for Two Classes

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Substitute the rank-1 form of $S_B$

For two classes, $S_B = (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}$. Inserting into the eigenvalue equation:

$$(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T} \mathbf{w} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Recognize the scalar factor

The quantity $(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T} \mathbf{w}$ is a **scalar**. Let us denote it $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T} \mathbf{w}$$

Then the equation becomes:

$$\alpha \,(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2) = \lambda S_W \mathbf{w}$$

$$\mathbf{w} = \frac{\alpha}{\lambda} S_W^{-1} (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)$$

Since $\mathbf{w}$ is defined only up to a scalar (the eigenvalue equation has infinitely many scaled solutions, but $J$ is scale-invariant), we may drop the factor $\frac{\alpha}{\lambda}$ and write:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}^{*} \propto S_W^{-1} (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)}$$

The proportionality constant is often chosen so that $\mathbf{w}^{*T} \mathbf{w}^{*} = 1$, i.e., $\mathbf{w}^{*} = \frac{S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)}{\Vert S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2) \Vert}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Number of Discriminant Directions

### 3.4.1 Step 1 — Rank of $S_B$

$$S_B = \sum_{k=1}^{C} N_k (\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m})(\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m})^{T}$$

Each outer product is rank 1. The sum is a linear combination of at most $C$ rank-1 matrices. However, since $\sum_{k} N_k(\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m}) = N\mathbf{m} - N\mathbf{m} = \mathbf{0}$, the deviations $\{\mathbf{m}_k - \mathbf{m}\}$ are linearly dependent (one relation among $C$ vectors), so the rank is at most $C-1$.

### 3.4.2 Step 2 — Rank of $S_W^{-1} S_B$

Assuming $S_W$ is invertible (or using its pseudo-inverse when singular), $\text{rank}(S_W^{-1} S_B) = \text{rank}(S_B) \le C-1$.

Therefore the matrix $S_W^{-1} S_B$ has at most $C-1$ **non-zero eigenvalues**, and the discriminant subspace has at most $C-1$ dimensions.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (2-D, 2-Class)

> [!NOTE]
> The following toy example has been constructed to give you a KTU-style numerical problem-solving template.

**Given 5 samples:**

| Sample | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^{T}$ | Class |
|--------|-------------------------------|-------|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | $(1, 2)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | $(2, 3)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | $(3, 4)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_4$ | $(6, 8)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_2$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_5$ | $(7, 9)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_2$ |

**Step 1 — Class means:**

$$\mathbf{m}_1 = \frac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1+2+3 \\ 2+3+4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{m}_2 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 6+7 \\ 8+9 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6.5 \\ 8.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Within-class scatters $S_1$ and $S_2$:**

For $\mathcal{C}_1$ (deviations from $\mathbf{m}_1$):

$$\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{m}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{m}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_3 - \mathbf{m}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$S_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

For $\mathcal{C}_2$ (deviations from $\mathbf{m}_2$):

$$\mathbf{x}_4 - \mathbf{m}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.5 \\ -0.5 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_5 - \mathbf{m}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$S_2 = 2 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 0.25 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$S_W = S_1 + S_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 2.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Inverse of $S_W$:**

$$\det(S_W) = (2.5)(2.5) - (0.5)(0.5) = 6.25 - 0.25 = 6.0$$

$$S_W^{-1} = \frac{1}{6.0}\begin{pmatrix} 2.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 2.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4167 & -0.0833 \\ -0.0833 & 0.4167 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Mean difference vector:**

$$\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 - 6.5 \\ 3 - 8.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -4.5 \\ -5.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Fisher direction:**

$$\mathbf{w}^{*} = S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2) = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4167 & -0.0833 \\ -0.0833 & 0.4167 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -4.5 \\ -5.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{w}^{*} = \begin{pmatrix} (0.4167)(-4.5) + (-0.0833)(-5.5) \\ (-0.0833)(-4.5) + (0.4167)(-5.5) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1.875 + 0.458 \\ 0.375 - 2.292 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1.417 \\ -1.917 \end{pmatrix}$$

Normalizing for unit length (optional):

$$\Vert \mathbf{w}^{*} \Vert = \sqrt{1.417^{2} + 1.917^{2}} = \sqrt{2.008 + 3.675} = \sqrt{5.683} \approx 2.384$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{w}}^{*} \approx \begin{pmatrix} -0.594 \\ -0.804 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Project the samples onto $\hat{\mathbf{w}}^{*}$:**

| Sample | $y = \hat{\mathbf{w}}^{*T}\mathbf{x}$ |
|--------|---------------------------------------|
| $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,2)$ | $-0.594 - 1.608 = -2.202$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_2 = (2,3)$ | $-1.188 - 2.412 = -3.600$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_3 = (3,4)$ | $-1.782 - 3.216 = -4.998$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_4 = (6,8)$ | $-3.564 - 6.432 = -9.996$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_5 = (7,9)$ | $-4.158 - 7.236 = -11.394$ |

A threshold of, say, $y_0 \approx -7$ perfectly separates the two classes — confirming that the Fisher direction has done its job.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PCA vs. FDA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **examination gold** for KTU — expect at least one 7-mark question asking to *compare and contrast* the two methods.

| Aspect | PCA | FDA (LDA) |
|--------|-----|-----------|
| Supervision | **Unsupervised** (no labels used) | **Supervised** (uses class labels) |
| Goal | Preserve **maximum variance** | Maximize **class separability** |
| Scatter criterion | Maximize $\mathbf{w}^{T} S_T \mathbf{w}$ | Maximize $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}}$ |
| Scatter used | Total scatter $S_T$ | Between $S_B$ and within $S_W$ |
| # of directions | Up to $d$ (full rank) | At most $C-1$ |
| Class distribution | Ignored | Assumes equal-covariance Gaussians for optimality |
| Best suited for | Data compression, denoising, visualization | Pre-processing for classification |
| Solution | Eigen-decomposition of $S_T$ | Generalized eigen-decomposition of $S_W^{-1} S_B$ |
| Failure mode | May discard low-variance but discriminative direction | Fails when $S_W$ is singular (small sample size) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — The FDA Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Feature Matrix X of size N x d] --> B[Separate Samples by Class Label]
    B --> C1[Class C1 samples]
    B --> C2[Class C2 samples]
    B --> C3[Class C3 samples for multi-class]

    C1 --> D1[Compute Class Mean m1]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Class Mean m2]
    C3 --> D3[Compute Class Mean m3]

    C1 --> E1[Compute Class Scatter S1]
    C2 --> E2[Compute Class Scatter S2]
    C3 --> E3[Compute Class Scatter S3]

    E1 --> F[Pool to Within-Class Scatter SW]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    D1 --> G[Compute Between-Class Scatter SB]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G

    F --> H[Form Rayleigh Quotient J of w]
    G --> H

    H --> I[Set Gradient to Zero]
    I --> J[Generalized Eigenvalue Problem SWinv SB w = lambda w]

    J --> K{Select Top C minus 1 Eigenvectors}
    K --> L[Form Projection Matrix W of size d x C minus 1]
    L --> M[Project Y = X W for Classification]
    M --> N[Classify via Threshold or Bayes Rule]

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style J fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style N fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Geometric Interpretation of Fisher Direction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original d-D Space with Two Classes] --> B[Compute m1 and m2]
    B --> C[Compute Within-Class Scatter SW]
    C --> D[Form Matrix M = SWinv of m1 minus m2]
    D --> E[Identify Fisher Direction w star]
    E --> F[Project all x onto w star]
    F --> G[Find Optimal Threshold y0 on 1-D Line]
    G --> H[Decision Boundary in d-D is a Hyperplane orthogonal to w star]

    style A fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Conceptual Contrast of PCA vs. FDA Directions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Two Overlapping Gaussians in 2D] --> P2[PCA: Direction of Maximum Total Variance]
    P1 --> P3[FDA: Direction of Maximum Between to Within Ratio]

    P2 --> P4[Projects onto line that may MIX classes]
    P3 --> P5[Projects onto line that SEPARATES classes cleanly]

    P4 --> P6[Result: Poor 1D Separability]
    P5 --> P7[Result: Excellent 1D Separability]

    style P2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P3 fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P6 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P7 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic for Exam:** If you draw the *PCA arrow* along the long axis of the elliptical data cloud and the *FDA arrow* between the two class centers (rotated to dodge the within-class spread), the difference becomes self-evident.

## 4.4 Mermaid — Workflow Inside `J(w)` Maximization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Initialize Direction w] --> stepB[Project data: y = wT x]
    stepB --> stepC[Compute Projected Means m1t and m2t]
    stepB --> stepD[Compute Projected Within-Class Variances]
    stepC --> stepE[Compute Numerator: m1t minus m2t squared]
    stepD --> stepF[Compute Denominator: Sum of variances]
    stepE --> stepG[Form Rayleigh Quotient J of w]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH[Compute Gradient of J wrt w]
    stepH --> stepI[Set Gradient to Zero]
    stepI --> stepJ[Solve SWinv SB w = lambda w]
    stepJ --> stepK[Pick Eigenvector with Largest Eigenvalue]
    stepK --> stepL[Output Optimal Fisher Direction w star]

    style stepJ fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style stepL fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common deduction:** students forget that $S_B$ is a **matrix** (not a scalar) in higher-class problems and end up writing $(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)$ alone for $S_B$. **Always** write the outer product $(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}$.
> 2. **Sign convention trap:** $\mathbf{w}^{*}$ can point in **either direction** along the same line; both are equally optimal. Examiners may mark a *wrong sign* as wrong — clarify by writing "direction up to a sign."
> 3. **The $\propto$ vs $=$ trap:** write $\mathbf{w}^{*} \propto S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)$ **only** for the two-class case. The general $C$-class case requires the full eigen-decomposition of $S_W^{-1} S_B$.
> 4. **Singular $S_W$:** if the number of samples is less than $d$, $S_W$ is singular — students must use the pseudo-inverse or PCA-preprocessing. Examiners often probe this.
> 5. **Confusion with LDA generative model:** Fisher Discriminant is a *discriminative* linear projection. LDA in the Bayesian sense is a *generative* model. KTU examiners sometimes equate them — clarify the difference if asked.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Fisher Linear Discriminant. State the Fisher criterion for two-class pattern classification.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Fisher Linear Discriminant (FLD)** is a linear projection technique that finds a direction $\mathbf{w}$ in the input feature space such that the projected samples of two classes have **maximum separation of class means** while having **minimum within-class scatter**.
>
> Given class means $\mathbf{m}_1, \mathbf{m}_2$, within-class scatter $S_W$, and between-class scatter $S_B = (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}$, the **Fisher criterion** is:
>
> $$J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}}$$
>
> The optimal $\mathbf{w}^{*}$ is the eigenvector of $S_W^{-1} S_B$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue. For two classes, $\mathbf{w}^{*} \propto S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)$.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between PCA and Fisher Discriminant Analysis in terms of supervision and objective function.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PCA | FDA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Supervision | Unsupervised — uses only $\mathbf{x}$ | Supervised — uses $(\mathbf{x}, y)$ |
| Objective | Maximize total variance: $\mathbf{w}^{T} S_T \mathbf{w}$ | Maximize separability: $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}}$ |
| Number of directions | Up to $d$ | At most $C-1$ |
| Use case | Reconstruction, denoising | Classification pre-processing |

> PCA finds directions of **maximum variance**, ignoring labels. FDA finds directions that **maximize class discrimination**, using the label information to weight the projection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions in the End Semester Exam carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts, typically $(a) = 7$ marks and $(b) = 7$ marks.

### Question B1A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and derive the **Fisher criterion** $J(\mathbf{w})$ for a two-class problem. Show clearly how the between-class and within-class scatter matrices are constructed.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Starting from $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$, derive the **generalized eigenvalue problem** $S_W^{-1} S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$. Hence obtain the closed-form solution $\mathbf{w}^{*} \propto S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** The derivation of $J(\mathbf{w})$ is shown in **§3.1** above. The key points to mention are:

1. The projected mean is $\tilde{m}_k = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{m}_k$.
2. The projected between-class scatter is $(\tilde{m}_1 - \tilde{m}_2)^{2} = \mathbf{w}^{T}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}$.
3. The projected within-class scatter is $\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}$ where $S_W = S_1 + S_2$.
4. The Fisher criterion is the ratio $J(\mathbf{w}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w}}$.

**Part (b):** The derivation of the GEV problem is shown in **§3.2** above. Key steps:

1. Impose the constraint $\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w} = 1$ via the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{T} S_B \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{T} S_W \mathbf{w} - 1)$.
2. Differentiate: $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 S_B \mathbf{w} - 2\lambda S_W \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$.
3. Rearrange: $S_W^{-1} S_B \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.
4. For two classes, substitute the rank-1 form of $S_B$ to get $\mathbf{w}^{*} \propto S_W^{-1}(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Many students incorrectly differentiate $\mathbf{w}^{T} A \mathbf{w}$ as $A\mathbf{w}$ instead of $2A\mathbf{w}$. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction. The factor of 2 cancels in the equation, but writing it correctly shows full understanding.

---

### Question B1B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the following two-class dataset, compute the class means, the within-class scatter matrix $S_W$, and the between-class scatter matrix $S_B$.

| Sample | $\mathbf{x}$ | Class |
|--------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | $(2, 3)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_1$ |
| 2 | $(4, 5)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_1$ |
| 3 | $(6, 8)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_2$ |
| 4 | $(8, 10)^{T}$ | $\mathcal{C}_2$ |

**(b)** [7 Marks] Hence find the **Fisher direction** $\mathbf{w}^{*}$ and **project** the four samples onto this direction. Comment on class separability in the 1-D projected space.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Computing the means:

$$\mathbf{m}_1 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 2+4 \\ 3+5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{m}_2 = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 6+8 \\ 8+10 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix}$$

Computing $S_1$ (deviations from $\mathbf{m}_1$):

$$\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{m}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{m}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$S_1 = 2 \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Computing $S_2$ (deviations from $\mathbf{m}_2$):

$$\mathbf{x}_3 - \mathbf{m}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_4 - \mathbf{m}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$S_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$S_W = S_1 + S_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Part (a) continued — Between-class scatter:**

$$S_B = (\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)(\mathbf{m}_1 - \mathbf{m}_2)^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} -4 \\ -5 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -4 & -5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 16 & 20 \\ 20 & 25 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Part (b):** Note that $S_W$ has determinant $= (4)(4) - (4)(4) = 0$ — it is **singular** because both classes lie on the line $x_2 = x_1 + 1$, i.e., 1-D data in 2-D. We must use the **pseudo-inverse**:

$$S_W^{+} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 8} \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -4 \\ -4 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{16}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -4 \\ -4 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.25 & -0.25 \\ -0.25 & 0.25 \end{pmatrix}$$

(The pseudo-inverse of a rank-1 matrix $a \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^{T}$ is $\frac{1}{a \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert^{4}} \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^{T}$, with $\mathbf{u} = (1,1)^{T}$, $a = 8$, $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = \sqrt{2}$.)

**Fisher direction:**
