Use the matrix identities $\dfrac{\partial\ln\vert\mathbf{A}\vert}{\partial\mathbf{A}} = (\mathbf{A}^{-1})^{\top}$ and $\dfrac{\partial\,\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{x}}{\partial\mathbf{A}^{-1}} = \mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\top}$. Setting $\dfrac{\partial Q_{\Sigma}}{\partial\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}^{-1}}=0$ and using the updated $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}}$ from §3.2.1:

$$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_{k}}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})\,(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}})(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}})^{\top}$$

### 3.2.3 Re-derivation of the Mixing-Coefficient Update

The terms in $Q$ involving $\pi_{k}$ are $\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{nk})\ln\pi_{k}$. We must maximise this subject to $\sum_{k}\pi_{k}=1$. Use a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{J} = \sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\gamma(z_{nk})\ln\pi_{k} + \lambda\!\left(\sum_{k=1}^{K}\pi_{k} - 1\right)$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\pi_{k}$:

$$\dfrac{\partial\mathcal{J}}{\partial\pi_{k}} = \frac{N_{k}}{\pi_{k}} + \lambda = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \pi_{k} = -\frac{N_{k}}{\lambda}$$

Sum both sides over $k$ and use $\sum_{k}\pi_{k}=1$:

$$1 = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{k=1}^{K}N_{k} = -\frac{N}{\lambda} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda = -N$$

Therefore:

$$\pi_{k}^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_{k}}{N}$$

This is the intuitively pleasing result that **the new mixing coefficient equals the fraction of "soft mass" assigned to component $k$**.

## 3.3 Why EM is Guaranteed to Increase the Log-Likelihood

EM alternates E and M steps. The EM theorem guarantees:

$$\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{new}}) \geq \mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$$

with equality only at a fixed point. The proof relies on the fact that the M-step maximises a *lower bound* (Jensen's inequality) on $\mathcal{L}$, and the E-step makes that lower bound **tight** at the current parameters. So the sequence of log-likelihoods is non-decreasing, ensuring convergence (to a *local* maximum — the model is non-convex in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$).

## 3.4 Numerical Example — A 1-D Toy GMM with $K=2$

**Setup.** Two true components: $\pi_{1}=0.4$, $\mu_{1}=0$, $\sigma_{1}=1$; $\pi_{2}=0.6$, $\mu_{2}=5$, $\sigma_{2}=1$. Suppose at iteration $t$ we have estimates $\hat{\pi}_{1}=0.5,\hat{\mu}_{1}=1,\hat{\sigma}_{1}=2$ and $\hat{\pi}_{2}=0.5,\hat{\mu}_{2}=4,\hat{\sigma}_{2}=2$. Evaluate responsibilities for one point $x=3$:

$$\mathcal{N}(3\mid 1,4) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\cdot 4}}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(3-1)^{2}}{2\cdot 4}\right] = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-0.5} \approx 0.1203$$

$$\mathcal{N}(3\mid 4,4) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-0.125} \approx 0.1747$$

$$\gamma(z_{1}) = \frac{0.5\times 0.1203}{0.5\times 0.1203 + 0.5\times 0.1747} = \frac{0.0601}{0.1475} \approx 0.408$$

$$\gamma(z_{2}) = 1 - 0.408 = 0.592$$

This is the **soft assignment**: $x=3$ is $40.8\%$ likely to come from component 1 and $59.2\%$ from component 2. In K-means, by contrast, $x=3$ would be **hard-assigned** to component 2 (the closer mean) with probability $1.0$ — a useful contrast to mention in viva voce.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision: Choosing $K$

- **Too small $K$** → under-fitting; one component tries to cover two clusters.
- **Too large $K$** → over-fitting; each component fits a single point (singular covariances).
- **Standard metrics** to choose $K$:
  - **Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC):** $\text{BIC} = -2\mathcal{L} + K_{\text{params}}\ln N$.
  - **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC):** $\text{AIC} = -2\mathcal{L} + 2K_{\text{params}}$.
  - **Cross-validated likelihood** on a held-out set.
- Choose the $K$ that **minimises** BIC/AIC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: EM Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: observed data X] --> B[Initialise parameters mu_k, Sigma_k, pi_k]
    B --> C{Evaluate convergence test}
    C -- Not converged --> D[E-Step: compute responsibilities gamma z_nk for all n,k]
    D --> E[M-Step: update mu_k from weighted means]
    E --> F[M-Step: update Sigma_k from weighted scatter]
    F --> G[M-Step: update pi_k from soft counts]
    G --> H[Compute new log-likelihood L theta]
    H --> C
    C -- Converged --> I[Output final mu_k, Sigma_k, pi_k]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Generative Process of a GMM (Sampling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Draw component index z from Categorical pi] --> B0[Lookup corresponding mu_z and Sigma_z]
    B0 --> C0[Draw sample x from N mu_z, Sigma_z]
    C0 --> D0[Output x and label z]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: GMM as a Two-Layer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1[Latent Layer]
        L1[Latent variable z with prior pi_k]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[Observation Layer]
        L2[Component Gaussians N x mu_k Sigma_k]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[Marginalisation]
        L3[Weighted sum yields p x]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Conceptual Comparison — K-Means vs GMM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KM[K-Means]
        KM1[Hard assignment] --> KM2[Spherical equal variance clusters] --> KM3[Minimises within cluster SSE]
    end
    subgraph GM[GMM]
        GM1[Soft assignment via responsibilities] --> GM2[Arbitrary full covariance ellipses] --> GM3[Maximises data log likelihood]
    end
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description (for the student's mental picture)

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a 2-D plane with three elliptical "blobs" of data. The GMM fits an ellipse (with arbitrary orientation and shape) to each blob. The **mean** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$ is the ellipse's centre, the **covariance** $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}$ encodes its orientation and spread, and the **mixing coefficient** $\pi_{k}$ is proportional to the blob's area. The contours of the *overall* density $p(\mathbf{x})$ look like the *union* of the individual ellipses weighted by $\pi_{k}$ — a "three-blob cloud".

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`  
> **Define a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). Write the mathematical expression for its probability density function and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Gaussian Mixture Model is a probabilistic model that expresses a complex multi-modal probability density as a weighted sum of $K$ multivariate Gaussian (Normal) component densities.

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_{k}\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k})$$

- $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k})$ is the $k^{\text{th}}$ component Gaussian with mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}$.  
- $\pi_{k}$ is the **mixing coefficient** representing the prior probability that a randomly drawn observation belongs to component $k$, with $\pi_{k}\geq 0$ and $\sum_{k=1}^{K}\pi_{k}=1$.

**(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — explanation of terms.)**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`  
> **What is the role of the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm in training a GMM? State the E-step and M-step update equations for the mixing coefficient $\pi_{k}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EM algorithm is used to estimate the GMM parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}=\{\pi_{k},\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}\}$ by iteratively maximising the data log-likelihood in the presence of the *latent* component-indicator variable $z$.

**E-step:** Compute the posterior responsibility for every $(n,k)$:

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_{k}\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}\pi_{j}\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{j},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{j})}$$

**M-step for $\pi_{k}$:** Using the soft-count $N_{k}=\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{nk})$,

$$\pi_{k}^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_{k}}{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})$$

**(1 mark — role of EM, 1 mark — E-step, 1 mark — M-step for $\pi_{k}$.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`
> **(a)** Derive the update equation for the mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$ of the $k^{\text{th}}$ component in a Gaussian Mixture Model under the EM algorithm. Clearly state the E-step quantity that feeds into this update. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** Given a 1-D dataset $X = \{1, 2, 3, 8, 9, 10\}$ and a GMM with $K=2$ components. At iteration $t$ the parameters are:
> - Component 1: $\pi_{1}=0.5$, $\mu_{1}=2$, $\sigma_{1}^{2}=1$
> - Component 2: $\pi_{2}=0.5$, $\mu_{2}=9$, $\sigma_{2}^{2}=1$
> 
> Compute the E-step responsibilities $\gamma(z_{n1})$ for the data point $x=3$ and $x=8$. Based on these, which cluster is more "uncertain" (i.e. has $\gamma$ closer to $0.5$)? (7 marks)

### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Derivation of the $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$ update (7 marks):**

The expected complete-data log-likelihood given the current responsibilities is:

$$Q(\boldsymbol{\theta},\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\gamma(z_{nk})\!\left[\ln\pi_{k} + \ln\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k})\right]$$

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Expand the Gaussian log and drop constants not depending on $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$:

$$Q_{\mu} = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})\,(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k})^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}) + \text{const}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Set $\dfrac{\partial Q_{\mu}}{\partial\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}} = \mathbf{0}$ and use the standard derivative identity $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial\boldsymbol{\mu}}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top}\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}) = -2\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Left-multiply by $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}$, define $N_{k}=\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{nk})$, and solve for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$:

$$\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}) = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_{k}}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})\,\mathbf{x}_{n}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — *E-step quantity required:* the responsibility $\gamma(z_{nk})$, which is the posterior probability $p(z_{k}=1\mid\mathbf{x}_{n})$ computed using Bayes' theorem at the current parameter values.

---

**(b) Numerical responsibility computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — Recall the responsibility formula:

$$\gamma(z_{n1}) = \frac{\pi_{1}\,\mathcal{N}(x_{n}\mid\mu_{1},\sigma_{1}^{2})}{\pi_{1}\,\mathcal{N}(x_{n}\mid\mu_{1},\sigma_{1}^{2}) + \pi_{2}\,\mathcal{N}(x_{n}\mid\mu_{2},\sigma_{2}^{2})}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — For $x=3$:

$$\mathcal{N}(3\mid 2,1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(3-2)^{2}}{2}\right] = \frac{e^{-0.5}}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.2420$$

$$\mathcal{N}(3\mid 9,1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(3-9)^{2}}{2}\right] = \frac{e^{-18}}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 5.89\times 10^{-9}$$

Numerator $= 0.5 \times 0.2420 = 0.1210$. Denominator $\approx 0.1210 + 2.94\times 10^{-9} \approx 0.1210$. Therefore:

$$\gamma(z_{31}) \approx \frac{0.1210}{0.1210} \approx 1.000$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — For $x=8$:

$$\mathcal{N}(8\mid 2,1) = \frac{e^{-18}}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 5.89\times 10^{-9}$$

$$\mathcal{N}(8\mid 9,1) = \frac{e^{-0.5}}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.2420$$

$$\gamma(z_{81}) \approx \frac{2.94\times 10^{-9}}{0.1210} \approx 2.43\times 10^{-8} \approx 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — Conclusion: Both points are essentially **certain** (responsibilities are $0$ or $1$). Point $x=3$ is *fully* assigned to component 1, and $x=8$ is *fully* assigned to component 2. Neither is genuinely "uncertain" because the two means are well-separated relative to the unit variances — the data is **easily clusterable** in this configuration. A point would be uncertain only if it lies near the midpoint between means *and* the Gaussians have wide overlap.

---

> ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **(a)** Explain why direct maximisation of the GMM log-likelihood using partial derivatives is intractable, and describe how the EM algorithm circumvents this problem using the concept of *latent variables*. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** For a 2-D dataset the EM algorithm has produced the following responsibilities $\gamma(z_{nk})$ for $N=4$ data points and $K=2$ components:
> 
> | Point n | $\gamma(z_{n1})$ | $\gamma(z_{n2})$ | $\mathbf{x}_{n}$ (column 1) | $\mathbf{x}_{n}$ (column 2) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 0.8 | 0.2 | 1 | 2 |
> | 2 | 0.7 | 0.3 | 2 | 1 |
> | 3 | 0.2 | 0.8 | 8 | 9 |
> | 4 | 0.1 | 0.9 | 9 | 8 |
> 
> Compute the updated mean vectors $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1}^{\text{new}}$ and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{2}^{\text{new}}$ using the M-step equation. (7 marks)

### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Why direct maximisation is intractable (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — The GMM log-likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N}\ln\!\left[\sum_{k=1}^{K}\pi_{k}\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k})\right]$$

The logarithm acts on a **sum**, not on the Gaussian itself. Taking the partial derivative w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$:

$$\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N}\frac{\pi_{k}\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}\pi_{j}\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{j},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{j})}\,\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — This derivative involves $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$ in **every** Gaussian (through the sum in the denominator). Therefore, setting $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}=0$ gives a system of *coupled, non-linear* equations in $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1},\dots,\boldsymbol{\mu}_{K}\}$ with **no closed-form solution**. Identical difficulty arises for $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}$ and $\pi_{k}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark)** — The fundamental reason is the **missing data**: we never observe which component generated $\mathbf{x}_{n}$. The latent variable $z_{nk}$ is unknown.

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — The EM algorithm circumvents this by:
- **E-step:** *Imputing* the missing $z_{nk}$ using the posterior $\gamma(z_{nk})$ given the current parameters.
- **M-step:** Maximising the *expected complete-data* log-likelihood $Q(\boldsymbol{\theta},\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$, in which the $\ln$ now sits **outside** the sum and the equations for each component are **decoupled**. Each M-step has a clean closed-form solution.

---

**(b) M-step mean update computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Recall the M-step equation:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_{k}}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})\,\mathbf{x}_{n}, \quad \text{where} \quad N_{k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N}\gamma(z_{nk})$$

**Step 2 (1 mark)** — Compute the soft-counts:

$$N_{1} = 0.8 + 0.7 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 1.8$$

$$N_{2} = 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.8 + 0.9 = 2.2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Compute the weighted first coordinate sum for component 1:

$$\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{n1})\,x_{n,1} = 0.8(1) + 0.7(2) + 0.2(8) + 0.1(9) = 0.8 + 1.4 + 1.6 + 0.9 = 4.7$$

$$\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{n1})\,x_{n,2} = 0.8(2) + 0.7(1) + 0.2(9) + 0.1(8) = 1.6 + 0.7 + 1.8 + 0.8 = 4.9$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — Therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_{1}^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{4.7}{1.8},\; \frac{4.9}{1.8}\right) \approx (2.611,\; 2.722)$$

**Step 5 (2 marks)** — For component 2:

$$\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{n2})\,x_{n,1} = 0.2(1) + 0.3(2) + 0.8(8) + 0.9(9) = 0.2 + 0.6 + 6.4 + 8.1 = 15.3$$

$$\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{n2})\,x_{n,2} = 0.2(2) + 0.3(1) + 0.8(9) + 0.9(8) = 0.4 + 0.3 + 7.2 + 7.2 = 15.1$$

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_{2}^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{15.3}{2.2},\; \frac{15.1}{2.2}\right) \approx (6.955,\; 6.864)$$

The two means are well-separated, confirming that the EM iterations are correctly pushing each component toward its natural data cluster.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on GMM/EM questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mixing-coefficient constraint** $\sum_{k}\pi_{k}=1$ when writing the GMM density. ($-1$ mark.)
> 2. **Confusing the E-step with the M-step** — the E-step computes $\gamma(z_{nk})$ using *current* parameters; the M-step updates the parameters using *fixed* $\gamma$. Do not mix the two. ($-2$ marks.)
> 3. **Forgetting to use the updated mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}}$** when computing the new covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}^{\text{new}}$ in the M-step. ($-1$ mark.)
> 4. **Sign error** in the Gaussian derivative: the sign of $(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k})$ flips when moving from gradient to zero. ($-1$ to $-2$ marks.)
> 5. **Skipping the normalisation** of $\gamma(z_{nk})$ in numerical problems — always divide by the sum over $k$. ($-1$ mark.)
> 6. **Treating K-means as identical to GMM** in viva/short-answer questions — K-means is the *hard-assignment, spherical-variance limit* of GMM, not the same algorithm. State the distinction explicitly for full credit. ($-1$ mark.)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (memorise these before the exam):**
> - GMM = weighted sum of $K$ multivariate Gaussians: $p(\mathbf{x})=\sum_{k}\pi_{k}\,\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k})$.
> - $\pi_{k}\geq 0$, $\sum_{k}\pi_{k}=1$ — the *priors* over the latent component.
> - Latent variable $z$ is a 1-of-$K$ indicator; $\pi_{k}=p(z_{k}=1)$.
> - EM alternates: **E-step** computes responsibilities $\gamma(z_{nk})$ via Bayes; **M-step** updates $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k},\pi_{k}$ in closed form.
> - E-step: $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \pi_{k}\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}) / \sum_{j}\pi_{j}\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{n}\mid\boldsymbol{\mu}_{j},\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{j})$.
> - M-step mean: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}} = \tfrac{1}{N_{k}}\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{nk})\mathbf{x}_{n}$.
> - M-step covariance: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}^{\text{new}} = \tfrac{1}{N_{k}}\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{nk})(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}})(\mathbf{x}_{n}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}^{\text{new}})^{\top}$.
> - M-step mixing: $\pi_{k}^{\text{new}} = N_{k}/N$, where $N_{k}=\sum_{n}\gamma(z_{nk})$.
> - EM guarantees **non-decreasing** log-likelihood; convergence to a **local** (not global) maximum.
> - K-means = GMM with $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{k}=\varepsilon\mathbf{I}$ and *hard* (0/1) responsibilities.
> - Choose $K$ via **BIC** or **AIC** (minimise), or via cross-validated held-out likelihood.
> - Common failures: **singular covariance** (component collapses on one point — add a regulariser $\varepsilon\mathbf{I}$); **poor initialisation** (use K-means to seed $\boldsymbol{\mu}_{k}$).
> - Standard stop criterion: $\vert\mathcal{L}^{(t+1)}-\mathcal{L}^{(t)}\vert < 10^{-4}$ or a max of 100–500 iterations.
> - Applications: speech modelling, image segmentation, anomaly detection, generative sampling, codebook design in vector quantisation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Parameter Methods - Non-Parametric Techniques for Density Estimation - Parzen-Window Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Parameter Methods - Non-Parametric Techniques for Density Estimation - Parzen-Window Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Parzen-Window Method** (also called the Parzen-Rosenblatt Window Method) is a fundamental non-parametric technique used to estimate the unknown probability density function $p(\mathbf{x})$ of a random variable from a finite set of samples $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ drawn from that distribution, without assuming any specific parametric form (such as Gaussian or exponential).

Mathematically, the Parzen-Window density estimator is defined as:

$$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

where $n$ is the total number of training samples, $D$ is the dimensionality of the feature space, $h > 0$ is the **window width** (also called the smoothing parameter or bandwidth), and $\varphi(\cdot)$ is the **kernel (window) function** that determines the shape and influence of each sample point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEAET755 Module 3):**
> Non-parametric methods make **no assumption** about the underlying distribution. The Parzen-Window technique is a *kernel-based density estimator* (KDE) where each training sample is replaced by a "bump" (window) of fixed width, and the resulting estimate is the normalized sum of all these bumps.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Casting Votes with a Flashlight"**
> Imagine you are in a dark, crowded hall and you cannot see the people. To figure out where the crowd is densest, you shine a circular flashlight of radius $h$ on the floor at any point $\mathbf{x}$ and count how many people fall inside that circle. Divide that count by the total number of people ($n$) and the area of the flashlight beam ($h^2$ in 2-D, or $h^D$ in general). The result is the estimated crowd density at that point.
> - The **flashlight beam** = the Parzen window $\varphi(\cdot)$ of width $h$.
> - The **people** = the training samples $\mathbf{x}_i$.
> - The **crowded area** = high estimated density $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$.
> - A **narrower beam (small $h$)** = sharp, jagged estimate (overfitting).
> - A **wider beam (large $h$)** = smooth, blurred estimate (underfitting).

This makes the Parzen-Window method a *voting scheme* — every training sample "votes" for the density at nearby points, and the votes are aggregated and normalized.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Non-parametric** — The number of parameters grows with $n$ (the number of samples), unlike Gaussian models where the parameters $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ are fixed in number.
- **Kernel Function $\varphi(\mathbf{u})$** — A non-negative, real-valued function that integrates to one. It defines the shape of the influence of a single sample.
- **Window Width (Bandwidth) $h$** — Controls the smoothness of the estimate. It is the *single most important* hyper-parameter.
- **Vol $V_n$** — The volume enclosed by a window of width $h$, equal to $h^D$ for hypercubic windows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Parzen-Window Method

The Parzen-Window density estimator works in a sequence of well-defined steps:

1. **Receive a set of $n$ unlabeled training samples** drawn i.i.d. from an unknown density $p(\mathbf{x})$.
2. **Choose a window function $\varphi(\mathbf{u})$** — commonly a hypercube (rectangular), Gaussian, or Epanechnikov kernel. The function must satisfy:
   $$\varphi(\mathbf{u}) \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \int \varphi(\mathbf{u}) \, d\mathbf{u} = 1$$
3. **Choose a window width $h > 0$** — this controls the trade-off between bias and variance.
4. **Center a window of width $h$ at every training sample** $\mathbf{x}_i$. The $i$-th window is:
   $$\varphi_h(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i) = \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$
5. **Sum the contributions** of all windows and divide by $n$ to obtain the estimate:
   $$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$
6. **Evaluate at the query point** $\mathbf{x}$ of interest to obtain $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$.

### 2.2 Standard Window Functions

| Kernel Name | Mathematical Form $\varphi(u)$ | Properties |
|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular (Parzen)** | $\varphi(u) = 1$ if $\vert u_j \vert \leq 1/2$ for all $j$, else $0$ | Simplest, discontinuous, original Parzen choice |
| **Gaussian** | $\varphi(u) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-u^2 / 2}$ | Smooth, infinitely differentiable, most popular in practice |
| **Epanechnikov** | $\varphi(u) = \dfrac{3}{4}(1 - u^2)$ if $\vert u \vert \leq 1$, else $0$ | Optimal in mean-squared-error sense, compact support |
| **Triangular** | $\varphi(u) = 1 - \vert u \vert$ if $\vert u \vert \leq 1$, else $0$ | Linear, compact support |

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor $1/h^D$?**
> When we scale the argument of $\varphi$ by $1/h$, the window becomes $h$ times wider. To keep the total area under the window equal to 1 (so that $\hat{p}$ remains a valid probability density), we must divide by the scaling factor $h^D$, where $D$ is the number of dimensions. This guarantees:
> $$\int \hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **How is it non-parametric?**
> Each sample contributes an independent bump; hence the model has $n$ degrees of freedom. There is no fixed parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ to learn. The only tunable hyper-parameter is $h$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Statement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Estimator** | $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{h^D} \varphi\!\left(\dfrac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$ |
| 2 | **Hypervolume of Window** | $V_n = h^D$ |
| 3 | **Expected Value (Mean)** | $E[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \int \dfrac{1}{h^D} \varphi\!\left(\dfrac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{v}}{h}\right) p(\mathbf{v}) \, d\mathbf{v}$ |
| 4 | **Mean-Squared Error** | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$ |
| 5 | **Consistency Condition** | $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} \hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x})$ iff $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} h(n) = 0$ and $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} n h^D(n) = \infty$ |
| 6 | **Bias for Rectangular Window** | $\text{Bias} \approx \dfrac{h^2}{24} \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x}) + O(h^4)$ |
| 7 | **Variance for Rectangular Window** | $\text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] \approx \dfrac{1}{n h^D} p(\mathbf{x}) \varphi(0)$ |
| 8 | **Number of Samples inside Window** | $k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varphi\!\left(\dfrac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$ |
| 9 | **Simplified Estimator (using $k$)** | $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{k/n}{V_n} = \dfrac{k}{n h^D}$ |
| 10 | **PDF Constraint** | $\int \hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} = 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> The Parzen-Window method is the foundation of modern **Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)**, used in anomaly detection, image segmentation, biometric systems (face/fingerprint recognition), and **non-parametric classifiers** like the $k_n$-nearest-neighbour rule. In KTU 2024 examinations, this topic is *high-yield* — questions appear in both Part A (definitions) and Part B (derivations + numericals).

### 2.5 Convergence and Asymptotic Properties

The Parzen estimator is a **pointwise consistent estimator** of $p(\mathbf{x})$ when the window width $h$ is made to depend on $n$ (written $h_n$) such that the two simultaneous limits hold:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} h_n(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} n h_n^D(\mathbf{x}) = \infty$$

The first condition ensures the **bias** tends to zero, while the second ensures the **variance** tends to zero. Together, they guarantee that the mean-squared error $\to 0$.

**Practical Bandwidth Choices:**
- $h_n \propto n^{-1/(D+4)}$ — Silverman's rule of thumb for Gaussian KDE (minimises asymptotic MISE).
- $h_n \propto n^{-1/D}$ — preserves probability mass (each window must contain enough samples on average).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Parzen-Window Estimator from First Principles

The Parzen-Window estimator is derived by approximating the underlying density $p(\mathbf{x})$ via a small region $R$ of volume $V$ centred at $\mathbf{x}$. The probability that a sample falls inside $R$ is:

$$P = \int_{R} p(\mathbf{x}') \, d\mathbf{x}'$$

The expected number of samples inside $R$ is $k = nP$. The sample-fraction estimator of $P$ is $k/n$. If $p(\mathbf{x})$ is approximately constant over the small region $R$, then $P \approx p(\mathbf{x}) V$, so:

$$p(\mathbf{x}) \approx \frac{k/n}{V}$$

To convert this rough approximation into a smooth kernel estimator, we let $R$ be a hypercube of side $h$ centred at $\mathbf{x}$, so $V = h^D$. We define the indicator-like **unit hypercube function**:

$$\varphi(\mathbf{u}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert u_j \vert \leq \tfrac{1}{2} \text{ for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, D \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Now define the kernel-scaled function as:

$$\varphi_h(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i) = \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

Substituting the indicator of the $i$-th sample falling inside the window into the density estimate formula:

$$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$

This is the **Parzen-Window density estimator**. The window width $h$ controls smoothness.

### 3.2 Proof that $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$ is a Valid Probability Density

We must verify non-negativity and unit integral. Since $\varphi(\cdot) \geq 0$ by construction, $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ trivially. For the unit-integral property:

$$\int \hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \int \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right) d\mathbf{x}$$

Apply the substitution $\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i)/h$, so $d\mathbf{x} = h^D \, d\mathbf{u}$:

$$\int \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi(\mathbf{u}) \, h^D \, d\mathbf{u} = \int \varphi(\mathbf{u}) \, d\mathbf{u} = 1$$

Therefore the integral over the sum reduces to $1 \cdot (1/n) \cdot n = 1$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Bias Derivation for the Rectangular Window

Let the true density $p(\mathbf{x})$ be twice continuously differentiable. Taylor-expand $p(\mathbf{v})$ around $\mathbf{x}$ in the integral form of the expected estimator:

$$p(\mathbf{v}) = p(\mathbf{x}) + (\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x})^T \nabla p(\mathbf{x}) + \tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x})^T \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x}) (\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x}) + \ldots$$

The expected value of the Parzen estimate is:

$$E[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \int \frac{1}{h^D} \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{v}}{h}\right) p(\mathbf{v}) \, d\mathbf{v}$$

Performing the change of variable $\mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{v})/h$ and substituting the Taylor series:

$$E[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \int \varphi(\mathbf{u}) \left[ p(\mathbf{x}) - h \mathbf{u}^T \nabla p(\mathbf{x}) + \tfrac{h^2}{2} \mathbf{u}^T \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{u} + \ldots \right] d\mathbf{u}$$

Using the symmetry of the rectangular window ($\int u_j \varphi(\mathbf{u}) d\mathbf{u} = 0$) and the moment $\int u_j^2 \varphi(\mathbf{u}) d\mathbf{u} = 1/12$:

$$E[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = p(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{h^2}{24} \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x}) + O(h^4)$$

Hence the **bias** is:

$$\text{Bias}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = E[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] - p(\mathbf{x}) \approx \frac{h^2}{24} \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x})$$

This shows that bias scales as $O(h^2)$ and vanishes as $h \to 0$.

### 3.4 Variance Derivation for the Rectangular Window

Since the samples are i.i.d. and the contribution of each sample to the estimator is independent:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = \frac{1}{n} \text{Var}\!\left[\frac{1}{h^D} \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)\right]$$

For a rectangular window where $\varphi(\mathbf{u}) \in \{0, 1\}$, this is essentially a Bernoulli variable. The variance of each indicator is:

$$\text{Var}\!\left[\frac{1}{h^D} \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)\right] \approx \frac{1}{h^{2D}} \cdot h^D \, p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{h^D}$$

Thus:

$$\text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] \approx \frac{1}{n h^D} p(\mathbf{x}) \varphi(0) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{n h^D}$$

This shows that variance scales as $O(1/(n h^D))$ and vanishes as $n h^D \to \infty$.

### 3.5 Mean-Squared Error (MSE) Synthesis

The MSE combines the two:

$$\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} \approx \frac{h^4}{576} \left[\nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x})\right]^2 + \frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{n h^D}$$

Optimising with respect to $h$ (setting $d(\text{MSE})/dh = 0$) gives the **optimal bandwidth**:

$$h^* \propto \left[\frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{n \left[\nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x})\right]^2}\right]^{\frac{1}{D+4}}$$

Substituting back gives the **optimal convergence rate**:

$$\text{MSE}^* = O\!\left(n^{-\frac{4}{D+4}}\right)$$

This rate depends on the dimensionality $D$ — a manifestation of the **curse of dimensionality**.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (1-D, Rectangular Window)

**Problem:** Given $n = 5$ one-dimensional samples $\mathcal{D} = \{2, 3, 4, 7, 9\}$, estimate the density at $\mathbf{x} = 5$ using a rectangular Parzen window of width $h = 2$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute the window volume in 1-D: $V_n = h^1 = 2$.

**Step 2** — For each sample, evaluate the indicator: $\varphi((5 - x_i)/2) = 1$ if $\vert (5 - x_i)/2 \vert \leq 1/2$, else $0$.

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $(5 - x_i)/2$ | $\vert (5 - x_i)/2 \vert$ | $\leq 0.5$? | $\varphi$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 1.5 | 1.5 | No | 0 |
| 2 | 3 | 1.0 | 1.0 | No | 0 |
| 3 | 4 | 0.5 | 0.5 | Yes | 1 |
| 4 | 7 | -1.0 | 1.0 | No | 0 |
| 5 | 9 | -2.0 | 2.0 | No | 0 |

**Step 3** — Count the inside samples: $k = 1$ (only $x_3 = 4$).

**Step 4** — Apply the estimator formula:

$$\hat{p}(5) = \frac{1}{n h} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varphi\!\left(\frac{5 - x_i}{h}\right) = \frac{1}{5 \cdot 2} \cdot 1 = 0.1$$

**Final Answer:** $\hat{p}(5) = 0.1$.

This is the density value at $\mathbf{x} = 5$ using the Parzen-Window method.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Parzen-Window Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Samples x1, x2, ..., xn] --> B[Choose Kernel Function phi]
    B --> C[Choose Window Width h]
    C --> D[Compute Window Volume Vn = h to the D]
    D --> E[Center Window at Each Sample xi]
    E --> F[Evaluate phi of x minus xi over h]
    F --> G[Sum All Kernel Contributions]
    G --> H[Divide Sum by n times Vn]
    H --> I[Output Density Estimate p hat of x]
    I --> J{Is Convergence Criterion Met}
    J -->|Yes| K[Final Density p hat of x]
    J -->|No| C
    K --> L[Apply to Classification or Anomaly Detection]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Window Width on Density Estimate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small h Value] --> B[High Variance, Low Bias]
    B --> C[Spiky, Over-fitted Estimate]
    D[Large h Value] --> E[Low Variance, High Bias]
    E --> F[Smooth, Under-fitted Estimate]
    G[Optimal h via Silverman Rule] --> H[Balanced Bias Variance Tradeoff]
    H --> I[Smooth, Generalisable Estimate]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Bias-Variance Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mean Squared Error of p hat] --> B[Bias Squared Term]
    A --> C[Variance Term]
    B --> D[Scales as h to the 4]
    C --> E[Scales as 1 over n h to the D]
    D --> F[Decreases as h shrinks]
    E --> G[Increases as h shrinks]
    F --> H[Find Optimal h that Minimises MSE]
    G --> H
    H --> I[h star proportional to n to the power minus 1 over D plus 4]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship to $k$-Nearest Neighbour Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Non-Parametric Density Estimation] --> B[Parzen Window with Fixed h]
    A --> C[k Nearest Neighbour with Fixed k]
    B --> D[Variable Volume, Constant Width]
    C --> E[Variable Width, Constant Volume]
    D --> F[Choose h by Silverman Rule]
    E --> G[Choose k by Cross Validation]
    F --> H[Smooth Global Density]
    G --> I[Adaptive Local Density]
```

### 4.5 Summary of Functional Architecture

The Parzen-Window method operates as a **pipeline**:

1. **Input Stage** — Collect $n$ i.i.d. samples.
2. **Configuration Stage** — Select kernel $\varphi$ and bandwidth $h$.
3. **Computation Stage** — Evaluate the kernel-sum at the query point.
4. **Validation Stage** — Check the convergence criterion ($h \to 0$, $n h^D \to \infty$).
5. **Output Stage** — Deliver $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})$ for downstream tasks (classification, anomaly detection, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the Parzen-Window method for non-parametric density estimation. State the general formula.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Parzen-Window method is a non-parametric technique for estimating an unknown probability density $p(\mathbf{x})$ from a finite set of samples, without assuming any specific functional form for the distribution.
> The general estimator is:
> $$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$
> where $n$ is the number of samples, $h$ is the window width, $D$ is the dimensionality, and $\varphi$ is the kernel function. Each training sample contributes a "bump" of width $h$ centred at itself, and the final estimate is the normalised sum of all such bumps. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the role of the window width $h$ in the Parzen-Window method? Mention the two conditions for consistency.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The window width $h$ (bandwidth) controls the smoothness of the density estimate. A small $h$ produces a spiky, high-variance estimate (overfitting), while a large $h$ produces a smooth, high-bias estimate (underfitting). The two conditions for pointwise consistency of the Parzen estimator are:
> 1. $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} h_n = 0$ (bias vanishes).
> 2. $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} n h_n^D = \infty$ (variance vanishes).
> Both conditions must hold simultaneously to ensure $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) \to p(\mathbf{x})$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the Parzen-Window density estimator from first principles. Explain the role of the kernel function $\varphi(\mathbf{u})$ and justify the factor $1/h^D$ in the formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10\}$ ($n = 6$, $D = 1$), estimate the density at $\mathbf{x} = 4$ using a rectangular Parzen window of width $h = 2$. Also compute the estimate at $\mathbf{x} = 6$ with the same $h$. Comment on the variation. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
> Start from the probability that a sample falls inside a small region $R$ of volume $V$ centred at $\mathbf{x}$:
> $$P = \int_R p(\mathbf{x}') \, d\mathbf{x}'$$
> Approximate $P \approx p(\mathbf{x}) V$ assuming $p$ is locally constant. Use the sample-fraction estimator $P \approx k/n$, where $k$ is the number of samples inside $R$. Hence:
> $$p(\mathbf{x}) \approx \frac{k/n}{V}$$
> Take $R$ as a hypercube of side $h$ so that $V = h^D$. Define the unit hypercube kernel $\varphi(\mathbf{u}) = 1$ if $\vert u_j \vert \leq 1/2$ for all $j$, else $0$. Then:
> $$\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{h^D} \, \varphi\!\left(\frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$$
> The factor $1/h^D$ compensates for the scaling of the kernel argument: when we replace $\mathbf{u}$ by $\mathbf{u}/h$, the volume of the support of the window grows by $h^D$. To preserve the unit-area property of a probability density, we must divide by $h^D$. This guarantees $\int \hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} = 1$. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**
> **At $\mathbf{x} = 4$, $h = 2$:**
> Compute $(4 - x_i)/2$ for each sample:
>
> | $i$ | $x_i$ | $(4 - x_i)/2$ | $\vert \cdot \vert$ | $\leq 0.5$? | $\varphi$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 1 | 1.5 | 1.5 | No | 0 |
> | 2 | 2 | 1.0 | 1.0 | No | 0 |
> | 3 | 3 | 0.5 | 0.5 | Yes | 1 |
> | 4 | 5 | -0.5 | 0.5 | Yes | 1 |
> | 5 | 8 | -2.0 | 2.0 | No | 0 |
> | 6 | 10 | -3.0 | 3.0 | No | 0 |
>
> $k = 2$, so $\hat{p}(4) = \dfrac{2}{6 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$. **(3 marks)**
>
> **At $\mathbf{x} = 6$, $h = 2$:**
>
> | $i$ | $x_i$ | $(6 - x_i)/2$ | $\vert \cdot \vert$ | $\leq 0.5$? | $\varphi$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 1 | 2.5 | 2.5 | No | 0 |
> | 2 | 2 | 2.0 | 2.0 | No | 0 |
> | 3 | 3 | 1.5 | 1.5 | No | 0 |
> | 4 | 5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | Yes | 1 |
> | 5 | 8 | -1.0 | 1.0 | No | 0 |
> | 6 | 10 | -2.0 | 2.0 | No | 0 |
>
> $k = 1$, so $\hat{p}(6) = \dfrac{1}{6 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833$. **(3 marks)**
>
> **Comment:** $\hat{p}(4) > \hat{p}(6)$ because the sample density around 4 (samples 3 and 5) is higher than around 6 (only sample 5). This is consistent with the underlying empirical distribution. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the bias-variance decomposition of the Parzen-Window estimator for a rectangular window. Derive expressions for the bias and variance, and state the optimal bandwidth $h^*$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Parzen-Window method with the $k$-Nearest-Neighbour (k-NN) method for density estimation. State at least three differences in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**
> The mean-squared error of the estimator at a point $\mathbf{x}$ is:
> $$\text{MSE}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] = E\!\left[(\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) - p(\mathbf{x}))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] + \text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})]$$
> **Bias derivation:** Using a Taylor expansion of $p(\mathbf{v})$ around $\mathbf{x}$ and exploiting the symmetry of the rectangular window:
> $$\text{Bias}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] \approx \frac{h^2}{24} \nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x}) + O(h^4)$$
> This bias tends to zero as $h \to 0$. **(2 marks)**
> **Variance derivation:** For i.i.d. samples and a rectangular window:
> $$\text{Var}[\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})] \approx \frac{1}{n h^D} p(\mathbf{x})$$
> This variance tends to zero as $n h^D \to \infty$. **(2 marks)**
> **MSE synthesis:** $\text{MSE} \approx \dfrac{h^4}{576} [\nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x})]^2 + \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x})}{n h^D}$.
> Differentiating with respect to $h$ and setting to zero gives:
> $$h^* \propto \left[\frac{p(\mathbf{x})}{n [\nabla^2 p(\mathbf{x})]^2}\right]^{\frac{1}{D+4}}$$
> The corresponding optimal MSE convergence rate is $O(n^{-4/(D+4)})$, which is the *best achievable* rate for non-parametric density estimators. **(3 marks)**

> **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> | Aspect | Parzen-Window Method | $k$-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Tuning Parameter** | Window width $h$ (fixed volume) | Number of neighbours $k$ (fixed count) |
> | **Volume around $\mathbf{x}$** | $V_n = h^D$ — *constant* across the feature space | $V_k$ — *variable*, grows in low-density regions |
> | **Adaptivity** | Non-adaptive (uniform smoothing) | Adaptive (local smoothing) |
> | **Sample Efficiency** | Needs more samples in low-density areas | Naturally allocates resolution where data exists |
> | **Boundary Behaviour** | Biased near edges of support | Better at boundaries |
> | **Computational Cost** | $O(n)$ per query (sum over all samples) | $O(n \log n)$ per query (needs a KD-tree or sorting) |
> | **Effect of choice of parameter** | Small $h$ → spiky estimate; large $h$ → over-smooth | Small $k$ → noisy estimate; large $k$ → over-smooth |
> | **Statistical Consistency** | Yes, with $h_n \to 0$ and $n h_n^D \to \infty$ | Yes, with $k_n \to \infty$ and $k_n / n \to 0$ |
>
> **(7 marks — full table required; partial credit for 3–4 correct rows.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/h^D$ factor** — Many students write $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum \varphi((\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i)/h)$ without the volume-normalisation. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing $h$ and $h^D$** — In 1-D, the volume is $h$; in 2-D, it is $h^2$. Always state the dimensionality clearly. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Missing the two consistency conditions** — Only writing $\lim h_n = 0$ is incomplete; the second condition $\lim n h_n^D = \infty$ is mandatory. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Numerical errors in window evaluation** — In numerical questions, students often compute $(x - x_i)/h$ incorrectly or forget to take the absolute value. Double-check the indicator condition $\vert u_j \vert \leq 1/2$. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Writing the rectangular kernel in vector form** — Students sometimes forget that the rectangular kernel in $D$ dimensions requires $\vert u_j \vert \leq 1/2$ for *all* $D$ components. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Confusing bias and variance trends** — Many students state that as $h$ decreases, both bias and variance decrease. The correct statement is: bias decreases but variance *increases*. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Parzen-Window method** is a non-parametric KDE technique that places a kernel of width $h$ at every training sample.
- The general formula is $\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{h^D} \varphi\!\left(\dfrac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_i}{h}\right)$.
- The window width $h$ is the **single most critical** hyper-parameter; it controls the bias-variance trade-off.
- **Consistency conditions:** $h_n \to 0$ and $n h_n^D \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$.
- For a **rectangular** window: bias $\approx h^2 \nabla^2 p / 24$; variance $\approx p/(n h^D)$.
- **Optimal bandwidth** scales as $h^* \propto n^{-1/(D+4)}$ (Silverman's rule of thumb uses a constant multiplied by this).
- The estimator is a **valid PDF** because $\varphi \geq 0$ and $\int \varphi \, d\mathbf{u} = 1$.
- **Difference from k-NN:** Parzen fixes the *volume*, k-NN fixes the *count* $k$.
- **Practical kernels:** Gaussian (smoothest), Epanechnikov (optimal MSE), Rectangular (original Parzen).
- The method suffers from the **curse of dimensionality** — MSE decay rate is $n^{-4/(D+4)}$, which slows drastically as $D$ grows.
- **KTU 2024 exam tip:** Always (i) state the formula with $1/h^D$, (ii) mention both consistency conditions, and (iii) justify the factor $1/h^D$ in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-Nearest Neighbour Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** K-Nearest Neighbour Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN)** method is a fundamental, non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm used extensively in pattern recognition for both **classification** and **regression** tasks. Formally, given an unknown test pattern $\mathbf{x}_q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a labeled training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the KNN algorithm classifies $\mathbf{x}_q$ by identifying the $K$ training samples closest to $\mathbf{x}_q$ in the feature space (using a predefined distance metric) and assigning the class label based on the **majority vote** (or weighted vote) of these $K$ neighbours.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the KNN method is positioned as a cornerstone of **statistical pattern recognition**, where decisions are deferred until query time — a paradigm known as **lazy learning** or **memory-based learning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** KNN is a *non-parametric* technique — it makes **no a priori assumptions** about the underlying probability distribution of the data, $P(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$. This property is crucial in real-world pattern recognition where distributional forms are often unknown.

## 1.2 The Two Operational Modes of KNN

| Mode | Output Type | Decision Rule |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **KNN Classification** | Discrete class label $\omega_j$ | Majority voting among $K$ neighbours |
| **KNN Regression** | Continuous numerical value $\hat{y}$ | Mean or weighted mean of $K$ neighbour outputs |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Neighbourhood Council"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you have just moved into a new neighbourhood and you want to know whether the locality is safe. Instead of reading a crime statistics report, you simply **ask your 5 nearest neighbours** (K = 5). If 4 out of 5 say it is safe, you conclude the neighbourhood is safe. You did not use any complex formula; you simply relied on the **proximity and consensus of the closest examples**. This is precisely how KNN operates in feature space.

The algorithm embodies the philosophical principle: **"A pattern is judged by the company it keeps."** Patterns that are close in feature space (small inter-point distance) are presumed to belong to the same conceptual class.

## 1.4 Why KNN Matters in Pattern Recognition

- **Zero training phase:** No model parameters are learned a priori. The entire training set is stored verbatim.
- **Asymptotically optimal:** As $N \to \infty$ and $K \to \infty$ with $K/N \to 0$, the KNN error rate is bounded above by **twice the Bayes error rate**.
- **Universally applicable:** Works for both linearly separable and highly non-linear class boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cover and Hart (1967) Theorem:** The asymptotic error rate of the 1-NN classifier is bounded as:
> $$\lim_{N \to \infty} P(\text{error} \mid \text{1-NN}) \leq 2 \cdot P^*(\text{Bayes error})$$
> This is the most celebrated theoretical result in non-parametric pattern recognition and is a guaranteed KTU high-yield item.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Operational Steps

The KNN algorithm executes in two distinct phases: an **offline storage phase** and an **online query phase**.

### Phase 1 — Offline Storage (Training)
1. Receive the labeled training dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. **Store** every training vector along with its label in memory. No computation is performed at this stage.
3. Optionally apply **feature normalization** (e.g., min-max scaling or Z-score standardization) to prevent attribute dominance.

### Phase 2 — Online Classification (Query Time)
1. Receive the unknown query pattern $\mathbf{x}_q \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
2. **Compute the distance** $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ between $\mathbf{x}_q$ and *every* training point $\mathbf{x}_i$.
3. **Sort** the distances in ascending order: $d_{(1)} \leq d_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq d_{(N)}$.
4. **Select** the $K$ nearest indices: $\mathcal{N}_K = \{i : d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) \in \{d_{(1)}, \ldots, d_{(K)}\}\}$.
5. **Aggregate** the labels via the decision rule.
6. **Output** the predicted class $\hat{\omega}(\mathbf{x}_q)$.

## 2.2 Distance Metrics — The Heart of KNN

The choice of distance metric $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$ is the single most important hyperparameter in KNN. Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ and $\mathbf{x}' = (x_1', x_2', \ldots, x_n')^T$.

### 2.2.1 Euclidean Distance ($L_2$ Norm)
The straight-line "as-the-crow-flies" distance — the most common choice.

$$d_E(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( x_j - x_j' \right)^2}$$

### 2.2.2 Manhattan / City-Block Distance ($L_1$ Norm)
Sum of absolute coordinate differences; useful when movement is constrained to a grid.

$$d_M(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - x_j' \vert$$

### 2.2.3 Minkowski Distance ($L_p$ Norm)
A generalized metric that subsumes Euclidean ($p=2$) and Manhattan ($p=1$) as special cases.

$$d_{L_p}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - x_j' \vert^p \right)^{\frac{1}{p}}, \quad p \geq 1$$

### 2.2.4 Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$ Norm)
The maximum coordinate-wise difference; useful in chess-board-like movement.

$$d_C(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \max_{j \in \{1, \ldots, n\}} \vert x_j - x_j' \vert$$

### 2.2.5 Mahalanobis Distance
A scale-invariant, correlation-aware metric that accounts for the covariance structure $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ of the data.

$$d_{Ma}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}')^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}')}$$

### 2.2.6 Cosine Distance
Measures angular dissimilarity; widely used in high-dimensional text and image pattern recognition.

$$d_{cos}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}'}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert_2 \, \Vert \mathbf{x}' \Vert_2}$$

## 2.3 Decision Rules

### 2.3.1 Majority Voting (Unweighted)
The query point is assigned to the class with the highest frequency among the $K$ neighbours.

$$\hat{\omega}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \arg\max_{j} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function, equal to **1** if the argument is true and **0** otherwise.

### 2.3.2 Distance-Weighted Voting
To reduce the influence of distant neighbours, each vote is weighted inversely by its distance.

$$w_i = \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) + \epsilon}, \quad \hat{\omega}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \arg\max_{j} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small positive constant (typically $10^{-6}$) to prevent division by zero.

### 2.3.3 KNN Regression Rule
For continuous-valued outputs, the prediction is the weighted average:

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \frac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} w_i}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - x_j')^2}$ | Continuous feature space |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance | $d_M = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - x_j' \vert$ | Grid-constrained movement |
| 3 | Minkowski ($L_p$) | $d_{L_p} = (\sum \vert x_j - x_j' \vert^p)^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ |
| 4 | Chebyshev | $d_C = \max_j \vert x_j - x_j' \vert$ | $L_\infty$ norm |
| 5 | Mahalanobis | $d_{Ma} = \sqrt{(\Delta\mathbf{x})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\Delta\mathbf{x})}$ | Requires covariance $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ |
| 6 | Cosine | $d_{cos} = 1 - \cos\theta$ | Scale-invariant |
| 7 | Majority Vote | $\hat{\omega} = \arg\max_j \sum \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$ | Discrete output |
| 8 | Weighted Vote | $\hat{\omega} = \arg\max_j \sum w_i \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$ | $w_i = 1/(d_i + \epsilon)$ |
| 9 | KNN Regression | $\hat{y} = \sum w_i y_i \,/\, \sum w_i$ | Continuous output |
| 10 | Asymptotic Error | $P_e \leq 2 P^*_{Bayes}$ | Cover-Hart bound |
| 11 | Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot n)$ per query | Linear in dataset size |
| 12 | Space Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot n)$ | Stores all training data |
| 13 | Optimal K rule | $K \approx \sqrt{N}$ | Heuristic starting point |
| 14 | Volume of $n$-ball | $V_n(r) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma(n/2 + 1)} r^n$ | Used in density estimation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | KNN Use Case |
|--------------------|--------------|
| **Biomedical Engineering** | ECG/EEG arrhythmia classification, tumor detection in MRI |
| **Remote Sensing** | Land-cover classification from satellite multispectral imagery |
| **Computer Vision** | Handwritten digit recognition (early MNIST benchmarks) |
| **Speech Recognition** | Phoneme classification in acoustic feature space |
| **Cybersecurity** | Anomaly / intrusion detection in network traffic |
| **Recommender Systems** | Collaborative filtering based on user-item similarity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "lazy"?** KNN is termed a *lazy learner* because it performs **no generalization during training**. All computational effort is deferred to query time, which is why KNN is often called a **memory-based** or **instance-based** learner. This contrasts sharply with *eager learners* like the Perceptron or Bayesian classifiers, which build an explicit discriminant model upfront.

## 2.6 The Bias-Variance Trade-off in Choosing K

| Choice of $K$ | Bias | Variance | Decision Boundary |
|---------------|------|----------|-------------------|
| $K = 1$ | Low | **High** | Highly irregular, overfits |
| $K = N$ | **High** | Low | Constant — entire space assigned to majority class |
| Optimal $K$ | Balanced | Balanced | Smooth, captures true class structure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A question that simply asks *"What happens when $K=1$ vs $K=N$?"* tests whether the student understands the bias-variance dichotomy. A common KTU framing: *"Justify the choice of K in KNN with reference to overfitting and underfitting."*

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Cover-Hart Asymptotic Bound

The celebrated Cover-Hart (1967) result states that the asymptotic error of the 1-NN rule is bounded above by **twice the Bayes error rate**. We present a rigorous sketch.

### Step 1: Define the Setup
Let $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ be a random pattern drawn from an unknown mixture density, and let $\omega^*(\mathbf{x})$ denote the **Bayes optimal class** (the one maximizing the posterior $P(\omega_j \mid \mathbf{x})$). Define the Bayes error at $\mathbf{x}$:

$$P^*(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x}) = 1 - P(\omega^* \mid \mathbf{x})$$

### Step 2: Define the 1-NN Error
For the 1-NN classifier using $N$ i.i.d. training samples, the nearest neighbour $\mathbf{x}_{(1)}$ is selected. The 1-NN classifier predicts $\omega(\mathbf{x}_{(1)})$. Its conditional error at $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$P(\text{1-NN error} \mid \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_{(1)}) = 1 - P(\omega(\mathbf{x}_{(1)}) \mid \mathbf{x})$$

### Step 3: Take the Asymptotic Limit
As $N \to \infty$, the nearest neighbour $\mathbf{x}_{(1)} \to \mathbf{x}$ almost surely (provided the sampling density is non-zero at $\mathbf{x}$). Therefore:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} P(\omega(\mathbf{x}_{(1)}) \mid \mathbf{x}) = P(\omega(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x})$$

### Step 4: Apply the Union Bound
The probability that **both** the 1-NN and the Bayes classifier err is at most the Bayes error itself. Using the inequality:

$$P(A \cup B) \leq P(A) + P(B)$$

with $A$ = {1-NN error} and $B$ = {Bayes error}, we get:

$$P(\text{1-NN error} \mid \mathbf{x}) \leq 2 \cdot P^*(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x})$$

### Step 5: Integrate Over All $\mathbf{x}$
Averaging over the marginal distribution of $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{1-NN error}) &= \int P(\text{1-NN error} \mid \mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} \\
&\leq 2 \int P^*(\text{error} \mid \mathbf{x}) \, p(\mathbf{x}) \, d\mathbf{x} \\
&= 2 \cdot P^*(\text{Bayes error})
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the derivation:

$$\boxed{\;P_e(\text{1-NN}) \leq 2 \, P^*_{Bayes}\;}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Computing Distances and Applying Majority Vote

**Problem (KTU-Style):** Given the 2-D training set below and a query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 1)^T$, classify using $K = 3$ with Euclidean distance.

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| $\mathbf{x}_1$ | 1 | 1 | $\omega_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_2$ | 2 | 2 | $\omega_1$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_3$ | 3 | 1 | $\omega_2$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_4$ | 4 | 3 | $\omega_2$ |
| $\mathbf{x}_5$ | 1 | 3 | $\omega_1$ |

### Step 1: Compute Euclidean Distances to $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 1)$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_1) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0} = 1.00$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_2) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (1-2)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 1} = 1.00$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_3) = \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0} = 1.00$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_4) = \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$$

$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_5) = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$

### Step 2: Sort the Distances in Ascending Order

| Rank | Point | Distance | Class |
|------|-------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}_1$ | 1.00 | $\omega_1$ |
| 2 | $\mathbf{x}_2$ | 1.00 | $\omega_1$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{x}_3$ | 1.00 | $\omega_2$ |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}_5$ | 2.24 | $\omega_1$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{x}_4$ | 2.83 | $\omega_2$ |

### Step 3: Select the Top $K = 3$ Neighbours

The 3-NN set is $\mathcal{N}_3 = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \mathbf{x}_3\}$, containing two $\omega_1$ and one $\omega_2$.

### Step 4: Apply Majority Vote

$$\text{Vote}(\omega_1) = 2, \quad \text{Vote}(\omega_2) = 1$$

$$\hat{\omega}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \omega_1$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Distance-Weighted Voting

**Problem:** Reclassify the same query point using $K = 5$ with inverse-distance weighting (no majority-based rejection).

### Step 1: Compute Weights

$$w_1 = \frac{1}{1.00 + 0.001} = 0.999, \quad w_2 = \frac{1}{1.00 + 0.001} = 0.999$$

$$w_3 = \frac{1}{1.00 + 0.001} = 0.999, \quad w_4 = \frac{1}{2.83 + 0.001} \approx 0.353$$

$$w_5 = \frac{1}{2.24 + 0.001} \approx 0.446$$

### Step 2: Aggregate Weighted Scores

Let $S_j = \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(y_i = \omega_j)$.

$$S_1 = w_1 + w_2 + w_5 = 0.999 + 0.999 + 0.446 = 2.444$$

$$S_2 = w_3 + w_4 = 0.999 + 0.353 = 1.352$$

### Step 3: Decision

$$\hat{\omega}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \arg\max\{S_1, S_2\} = \arg\max\{2.444, 1.352\} = \omega_1$$

The prediction remains $\omega_1$ but with a stronger confidence margin.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rule Sensitivity

| Scenario | Best Metric | Justification |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| Continuous physical measurements (e.g., height, weight) | Euclidean ($L_2$) | Isotropic feature scales, no prior covariance info |
| Categorical / ordinal data | Hamming | Counts feature mismatches |
| High-dimensional sparse vectors (text) | Cosine | Angular separation matters, not magnitude |
| Correlated features (e.g., height & weight) | Mahalanobis | Removes redundancy via $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$ |
| Grid-aligned movement (urban routing) | Manhattan ($L_1$) | Reflects real movement constraints |
| Image pixel intensities (rotationally robust) | Chebyshev | Maximum deviation in any channel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In KTU examinations, a common trick question is to provide a dataset where features have *different units or scales* (e.g., age in years vs. income in lakhs). Without **normalization**, the Euclidean distance will be dominated by the larger-scale feature. Always normalize using Z-score:
> $$x_j^{\text{norm}} = \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$
> Failure to normalize is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation keys.

## 3.5 The Curse of Dimensionality

As the feature dimension $n$ grows, the volume of the unit hypersphere in $[0,1]^n$ shrinks exponentially. The fraction of the unit cube occupied by an inscribed hypersphere of radius 0.5 is:

$$f(n) = \frac{V_n(0.5)}{1^n} = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{2^n \cdot \Gamma(n/2 + 1)}$$

For $n = 2$: $f(2) = \pi/4 \approx 0.785$. For $n = 10$: $f(10) \approx 0.0025$. For $n = 20$: $f(20) \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$.

**Consequence:** In high dimensions, the $K$ nearest neighbours of $\mathbf{x}_q$ are *almost as far* as the $K$ farthest points. The notion of "nearness" loses discriminative power — a phenomenon Beyer et al. (1999) termed the **"curse of dimensionality"** that cripples KNN.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When asked *"Why does KNN fail in high dimensions?"*, your answer must reference: (1) distance concentration, (2) exponential growth of the unit-ball volume ratio, and (3) exponential data sparsity requiring infeasibly large $N$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: KNN Algorithm Operational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Training Set D] --> B[Store All N Training Patterns in Memory]
    B --> C[Apply Feature Normalization: Z-score or Min-Max]
    C --> D[End of Training Phase]
    D --> E[Receive Query Pattern xq]
    E --> F[Compute Distance d xq xi for all i in 1 to N]
    F --> G[Sort All Distances in Ascending Order]
    G --> H[Select the K Smallest Distances]
    H --> I{Weighted Voting?}
    I -- No --> J[Majority Vote: Count Labels Among K Neighbours]
    I -- Yes --> K[Assign Inverse Distance Weights: wi = 1 di plus epsilon]
    K --> L[Compute Weighted Sum per Class]
    J --> M[Argmax over Classes]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Output Predicted Class omega hat xq]
    N --> O[End of Query Phase]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a KNN System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OFFLINE[Offline Training Module]
        T1[Raw Training Data] --> T2[Preprocessing]
        T2 --> T3[Feature Normalization]
        T3 --> T4[Storage in Pattern Library]
    end
    
    subgraph ONLINE[Online Classification Module]
        Q1[Unknown Query xq] --> Q2[Preprocessing]
        Q2 --> Q3[Feature Normalization]
        Q3 --> Q4[Distance Computation Engine]
        T4 --> Q4
        Q4 --> Q5[Distance Sort and K-Selection]
        Q5 --> Q6[Label Aggregation Module]
        Q6 --> Q7[Decision Output: omega hat]
    end
    
    OFFLINE --> ONLINE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Boundary Evolution with K

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1["K = 1: Highly Jagged Boundary, Overfit, High Variance, Low Bias"] --> K2["K = 3: Smoother Boundary, Balanced"]
    K2 --> K3["K = 7: Even Smoother, Some Local Detail Lost"]
    K3 --> K4["K = N: Single Global Majority, Underfit, High Bias, Low Variance"]
    
    K1 -.->|Increase K| K2
    K2 -.->|Increase K| K3
    K3 -.->|Increase K| K4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: KNN Classification Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Query Point xq] --> D1[Compute d1: distance to x1]
    Q --> D2[Compute d2: distance to x2]
    Q --> D3[Compute d3: distance to x3]
    Q --> D4[Compute d4: distance to x4]
    Q --> D5[Compute d5: distance to x5]
    
    D1 --> S[Sort Distances]
    D2 --> S
    D3 --> S
    D4 --> S
    D5 --> S
    
    S --> SEL[Select K = 3 Nearest]
    SEL --> M[Majority Vote: Class omega 1 or omega 2]
    M --> OUT[Final Classification: omega hat]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:** The block diagrams above map the *information flow* in a KNN system. The key insight for KTU students is the **separation of offline storage from online computation** — this is the architectural hallmark distinguishing instance-based learners from parametric ones like the Bayes classifier or Perceptron.
