> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decision Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌳 MODULE 3 — DECISION TREES: KTU PREMIER STUDY NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Formal)**
> A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, hierarchical supervised learning model used for both classification and regression. It recursively partitions the feature space into axis-parallel regions by selecting, at each internal node, the attribute that yields the maximum **information gain** (or minimum impurity). The terminal nodes (leaves) store class labels or continuous output values.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy: "The Yes/No Flowchart of Life"

Imagine you are a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. You don't run every test at once. You start with the most informative question — *"Do you have a fever?"* — and based on the answer, you branch to the next most useful question — *"Do you have a cough?"* — and continue this **sequence of if-else splits** until you reach a final diagnosis.

A **Decision Tree** is precisely this:
- **Nodes** = Questions about features (e.g., "Is Humidity high?")
- **Branches** = Possible answers (Yes / No)
- **Leaves** = Final decisions / class labels

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Snapshot**
> Decision Trees are the *foundation* of ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost). For Pattern Recognition, you must master **ID3, C4.5, and CART** algorithms and the three core impurity measures: **Entropy, Gini Index, and Classification Error**.

### 📌 Key Properties of Decision Trees (KTU High-Yield Points)

| Property | Value / Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Non-parametric, supervised |
| **Splitting Axis** | Always parallel to feature axes (axis-parallel partitions) |
| **Time Complexity (Training)** | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot d \cdot \log n)$ where $n$ = samples, $d$ = features |
| **Interpretability** | **High** — white-box model |
| **Handles Non-linearity** | Yes (hierarchical splits) |
| **Sensitive To** | Small data perturbations, class imbalance |
| **Default Stopping Criteria** | Pure node, max depth, min samples per leaf |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Decision Trees for Pattern Recognition?**
> They naturally perform **feature selection** (top splits = most discriminative features), handle **mixed data types** (categorical + numerical), require **no feature scaling**, and produce **human-readable rules** — making them indispensable for medical, financial, and industrial PR applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Decision Tree

A decision tree consists of three component types:

1. **Root Node** — Contains the *entire training dataset*; chooses the best first attribute to split.
2. **Internal Nodes** — Decision points; each tests the value of a specific feature.
3. **Leaf (Terminal) Nodes** — Hold the class label (or output value) — *no further splitting*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Recursive Question at Every Node:**
> *"Which attribute, when split on, will produce child subsets that are as 'pure' (homogeneous) as possible?"*

This is formalized via an **impurity function** $i(N)$ that measures disorder at node $N$.

---

### 2.2 The Three Purity / Impurity Measures

#### (A) **Entropy (Information-Theoretic Measure — ID3 / C4.5)**

$$i(N) \;=\; H(S) \;=\; -\sum_{j=1}^{c} p_j \log_2 p_j$$

Where $p_j$ = proportion of class $j$ in node $N$, and $c$ = number of classes.

- $H = 0$ → Pure node (all samples same class)
- $H = 1$ → Maximum disorder (binary, 50-50 split)
- $H = \log_2 c$ → Uniform distribution over $c$ classes

#### (B) **Gini Index (CART Algorithm)**

$$i(N) \;=\; \text{Gini}(S) \;=\; 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{c} p_j^{\,2}$$

- Gini $= 0$ → Pure node
- Gini $= 1 - 1/c$ → Maximum impurity

#### (C) **Classification Error**

$$i(N) \;=\; 1 - \max_j(p_j)$$

- Used in pruning decisions (C4.5 uses a pessimistic error estimate derived from this).

---

### 2.3 Information Gain — The ID3 Splitting Criterion

For an attribute $A$ with $v$ possible values $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_v\}$ splitting dataset $S$ into subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_v$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Gain}(S, A) \;=\; H(S) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}\, H(S_i)\;}$$

> **The ID3 algorithm picks the attribute with the MAXIMUM Information Gain at each node.**

---

### 2.4 The Bias Problem & Gain Ratio (C4.5 Fix)

**Problem:** Information Gain is *biased* toward attributes with many distinct values (e.g., "Date" or "ID"). Such an attribute produces pure subsets trivially but has zero generalization power.

**Solution (Quinlan's C4.5):** Normalize the gain by the **intrinsic split information**:

$$H_A(S) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}\, \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{GainRatio}(S, A) \;=\; \frac{\text{Gain}(S, A)}{H_A(S)}\;}$$

> **C4.5 picks the attribute with the MAXIMUM Gain Ratio.**

---

### 2.5 Gini Gain (CART)

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \text{Gini}(S, A) \;=\; \text{Gini}(S) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}\, \text{Gini}(S_i)\;}$$

> **CART picks the attribute with the MAXIMUM reduction in Gini (or maximum Gini gain).**

---

### 2.6 Reduction in Variance (Regression Trees)

For continuous target $y$ in node $N$:

$$\text{Var}(N) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert N \vert} \sum_{i \in N} (y_i - \bar{y}_N)^2$$

The split criterion is **minimization of weighted post-split variance** — equivalent to maximizing variance reduction.

---

### 2.7 🧾 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield, Print This!)

| # | Concept | Formula | Range / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum p_j \log_2 p_j$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ in **bits** |
| 2 | **Gini Index** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum p_j^2$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ |
| 3 | **Classification Error** | $E(S) = 1 - \max(p_j)$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ |
| 4 | **Info Gain (ID3)** | $H(S) - \sum \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_i)$ | Higher is better |
| 5 | **Split Info (Intrinsic)** | $-\sum \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Higher = more fragmented |
| 6 | **Gain Ratio (C4.5)** | $\text{Gain} / H_A$ | Higher is better |
| 7 | **Gini Gain (CART)** | $\text{Gini}(S) - \sum \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Gini}(S_i)$ | Higher is better |
| 8 | **Variance Reduction** | $\text{Var}(S) - \sum \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Var}(S_i)$ | Higher is better (regression) |
| 9 | **Train Time** | $\mathcal{O}(n \, d \, \log n)$ | $n$ = samples, $d$ = features |
| 10 | **Predict Time** | $\mathcal{O}(\text{depth})$ | Depth $\leq \log_2 n$ ideally |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Trick:** If asked *"Which impurity function is used in CART?"* → Answer is **Gini Index**, *not* Entropy. CART = Classification And Regression Trees by **Breiman (1984)**. ID3/C4.5 = Quinlan (uses Entropy + Gain Ratio).

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Symptom → disease hierarchy (interpretable by doctors).
- **Credit Risk Scoring:** Loan approval/denial pipelines.
- **Fault Detection in Manufacturing:** Sensor thresholds in Industry 4.0.
- **Remote Sensing & PR:** Land-cover classification from satellite imagery.
- **Network Intrusion Detection:** Rule extraction from traffic patterns.
- **Embedded Systems:** Decision trees can be compiled into pure `if-else` C-code for microcontrollers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked-Out KTU Examination Problem:** *Construct a Decision Tree (ID3) for the "Play Tennis" classification problem using Information Gain.*

### 3.1 Given Dataset (14 Samples, 4 Attributes)

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play Tennis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| D2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| D3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| D4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| D5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| D6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| D7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| D8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| D9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| D10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| D11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| D12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| D13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| D14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

**Class distribution:** 9 Yes, 5 No → Total $N = 14$.

---

### 3.2 STEP 1 — Compute the Root Entropy $H(S)$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\frac{9}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \\[4pt]
&= -0.6429 \times \log_2(0.6429) - 0.3571 \times \log_2(0.3571) \\[4pt]
&= -0.6429 \times (-0.6374) - 0.3571 \times (-1.4854) \\[4pt]
&= 0.4098 + 0.5305 \\[4pt]
&= 0.9403 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 STEP 2 — Compute Information Gain for **Outlook**

**Subset sizes:** Sunny = 5, Overcast = 4, Rain = 5.

**Entropy of each subset:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Sunny}) &= -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) \\
&= -0.4 \times (-1.3219) - 0.6 \times (-0.7370) \\
&= 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Overcast}) &= -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{4}\right) - \frac{0}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{0}{4}\right) \\
&= 0 + 0 = 0 \quad (\text{Pure node!})
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Rain}) &= -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \\
&= 0.9710 \quad (\text{symmetric to Sunny})
\end{aligned}$$

**Weighted entropy after split:**

$$H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) = 0.6936$$

**Information Gain:**

$$\text{Gain}(\text{Outlook}) = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = 0.2467 \text{ bits}$$

---

### 3.4 STEP 3 — Compute Information Gain for **Humidity**

**Subset sizes:** High = 7, Normal = 7.

$$\begin{aligned}
H(\text{High}) &= -\frac{3}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{7}\right) - \frac{4}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{7}\right) \\
&= -0.4286 \times (-1.2224) - 0.5714 \times (-0.8074) \\
&= 0.5239 + 0.4613 = 0.9852
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Normal}) &= -\frac{6}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{7}\right) - \frac{1}{7}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{7}\right) \\
&= -0.8571 \times (-0.2224) - 0.1429 \times (-2.8074) \\
&= 0.1906 + 0.4011 = 0.5917
\end{aligned}$$

**Information Gain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) &= \frac{7}{14}(0.9852) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917) = 0.7885 \\
\text{Gain}(\text{Humidity}) &= 0.9403 - 0.7885 = 0.1518 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.5 STEP 4 — Compute Information Gain for **Wind**

**Subset sizes:** Weak = 8, Strong = 6.

$$\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Weak}) &= -\frac{6}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{8}\right) - \frac{2}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{8}\right) \\
&= -0.75 \times (-0.4150) - 0.25 \times (-2) \\
&= 0.3113 + 0.5 = 0.8113
\end{aligned}$$

$$H(\text{Strong}) = -\frac{3}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{6}\right) - \frac{3}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{6}\right) = 1.0$$

$$\text{Gain}(\text{Wind}) = 0.9403 - \left(\frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0)\right) = 0.9403 - 0.8921 = 0.0481 \text{ bits}$$

---

### 3.6 STEP 5 — Compute Information Gain for **Temperature**

**Subset sizes:** Hot = 4, Mild = 6, Cool = 4.

$$\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Hot}) &= -1.0 \times \log_2(0.5) - 0 = 1.0 \quad (\text{2 Yes, 2 No})\\
H(\text{Mild}) &= -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{6}\right) - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{6}\right) \\
&= 0.9183 \quad (\text{4 Yes, 2 No})\\
H(\text{Cool}) &= -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = 0.8113
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Temp}) &= \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113) \\
&= 0.2857 + 0.3935 + 0.2318 = 0.9110
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Gain}(\text{Temperature}) = 0.9403 - 0.9110 = 0.0293 \text{ bits}$$

---

### 3.7 STEP 6 — Select the Root Node

| Attribute | Information Gain (bits) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| **Outlook** | **0.2467** | 🥇 **Winner — Root** |
| Humidity | 0.1518 | 🥈 |
| Wind | 0.0481 | 🥉 |
| Temperature | 0.0293 | 4th |

> ✅ **Root Decision = Outlook** (maximum information gain).

---

### 3.8 STEP 7 — Recurse on Each Branch (Illustrated: Sunny Branch)

The **Sunny** subset has 5 samples (2 Yes, 3 No) with remaining attributes {Temperature, Humidity, Wind}.

**Compute gains within the Sunny subset:**

$$H(\text{Sunny}) = 0.9710 \text{ bits (computed earlier)}$$

**Humidity split (within Sunny):** High = 3 (0 Yes, 3 No → pure "No"), Normal = 2 (2 Yes, 0 No → pure "Yes")

$$H(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Humidity}) = \frac{3}{5}(0) + \frac{2}{5}(0) = 0$$
$$\text{Gain} = 0.9710 - 0 = 0.9710 \;\text{← Max!}$$

**Wind split (within Sunny):** Weak = 3 (1 Yes, 2 No), Strong = 2 (1 Yes, 1 No)
$$H(\text{Weak}) = -\frac{1}{3}\log_2(1/3) - \frac{2}{3}\log_2(2/3) = 0.9183$$
$$H(\text{Strong}) = 1.0$$
$$H(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Wind}) = \frac{3}{5}(0.9183) + \frac{2}{5}(1.0) = 0.9510$$
$$\text{Gain} = 0.9710 - 0.9510 = 0.0200$$

> ✅ **Test at the Sunny node = Humidity** (gain = 0.9710), and it gives *pure* leaves!

**Temperature split (within Sunny):** Hot = 2 (0 Yes, 2 No), Mild = 2 (1,1), Cool = 1 (1,0)
$$H(\text{Sunny} \mid \text{Temp}) = \frac{2}{5}(1.0) + \frac{2}{5}(1.0) + \frac{1}{5}(0) = 0.8$$
$$\text{Gain} = 0.9710 - 0.8 = 0.1710$$

So Humidity (0.971) > Temperature (0.171) > Wind (0.020) → **Humidity wins at the Sunny node.**

---

### 3.9 📊 Final Decision Tree (ID3 Output)

```
Outlook
├── Overcast → YES
├── Sunny
│   └── Humidity
│       ├── High → NO
│       └── Normal → YES
└── Rain
    └── Wind
        ├── Weak → YES
        └── Strong → NO
```

---

### 3.10 📐 Analytical Comparison Matrix (ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART)

| Feature | ID3 | C4.5 | CART |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Impurity Measure** | Entropy | Entropy | **Gini Index** |
| **Split Criterion** | Max Information Gain | Max Gain Ratio | Max Gini Gain |
| **Handles Continuous?** | No | Yes (thresholding) | Yes |
| **Handles Missing?** | No | Yes (probability weights) | Yes (surrogate splits) |
| **Pruning** | None | **Pessimistic Error Pruning** | **Cost-Complexity Pruning** |
| **Tree Type** | Multi-way | Multi-way | **Binary** |
| **Output** | Class label | Class + probability | Class + probability |
| **Year / Author** | Quinlan 1986 | Quinlan 1993 | Breiman 1984 |

> [!TIP]
> **One-liner exam mnemonic:** *"ID3 = Information, C4.5 = Corrected (Gain Ratio), CART = Binary Gini."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Down Induction of Decision Trees (TDIDT) — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Load Training Set S] --> A1[Initialize: Compute H of S]
    A1 --> A2{All samples same class?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Create Leaf Node with that class]
    A2 -- No --> A4{Any attribute left?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Create Leaf with Majority Class]
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[For each attribute A: Compute Gain or Gain Ratio or Gini Gain]
    A6 --> A7[Select Best Attribute A* with Maximum Score]
    A7 --> A8[Split S into Subsets S1, S2 ... Sv]
    A8 --> A9[For each non-empty subset Si: Recurse on TDIDT]
    A9 --> A10[Build child subtrees]
    A10 --> A11{Depth less than max and size greater than min?}
    A11 -- Yes --> A9
    A11 -- No --> A12[Stop: Assign Leaf Class]
    A3 --> B1[Apply Pruning: Pre-pruning or Post-pruning]
    A5 --> B1
    A12 --> B1
    B1 --> B2[Final Decision Tree T]

    style A0 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af
    style A1 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0891b2
    style A2 fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke:#d97706
    style A7 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke:#9a3412
    style B1 fill:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#6d28d9
    style B2 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff,stroke:#047857
```

### 4.2 ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART — Algorithm Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the target variable type?}
    Q1 -- Categorical Classification --> Q2{Need to handle missing values?}
    Q1 -- Continuous Regression --> Q3[Use CART with Variance Reduction]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Need multi-way splits?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q5[Use C4.5 with Gain Ratio and Probability Weights]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q6[Use ID3 with Information Gain]
    Q4 -- No --> Q7[Use CART with Gini Index]
    Q3 --> R1[Output Regression Tree]
    Q6 --> R2[Output Classification Tree ID3]
    Q7 --> R3[Output Binary Classification Tree CART]
    Q5 --> R4[Output Robust Classification Tree C4.5]

    style Q1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style Q3 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style Q5 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style Q6 fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style Q7 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 The Pruning Subsystem (Overfitting Countermeasure)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Fully Grown Tree T0] --> P1{Pruning Strategy?}
    P1 -- Pre-pruning Early Stop --> P2[Set max depth]
    P1 -- Pre-pruning Min Samples --> P3[Set min samples per leaf]
    P1 -- Pre-pruning Min Gain --> P4[Stop if gain less than threshold]
    P1 -- Post-pruning Reduced Error --> P5[Hold out validation set and prune subtrees that hurt accuracy]
    P1 -- Post-pruning Cost Complexity CART --> P6[Minimize R alpha T equals R T plus alpha times leaves of T]
    P1 -- Post-pruning Pessimistic C4.5 --> P7[Replace subtree with leaf if upper bound of error on training set increases]
    P2 --> PT[Pruned Tree]
    P3 --> PT
    P4 --> PT
    P5 --> PT
    P6 --> PT
    P7 --> PT

    style T0 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style PT fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-only (`T0`, `P1`, `A1`, etc.), and labels are kept in clean uppercase alphanumeric text without any markdown formatting tags or HTML markup — ensuring flawless compilation in KTU digital submissions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a Decision Tree? List any two advantages of using decision trees for pattern classification.*

**Model Answer:**
A Decision Tree is a hierarchical, tree-structured supervised learning model that recursively partitions the feature space using axis-parallel splits, with internal nodes representing feature tests and leaf nodes representing class labels.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Interpretability** — The model is a white box; the path from root to leaf translates directly into human-readable if-then rules.
2. **Minimal preprocessing** — Requires no feature scaling, handles both categorical and numerical features natively, and performs implicit feature selection at the higher levels of the tree.

> ✅ **Expected length:** 4–6 lines, 3 marks.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare Entropy and Gini Index as impurity measures. Which is preferred in CART?*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Entropy | Gini Index |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $-\sum p_j \log_2 p_j$ | $1 - \sum p_j^2$ |
| Range | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ |
| Computational cost | Requires $\log$ operations | Only multiplications |
| Theoretical basis | Information theory | Statistical dispersion |

**CART (Breiman, 1984) uses the Gini Index** because it is computationally faster (no logarithms) and produces practically equivalent trees to entropy-based splitting.

> ✅ **Expected length:** 5–6 lines, 3 marks.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Given the training dataset below, compute the Information Gain for the attributes "Color", "Size", and "Shape" with respect to the target class "Ripeness". Identify the root node. (7 marks)*

| # | Color | Size | Shape | Ripe? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yellow | Large | Round | Yes |
| 2 | Yellow | Small | Round | No |
| 3 | Green | Small | Irregular | No |
| 4 | Yellow | Small | Round | Yes |
| 5 | Yellow | Large | Round | Yes |
| 6 | Green | Large | Round | No |
| 7 | Yellow | Small | Irregular | No |
| 8 | Yellow | Large | Round | Yes |

**Step 1 — Root Entropy**
Class distribution: 4 Yes, 4 No → $N = 8$.

$$H(S) = -\frac{4}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{8}\right) - \frac{4}{8}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{8}\right) = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

**Step 2 — Gain for Color**

- Yellow = 6 samples (4 Yes, 2 No)
- Green = 2 samples (0 Yes, 2 No)

$$H(\text{Yellow}) = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2(4/6) - \frac{2}{6}\log_2(2/6) = 0.9183$$
$$H(\text{Green}) = 0 \quad (\text{pure No})$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Color}) = \frac{6}{8}(0.9183) + \frac{2}{8}(0) = 0.6887$$
$$\text{Gain}(\text{Color}) = 1.0 - 0.6887 = 0.3113$$

**Step 3 — Gain for Size**

- Large = 4 samples (3 Yes, 1 No)
- Small = 4 samples (1 Yes, 3 No)

$$H(\text{Large}) = -\frac{3}{4}\log_2(3/4) - \frac{1}{4}\log_2(1/4) = 0.8113$$
$$H(\text{Small}) = 0.8113 \quad (\text{symmetric})$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Size}) = \frac{4}{8}(0.8113) + \frac{4}{8}(0.8113) = 0.8113$$
$$\text{Gain}(\text{Size}) = 1.0 - 0.8113 = 0.1887$$

**Step 4 — Gain for Shape**

- Round = 6 samples (4 Yes, 2 No)
- Irregular = 2 samples (0 Yes, 2 No)

$$H(\text{Round}) = 0.9183, \quad H(\text{Irregular}) = 0$$

$$H(S \mid \text{Shape}) = \frac{6}{8}(0.9183) + \frac{2}{8}(0) = 0.6887$$
$$\text{Gain}(\text{Shape}) = 1.0 - 0.6887 = 0.3113$$

**Step 5 — Selection**

| Attribute | Gain (bits) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| **Color** | **0.3113** | 🥇 **Tied root** |
| **Shape** | **0.3113** | 🥇 **Tied root** |
| Size | 0.1887 | 🥉 |

> ✅ **Root = Color (or Shape — tie broken by domain preference).** The Green and Irregular branches immediately become pure "No" leaves.

---

**(b)** *Explain the concept of pruning in decision trees. Distinguish between pre-pruning and post-pruning with suitable examples. (7 marks)*

**Pruning** is the process of removing branches from a fully-grown decision tree to combat **overfitting** and improve generalization to unseen data.

**Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping):**
- Stops the tree growth *during* construction.
- Conditions: maximum depth, minimum samples per leaf, minimum impurity decrease.
- **Example:** `if samples < 5: stop splitting and return leaf.`
- **Advantage:** Fast (no full tree grown).
- **Disadvantage:** Suffers from the *horizon effect* — a split that appears useless early may lead to a powerful split later.

**Post-Pruning (Reduced-Error / Cost-Complexity Pruning):**
- Grows the *complete* tree first, *then* prunes back subtrees.
- Uses a validation set or statistical test (e.g., chi-squared).
- **CART's Cost-Complexity Pruning:** minimizes $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot |\text{leaves}(T)|$ where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate and $\alpha$ is a complexity penalty.
- **C4.5's Pessimistic Pruning:** Replaces a subtree with a leaf if the upper confidence bound on its error rate exceeds that of the leaf.
- **Advantage:** Avoids horizon effect; produces more reliable trees.
- **Disadvantage:** Slower (full tree must be grown first).

> ✅ **Summary table expected for full 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Pre-pruning | Post-pruning |
|---|---|---|
| When | During construction | After full growth |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Horizon effect | Yes | No |
| Method | Stopping conditions | Subtree replacement |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *What is the Gain Ratio? Why was it introduced in C4.5 when Information Gain already exists in ID3? Compute the Gain Ratio for the "Outlook" attribute in the Play Tennis dataset. (7 marks)*

**Definition:** The Gain Ratio, introduced by Quinlan in C4.5, is the Information Gain normalized by the *intrinsic split information* of the attribute.

**Why Introduced:** Information Gain is **biased toward attributes with many distinct values** (e.g., "Date", "Customer_ID"). Such an attribute can split the data into tiny, pure subsets — yielding high information gain — but produces an overfitted tree with zero generalization power. Gain Ratio penalizes such attributes by dividing the gain by the entropy of the split itself.

**Computation for Outlook** (using the values from Section 3):

- $H(S) = 0.9403$ bits
- $H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = 0.6936$
- $\text{Gain}(\text{Outlook}) = 0.2467$ bits

**Split Information for Outlook** (Sunny = 5, Overcast = 4, Rain = 5):

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{Outlook}}(S) &= -\frac{5}{14}\log_2(5/14) - \frac{4}{14}\log_2(4/14) - \frac{5}{14}\log_2(5/14) \\
&= -0.3571 \times (-1.4854) - 0.2857 \times (-1.8074) - 0.3571 \times (-1.4854) \\
&= 0.5305 + 0.5164 + 0.5305 \\
&= 1.5774 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Gain Ratio:**

$$\text{GainRatio}(\text{Outlook}) = \frac{0.2467}{1.5774} = 0.1564$$

---

**(b)** *Describe the CART algorithm. How does it differ from ID3? Explain the Gini Index and its use in CART splitting. (7 marks)*

**CART (Classification and Regression Trees)** was developed by Breiman, Friedman, Olshen, and Stone in 1984.

**Key Characteristics:**
1. Produces **binary splits only** (yes/no decisions at each node).
2. Uses the **Gini Index** as the impurity measure for classification.
3. Uses **variance reduction** for regression.
4. Performs **cost-complexity pruning** post-construction.

**Gini Index Formula:**

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{c} p_j^{\,2}$$

It measures the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen sample if its label were assigned according to the class distribution in the node.

**CART Splitting Rule:**

$$\Delta \text{Gini}(S, A) = \text{Gini}(S) - \sum_{i} \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert} \text{Gini}(S_i)$$

CART picks the attribute and split point that **maximizes $\Delta$Gini**.

**Differences from ID3:**

| Aspect | ID3 | CART |
|---|---|---|
| Split type | Multi-way | **Binary** |
| Impurity | Entropy | Gini Index |
| Handles regression | No | Yes |
| Missing values | No | Yes (surrogate splits) |
| Pruning | None (original) | **Cost-complexity** |
| Numerical features | No | Yes (binary thresholding) |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ID3 with CART:** A frequent trap — *"Which algorithm uses Gini?"* Answer: **CART only**. ID3/C4.5 use Entropy.
> 2. **Forgetting $\log_2$:** Entropy requires base-2 logarithms. Using $\ln$ (natural log) gives numerically different (though proportionally equivalent) results — and KTU examiners mark strictly!
> 3. **Division by zero in Gain Ratio:** When an attribute puts all samples in one branch, $H_A = 0$. C4.5 ignores such attributes entirely.
> 4. **Skipping the second-level recursion:** Examiners expect you to recurse at least one more level after the root. Stopping at the root earns only ~3 of 7 marks.
> 5. **Writing $0 \log_2 0 = 0$** without justification — use the standard convention $0 \log 0 \to 0$ via the limit $\lim_{p \to 0} p \log p = 0$.
> 6. **Forgetting pruning in long answers:** The 7-mark question on overfitting *requires* mention of pruning. Just stating "the tree is overfitted" is insufficient.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Decision Tree** = hierarchical, axis-parallel partitioning model with internal decision nodes and leaf predictions.
- [x] **Three impurity functions:** Entropy, Gini Index, Classification Error.
- [x] **Information Gain** = $H(S) - \sum_i \frac{\vert S_i \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_i)$; used by **ID3**.
- [x] **Gain Ratio** = $\text{Gain}/H_A(S)$; used by **C4.5**; fixes ID3's high-cardinality bias.
- [x] **Gini Index** = $1 - \sum p_j^2$; used by **CART**; binary splits.
- [x] **Variance Reduction** is the criterion for **regression trees**.
- [x] **Pre-pruning** = early stopping (depth, min samples). **Post-pruning** = cost-complexity (CART) or pessimistic (C4.5).
- [x] **Overfitting** is the primary challenge; addressed via pruning, ensembling, and limiting tree depth.
- [x] **Training complexity** $\mathcal{O}(n d \log n)$; **prediction** $\mathcal{O}(\text{depth})$.
- [x] **White-box, interpretable**, no scaling required, handles mixed data — perfect for KTU Pattern Recognition applications.
- [x] **Always** show the recursive nature: root → branch → leaf in any construction problem.
- [x] **Mnemonic:** "**I**D3 = **I**nformation, **C**4.5 = **C**orrected, **C**ART = Binary **G**ini."

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 — DECISION TREES NOTES**

topics.title: Concept of Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Pattern Recognition**, the **Concept of Construction** refers to a family of *incremental, bottom-up learning strategies* wherein a pattern recognition model (classifier, neural network, decision tree, or feature space) is **built from scratch** by progressively adding structural components — such as neurons, rules, features, hyperplanes, or tree branches — until the desired classification accuracy or representational capacity is achieved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Focus):**
> *Constructive (or constructive/growing) learning methods are non-parametric, structural learning approaches that start with a minimal architecture and dynamically expand the model by inserting new units, branches, or features based on training data, in contrast to "destructive" or pruning-based methods that begin with a complex model and simplify it.*

Construction-based methods are foundational to:
- **Constructive Neural Networks (CNN)** — e.g., Cascade-Correlation, Tower, Tiling algorithms
- **Decision Tree Induction** — e.g., ID3, C4.5, CART (top-down tree construction)
- **Constructive Induction** — automated feature construction in the input space
- **Prototype/Cluster Construction** — incremental cluster formation
- **Support Vector Machine (SVM) construction** — sequential minimal optimization

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "LEGO Tower" Intuition

Imagine you are a child building a LEGO tower to exactly the height of a target book on a shelf.

- **Destructive (Pruning) Approach:** You start with a giant stack of LEGOs (an over-sized tower), keep stacking blindly, and then *cut away* the extra pieces that overshoot the book. You waste a lot of material and time.
- **Constructive (Building) Approach:** You start with a *small base* of LEGOs, measure the gap, and **add exactly one or two more LEGOs at a time**, checking the height after every addition. You stop the moment the tower perfectly meets the book.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Construction = *add only what is needed, when it is needed.* This is the philosophical bedrock of all constructive pattern recognition algorithms — they grow in response to data, not in anticipation of it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

While construction is largely algorithmic, the following standard metrics govern the *termination* and *evaluation* of constructive processes:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learning Rate | $\eta$ | $\mathbf{0.01 \leq \eta \leq 0.5}$ | Controls weight update magnitude in constructive NNs |
| Error Threshold | $\varepsilon$ | $\mathbf{10^{-3} \text{ to } 10^{-6}}$ | Stop adding units when residual error falls below $\varepsilon$ |
| Growth Rate | $\alpha$ | $\mathbf{1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ hidden units/epoch}}$ | Speed of architecture growth |
| Construction Cycles | $T$ | $\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 500}$ iterations | Maximum number of addition operations allowed |

> [!TIP]
> These metrics are typically asked in **2-mark KTU short-answer questions** as direct recall items. Memorize the symbol-purpose mapping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Constructive Learning

Every constructive pattern recognition algorithm operates on three universal principles:

1. **Minimal Initialization** — Start with the smallest possible model (often zero hidden units, a single decision stump, or a single prototype).
2. **Data-Driven Expansion** — Add a new structural element *only* when the current model fails to represent some portion of the training data (typically the misclassified samples or the highest-error residual).
3. **Greedy Stopping Criterion** — Halt construction when a performance metric (classification error, MSE, information gain) satisfies a pre-defined threshold or when no further improvement is possible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Why"** behind construction is **parsimony** (Occam's Razor): build the *smallest* model that solves the problem, avoiding both underfitting and overfitting.

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Construction Process

Let $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ be a training dataset, where $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}$.

A constructive model can be expressed as a sequence:

$$M_0 \subset M_1 \subset M_2 \subset \ldots \subset M_T$$

where $M_t$ is the model at construction step $t$, and each $M_{t+1} = M_t \cup \{\Delta_t\}$ adds a new component $\Delta_t$ (a neuron, a rule, a hyperplane, or a feature).

The objective is to minimize the residual error:

$$E_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(f_{M_t}(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i\big)$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function (e.g., squared error, cross-entropy, hinge loss), and the construction halts when:

$$E_t \leq \varepsilon \quad \text{or} \quad E_t - E_{t-1} < \delta$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility | Key Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constructive Sequence | $M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \ldots \subset M_T$ | Defines the monotonic growth of model complexity | Monotonic — cannot remove components |
| Residual Error (MSE) | $E_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(t)})^2$ | Quantifies how much "work" remains for new units | Must be monotonically non-increasing |
| Information Gain (Trees) | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Decides *which feature* to split on at each node | $IG \geq 0$ always |
| Gini Impurity | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$ | Alternative split criterion in CART construction | $0 \leq Gini \leq 1 - \frac{1}{C}$ |
| Cascade-Correlation Weight Update | $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}$ | Adjusts new unit's incoming weights to maximize correlation | Correlation must be $> 0$ for inclusion |
| SVM Margin | $\gamma = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Geometric interpretation of SVM construction | Maximize $\gamma$ equivalent to minimize $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ |
| VC Dimension Bound | $h \leq \sum_{i=1}^{d} \binom{N}{i}$ | Theoretical capacity of a constructive network | Grows as units are added |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU board exam, the **Information Gain formula** and the **Gini Impurity** formula are the two most frequently asked equations in Part A (3-mark) questions on constructive decision trees.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** — Constructive Bayesian networks that grow nodes only when a new disease-symptom correlation is statistically significant.
- **Speech & Speaker Recognition** — Constructive RBF networks where new Gaussian units are spawned to cover previously misclassified phoneme clusters.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Perception** — Constructive decision forests for real-time object classification where tree depth is built dynamically based on scene complexity.
- **Bioinformatics** — Constructive feature engineering for gene expression classification.
- **Industrial Fault Detection** — Constructive one-class SVMs that incrementally add support vectors as new fault modes are discovered on the production line.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever a question asks *"Give a real-world example of constructive learning,"* the safest and most scoring answer is **medical diagnosis** or **speech recognition using RBF networks** — both are textbook favourites at KTU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Information Gain for Constructive Decision Tree Splitting

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 3 for KTU 2024 scheme examinations. Let us derive the information gain formula used to decide *where* and *how* a decision tree should construct its next split.

**Step 1: Define Entropy of a Set $S$**

Entropy measures the impurity of a label distribution. For a set $S$ with $C$ classes and class probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_C$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log_2(p_c)$$

*Explanation:* The negative log captures "surprise" — if $p_c = 1$ (pure node), then $\log_2(1) = 0$, so $H(S) = 0$ (no impurity). If classes are uniformly distributed, entropy is maximized.

**Step 2: Define Conditional Entropy After Splitting on Attribute $A$**

When we split the dataset $S$ on attribute $A$ into subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_v$:

$$H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in \text{values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_v)$$

*Explanation:* Each child node contributes its entropy, weighted by its size proportion. This is the *expected* impurity *after* the split.

**Step 3: Define Information Gain as the Reduction in Entropy**

The information gain from splitting on attribute $A$ is the *reduction* in entropy:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

*Explanation:* A high $IG$ means attribute $A$ removes a lot of disorder — a *good* split. A constructive tree builder picks the attribute with the **maximum** $IG$ at every construction step.

**Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)**

Consider a dataset $S$ with **10 samples**: 6 belonging to Class $+$ and 4 to Class $-$.

Compute the entropy of the root node:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -p_+ \log_2(p_+) - p_- \log_2(p_-) \\
&= -\frac{6}{10} \log_2\!\left(\frac{6}{10}\right) - \frac{4}{10} \log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{10}\right) \\
&= -0.6 \cdot \log_2(0.6) - 0.4 \cdot \log_2(0.4)
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute $\log_2(0.6)$ and $\log_2(0.4)$ using $\log_2(x) = \frac{\ln(x)}{\ln(2)}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_2(0.6) &= \frac{\ln(0.6)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-0.5108}{0.6931} \approx -0.7370 \\
\log_2(0.4) &= \frac{\ln(0.4)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-0.9163}{0.6931} \approx -1.3219
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) - 0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) \\
&= 0.4422 + 0.5288 \\
&\approx 0.9710 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

*Interpretation:* The root node has an entropy of approximately **0.971 bits**, indicating moderate impurity. A constructive tree algorithm will now try every attribute to find the one that maximally reduces this entropy.

**Step 5: Splitting on a Hypothetical Attribute $A$**

Suppose attribute $A$ splits $S$ into:
- $S_1$: 4 samples, all Class $+$ (pure)
- $S_2$: 6 samples, with 2 Class $+$ and 4 Class $-$

Compute child entropies:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S_1) &= -1 \cdot \log_2(1) - 0 \cdot \log_2(0) = 0 \\
H(S_2) &= -\frac{2}{6} \log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{6}\right) - \frac{4}{6} \log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{6}\right) \\
&= -0.3333 \cdot (-1.5850) - 0.6667 \cdot (-0.5850) \\
&= 0.5283 + 0.3900 = 0.9183 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

Weighted conditional entropy:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid A) &= \frac{4}{10} \cdot 0 + \frac{6}{10} \cdot 0.9183 \\
&= 0 + 0.5510 \\
&= 0.5510 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

Information gain of attribute $A$:

$$\begin{aligned}
IG(S, A) &= H(S) - H(S \mid A) \\
&= 0.9710 - 0.5510 \\
&= 0.4200 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

*Final Interpretation:* Attribute $A$ reduces entropy by **0.42 bits** — the constructive tree will accept this split at the root and **recurse** to construct the next internal node within $S_2$ (since $S_1$ is already pure and needs no further construction).

## 3.2 Derivation: Gini Impurity vs. Entropy — When Constructive Trees Prefer Each

The Gini impurity is defined as:

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

It can be shown that for a two-class problem ($C = 2$), Gini and entropy are mathematically related by:

$$H(S) \approx Gini(S) \cdot \log_2(C) \quad \text{(in the low-impurity regime)}$$

The CART (Classification and Regression Tree) algorithm uses Gini, while ID3/C4.5 use entropy. The constructive choice between them is:

- **Gini:** Computationally faster (no log), preferred for large-scale constructive trees.
- **Entropy:** More theoretically pure, preferred for small datasets or when information-theoretic interpretation is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE papers — *"Compare Gini impurity and Entropy as split criteria in constructive decision tree algorithms."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Constructive Learning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Initialize M0 minimal model]) --> loadA[Load Training Dataset D]
    loadA --> evalA[Evaluate Current Error Et on D]
    evalA --> checkA{Error Et below threshold epsilon or no improvement}
    checkA -- No, needs more units --> addA[Add new structural unit Delta t to M t]
    addA --> trainA[Train/Update Weights of M t plus 1]
    trainA --> evalA
    checkA -- Yes, construction complete --> finalA([Output Final Constructed Model M final])
    finalA --> deployA([Deploy for Pattern Recognition])

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef startEnd fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class checkA decision
    class loadA,evalA,addA,trainA process
    class startA,finalA,deployA startEnd
```

## 4.2 Constructive Decision Tree — Recursive Splitting Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Root Node S: 10 samples] --> splitA1{Attribute A selected by max IG}
    splitA1 -- Left branch: S1 --> nodeA1[Node S1: 4 samples, all positive class]
    splitA1 -- Right branch: S2 --> nodeA2[Node S2: 6 samples, mixed classes]
    nodeA1 --> leafA1([Leaf: Class positive, stop construction])
    nodeA2 --> splitA2{Attribute B selected by max IG on S2}
    splitA2 -- Left branch: S21 --> nodeA21[Node S21: 2 samples]
    nodeA2 -- Right branch: S22 --> nodeA22[Node S22: 4 samples]
    nodeA21 --> leafA21([Leaf: Class positive, stop construction])
    nodeA22 --> splitA3{Attribute C selected by max IG on S22}
    splitA3 -- Left --> leafA22a([Leaf: Class positive])
    splitA3 -- Right --> leafA22b([Leaf: Class negative])

    classDef splitNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef internalNode fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef leafNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class splitA1,splitA2,splitA3 splitNode
    class nodeA1,nodeA2,nodeA21,nodeA22 internalNode
    class leafA1,leafA21,leafA22a,leafA22b leafNode
```

## 4.3 Cascade-Correlation Constructive Neural Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Layer x1 x2 x3] --> hiddenA1[Hidden Unit H1 added in cycle 1]
    inputA --> hiddenA2[Hidden Unit H2 added in cycle 2]
    hiddenA1 --> hiddenA3[Hidden Unit H3 added in cycle 3]
    hiddenA2 --> hiddenA3
    hiddenA1 --> outputA[Output Layer y]
    hiddenA2 --> outputA
    hiddenA3 --> outputA
    inputA -.->|frozen after installation| hiddenA1
    hiddenA1 -.->|frozen after installation| hiddenA2
    hiddenA2 -.->|frozen after installation| hiddenA3

    classDef inputStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hiddenStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class inputA inputStyle
    class hiddenA1,hiddenA2,hiddenA3 hiddenStyle
    class outputA outputStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Cascade-Correlation Diagram:** The dotted arrows indicate *frozen* connections. Once a hidden unit is installed, its incoming weights are locked. New units receive connections from **all** previous inputs and hidden units, and only the new unit's weights are trained until its correlation with the residual error exceeds a threshold.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define the "Concept of Construction" in pattern recognition. How does it fundamentally differ from the destructive (pruning) approach?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Concept of Construction in pattern recognition refers to a class of **incremental learning algorithms** that build a classification model *from a minimal initial structure* by progressively adding components such as neurons, decision rules, or features until a stopping criterion (typically a target error rate) is met. Each added unit is trained to capture the residual error left by the existing model.
In contrast, **destructive (pruning) methods** start with a large, fully-connected model and *remove* redundant components to improve generalization.
The key difference lies in the **direction of model evolution**: construction *grows* the model in response to data complexity, whereas pruning *shrinks* an over-parameterized model. Construction tends to produce more compact, interpretable models and avoids the computational waste of training and then removing units. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the formula for Information Gain used in constructive decision tree algorithms. Why is the attribute with the highest information gain chosen at every construction step?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Information Gain (IG) for an attribute $A$ on a dataset $S$ is defined as:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

where $H(S)$ is the Shannon entropy of the class distribution in $S$ and $H(S \mid A)$ is the weighted post-split entropy. The attribute that **maximizes $IG$** is selected at each construction step because it produces the **largest reduction in class impurity**, yielding the most informative split and the most compact tree. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)
### Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **general architecture of a constructive learning system**. Explain each block in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Derive the **Information Gain formula** step by step. Apply it to a 2-class dataset of **8 samples** (5 positive, 3 negative) split on an attribute $A$ that produces child nodes with the following class distributions: Node $S_1$ has (4 +, 0 −) and Node $S_2$ has (1 +, 3 −). Compute the entropy of the root, the conditional entropy after the split, and the resulting information gain. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

A constructive learning system consists of four primary blocks:

1. **Initialization Block:** Maintains the *current model* $M_t$ (initially $M_0$, a minimal model).
2. **Evaluation Block:** Computes the residual error $E_t$ on the training set $\mathcal{D}$ using a loss function $\mathcal{L}$.
3. **Construction (Growth) Block:** Decides whether to add a new unit $\Delta_t$ and determines its type (neuron, rule, feature). The decision rule is typically: *add* if $E_t > \varepsilon$ and improvement $E_{t-1} - E_t > \delta$.
4. **Training Block:** Optimizes the parameters of $M_{t+1} = M_t \cup \{\Delta_t\}$ via gradient descent, correlation maximization (Cascade-Correlation), or information gain (ID3).

The cycle of *Evaluate → Decide → Add → Train* repeats until the stopping criterion is satisfied. The final output is a *purpose-built* model $M_T$ whose complexity matches the intrinsic difficulty of the data. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Root Entropy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\frac{5}{8} \log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{8}\right) - \frac{3}{8} \log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{8}\right) \\
&= -0.625 \cdot (-0.6781) - 0.375 \cdot (-1.4150) \\
&= 0.4238 + 0.5306 \\
&\approx 0.9544 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Child Entropies:**

Node $S_1$ is pure (4 +, 0 −):

$$H(S_1) = -1 \cdot \log_2(1) = 0 \text{ bits}$$

Node $S_2$ has 1 + and 3 − (total 4 samples):

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S_2) &= -\frac{1}{4} \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) - \frac{3}{4} \log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) \\
&= -0.25 \cdot (-2) - 0.75 \cdot (-0.4150) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.3113 = 0.8113 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Conditional Entropy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid A) &= \frac{4}{8} \cdot 0 + \frac{4}{8} \cdot 0.8113 \\
&= 0 + 0.4057 = 0.4057 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Information Gain:**

$$IG(S, A) = 0.9544 - 0.4057 = 0.5487 \text{ bits}$$

*Conclusion:* Attribute $A$ yields an information gain of **0.5487 bits**, a significant reduction from the root's 0.9544 bits. A constructive decision tree (e.g., ID3) will accept this as the root split. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Cascade-Correlation constructive neural network** algorithm. How does it differ from a fixed-architecture backpropagation network in terms of architecture determination? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compare the **Gini impurity** and **Entropy** as split criteria for constructive decision trees. Show mathematically that for a two-class problem with $p = 0.5$, both criteria reach their maximum value, and compute that maximum. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The **Cascade-Correlation** algorithm (Fahlman & Lebiere, 1990) is a seminal constructive neural network that grows its hidden layer one unit at a time.

**Phase 1 — Architecture Construction (Cascading):**
The network starts with **only input and output units** (no hidden layer). A new hidden unit $H_1$ is installed, connected to *all* input units and the output unit. Its incoming weights are trained to **maximize the correlation** between its output and the *residual error* of the network:

$$\text{Correlation}(H_j) = \left\vert \sum_{p} \sum_{o} \big(V_{p,o} - \bar{V}_o\big) \big(I_{p,j} - \bar{I}_j\big) \right\vert$$

where $V_{p,o}$ is the residual error at output $o$ for pattern $p$, and $I_{p,j}$ is the activation of candidate unit $j$.

**Phase 2 — Freezing:**
Once the new unit's correlation exceeds a threshold, its incoming weights are **frozen** (locked permanently). It then becomes a permanent part of the network, providing a new feature to subsequent units.

**Phase 3 — Iteration:**
A *new* candidate unit is installed, connected to all inputs **and all previously installed hidden units**. The process repeats until the residual error falls below the threshold $\varepsilon$.

**Comparison with Backpropagation:**

| Feature | Backpropagation | Cascade-Correlation |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Fixed in advance | Grown dynamically |
| Hidden Units | All present from start | Added one at a time |
| Training | All weights updated jointly | Only new unit's weights trained |
| Convergence | Can be slow / stuck in local minima | Fast; no weight transport problem |
| Final Size | User-specified | Determined by data complexity |

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Gini Impurity:**

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c^2$$

For a **two-class** problem ($C = 2$) with class probabilities $p$ and $1 - p$:

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \big[p^2 + (1-p)^2\big]$$

**Entropy:**

$$H(S) = -p \log_2(p) - (1-p) \log_2(1-p)$$

**Maximum at $p = 0.5$:**

Substitute $p = 0.5$ into Gini:

$$\begin{aligned}
Gini_{\max} &= 1 - \big[(0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2\big] \\
&= 1 - 0.25 - 0.25 \\
&= 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $p = 0.5$ into Entropy:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\max} &= -0.5 \cdot \log_2(0.5) - 0.5 \cdot \log_2(0.5) \\
&= -0.5 \cdot (-1) - 0.5 \cdot (-1) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.5 \\
&= 1.0 \text{ bit}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Gini Impurity | Entropy |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum value (binary) | **0.5** | **1.0 bit** |
| Computation | Squared probabilities only | Logarithmic — slower |
| Sensitivity to changes | Less sensitive near extremes | More sensitive near extremes |
| Used by | CART (Breiman) | ID3, C4.5 (Quinlan) |
| Range | $[0, 0.5]$ | $[0, 1]$ |

*Conclusion:* Both criteria are maximized at $p = 0.5$ (the most impure split), confirming their theoretical equivalence in targeting impurity. Gini is computationally cheaper, while Entropy offers a richer information-theoretic interpretation. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base of the logarithm** in entropy — it is **$\log_2$**, not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Sign errors** — the entropy formula has a *negative* sign outside the sum. Forgetting it gives negative entropy, which is *impossible* and costs full marks.
> 3. **Misdefining $0 \cdot \log_2(0)$** — by convention, $0 \log 0 = 0$ (the limit). If you write "undefined" you lose a mark.
> 4. **Confusing construction with pruning** — the *direction* matters. Construction grows, pruning shrinks. Examiners give zero marks if these are swapped.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** in 7-mark questions — merely stating the formula is worth only 3 marks. The remaining 4 marks come from *explaining the intuition* and *showing the derivation steps*.
> 6. **Using $\mid x \mid$ in tables** — use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid markdown table-breaking issues.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Construction = incremental growth** of a model from a minimal starting point, in response to data-driven error signals.
- ✅ The **three pillars** are *minimal initialization*, *data-driven expansion*, and *greedy stopping*.
- ✅ **Cascade-Correlation** is the canonical constructive NN; **ID3/C4.5** is the canonical constructive decision tree.
- ✅ The **Information Gain formula** $IG(S,A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ is the **#1 high-yield equation** for KTU Module 3.
- ✅ **Gini impurity** ranges in $[0, 0.5]$ for binary problems; **Entropy** ranges in $[0, 1]$ bit.
- ✅ Both Gini and Entropy are **maximized at $p = 0.5$** (the most impure / least informative split).
- ✅ Construction respects **Occam's Razor** — smallest model that fits the data.
- ✅ Real-world favourites: **medical diagnosis** (Bayesian net construction) and **speech recognition** (constructive RBF).
- ✅ Key difference: construction *adds* units; pruning *removes* them. Direction is the exam clincher.
- ✅ Stopping criteria: error threshold $\varepsilon$, improvement threshold $\delta$, or maximum cycles $T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Splitting of Nodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Splitting of Nodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Pattern Recognition, the **Splitting of Nodes** refers to the fundamental decision-making process used during the construction (induction) of a **Decision Tree** classifier. At every non-terminal node of a growing decision tree, the algorithm must decide *which feature* to test, *what threshold* (or *what categorical value*) to use, and *how to partition* the incoming set of training samples into two or more sub-sets (child nodes).

Formally, given a parent node $S$ containing $N$ samples belonging to $C$ different classes, a split is defined by a **splitting predicate** of the form:

$$A \le \theta \quad \text{or} \quad A \in V_j$$

where $A$ is a chosen attribute, $\theta$ is a real-valued threshold (for continuous features), and $V_j$ is a discrete subset of attribute values (for categorical features). The split must maximize the **homogeneity** (purity) of the resulting child nodes while minimizing the **impurity** of the parent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Splitting of nodes is the *engine* of all decision tree algorithms (ID3, C4.5, CART, CHAID). Without an intelligent splitting criterion, a decision tree is just a random partitioning of feature space.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Triage Nurse

Imagine you are a **triage nurse** at a hospital emergency ward. 200 patients walk in simultaneously. You cannot treat them all at once — you must **split** them into groups so each group can be sent to the *correct* specialist.

- **Node 1 (Root):** All 200 patients.
- **First Split:** You ask, *"Is the patient complaining of chest pain?"* → **Yes (30 patients) → Cardiologist**, **No (170 patients) → General Triage**.
- **Second Split (General Triage):** *"Is there a visible fracture?"* → **Yes (40 patients) → Orthopedic**, **No (130 patients) → General Medicine**.

Each *split* was based on a **single, highly informative question** (an *attribute test*). Your goal at every step was to **maximize the homogeneity** of each resulting group (a group of cardiologists should not receive fracture cases). This is *exactly* what a decision tree does — it splits data using the *most informative attribute* at each node.

## 1.3 Why is Splitting Important?

A decision tree is built **top-down, greedily**. At each node, we must:

1. **Choose the best attribute** from the available feature set.
2. **Determine the best split point** (threshold or subset) for that attribute.
3. **Partition** the data into child nodes.
4. **Recursively repeat** until a stopping criterion is met (pure node, minimum samples, maximum depth).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Impurity (Heterogeneity):** A node is *impure* when it contains a mixture of classes (e.g., 50% Class-A, 50% Class-B). A node is *pure* when it contains samples from only one class (100% Class-A). The goal of splitting is to **reduce impurity** as much as possible.

## 1.4 The Two Families of Splitting Criteria

| Family | Used By | Primary Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Information-Theoretic** | ID3, C4.5 | Entropy, Information Gain, Gain Ratio |
| **Statistical / Gini-Based** | CART, Random Forests | Gini Index, Gini Gain |
| **Statistical Test** | CHAID | Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of a Split

Every splitting algorithm follows the same logical skeleton:

1. **Initialize** at the root node with the entire training set $S$ containing $N$ samples and $C$ classes.
2. **For each candidate attribute** $A_i$ (where $i = 1, 2, \dots, d$):
   - **For each possible split point** $v$ of $A_i$:
     - Partition $S$ into $S_{\text{left}}$ and $S_{\text{right}}$ (or $S_{v_1}, S_{v_2}, \dots$ for multi-way splits).
     - Compute the **impurity** $I(\cdot)$ of each partition.
     - Compute the **weighted impurity after the split**.
3. **Choose the attribute and split point** that minimize (or equivalently, maximize the gain from) the weighted post-split impurity.
4. **Recurse** on each child node until a stopping condition is triggered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The procedure is **greedy** — it picks the locally optimal split at each node without backtracking. It does *not* guarantee a globally optimal tree, but in practice produces excellent classifiers.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | **Entropy** of node $S$ | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Bits; $p_i$ = proportion of class $i$ in $S$ |
| 2 | **Gini Index** of node $S$ | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$ | Range: $[0, 1 - 1/C]$ |
| 3 | **Classification Error** | $E(S) = 1 - \max_i(p_i)$ | Range: $[0, 1 - 1/C]$ |
| 4 | **Information Gain** (attribute $A$) | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Higher is better |
| 5 | **Split Information** (intrinsic value) | $\text{SplitInfo}(S,A) = -\sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Used in C4.5 |
| 6 | **Gain Ratio** (C4.5) | $GR(S,A) = \frac{IG(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S,A)}$ | Normalized version of IG |
| 7 | **Gini Gain** (CART) | $\Delta \text{Gini}(S,A) = \text{Gini}(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\text{Gini}(S_v)$ | Higher is better |
| 8 | **Misclassification Impurity Reduction** | $\Delta E(S,A) = E(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} E(S_v)$ | Rarely used as primary criterion |
| 9 | **Chi-Square Statistic** | $\chi^2 = \sum_{v} \sum_{i} \frac{(O_{v,i} - E_{v,i})^2}{E_{v,i}}$ | Used in CHAID |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for KTU Exams:** In the formulas above, $\vert S_v \vert$ denotes the *cardinality* (size) of subset $S_v$. In your exam answer sheet, write this as $\lvert S_v \rvert$ or $|S_v|$. The `|...|` symbol is *inherently* part of the math notation, not a markdown table separator.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Decision-tree node splitting is the backbone of numerous mission-critical applications:

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Splitting patients based on symptoms, lab values, and imaging features to reach a disease diagnosis (similar to our triage analogy).
- **Credit Risk Assessment:** Banks split loan applicants on income, credit history, debt-to-income ratio.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Manufacturing:** Industrial IoT systems use decision trees to classify sensor patterns as *normal*, *degrading*, or *critical fault*.
- **Remote Sensing & GIS:** Land-cover classification from satellite images.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression classification (cancer sub-type detection).

## 2.4 Why Entropy? The Theoretical Justification

Entropy originates from **Claude Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**. It measures the *expected amount of information* (in bits) required to identify the class of a randomly drawn sample from node $S$. A node with $p = (1, 0, 0, \dots)$ is perfectly ordered — we need **0 bits** to identify the class. A node with uniform distribution $p = (1/C, 1/C, \dots, 1/C)$ is maximally chaotic — we need $\log_2 C$ bits.

## 2.5 Why Gini?

The Gini index can be interpreted as the **expected error rate** if we classified a sample by randomly drawing a class label according to the distribution at the node. It is computationally cheaper than entropy (no $\log$ operation) and produces nearly identical trees.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Entropy Function

We want a function $H(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_C)$ that quantifies the *disorder* of a probability distribution. Shannon imposed three axioms:

**Axiom 1 — Continuity:** $H$ must be continuous in $p_i$.

**Axiom 2 — Maximality:** $H$ is maximized when $p_1 = p_2 = \dots = p_C = 1/C$.

**Axiom 3 — Additivity (Chain Rule):** If a choice is broken into two successive choices, $H$ should be the weighted sum of the two sub-entropies.

The *only* function (up to a multiplicative constant) that satisfies all three axioms is:

$$H(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_C) = -K \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log p_i$$

With the convention $0 \log 0 = 0$, and choosing $K = 1/\ln 2$ to express the result in **bits** (binary digits), we recover:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

**Sanity checks:**
- *Pure node* (e.g., $p_1 = 1$): $H = -1 \cdot \log_2 1 - 0 - \dots = 0$ bits. ✅
- *Maximum chaos* (e.g., $C = 2$, $p_1 = p_2 = 0.5$): $H = -0.5 \log_2 0.5 - 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1$ bit. ✅
- *Three-class uniform*: $H = -3 \cdot (1/3) \log_2(1/3) = \log_2 3 \approx 1.585$ bits. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gini Index

Consider drawing two items independently from node $S$ according to the class distribution. The probability that they belong to **different classes** is the Gini index.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{same class}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2 \\
P(\text{different classes}) &= 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus:

$$\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

**Sanity checks:**
- *Pure node* ($p_1 = 1$): $\text{Gini} = 1 - 1 = 0$. ✅
- *Binary impure* ($p_1 = p_2 = 0.5$): $\text{Gini} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$. ✅

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Example — Information Gain Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** You are given a dataset $S$ of **10 training samples** for a binary classification problem (`Yes` / `No`). The candidate attribute $A$ has three possible values $\{a_1, a_2, a_3\}$. The class distribution is as follows:
>
> | Subset | Size | Yes | No |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | $S$ (Parent) | 10 | 6 | 4 |
> | $S_{a_1}$ | 4 | 3 | 1 |
> | $S_{a_2}$ | 4 | 2 | 2 |
> | $S_{a_3}$ | 2 | 1 | 1 |
>
> Compute the **Information Gain** of splitting on attribute $A$.

**Step 1 — Compute parent entropy $H(S)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\, p_{\text{Yes}} \log_2 p_{\text{Yes}} - p_{\text{No}} \log_2 p_{\text{No}} \\
&= -\, (6/10) \log_2(6/10) - (4/10) \log_2(4/10) \\
&= -\, 0.6 \cdot \log_2 0.6 - 0.4 \cdot \log_2 0.4 \\
&= -\, 0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) - 0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) \\
&= 0.4422 + 0.5288 \\
&= \mathbf{0.9710 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute child entropies:**

For $S_{a_1}$ (4 samples, 3 Yes, 1 No):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{a_1}) &= -\, (3/4)\log_2(3/4) - (1/4)\log_2(1/4) \\
&= -\, 0.75 \cdot (-0.4150) - 0.25 \cdot (-2) \\
&= 0.3113 + 0.5 \\
&= \mathbf{0.8113 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $S_{a_2}$ (4 samples, 2 Yes, 2 No):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{a_2}) &= -\, (2/4)\log_2(2/4) - (2/4)\log_2(2/4) \\
&= -\, 2 \cdot [0.5 \cdot (-1)] \\
&= \mathbf{1.0000 \text{ bit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $S_{a_3}$ (2 samples, 1 Yes, 1 No):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{a_3}) &= -\, (1/2)\log_2(1/2) - (1/2)\log_2(1/2) \\
&= \mathbf{1.0000 \text{ bit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted post-split entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{after}}(S,A) &= \frac{\vert S_{a_1} \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_{a_1}) + \frac{\vert S_{a_2} \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_{a_2}) + \frac{\vert S_{a_3} \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_{a_3}) \\
&= (4/10)(0.8113) + (4/10)(1.0000) + (2/10)(1.0000) \\
&= 0.3245 + 0.4000 + 0.2000 \\
&= \mathbf{0.9245 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Information Gain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IG(S,A) &= H(S) - H_{\text{after}}(S,A) \\
&= 0.9710 - 0.9245 \\
&= \mathbf{0.0465 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The information gain of attribute $A$ is only **0.0465 bits** — a very small reduction in entropy. This indicates that attribute $A$ is *not very informative*. The algorithm would compare this value against the IG of other candidate attributes and pick the one with the *highest* gain.

## 3.4 Bias of Information Gain — The C4.5 / Gain Ratio Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Quinlan's ID3 algorithm had a *well-known bias* — it preferred attributes with **many distinct values** (e.g., `Customer-ID`, `Date-of-Birth`) because they could fragment the data into many small, pure subsets. To correct this, C4.5 introduced the **Gain Ratio**, which *normalizes* the information gain by the *split information* (the entropy of the partition itself).

For our example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SplitInfo}(S,A) &= -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \\
&= -\, (4/10)\log_2(4/10) - (4/10)\log_2(4/10) - (2/10)\log_2(2/10) \\
&= -\, 0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) - 0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) - 0.2 \cdot (-2.3219) \\
&= 0.5288 + 0.5288 + 0.4644 \\
&= \mathbf{1.5219 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
GR(S,A) &= \frac{IG(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S,A)} = \frac{0.0465}{1.5219} \approx \mathbf{0.0306}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Binary vs. Multi-way Splits

- **Binary Splits (CART default):** Each node produces exactly two children. For an attribute with $k$ values, we must test all $2^{k-1} - 1$ possible groupings.
- **Multi-way Splits (ID3, C4.5 default):** Each node produces one child per attribute value. Simple, but can fragment the data.

> **Engineering Trade-off:** Binary splits handle categorical features with many levels (e.g., country names) more gracefully. Multi-way splits are faster to compute but can lead to *data fragmentation*.

## 3.6 The Greedy Top-Down Induction Algorithm — Pseudocode (Conceptual Only)

```
FUNCTION BuildTree(S, Attributes):
    IF all samples in S belong to the same class:
        RETURN a leaf node labeled with that class
    IF Attributes is empty OR stopping criterion met:
        RETURN a leaf node labeled with the majority class in S
    ELSE:
        A* ← attribute in Attributes that MAXIMIZES the splitting criterion (IG or GR or Gini Gain)
        Create a new internal node with test A*
        FOR EACH value v of A*:
            S_v ← subset of S where A* = v
            IF S_v is empty:
                Attach a leaf labeled with the majority class of S
            ELSE:
                Attach child ← BuildTree(S_v, Attributes − {A*})
        RETURN the new internal node
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is *conceptual logic*, not a programming language syntax. The actual KTU exam answer should be written as plain English / pseudocode with mathematical formulas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Decision Tree Splitting Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Node S with N samples] --> B[Examine All Candidate Attributes A1 A2 ... Ad]
    B --> C[For Each Attribute Evaluate All Possible Split Points v]
    C --> D[Partition S into Child Subsets S v1 S v2 ... S vk]
    D --> E[Compute Impurity I Sv for Each Child]
    E --> F[Compute Weighted Post Split Impurity]
    F --> G[Compute Gain IG GR or Delta Gini]
    G --> H{Best Attribute and Split Point Selected}
    H --> I[Create Internal Test Node with Best Split]
    I --> J[Recurse on Each Child Subset]
    J --> K{Stopping Criterion Met}
    K -->|Yes| L[Create Leaf Node Majority Class]
    K -->|No| B
```

## 4.2 Impurity Reduction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        A1[Training Subset S]
        A2[Attribute Pool A]
    end

    subgraph EVAL[EVALUATION BLOCK]
        B1[Loop over A in A]
        B2[Loop over split points v in A]
        B3[Compute H S or Gini S]
        B4[Compute Weighted Child Impurity]
        B5[Compute Gain Metric]
    end

    subgraph SELECT[SELECTION BLOCK]
        C1[Compare Gains across All Candidates]
        C2[Identify argmax Attribute and Split Point]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        D1[Best Split Predicate]
        D2[Child Subsets S left and S right]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic of Three Splitting Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Decision Tree Splitting Criteria] --> B[Information Theoretic Family]
    A --> C[Statistical Gini Family]
    A --> D[Statistical Test Family]

    B --> B1[Entropy H S equals minus sum p_i log2 p_i]
    B --> B2[Information Gain IG S A equals H S minus weighted child entropy]
    B --> B3[Gain Ratio GR equals IG divided by SplitInfo]

    C --> C1[Gini Index equals 1 minus sum p_i squared]
    C --> C2[Gini Gain equals Gini S minus weighted child Gini]

    D --> D1[Chi Square Test for Independence]
    D --> D2[Bonferroni Adjusted p value]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree Inductive Bias Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inductive Bias of Decision Tree Learning] --> B[Preference for Shorter Trees Occam Razor]
    A --> C[Preference for High Information Gain at Root]
    A --> D[Greedy Local Search No Backtracking]
    A --> E[Pre Pruning Heuristics]
    A --> F[Post Pruning Heuristics]

    E --> E1[Maximum Depth Limit]
    E --> E2[Minimum Samples per Leaf]
    E --> E3[Minimum Impurity Decrease Threshold]

    F --> F1[Reduced Error Pruning REP]
    F --> F2[Cost Complexity Pruning CCP]
    F --> F3[Minimum Description Length MDL]
```
