## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define pruning. Why is it necessary in decision tree induction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Pruning is a technique used in decision tree learning to reduce the size of the tree by removing branches that have little predictive power, thereby improving the model's generalization to unseen data.
- **Necessity (1.5 marks):** A fully grown decision tree achieves zero training error but suffers from **overfitting** — it memorizes noise in the training set. Pruning combats overfitting by simplifying the tree, reduces model complexity, speeds up inference, and improves interpretability. It embodies the Occam's Razor principle: simpler trees generalize better.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between pre-pruning and post-pruning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pre-pruning (1.5 marks):** Also called early stopping, it halts the tree growth *during* induction based on criteria such as minimum information gain, minimum number of samples, or maximum depth. It is computationally cheaper but suffers from the horizon effect.
- **Post-pruning (1.5 marks):** Also called backward pruning, the tree is fully grown first and then subtrees are replaced by leaves if a pruning criterion (validation error, pessimistic error, cost-complexity) is satisfied. It is more accurate but computationally expensive and may require a separate validation set.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common mistakes students make that cost 2–3 marks:
> 1. Confusing **cost-complexity pruning** (CART) with **pessimistic error pruning** (Quinlan) — remember CART uses an explicit α, while PEP uses the binomial upper bound.
> 2. Forgetting to **define variables** in formulas (e.g., $N$, $f$, $\alpha$, $z$) before using them.
> 3. Skipping the **horizon effect** discussion when comparing pre- vs post-pruning.
> 4. In numeric problems, failing to **substitute values step-by-step** — examiners want to see the arithmetic, not just the final number.
> 5. Not drawing the **pruned tree diagram** when the question asks to "prune the given tree" — diagrams carry 2–3 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Cost-Complexity Pruning Focus

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the cost-complexity pruning algorithm used in CART. Derive the expression for the effective alpha $\alpha_{\text{eff}}(T_t)$ used to find the weakest link. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Cost-complexity pruning, introduced by Breiman et al. in the CART algorithm, is a post-pruning technique that balances misclassification cost against tree complexity via a tunable parameter $\alpha$.

2. **Cost-Complexity Function (2 marks):**
The objective is to minimize

$$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert \tilde{T} \vert$$

where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate of tree $T$ on training data, $\vert \tilde{T} \vert$ is the number of leaves, and $\alpha \ge 0$ is the complexity penalty.

3. **Weakest Link Derivation (3 marks):**
For an internal node $t$ with subtree $T_t$, define the error if collapsed to a leaf as $R(t)$ and the error of the subtree as $R(T_t)$. The number of leaves saved is $\vert \tilde{T}_t \vert - 1$. Setting the cost-complexity of the leaf equal to that of the subtree:

$$R(t) + \alpha \cdot 1 = R(T_t) + \alpha \cdot \vert \tilde{T}_t \vert$$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha_{\text{eff}}(T_t) = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert \tilde{T}_t \vert - 1}$$

4. **Algorithm (1 mark):** At each iteration, find the node with the smallest $\alpha_{\text{eff}}$, prune that subtree into a leaf, and repeat until only the root remains. Cross-validation on a held-out set selects the final $T_\alpha^*$.

**(b)** Compare pre-pruning and post-pruning techniques with suitable examples. What is the horizon effect? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Pre-pruning definition + 2 examples (2 marks):** Stop growing the tree when (i) all instances at a node belong to the same class, (ii) information gain falls below a threshold $\epsilon$, (iii) number of samples falls below a minimum, (iv) chi-squared test fails.

2. **Post-pruning definition + 2 examples (2 marks):** Grow the full tree, then apply (i) Reduced Error Pruning using a validation set, (ii) Pessimistic Error Pruning using Quinlan's binomial bound, (iii) Cost-Complexity Pruning using CART's $\alpha$, (iv) Minimum Error Pruning using a probabilistic model.

3. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** Pre-pruning is faster but suffers from the horizon effect; post-pruning is slower but produces more accurate trees because it sees the full structure.

4. **Horizon Effect (1 mark):** The horizon effect occurs when a pre-pruning rule stops a split that *seems* unpromising at the current node, but a *deeper* subtree below it would have been highly informative. Pre-pruning cannot look beyond the current horizon, so it mistakenly prunes useful structure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Pessimistic Error Pruning Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain Quinlan's Pessimistic Error Pruning with the necessary formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept (1 mark):** Pessimistic Error Pruning (PEP) is a post-pruning technique that estimates a node's *pessimistic* (upper-bound) error rate directly from the training data, without requiring a separate validation set. The intuition is that the resubstitution error underestimates the true error, so we add a statistical correction.

2. **Formula (2 marks):** Given a node with $N$ training instances and $f$ misclassifications, the pessimistic error upper bound is:

$$e = \frac{f + \frac{z^2}{2} + z\sqrt{\frac{f}{N} - \frac{f^2}{N} + \frac{z^2}{4}}}{N + z^2}$$

where $z$ is the confidence factor (Quinlan's default $z = 0.69$ for 50% confidence).

3. **Pruning Decision Rule (2 marks):** For a node $t$ with subtree $T_t$ having leaves $\tilde{T}_t$, prune the subtree into a leaf if:

$$e(t) \le \sum_{s \in \tilde{T}_t} e(s)$$

4. **Advantages & Limitations (2 marks):**
- *Advantages:* No validation set needed; uses only training data; fast.
- *Limitations:* The binomial assumption is an approximation; very conservative (prunes more than necessary) for small datasets; the choice of $z$ affects the result.

**(b)** A fully grown decision tree has a root node with $N = 50$ training instances, of which $f = 6$ are misclassified. The root has two child leaves, each with $(N_i, f_i) = (25, 2)$ and $(N_j, f_j) = (25, 4)$. Using Quinlan's pessimistic error formula with $z = 0.69$, determine whether the tree should be pruned. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $e(t)$ for the root (1 mark):**
Substituting $N = 50$, $f = 6$, $z = 0.69$:

$$e(t) = \frac{6 + \frac{0.69^2}{2} + 0.69\sqrt{\frac{6}{50} - \frac{36}{50 \cdot 50} + \frac{0.69^2}{4}}}{50 + 0.476}$$

Calculate numerator pieces:
- $\frac{z^2}{2} = \frac{0.4761}{2} = 0.2381$
- $\frac{f}{N} = \frac{6}{50} = 0.12$
- $\frac{f^2}{N} \cdot \frac{1}{N} = \frac{36}{50} \cdot \frac{1}{50} = 0.0144$
- $\frac{z^2}{4} = \frac{0.4761}{4} = 0.1190$
- Inside root: $0.12 - 0.0144 + 0.1190 = 0.2246$
- $\sqrt{0.2246} = 0.4740$
- $z \cdot \sqrt{\cdots} = 0.69 \times 0.4740 = 0.3271$

Numerator: $6 + 0.2381 + 0.3271 = 6.5652$
Denominator: $50.476$

$$e(t) = \frac{6.5652}{50.476} \approx 0.1301$$

**Step 2 — Compute $e(s_i)$ for leaf 1: $N = 25, f = 2$ (1 mark):**
- $\frac{z^2}{2} = 0.2381$
- $\frac{f}{N} = 0.08$
- $\frac{f^2}{N \cdot N} = \frac{4}{625} = 0.0064$
- $\frac{z^2}{4} = 0.1190$
- Inside root: $0.08 - 0.0064 + 0.1190 = 0.1926$
- $\sqrt{0.1926} = 0.4389$
- $0.69 \times 0.4389 = 0.3028$
- Numerator: $2 + 0.2381 + 0.3028 = 2.5409$
- Denominator: $25.476$
- $e(s_i) = \frac{2.5409}{25.476} \approx 0.0997$

**Step 3 — Compute $e(s_j)$ for leaf 2: $N = 25, f = 4$ (1 mark):**
- $\frac{f}{N} = 0.16$
- $\frac{f^2}{N^2} = \frac{16}{625} = 0.0256$
- Inside root: $0.16 - 0.0256 + 0.1190 = 0.2534$
- $\sqrt{0.2534} = 0.5034$
- $0.69 \times 0.5034 = 0.3473$
- Numerator: $4 + 0.2381 + 0.3473 = 4.5854$
- Denominator: $25.476$
- $e(s_j) = \frac{4.5854}{25.476} \approx 0.1800$

**Step 4 — Apply the Pruning Rule (1 mark):**
$$\sum e(\text{leaves}) = 0.0997 + 0.1800 = 0.2797$$
$$e(\text{root as leaf}) = 0.1301$$

Since $e(t) = 0.1301 \le 0.2797 = \sum e(s)$, the subtree **should be pruned** into a single leaf.

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The pessimistic error of the root (0.1301) is less than the sum of pessimistic errors of its two leaf children (0.2797), so collapsing the subtree into a single leaf is statistically justified. The pruned tree contains only the root node, classified by majority class.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pruning (Module 3, PEAET755):**
>
> - **Pruning** = reducing tree complexity to combat **overfitting**.
> - **Pre-pruning** = stop *during* growth (gain threshold, min samples, max depth). Suffers from **horizon effect**.
> - **Post-pruning** = stop *after* full growth (REP, PEP, CCP, MEP). More accurate, no horizon effect.
> - **CART Cost-Complexity:** $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \vert \tilde{T} \vert$. Effective alpha: $\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \frac{R(t) - R(T_t)}{\vert \tilde{T}_t \vert - 1}$.
> - **Quinlan PEP formula:** $e = \frac{f + z^2/2 + z\sqrt{f/N - f^2/N + z^2/4}}{N + z^2}$, with default $z = 0.69$.
> - **Pruning rule:** collapse subtree to leaf if pessimistic error of leaf ≤ sum of pessimistic errors of subtree leaves.
> - **Occam's Razor** justifies pruning: simpler models with equal training error generalize better.
> - **REP** requires a **validation set**; **PEP** does not (uses training-data binomial bound).
> - **Trade-off:** Pruning increases *training* error but decreases *generalization (test)* error.
> - In KTU exams, **always** define every variable in your formulas before plugging in numbers.
> - In numeric problems, show **step-by-step arithmetic** — partial credit is awarded for correct intermediate steps.
> - Remember to draw a **diagram of the pruned tree** when asked to "show the result" — it earns easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Discriminant Based Algorithm - Perceptron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Discriminant Based Algorithm - Perceptron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The **Perceptron** is a *linear discriminant-based supervised learning algorithm* introduced by **Frank Rosenblatt (1957)**. It is a single-layer artificial neural network that constructs a *hyperplane decision boundary* to separate two linearly separable classes in a $d$-dimensional feature space. Mathematically, it learns a linear discriminant function $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0$ whose sign determines the class label.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Perceptron belongs to the family of **Linear Discriminant Functions (LDFs)**, where the class-conditional decision rule is purely a linear function of the input features. It forms the **atomic building block** of every modern deep learning architecture — multilayer perceptrons, convolutional networks, and transformers are all non-linear stacks of perceptron-like linear threshold units.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club Door"

Imagine a strict **bouncer** at the entrance of an exclusive club. His job is to decide who gets in and who doesn't, based on a set of attributes:

- **Clothing style** (formal $\rightarrow$ high score, casual $\rightarrow$ low score)
- **Behaviour** (calm $\rightarrow$ positive, rowdy $\rightarrow$ negative)
- **Age** (older $\rightarrow$ safer, very young $\rightarrow$ risky)

He mentally assigns an **importance weight** to each attribute. For a person standing at the door, he computes:

$$
\text{Score} = (\text{weight}_1 \times \text{style}) + (\text{weight}_2 \times \text{behaviour}) + (\text{weight}_3 \times \text{age}) + \text{base\_bias}
$$

- If $\text{Score} \geq 0$ → **"Welcome in!"** (Class $+1$)
- If $\text{Score} < 0$ → **"Sorry, not tonight."** (Class $-1$)

If the bouncer makes a **wrong decision** (lets a rowdy person in, or rejects a well-dressed guest), he **adjusts the weights** of his mental model. Over time, his decision boundary becomes sharper. **This is exactly how the Perceptron learns.**

## 1.3 Why "Linear Discriminant"?

The classification rule is a **hyperplane** of the form $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$, which is a linear equation. Hence the algorithm is *linear*. A sample $\mathbf{x}$ is classified by the **sign** of this linear function, making it a *discriminant* function — it directly discriminates between classes without estimating probability densities.

## 1.4 Key Terminology — KTU High-Yield Glossary

> [!NOTE] **Mandatory Vocabulary for Module 3**
> - **Discriminant Function $g(\mathbf{x})$** — A scalar function whose value dictates class membership.
> - **Weight Vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$** — Coefficients of the linear combination of features.
> - **Bias $w_0$ (or $b$)** — Threshold offset that shifts the decision boundary away from the origin.
> - **Augmented Vector** — Concatenating a constant $1$ to $\mathbf{x}$ to absorb the bias into $\mathbf{w}$.
> - **Activation Function** — The non-linearity (here, the sign/sgn function) that produces the class output.
> - **Perceptron Criterion** — The cost function minimised by the learning rule.
> - **Linearly Separable** — Existence of a hyperplane that perfectly classifies all training samples.
> - **Convergence** — Finite-step stopping guarantee under the Novikoff theorem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model — Anatomy of a Perceptron

A perceptron operates on a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d]^{T}$ and consists of **five functional units**:

| Stage | Symbol | Operation |
|:------|:-------|:----------|
| 1. Input | $\mathbf{x}$ | Receive a $d$-dimensional feature vector |
| 2. Synaptic Weights | $\mathbf{w}$ | Multiply each input by a learnable weight |
| 3. Summation | $u$ | $u = \sum_{i=1}^{d} w_i x_i + w_0 = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0$ |
| 4. Activation | $\varphi(\cdot)$ | Apply the sign function: $\varphi(u) = \text{sgn}(u)$ |
| 5. Output | $\hat{y}$ | Class label $\in \{-1, +1\}$ |

> [!NOTE] **Augmented Vector Trick**
> Define $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = [1, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d]^{T}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{w}} = [w_0, w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_d]^{T}$. Then the discriminant collapses to the elegant form $g(\mathbf{x}) = \tilde{\mathbf{w}}^{T}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$, eliminating the bias term as a separate entity.

## 2.2 The Decision Rule

$$
\hat{y} = \text{sgn}\left(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0\right) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0 > 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0 < 0 \end{cases}
$$

The **decision boundary** is the geometric locus where $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, i.e., the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$.

- The **normal vector** of the hyperplane is $\mathbf{w}$.
- The **perpendicular distance** from any point $\mathbf{x}_k$ to the hyperplane is $\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k + w_0 \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

## 2.3 The Perceptron Learning Rule

### 2.3.1 Perceptron Criterion Function

The perceptron attempts to **minimise the number of misclassifications**. The classical criterion is:

$$
J_{p}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{\mathbf{x}_k \in \mathcal{M}} (-\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k \, y_k)
$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of **misclassified samples** and $y_k \in \{-1, +1\}$ is the true label. This is always non-negative because for any misclassified pair, $y_k$ and $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k$ share the same sign, making their product positive, and the negative sign makes $J_p$ non-negative.

### 2.3.2 Update Rule (Stochastic / Sample-by-Sample)

For a learning rate $\eta > 0$ and a misclassified sample $(\mathbf{x}_k, y_k)$:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{w}^{(\text{new})} = \mathbf{w}^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, y_k \, \mathbf{x}_k}
$$

$$
\boxed{w_0^{(\text{new})} = w_0^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, y_k}
$$

> [!TIP] **Intuition Behind the Sign**
> - If $y_k = +1$ but predicted as $-1$ (negative side of hyperplane), the update *pushes* $\mathbf{w}$ in the direction of $\mathbf{x}_k$ so the hyperplane rotates towards the correct side.
> - If $y_k = -1$ but predicted as $+1$, the update *pushes* $\mathbf{w}$ away from $\mathbf{x}_k$ for the same reason.

## 2.4 Algorithm — Compact Statement

> [!IMPORTANT] **Perceptron Training Algorithm (Pseudocode in Mathematical Form)**
> **Given:** Training set $\{(\mathbf{x}_1, y_1), \ldots, (\mathbf{x}_N, y_N)\}$ with $y_k \in \{-1, +1\}$; learning rate $\eta \in (0, 1]$; max epochs $E_{\max}$.
> 1. **Initialise** $\mathbf{w}^{(0)} \leftarrow \mathbf{0}$, $w_0^{(0)} \leftarrow 0$, epoch $e \leftarrow 0$, error flag $\leftarrow \text{true}$.
> 2. **Repeat while** $e < E_{\max}$ **and** error flag = true:
>        **(a)** Set error flag $\leftarrow$ false.
>        **(b)** For each $k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$:
>            • Compute $\hat{y}_k = \text{sgn}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k + w_0)$.
>            • If $\hat{y}_k \neq y_k$ (misclassification):
>                – $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta \, y_k \, \mathbf{x}_k$
>                – $w_0 \leftarrow w_0 + \eta \, y_k$
>                – Set error flag $\leftarrow$ true.
>        **(c)** $e \leftarrow e + 1$.
> 3. **Return** $\mathbf{w}^{\ast}, w_0^{\ast}$.

## 2.5 Novikoff's Convergence Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT] **Theoretical Foundation (Board-Favourite Theorem)**
> **Statement (Novikoff, 1962):** Let the training data be linearly separable with margin $\gamma > 0$, i.e., there exists a unit vector $\mathbf{w}^{\ast}$ with $\Vert \mathbf{w}^{\ast} \Vert = 1$ such that $y_k (\mathbf{w}^{\ast T}\mathbf{x}_k) \geq \gamma$ for all $k$. Assume $\Vert \mathbf{x}_k \Vert \leq R$. Then the perceptron algorithm makes **at most** $\left(\dfrac{R}{\gamma}\right)^{2}$ misclassifications before convergence.
>
> **Implication:** Convergence is **finite** and **data-dependent** (via $R$ and $\gamma$).

## 2.6 Limitations — The Honest Truth

| Limitation | Engineering Consequence |
|:-----------|:------------------------|
| **Linear separability required** | Fails on the **XOR problem**, the canonical Minsky-Papert (1969) counter-example |
| **No probability output** | Cannot express confidence; only hard $-1$ / $+1$ decisions |
| **Non-unique solution** | Final hyperplane depends on initial weights and sample order |
| **Sensitive to outliers** | A single noisy point in the training set can destabilise the boundary |
| **Slow convergence** | On "almost separable" data, the margin $\gamma$ is tiny and iterations explode |

## 2.7 Variants Worth Knowing

- **Batch Perceptron:** Update by summing the corrections over an entire epoch:
$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta \displaystyle\sum_{k \in \mathcal{M}} y_k \mathbf{x}_k$.
- **Pocket Algorithm (Gallant, 1986):** Keep the "best-so-far" weights in a pocket to handle non-separable data.
- **Multiclass Perceptron:** Use one perceptron per class with winner-takes-all decoding.
- **Structured Perceptron:** Extends to sequence labelling (Collins, 2002) — the workhorse of NLP tagging.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR):** First commercial neural application (Rosenblatt's Mark I perceptron, 1958).
- **Spam filtering:** Pre-deep-learning baseline.
- **Embedded edge AI:** Single-layer perceptron is the lightest possible classifier for microcontrollers.
- **Foundation for deep learning:** All neurons in modern networks descend from Rosenblatt's perceptron.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| **S.No.** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Notation / Units** |
|:---------:|:------------------------|:----------------------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Linear Discriminant | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0$ | $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, $w_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Augmented Form | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \tilde{\mathbf{w}}^{T}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ | $\tilde{\mathbf{w}}, \tilde{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$ |
| 3 | Decision Rule | $\hat{y} = \text{sgn}(g(\mathbf{x}))$ | $\hat{y} \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 4 | Decision Boundary | $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ | Hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 5 | Geometric Distance | $r = \dfrac{\vert g(\mathbf{x}) \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Distance (units of $\mathbf{x}$) |
| 6 | Perceptron Criterion | $J_p(\mathbf{w}) = \displaystyle\sum_{\mathbf{x}_k \in \mathcal{M}} -y_k \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k$ | Scalar, $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Weight Update | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \eta \, y_k \mathbf{x}_k$ | $\eta > 0$ (learning rate) |
| 8 | Bias Update | $w_0 \leftarrow w_0 + \eta \, y_k$ | Scalar |
| 9 | Margin | $\gamma = \min_{k} y_k \mathbf{w}^{\ast T}\mathbf{x}_k$ | $\gamma > 0$ for separability |
| 10 | Convergence Bound | $N_{\text{mistakes}} \leq \left(\dfrac{R}{\gamma}\right)^{2}$ | Finite integer |
| 11 | Bound on $\Vert \mathbf{x}_k \Vert$ | $R = \max_{k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_k \Vert$ | $R > 0$ |
| 12 | Gradient of $J_p$ | $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J_p = \displaystyle\sum_{\mathbf{x}_k \in \mathcal{M}} -y_k \mathbf{x}_k$ | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 13 | Optimality Condition | $\mathbf{w}^{\ast T}\mathbf{x}_k \, y_k > 0 \quad \forall k$ | Strict separability |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From the Perceptron Criterion to the Update Rule

We want a learning rule that **decreases** $J_p(\mathbf{w})$ when a weight is updated. We use the **method of gradient descent**:

**Step 1 — Define the criterion.** For a *single* misclassified sample $(\mathbf{x}_k, y_k)$, the contribution to the criterion is

$$
J_p^{(k)}(\mathbf{w}) = -y_k \, \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k
$$

Because $y_k$ and the predicted $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k$ have the **same sign** (misclassification), their product is positive, and the negative sign makes $J_p^{(k)} > 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient.**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J_p^{(k)} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \left[ -y_k \, \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k \right] = -y_k \, \mathbf{x}_k
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the gradient-descent step.** Standard gradient descent updates weights in the **negative gradient** direction:

$$
\mathbf{w}^{(\text{new})} = \mathbf{w}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J_p^{(k)} = \mathbf{w}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \left( -y_k \mathbf{x}_k \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.** The two negatives cancel:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{w}^{(\text{new})} = \mathbf{w}^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, y_k \, \mathbf{x}_k}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify that the update reduces the criterion.** Take the dot product of the change $\Delta \mathbf{w} = \eta y_k \mathbf{x}_k$ with the negative gradient:

$$
\Delta \mathbf{w} \cdot \left( -\nabla J_p \right) = \eta y_k \mathbf{x}_k \cdot y_k \mathbf{x}_k = \eta \, y_k^{2} \, \Vert \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^{2} \geq 0
$$

This proves the update monotonically reduces $J_p$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Favourite Style)

> [!NOTE] **Problem Setup**
> Train a perceptron on the following 2-D, two-class dataset with $\eta = 1$:
> | Sample | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
> |:------:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
> | $\mathbf{x}_1$ | $1$ | $1$ | $+1$ |
> | $\mathbf{x}_2$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ |
> | $\mathbf{x}_3$ | $2$ | $-1$ | $+1$ |
>
> Start with $\mathbf{w}^{(0)} = [0, 0]^{T}$, $w_0^{(0)} = 0$.

### Epoch 1

**Iteration 1 — Sample $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,1), y_1 = +1$:**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}_1) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}_1 + w_0 = 0 + 0 = 0
$$

Convention: treat $g = 0$ as a **misclassification** (boundary case). Update:

$$
\mathbf{w} = [0,0]^{T} + 1 \cdot (+1) \cdot [1, 1]^{T} = [1, 1]^{T}, \quad w_0 = 0 + 1 \cdot (+1) = 1
$$

**Iteration 2 — Sample $\mathbf{x}_2 = (-1,-1), y_2 = -1$:**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}_2) = (1)(-1) + (1)(-1) + 1 = -1
$$

$$
\hat{y}_2 = \text{sgn}(-1) = -1 \;\; \checkmark \text{ correct, no update}
$$

**Iteration 3 — Sample $\mathbf{x}_3 = (2,-1), y_3 = +1$:**

$$
g(\mathbf{x}_3) = (1)(2) + (1)(-1) + 1 = 2
$$

$$
\hat{y}_3 = \text{sgn}(2) = +1 \;\; \checkmark \text{ correct, no update}
$$

**End of Epoch 1** — No misclassification occurred in iterations 2 and 3, but iteration 1 triggered an update. Continue.

### Epoch 2

**Iteration 1 — $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,1), y_1 = +1$:** $g = (1)(1)+(1)(1)+1 = 3 \rightarrow \hat{y}=+1$ ✓

**Iteration 2 — $\mathbf{x}_2 = (-1,-1), y_2 = -1$:** $g = -1-1+1=-1 \rightarrow \hat{y}=-1$ ✓

**Iteration 3 — $\mathbf{x}_3 = (2,-1), y_3 = +1$:** $g = 2-1+1=2 \rightarrow \hat{y}=+1$ ✓

**No errors! Algorithm has converged.**

**Final decision boundary:**

$$
\mathbf{w}^{\ast T}\mathbf{x} + w_0^{\ast} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_1 + x_2 + 1 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x_2 = -x_1 - 1
$$

### 3.2.1 Verification of the Boundary

| Sample | $(x_1, x_2)$ | $x_2 + x_1 + 1$ | Sign | Matches $y$? |
|:------:|:------------:|:---------------:|:----:|:------------:|
| 1 | $(1,1)$ | $3 > 0$ | $+$ | $+1$ ✓ |
| 2 | $(-1,-1)$ | $-1 < 0$ | $-$ | $-1$ ✓ |
| 3 | $(2,-1)$ | $2 > 0$ | $+$ | $+1$ ✓ |

The boundary is geometrically a line in the 2-D plane. For Class $+1$ (samples 1 and 3), the line $x_2 = -x_1 - 1$ correctly places them on the upper side; for Class $-1$ (sample 2), the lower side.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Convergence Bound (Novikoff Theorem Sketch)

**Setup.** Assume there exists a weight vector $\mathbf{w}^{\ast}$ with $\Vert \mathbf{w}^{\ast} \Vert = 1$ and a margin $\gamma > 0$ such that for all $k$:

$$
y_k (\mathbf{w}^{\ast T}\mathbf{x}_k) \geq \gamma, \quad \Vert \mathbf{x}_k \Vert \leq R
$$

**Step 1 — Lower bound on the dot product with $\mathbf{w}^{\ast}$.** Each correct update **increases** the projection:

$$
\mathbf{w}^{(t+1) T} \mathbf{w}^{\ast} = \mathbf{w}^{(t)T}\mathbf{w}^{\ast} + \eta \, y_k \, \mathbf{x}_k^{T} \mathbf{w}^{\ast}
$$

Since $y_k \mathbf{x}_k^{T}\mathbf{w}^{\ast} \geq \gamma$ and using $\eta = 1$:

$$
\mathbf{w}^{(t+1) T} \mathbf{w}^{\ast} \geq \mathbf{w}^{(t)T}\mathbf{w}^{\ast} + \gamma
$$

By induction, after $M$ mistakes:

$$
\mathbf{w}^{(M) T} \mathbf{w}^{\ast} \geq M \gamma
$$

**Step 2 — Upper bound via Cauchy-Schwarz.** Because $\Vert \mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} \Vert^{2} = \Vert \mathbf{w}^{(t)} + y_k \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} \Vert^{2} &= \Vert \mathbf{w}^{(t)} \Vert^{2} + 2 y_k \mathbf{w}^{(t)T}\mathbf{x}_k + \Vert \mathbf{x}_k \Vert^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **misclassified** sample, $y_k \mathbf{w}^{(t)T}\mathbf{x}_k < 0$, so we can upper bound:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} \Vert^{2} \leq \Vert \mathbf{w}^{(t)} \Vert^{2} + R^{2}
$$

By induction:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{w}^{(M)} \Vert^{2} \leq M R^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine using Cauchy-Schwarz.** Cauchy-Schwarz states $\mathbf{w}^{(M)T}\mathbf{w}^{\ast} \leq \Vert \mathbf{w}^{(M)} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w}^{\ast} \Vert = \Vert \mathbf{w}^{(M)} \Vert$:

$$
M \gamma \leq \mathbf{w}^{(M)T}\mathbf{w}^{\ast} \leq \Vert \mathbf{w}^{(M)} \Vert \leq \sqrt{M} \, R
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
M^{2} \gamma^{2} \leq M R^{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad M \leq \left(\frac{R}{\gamma}\right)^{2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The perceptron makes at most $(R/\gamma)^{2}$ mistakes. This bound is **independent** of the dimension $d$ — a remarkable result.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cases Where the Perceptron Fails

| Case | Reason | Illustration |
|:-----|:-------|:-------------|
| **XOR** | Data not linearly separable | Output $0$ for inputs $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$; output $1$ for $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$ |
| **Concentric circles** | Decision boundary is non-linear (radial) | No single hyperplane can separate |
| **Class overlap** | $J_p$ oscillates; no convergence | Fisher's Iris *versicolor* vs *virginica* in 1-D |
| **High noise** | Single noisy point pulls the boundary dramatically | Outlier in 2-D plane |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Perceptron Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT x1"] --> SUM["SUMMATION UNIT"]
    B["INPUT x2"] --> SUM
    C["INPUT x3"] --> SUM
    D["INPUT x4"] --> SUM
    W1["WEIGHT w1"] --> SUM
    W2["WEIGHT w2"] --> SUM
    W3["WEIGHT w3"] --> SUM
    W4["WEIGHT w4"] --> SUM
    BIAS["BIAS w0"] --> SUM
    SUM --> ACT["ACTIVATION FUNCTION sgn"]
    ACT --> OUT["OUTPUT CLASS +1 or -1"]
    ERR["ERROR SIGNAL y minus hat y"] -.-> W1
    ERR -.-> W2
    ERR -.-> W3
    ERR -.-> W4
    ERR -.-> BIAS
    ERR -.-> SUM
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> The four inputs $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4]$ are multiplied elementwise by the weights $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4]$, summed with the bias $w_0$, passed through the sgn activation, and yield a binary output. The **dashed** feedback arrows represent the error-driven weight update pathway.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Perceptron Learning Algorithm (Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["START"] --> P2["INITIALISE w = 0, w0 = 0, eta, epoch = 0"]
    P2 --> P3["epoch LESS THAN Emax"]
    P3 -- YES --> P4["FOR k = 1 TO N"]
    P4 --> P5["COMPUTE hat yk = sgn of w transpose xk PLUS w0"]
    P5 --> P6["hat yk EQUAL TO yk"]
    P6 -- YES --> P7["NO UPDATE, go to next sample"]
    P6 -- NO --> P8["UPDATE w = w PLUS eta yk xk"]
    P8 --> P9["UPDATE w0 = w0 PLUS eta yk"]
    P9 --> P10["SET error flag = true"]
    P10 --> P7
    P7 --> P11["MORE SAMPLES REMAINING"]
    P11 -- YES --> P5
    P11 -- NO --> P12["INCREMENT epoch"]
    P12 --> P13["error flag EQUALS false"]
    P13 -- YES --> P14["RETURN final w, w0 and STOP"]
    P13 -- NO --> P3
    P3 -- NO --> P14
```

> [!TIP] **Convergence Logic**
> The outer loop terminates either when the **error flag** stays false across an entire epoch (perfect classification) or when the epoch counter hits $E_{\max}$ (the user-defined cap to prevent infinite looping on non-separable data).

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Linear Decision Boundary in 2-D Feature Space

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PLANE ["2 D FEATURE SPACE x1 x2"]
        POS["REGION CLASS PLUS 1"]
        NEG["REGION CLASS MINUS 1"]
        HPL["DECISION BOUNDARY w1 x1 PLUS w2 x2 PLUS w0 EQUALS 0"]
    end
    P1S["SAMPLE POSITIVE 1"] -.-> POS
    P2S["SAMPLE POSITIVE 2"] -.-> POS
    N1S["SAMPLE NEGATIVE 1"] -.-> NEG
    N2S["SAMPLE NEGATIVE 2"] -.-> NEG
    POS --- HPL
    NEG --- HPL
```

> [!NOTE] **Geometric Meaning**
> The hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ is the perpendicular bisector between the two convex hulls of the classes. The weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is the **outward normal** of this hyperplane. In 2-D, this is a straight line; in 3-D, a flat plane; in higher dimensions, a true hyperplane.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block Diagram of Training Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1["TRAINING DATA xk yk"] --> Q2["PERCEPTRON MODEL"]
    Q2 --> Q3["PREDICT hat yk"]
    Q3 --> Q4["COMPARE hat yk WITH yk"]
    Q4 -- CORRECT --> Q5["KEEP w UNCHANGED"]
    Q4 -- WRONG --> Q6["COMPUTE UPDATE Delta w = eta yk xk"]
    Q6 --> Q7["UPDATE w AND w0"]
    Q7 --> Q2
    Q5 --> Q8["NEXT EPOCH OR STOP"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Module 3 Takeaway**
> This feedback loop is the **soul** of supervised learning. The perceptron is the simplest non-trivial instance; logistic regression, SVMs, and neural networks all have analogous update loops with more sophisticated cost functions and update rules.
