# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: ` [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define clustering. Why is it considered an unsupervised learning problem?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Clustering is the process of organizing a collection of unlabeled data points into groups (clusters) such that points in the same group are more similar to each other than to points in other groups. It is called *unsupervised* because the algorithm receives **no class labels, target outputs, or reward signal** during training — it must discover the natural structure of the data purely from the geometric or statistical properties of the feature vectors. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for properties, 1 for explaining "unsupervised".)

---

### Question 2: ` [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three linkage criteria used in agglomerative hierarchical clustering.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Single Linkage (MIN):** Distance between two clusters is the *minimum* distance between any pair of points, one from each cluster. Tends to produce long, chain-like clusters. (1 mark)
> 2. **Complete Linkage (MAX):** Distance between two clusters is the *maximum* distance between any pair of points, one from each cluster. Produces compact, equal-diameter clusters; sensitive to outliers. (1 mark)
> 3. **Average Linkage (UPGMA):** Distance between two clusters is the *mean* of all cross-pair distances. A balanced compromise between single and complete linkage. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — ` [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm. State its objective function and prove that the optimal centroid update is the sample mean of the assigned points.

**(b) [7 marks]** Given the 2-D data points $P_1(2, 10)$, $P_2(2, 5)$, $P_3(8, 4)$, $P_4(5, 8)$, $P_5(7, 5)$, $P_6(6, 4)$, $P_7(1, 2)$, $P_8(4, 9)$ and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2 = (5, 8)$, perform K-Means clustering with $K = 2$ using Euclidean distance. Show all iterations and report the final cluster centroids and the value of the objective function $J$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The K-Means algorithm partitions $N$ data points into $K$ non-overlapping clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS):**

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} \Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

**Algorithm (Lloyd's iteration, 1957):**

1. **Initialize** $K$ centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ (randomly, by Forgy method, or via K-Means++).
2. **Repeat until convergence:**
   - **Assignment step:** $c^{(i)} = \arg\min_{j} \Vert x^{(i)} - \mu_j \Vert^2$
   - **Update step:** $\mu_j = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{i : c^{(i)} = j} x^{(i)}$
3. **Stop** when assignments no longer change (or $J$ decreases by less than $\varepsilon$).

**Proof of optimality of the sample-mean update (see §3.1 for full derivation):** Taking $\partial S_j / \partial \mu_j = 0$ and solving yields $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert}\sum_{x^{(i)} \in C_j} x^{(i)}$. The Hessian $2\vert C_j \vert I_d$ is positive definite, confirming this is a *unique global minimum* of the within-cluster sum of squares for that fixed assignment. (7 marks: 2 for objective, 2 for algorithm, 3 for proof.)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

See §3.2 above. The complete step-by-step iteration is shown there. Key result:

- $C_1 = \{(2, 10), (2, 5), (1, 2)\}$, $\mu_1 = (5/3, 17/3) \approx (1.67, 5.67)$
- $C_2 = \{(8, 4), (5, 8), (7, 5), (6, 4), (4, 9)\}$, $\mu_2 = (6, 6)$
- Algorithm converges in 3 iterations.
- $J_{\text{final}} = \dfrac{100}{3} + 32 = \dfrac{196}{3} \approx 65.33$

(7 marks: 2 for iteration 1, 2 for iteration 2, 2 for iteration 3 and final $J$, 1 for the convergence statement.)

---

### Question B — ` [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 — Alternative]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss agglomerative hierarchical clustering in detail. Explain the construction of a dendrogram and discuss any three linkage criteria with their formulas.

**(b) [7 marks]** Given four 2-D points $A(1, 1)$, $B(2, 1)$, $C(5, 4)$, $D(5, 5)$, perform agglomerative hierarchical clustering using **single linkage** and Euclidean distance. Show all merge steps and draw the resulting dendrogram. Also identify the cluster assignment when the dendrogram is cut at height $h = 2.5$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

Agglomerative hierarchical clustering builds a hierarchy of clusters in a **bottom-up** fashion (the opposite — divisive — proceeds top-down and is rarely used in practice). The algorithm:

1. Begins with $N$ singleton clusters (each point is its own cluster).
2. At each step, identifies the two *closest* clusters according to a chosen linkage criterion.
3. Merges them into a single new cluster.
4. Updates the proximity matrix to reflect the new cluster.
5. Repeats steps 2–4 until a single cluster containing all $N$ points remains, or a stopping criterion (number of clusters $K$, or maximum linkage distance) is met.

The output is a **dendrogram** — a binary tree where:
- Each leaf represents a single data point.
- Each internal node represents a merge of two sub-clusters.
- The **height of an internal node** equals the linkage distance at which the merge occurred.

Reading a dendrogram: cutting horizontally at any height $h$ yields a flat partition. If we want exactly $K$ clusters, we cut at the height where the horizontal line crosses $K$ vertical lines.

**Three linkage criteria:**

$$d_{\text{single}}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a,\, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$

$$d_{\text{complete}}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a,\, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$

$$d_{\text{average}}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x \in C_a} \sum_{y \in C_b} d(x, y)$$

Single linkage tends to produce *chaining*; complete linkage produces *tight, compact* clusters; average linkage balances the two. (7 marks: 2 for algorithm, 2 for dendrogram explanation, 3 for linkage formulas and discussion.)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

See §3.3 above. The detailed merge steps are:

| Step | Merge | Height | Resulting Clusters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | 0 | $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}$ |
| 1 | $A \cup B$ | 1.00 | $\{A, B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}$ |
| 2 | $C \cup D$ | 1.00 | $\{A, B\}, \{C, D\}$ |
| 3 | $\{A, B\} \cup \{C, D\}$ | 4.24 | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ |

**Dendrogram:** Heights of merges are $1, 1, 4.24$ — see the textual tree in §4.4.

**Cut at $h = 2.5$:** Since $2.5$ lies between the first/second merges (height 1) and the final merge (height 4.24), the dendrogram has *two* branches still unmerged. Hence we obtain **$K = 2$ clusters**: $C_1 = \{A, B\}$ and $C_2 = \{C, D\}$.

(7 marks: 1 for initial matrix, 2 each for first two merges, 1 for final merge, 1 for cut interpretation.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE (Module 4):**
> 1. **Confusing K-Means and K-Medoids:** K-Means uses the *mean* (sensitive to outliers). K-Medoids use the *medoid* — the most centrally located actual point in the cluster. Do NOT interchange them in answers.
> 2. **Forgetting to update centroids:** Many students do the assignment step correctly but skip the *update* step. The exam explicitly asks for "all iterations" — every iteration *must* have both an assignment AND a centroid update.
> 3. **Using the wrong linkage formula in hierarchical clustering:** "Distance between clusters" is *not* the distance between centroids unless you specifically use **centroid linkage**. Single, complete, and average linkages all use *cross-pair* distances.
> 4. **Reporting $J$ in the wrong units:** $J$ has units of (feature unit)². If asked for $J$, present the numerical value AND the formula. The examiner awards 1 mark for stating the formula.
> 5. **Not drawing a dendrogram when asked:** A textual description earns partial credit; a properly drawn dendrogram with *merge heights labeled* earns full marks. Use the Mermaid-style tree shown in §4.4.
> 6. **Conflating "agglomerative" with "divisive":** Agglomerative = bottom-up. Divisive = top-down. The KTU 2024 syllabus almost exclusively asks about agglomerative.
> 7. **Assuming K-Means always finds the global minimum:** It does not. The result is initialization-dependent. Always run K-Means multiple times with different seeds and report the run with the lowest $J$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this before the exam):**
>
> - Clustering is **unsupervised**; the algorithm has **no labels** to learn from.
> - K-Means is **prototype-based** and produces a **flat partition**; it needs $K$ in advance.
> - The K-Means objective is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / inertia)**.
> - The optimal centroid for a fixed assignment is the **sample mean** of the points in that cluster — *proof via gradient = 0*.
> - K-Means has **two steps per iteration**: Assignment and Update. Both must be shown.
> - K-Means is **guaranteed to converge** to a *local* minimum in a *finite* number of steps.
> - K-Means time complexity is $O(NKT)$ per iteration.
> - Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is **bottom-up**: merge the two closest clusters at every step.
> - A **dendrogram** is the canonical output; cut it horizontally to obtain a flat partition.
> - **Single linkage** = min of cross-pair distances. **Complete linkage** = max. **Average linkage** = mean. **Ward's** = minimizes WCSS increase.
> - Use **Euclidean distance** unless the question specifies otherwise. State the metric.
> - Hierarchical clustering is **deterministic** (no random initialization).
> - Hierarchical time complexity is $O(N^3)$ in the naive implementation.
> - The **Elbow method** and the **Silhouette coefficient** are the two standard tools to choose $K$.
> - Common pitfalls: confusing linkage with centroid distance; forgetting to update centroids; not labeling dendrogram heights; using $J$ as a global optimum rather than a local one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cluster Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET755 - PATTERN RECOGNITION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cluster Validation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Cluster Validation** is the formal process of objectively and quantitatively evaluating the quality, reliability, and meaningfulness of clustering results produced by an unsupervised learning algorithm. It assesses three fundamental questions: (1) **Clustering Tendency** — whether the data possesses any non-random grouping structure at all, (2) **Clustering Quality** — how well the discovered partition fits the data given a fixed number of clusters *k*, and (3) **Clustering Stability** — how robust the partition is under perturbations of the data or algorithm parameters. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In unsupervised learning, ground-truth labels are typically *absent*. Therefore, cluster validation is the *only* objective mechanism to confirm whether a clustering algorithm has discovered a genuine structure or merely imposed an arbitrary grouping on noise. The three families of validation techniques recognized in the KTU 2024 Pattern Recognition syllabus are: | Family | Ground Truth Required? | Purpose | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **External Indices** | Yes | Compares discovered clusters with a known reference partition. | | **Internal Indices** | No | Uses only intrinsic information (data + cluster labels). | | **Relative Indices** | No | Compares multiple partitions produced by varying parameters. | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Warehouse Inspector" Imagine you are an inspector at a large logistics warehouse where a robot has automatically sorted thousands of mixed parcels into bins. * **Clustering** is the robot's sorting action. * **Cluster Validation** is your quality check. You walk in with three different quality control approaches: 1. **External Validation** → You have the *original shipping manifest* (ground truth). You count how many parcels ended up in the correct bin. This is the most reliable check, but you rarely have the manifest in real life. 2. **Internal Validation** → You don't have a manifest, so you judge by *intrinsic harmony* — are the parcels inside each bin very similar to each other, and are bins very different from one another? You measure the *purity of each bin* and the *separation between bins* using only the parcels' physical dimensions. 3. **Relative Validation** → You ask the robot to sort the parcels into 2 bins, 3 bins, 4 bins, and so on. You then ask: *"In which scenario are the bins the tightest and most distinct?"* The best value of *k* wins. ### 1.3 Core Importance > [!IMPORTANT] > Without validation, a clustering result is *just a guess*. Validation transforms clustering from an art into a measurable engineering discipline — critical in medical diagnosis, customer segmentation, and anomaly detection. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 External Validation Indices (Ground Truth Available) External indices require a **reference partition** $P^*$ and a **discovered partition** $P$. The comparison is built upon a fundamental **contingency table** of size $2 \times 2$ derived from counting pairwise relationships between the two partitions. For every pair of data points $(x_i, x_j)$, four possibilities exist: | Pair Relationship | Same in $P^*$ | Different in $P^*$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Same in $P$** | $a$ (SS) | $b$ (SD) | | **Different in $P$** | $c$ (DS) | $d$ (DD) | Where: * $a$ = pairs in the same cluster in both partitions. * $b$ = pairs together in $P$ but separated in $P^*$. * $c$ = pairs separated in $P$ but together in $P^*$. * $d$ = pairs separated in both partitions. * Total number of pairs: $M = \binom{N}{2} = a + b + c + d$. #### 2.1.1 Rand Index (RI) Measures the fraction of pairs on which the two partitions agree (either both together or both separated). $$ RI = \frac{a + d}{a + b + c + d} = \frac{a + d}{\binom{N}{2}} $$ Range: $[0, 1]$. Higher is better. #### 2.1.2 Jaccard Coefficient (JC) Only considers the "together" agreement, ignoring the "separated" agreement. $$ JC = \frac{a}{a + b + c} $$ Range: $[0, 1]$. Higher is better. Tends to penalize false negatives more than the Rand Index. #### 2.1.3 Fowlkes–Mallows Index (FMI) Geometric mean of precision and recall on pair assignments. $$ FMI = \frac{a}{\sqrt{(a + b)(a + c)}} $$ #### 2.1.4 F-Measure (Precision/Recall on Clusters) For each discovered cluster $C_j$ and reference cluster $\Omega_i$, compute precision and recall, then take the max $F(i,j)$ and average weighted by cluster size. $$ F = \sum_i \frac{n_i}{N} \max_j F(i,j) \quad ; \quad F(i,j) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{prec} \cdot \text{rec}}{\text{prec} + \text{rec}} $$ ### 2.2 Internal Validation Indices (No Ground Truth) #### 2.2.1 Silhouette Coefficient (Rousseeuw, 1987) For each point $x_i$ in cluster $C_k$: * $a(i)$ = **mean intra-cluster distance** (how well $x_i$ fits its own cluster). * $b(i)$ = **smallest mean inter-cluster distance** to any other cluster. * **Silhouette score** of $x_i$: $$ s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}} $$ * **Overall Silhouette** = mean of $s(i)$ for all $N$ points. Range: $[-1, +1]$. Value near $+1$ indicates well-clustered; near $0$ indicates overlapping clusters; negative implies potential misclassification. #### 2.2.2 Davies–Bouldin Index (DB) Lower is better. Based on the ratio of intra-cluster scatter to inter-cluster separation. $$ DB = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i} \left( \frac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}} \right) $$ Where $S_i$ is the average distance of points in cluster $i$ to its centroid, and $M_{ij}$ is the distance between centroids of clusters $i$ and $j$. #### 2.2.3 Dunn Index (D) $$ D = \frac{\min_{1 \leq i < j \leq k} \delta(C_i, C_j)}{\max_{1 \leq m \leq k} \Delta(C_m)} $$ Where $\delta(C_i, C_j)$ is the inter-cluster distance (smallest pairwise distance between clusters) and $\Delta(C_m)$ is the intra-cluster diameter. Higher is better. Sensitive to noise. #### 2.2.4 Calinski–Harabasz Index (CH) — Variance Ratio Criterion $$ CH = \frac{SS_B / (k-1)}{SS_W / (N-k)} $$ Where $SS_B$ = between-cluster sum of squares, $SS_W$ = within-cluster sum of squares. Higher is better. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** When writing indices, *always* state the **range**, the **optimal direction** (maximize/minimize), and any **assumptions** (e.g., Euclidean distance, convex clusters). Examiners award 1 mark for a properly framed interpretation. | Index | Type | Formula (Concise) | Range | Optimal | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Rand Index | External | $RI = (a+d) / \binom{N}{2}$ | $[0,1]$ | Max | | Jaccard | External | $JC = a / (a+b+c)$ | $[0,1]$ | Max | | Fowlkes–Mallows | External | $FMI = a / \sqrt{(a+b)(a+c)}$ | $[0,1]$ | Max | | F-Measure | External | $F = \sum_i (n_i/N) \max_j F(i,j)$ | $[0,1]$ | Max | | Silhouette | Internal | $s(i) = (b(i)-a(i))/\max(a(i),b(i))$ | $[-1,1]$ | Max | | Davies–Bouldin | Internal | $DB = (1/k) \sum_i \max_j (S_i+S_j)/M_{ij}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Min | | Dunn | Internal | $D = \min_{ij}\delta / \max_m \Delta$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Max | | Calinski–Harabasz | Internal | $CH = (SS_B/(k-1))/(SS_W/(N-k))$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Max | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility * **Bioinformatics:** Validating gene-expression clusters in cancer subtyping (Silhouette + F-measure on known cell lines). * **Image Segmentation:** Choosing the right *k* in color quantization using Calinski–Harabasz. * **Anomaly Detection:** Negative silhouette scores flag potential outliers. * **Customer Analytics:** Davies–Bouldin to robustly compare different segmentation solutions under noisy transactional data. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pair-Counting Foundation for External Indices **Step 1 — Set up the pair counts.** Given two partitions $P = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ (discovered) and $P^* = \{\Omega_1, \Omega_2, \dots, \Omega_g\}$ (reference) of the same $N$ data points, define the contingency entry: $$ n_{ij} = \vert C_i \cap \Omega_j \vert $$ So the contingency matrix is $\mathbf{N} = [n_{ij}]$ of size $k \times g$ with row sums $n_{i\cdot}$ and column sums $n_{\cdot j}$. **Step 2 — Express totals.** Total "together in $P$" pairs: $$ a + b = \sum_i \binom{n_{i\cdot}}{2} $$ Total "together in $P^*$" pairs: $$ a + c = \sum_j \binom{n_{\cdot j}}{2} $$ Total "together in *both*" pairs: $$ a = \sum_{i,j} \binom{n_{ij}}{2} $$ Total "separated in both" pairs: $$ d = \binom{N}{2} - (a+b+c) = \binom{N}{2} - \sum_i \binom{n_{i\cdot}}{2} - \sum_j \binom{n_{\cdot j}}{2} + \sum_{ij} \binom{n_{ij}}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Compute each external index** by substituting into the formulas of Section 2. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — External Validation Consider 8 points, ground truth has 2 clusters $\{1,2,3,4\}$ and $\{5,6,7,8\}$. Discovered partition $P = \{1,2,5\}, \{3,4,6,7,8\}$. **Contingency Matrix:** $$ \mathbf{N} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} $$ Row sums: $n_{1\cdot}=3$, $n_{2\cdot}=5$. Column sums: $n_{\cdot 1}=4$, $n_{\cdot 2}=4$. Total $N=8$. **Step 1 — Compute $a$:** $$ a = \binom{2}{2} + \binom{1}{2} + \binom{2}{2} + \binom{3}{2} = 1 + 0 + 1 + 3 = 5 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $a+b$ and $a+c$:** $$ a+b = \binom{3}{2} + \binom{5}{2} = 3 + 10 = 13 $$ $$ a+c = \binom{4}{2} + \binom{4}{2} = 6 + 6 = 12 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $b$, $c$, $d$:** $$ b = 13 - 5 = 8 $$ $$ c = 12 - 5 = 7 $$ $$ d = \binom{8}{2} - 5 - 8 - 7 = 28 - 20 = 8 $$ **Step 4 — Verify totals:** $a+b+c+d = 5+8+7+8 = 28 = \binom{8}{2}$. ✔ **Step 5 — Compute Rand Index:** $$ RI = \frac{a+d}{a+b+c+d} = \frac{5+8}{28} = \frac{13}{28} \approx 0.464 $$ **Step 6 — Compute Jaccard Coefficient:** $$ JC = \frac{a}{a+b+c} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 $$ **Step 7 — Compute Fowlkes–Mallows Index:** $$ FMI = \frac{a}{\sqrt{(a+b)(a+c)}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{13 \times 12}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{156}} \approx 0.400 $$ > [!NOTE] > The relatively low values reflect that the discovered partition placed point 5 in the wrong cluster — a common real-world scenario where external validation quantifies the algorithm's error rate. ### 3.3 Derivation: Silhouette Coefficient Geometric Meaning **Step 1 — Define intra-cluster cohesion $a(i)$.** For $x_i$ in cluster $C_I$: $$ a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_I \vert - 1} \sum_{x_j \in C_I,\, j \neq i} d(x_i, x_j) $$ This is the mean dissimilarity of $x_i$ to all other points in its own cluster. **Step 2 — Define nearest-neighbor cluster separation $b(i)$.** $$ b(i) = \min_{J \neq I} \frac{1}{\vert C_J \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_J} d(x_i, x_j) $$ This is the mean distance to the points in the *closest* neighboring cluster. **Step 3 — Construct the silhouette.** $$ s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}} $$ **Interpretation proof:** * If $b(i) \gg a(i)$ → $s(i) \to +1$ (well-clustered). * If $b(i) \approx a(i)$ → $s(i) \to 0$ (on cluster boundary). * If $b(i) \ll a(i)$ → $s(i) \to -1$ (possibly misassigned). **Step 4 — Aggregate.** Overall silhouette is the mean: $$ S = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} s(i) $$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Validator | Scenario | Recommended Index | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Ground truth available, small data | F-Measure + Rand | Interpretable, no zero-count issues | | Ground truth available, large data | Adjusted Rand (ARI) | Corrects for chance agreement | | No ground truth, convex clusters | Silhouette / Calinski–Harabasz | Fast, geometrically meaningful | | No ground truth, non-convex / arbitrary shapes | Dunn / Density-based validity | Handles non-spherical clusters | | Comparing multiple *k* values | Davies–Bouldin / CH | Compact and discriminative | | High noise levels | Silhouette with robust distance | Resistant to single outliers | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Cluster Validation Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Have a Clustering Result] --> nodeB{Is Ground Truth
Available?} nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC[External Validation] nodeB -- No --> nodeD{Goal of Validation?} nodeC --> nodeC1[Compute Contingency
Matrix N_ij] nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Pair Counting
a b c d] nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Apply External
Indices: RI, JC,
FMI, F-Measure] nodeD -- Compare k values --> nodeE[Relative Validation] nodeD -- Assess Quality --> nodeF[Internal Validation] nodeE --> nodeE1[Elbow Method] nodeE --> nodeE2[Gap Statistic] nodeE --> nodeE3[Run Algorithm
for k = 2 to k_max] nodeF --> nodeF1[Internal Indices:
Silhouette, DB,
Dunn, CH] nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Cluster Tendency
Test: Hopkins] nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Final Quality
Assessment] nodeC3 --> nodeF3 nodeE3 --> nodeF3 nodeF3 --> nodeG[Report Validation
Score and Recommendation] style nodeA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeC1 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeC2 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeC3 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeE1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeE2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeE3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeF1 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeF2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeF3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Index Architecture (Silhouette) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Input Stage] nodeS1A[Dataset X
size N] --> nodeS1B[Distance Matrix D
NxN] nodeS1B --> nodeS1C[Discovered
Partition P] end subgraph S2[Computation Stage] nodeS1C --> nodeS2A[For each point x_i:
Compute a_i] nodeS1C --> nodeS2B[For each point x_i:
Compute b_i] nodeS2A --> nodeS2C[s_i = b_i - a_i / max] nodeS2B --> nodeS2C end subgraph S3[Output Stage] nodeS2C --> nodeS3A[Per-point s_i] nodeS2C --> nodeS3B[Overall S = mean s_i] nodeS3B --> nodeS3C{Interpret} nodeS3C -- Near +1 --> nodeS3D[Well clustered] nodeS3C -- Near 0 --> nodeS3E[Overlapping] nodeS3C -- Near -1 --> nodeS3F[Misassigned] end style S1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S3 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeS1A fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS1B fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS1C fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS2A fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS2B fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS2C fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS3A fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS3B fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS3C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS3D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS3E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeS3F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Validation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA1[Raw Data X] --> nodeA2[Preprocessing:
Normalization and
Dimensionality Reduction] nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Clustering
Algorithm:
K-Means, Hierarchical,
DBSCAN, etc.] nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Discovered
Partition P] nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Internal
Validation Engine] nodeA4 --> nodeA6[External
Validation Engine] nodeA4 --> nodeA7[Relative
Validation Engine] nodeA5 --> nodeA8[Quality Score] nodeA6 --> nodeA9[Match Score] nodeA7 --> nodeA10[Optimal k] nodeA8 --> nodeA11[Decision
Aggregator] nodeA9 --> nodeA11 nodeA10 --> nodeA11 nodeA11 --> nodeA12[Final Report:
Cluster Validity and
Stability] style nodeA1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeA2 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA3 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeA5 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA6 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA7 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA8 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA9 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA10 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeA11 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeA12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define cluster validation. Differentiate between external and internal validation indices with one example each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Cluster validation is the process of objectively evaluating the quality of clustering results. * **External Indices:** Use a pre-known ground-truth label set for comparison. *Example: Rand Index, F-Measure.* (1 mark) * **Internal Indices:** Use only intrinsic data properties (distances, density) without any external reference. *Example: Silhouette Coefficient, Davies–Bouldin Index.* (1 mark) * **Key Difference:** External indices measure *agreement* with ground truth; internal indices measure *cohesion vs. separation* of the discovered clusters. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the Silhouette Coefficient. What does a negative value signify?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Silhouette Coefficient $s(i)$ for a point $x_i$ is defined as: $s(i) = (b(i) - a(i)) / \max\{a(i), b(i)\}$, where $a(i)$ is the mean distance to other points in the same cluster and $b(i)$ is the mean distance to points in the nearest other cluster. (2 marks) A **negative value** indicates that, on average, the point is *closer to points in a neighboring cluster* than to points in its own assigned cluster, suggesting the point may be **misassigned** or that the cluster structure is poor. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- **Q3. (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] (7 Marks)** *Explain External Validation Indices. Derive the Rand Index and Jaccard Coefficient from the pair-counting contingency table.* **Model Solution:** External validation indices compare the discovered partition $P$ with a ground-truth partition $P^*$. The foundation is the pair-counting matrix built from the contingency table entries $n_{ij} = \vert C_i \cap \Omega_j \vert$. For $N$ data points, total pairs $= \binom{N}{2}$. Each pair $(x_i, x_j)$ falls into one of four categories: 1. **SS** ($a$): Together in both $P$ and $P^*$. 2. **SD** ($b$): Together in $P$, separated in $P^*$. 3. **DS** ($c$): Separated in $P$, together in $P^*$. 4. **DD** ($d$): Separated in both. **Derivation of $a, b, c, d$ from contingency matrix:** Number of pairs together in $P$: $\sum_i \binom{n_{i\cdot}}{2}$ — these are split into $a$ (also together in $P^*$) and $b$ (separated in $P^*$). $\Rightarrow a + b = \sum_i \binom{n_{i\cdot}}{2}$ Number of pairs together in $P^*$: $\Rightarrow a + c = \sum_j \binom{n_{\cdot j}}{2}$ Number of pairs together in both: $\Rightarrow a = \sum_{i,j} \binom{n_{ij}}{2}$ Therefore: $b = \sum_i \binom{n_{i\cdot}}{2} - a$ $c = \sum_j \binom{n_{\cdot j}}{2} - a$ $d = \binom{N}{2} - (a + b + c)$ **Rand Index:** Fraction of pairs on which the two partitions agree: $RI = (a + d) / (a + b + c + d) = (a + d) / \binom{N}{2}$ — range $[0,1]$, higher is better. (3 marks) **Jaccard Coefficient:** Only counts "together" agreement, ignoring "separated" pairs: $JC = a / (a + b + c)$ — range $[0,1]$, higher is better. It penalizes false negatives more strictly. (3 marks) **Comparison:** RI includes the "both separated" agreement (often very large), making it biased toward high values; JC is more discriminative for sparse-pair problems. (1 mark) --- **Q3. (b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] (7 Marks)** *With a suitable example dataset, compute the Rand Index and Jaccard Coefficient. Discuss the limitations of these indices.* **Model Solution:** Consider 6 points with ground truth $P^* = \{1,2,3\}, \{4,5,6\}$ and discovered partition $P = \{1,2,4\}, \{3,5,6\}$. **Contingency Matrix:** $$ \mathbf{N} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$ Row sums: $n_{1\cdot}=3$, $n_{2\cdot}=3$. Column sums: $n_{\cdot 1}=3$, $n_{\cdot 2}=3$. $N=6$. **Compute $a$:** $a = \binom{2}{2} + \binom{1}{2} + \binom{1}{2} + \binom{2}{2} = 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 2$ **Compute $a+b$:** $a + b = \binom{3}{2} + \binom{3}{2} = 3 + 3 = 6 \Rightarrow b = 4$ **Compute $a+c$:** $a + c = \binom{3}{2} + \binom{3}{2} = 6 \Rightarrow c = 4$ **Compute $d$:** $d = \binom{6}{2} - 2 - 4 - 4 = 15 - 10 = 5$ **Rand Index:** $RI = (2 + 5) / 15 = 7/15 \approx 0.467$ (2 marks) **Jaccard Coefficient:** $JC = 2 / (2 + 4 + 4) = 2/10 = 0.200$ (2 marks) **Limitations:** (3 marks) 1. Both assume ground truth is available — often unrealistic. 2. RI is biased toward unity for large datasets with many pairs. 3. Neither metric handles multi-class cost asymmetry (errors in minority classes are weighted equally). 4. They cannot validate the *number* of clusters, only the *quality* against an existing reference. 5. Sensitive to label permutations and matching conventions. --- ### 5.3 KTU Part B — Alternate Choice (Full 14 Marks) --- **Q4. (a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (7 Marks)** *Explain Internal Validation Indices. Derive the Silhouette Coefficient with its geometric interpretation.* **Model Solution:** Internal validation indices evaluate cluster quality using only the data and the discovered labels — no ground truth required. **Cohesion Measure $a(i)$:** For a point $x_i$ in cluster $C_I$: $$ a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_I \vert - 1} \sum_{x_j \in C_I,\, j \neq i} d(x_i, x_j) $$ This is the mean intra-cluster distance — measures how tightly $x_i$ is bound to its own cluster. **Separation Measure $b(i)$:** The smallest mean distance from $x_i$ to any other cluster $C_J$: $$ b(i) = \min_{J \neq I} \frac{1}{\vert C_J \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_J} d(x_i, x_j) $$ **Silhouette Score:** $$ s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}} $$ **Geometric Interpretation:** (2 marks) * $b(i) \gg a(i) \Rightarrow s(i) \to +1$: Point is well inside its cluster, far from any boundary. * $b(i) \approx a(i) \Rightarrow s(i) \to 0$: Point lies on the cluster boundary. * $b(i) \ll a(i) \Rightarrow s(i) \to -1$: Point is closer to a foreign cluster; possibly misassigned. **Aggregate Metric:** $S = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i s(i)$. Range $[-1, +1]$, higher is better. (1 mark) **Advantages:** (1 mark) * Model-free, works for any distance metric. * Provides both global and per-point quality. * Useful for choosing *k* by selecting the $k$ that maximizes $S$. --- **Q4. (b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (7 Marks)** *Explain the Davies–Bouldin Index and Dunn Index. Compare their advantages and limitations.* **Model Solution:** **Davies–Bouldin (DB) Index:** (3 marks) $DB = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i} R_{ij}$ where $R_{ij} = (S_i + S_j) / M_{ij}$. * $S_i$ = average distance of points in $C_i$ to centroid $\mu_i$. * $M_{ij}$ = distance between centroids $\mu_i$ and $\mu_j$. * **Range:** $[0, \infty)$, **lower is better**. * **Logic:** For each cluster, finds the *worst* (most similar) neighbor, then averages. Smaller $R_{ij}$ means cluster $i$ is compact and well-separated from $j$. **Dunn Index (D):** (3 marks) $D = \frac{\min_{i \neq j} \delta(C_i, C_j)}{\max_m \Delta(C_m)}$ * $\delta(C_i, C_j)$ = smallest inter-cluster distance (e.g., min pairwise). * $\Delta(C_m)$ = largest intra-cluster diameter. * **Range:** $[0, \infty)$, **higher is better**. * **Logic:** A high $D$ means clusters are tightly packed internally and well-separated from each other. **Comparison:** (1 mark) | Aspect | DB | Dunn | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Computational cost | $O(k^2 \cdot N)$ | $O(k^2 \cdot N^2)$ | | Noise sensitivity | Low | High | | Non-convex clusters | Works well | Works well | | Best for | Quick *k* selection | Small datasets, fine analysis | --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Cluster Validation Questions:** > 1. **Formula confusion:** Students mix up the Rand Index numerator $(a+d)$ with the Jaccard numerator $(a)$. The Rand Index counts *both agreements*; the Jaccard only counts *positive agreement*. (–1 to –2 marks) > 2. **Direction error:** Writing that Davies–Bouldin or Dunn should be *maximized* — DB must be **minimized**; Dunn must be **maximized**. (–1 mark) > 3. **Missing assumptions:** Internal indices assume Euclidean distance and convex clusters. Stating this assumption earns an extra mark. > 4. **Pair-counting arithmetic error:** In the contingency table derivation, forgetting that $\sum_j \binom{n_{\cdot j}}{2} = a + c$ (not just $c$). This causes cascading errors. > 5. **Not specifying $k$:** When comparing internal indices, always state the number of clusters $k$ and total points $N$ in the answer. > 6. **Confusing external with internal:** External indices *require* ground truth — mentioning this explicitly earns full credit. ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Cluster Validation** * **Cluster Validation** evaluates: Tendency, Quality, and Stability of clustering. * **External Indices** require ground truth; built on **pair-counting** ($a, b, c, d$). * **Internal Indices** use intrinsic properties — distances and densities. * **Relative Indices** compare partitions across different $k$ or algorithm runs. * **Rand Index** = $(a+d)/\binom{N}{2}$ → maximize; **Jaccard** = $a/(a+b+c)$ → maximize; **FMI** uses geometric mean of pair precision/recall. * **Silhouette** → range $[-1,+1]$, maximize; positive = well clustered, negative = misassigned. * **Davies–Bouldin** → minimize; ratio of intra-cluster scatter to inter-cluster separation. * **Dunn Index** → maximize; ratio of minimum inter-cluster distance to maximum intra-cluster diameter. * **Calinski–Harabasz** → maximize; ratio of between-cluster to within-cluster sum of squares. * **Contingency matrix** $n_{ij} = \vert C_i \cap \Omega_j \vert$ is the foundation of all external indices. * **Key engineering applications:** Bioinformatics (gene clustering), image segmentation, customer analytics, anomaly detection. * **Most common exam trap:** Wrong optimal direction (max vs. min) for DB and Dunn. * **Always state the range and optimal direction** of any index in KTU answers.
